topics.title: Typical MEMS and Microsystems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Typical MEMS and Microsystems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of MEMS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)** are miniaturized mechanical and electro-mechanical elements (typically in the range of **1 µm to 1000 µm** in critical dimension) that are fabricated using batch-processing techniques originally developed for the integrated circuit (IC) industry. A MEMS device typically integrates sensors, actuators, mechanical structures, and electronics on a common silicon substrate to perform a sensing or actuation function at the micro-scale.

A **Microsystem**, in the broader sense, is the complete functional package consisting of the **MEMS core**, the surrounding **microelectronics** (signal conditioning, ADCs, drivers), the **packaging**, and the **interface** to the macroscopic world. While MEMS refers specifically to the moving/flexing microstructures, the microsystem is the integrated product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The term *Typical MEMS and Microsystems* encompasses (a) historical evolution, (b) generalized block-level architecture, (c) representative commercial devices (accelerometer, gyroscope, pressure sensor, microfluidic chip, inkjet printhead), and (d) the broad application taxonomy spanning automotive, biomedical, consumer electronics, and industrial domains.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are looking at the world through a powerful magnifying glass. Everything you normally see — motors, gears, pressure gauges, pumps, and even chemical laboratories — when shrunk to the size of a single grain of sand or smaller, still works. That is the essence of MEMS.

**Real-World Analogy (The Tiny Toolbox):** Think of a Swiss Army knife. A traditional Swiss Army knife combines a knife, scissors, screwdriver, file, and bottle opener in a single compact object. A MEMS chip does exactly the same thing — but instead of metal tools, it integrates an accelerometer, a gyroscope, a magnetometer, and a pressure sensor on a single silicon die the size of a pencil eraser. The "knife" of MEMS is a **proof mass** that moves under acceleration; the "screwdriver" might be a **micro-cantilever** that bends under stress; the "scissors" could be **micro-gears** that rotate via electrostatic comb drives. The silicon chip is the "handle" that holds all these tools together and connects them electrically to the outside world.

> [!TIP]
> The fundamental difference between a MEMS device and a conventional IC is that the MEMS structure **physically moves or deforms** in response to a stimulus, whereas a transistor only switches electrical signals. This mechanical motion is the key to converting physical phenomena (acceleration, pressure, rotation, chemical binding) into electrical signals.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in MEMS

The design and analysis of MEMS devices rely on a fixed set of physical constants. The following values are the **gold-standard** numbers used throughout the MEMS literature and KTU reference solutions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Constants for MEMS Scaling Analysis:**
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_0 = 101{,}325 \; \text{Pa}$
> - **Density of silicon (single-crystal, $\langle 100 \rangle$):** $\rho_{Si} = 2330 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
> - **Young's modulus of single-crystal silicon:** $E_{Si} = 190 \; \text{GPa}$ (anisotropic, varies with crystallographic direction)
> - **Density of air at STP:** $\rho_{air} = 1.204 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
> - **Dynamic viscosity of air at STP:** $\mu_{air} = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{Pa \cdot s}$
> - **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{F/m}$
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
> - **Typical MEMS feature size range:** **1 µm to 1000 µm**
> - **Typical MEMS die size range:** **0.1 mm² to 100 mm²**
> - **Typical cost target per die:** **\$0.10 to \$5.00** (consumer-grade)

## 1.4 Why MEMS Matter — The Broader Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rationale:** MEMS enables the realization of devices that are smaller, lighter, cheaper, and often more sensitive than their macroscopic counterparts. Batch fabrication (simultaneously producing hundreds of devices on a single wafer) drives down unit cost, while the small size enables new applications — such as in-vivo biomedical implants, smart-phone inertial navigation, and disposable lab-on-chip diagnostics — that are physically impossible at the macro-scale.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generalized Block-Level Architecture of a Microsystem

Every microsystem, regardless of its specific application, can be decomposed into **five fundamental building blocks**. This decomposition is the standard framework taught at KTU and forms the basis of multiple 14-mark questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Canonical Blocks of a Microsystem:**
> 1. **Sensing / Actuating Element (Transducer Core)** — converts the physical stimulus into a mechanical, electrical, or chemical signal, or vice versa.
> 2. **Signal Conditioning Electronics (ASIC)** — amplifies, filters, linearizes, and digitizes the raw transducer output.
> 3. **Data Processing Unit (MCU / DSP / Microprocessor)** — applies calibration, compensation (e.g., temperature), and higher-level algorithms.
> 4. **Communication Interface** — transmits the processed signal to the external world (analog voltage, I²C, SPI, USB, CAN, BLE, Wi-Fi).
> 5. **Packaging and Interconnects** — provides mechanical protection, environmental sealing, electrical connectivity, and thermal management.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

- **Step 1 — Physical Stimulus Detection:** The environment exerts a physical quantity (force, pressure, acceleration, chemical concentration) on the sensing element.
- **Step 2 — Transduction:** The MEMS structure deforms, displaces, or changes its electrical property (capacitance, resistance, piezoelectric charge) in response to the stimulus.
- **Step 3 — Signal Extraction:** The change in the electrical property is detected as a voltage, current, or charge variation by the readout circuit.
- **Step 4 — Conditioning:** The raw signal is amplified, low-pass filtered to remove high-frequency noise, and temperature-compensated.
- **Step 5 — Digitization and Processing:** An analog-to-digital converter (ADC) digitizes the signal; the digital data is processed, stored, or transmitted.
- **Step 6 — Output Delivery:** The processed information is delivered to the user (display, controller, cloud server) via a standardized communication interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **BioMEMS Perspective:** In a BioMEMS microsystem (the focus of PEEBT743), the sensing element often interfaces directly with a biological fluid (blood, saliva, cell suspension). The package must therefore provide **biocompatibility** (ISO 10993 compliance), **hermetic sealing** for the electronics, and a **fluidic interface** (microchannels, ports) for sample delivery.

## 2.2 Classification of MEMS Devices — Engineering Taxonomy

MEMS devices are conventionally classified by the **type of input stimulus** they handle or the **energy domain** in which they operate. The KTU syllabus expects familiarity with the following six major families.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Major MEMS Device Families:**
> - **Inertial Sensors:** Accelerometers, gyroscopes, inclinometers (sensing linear or angular acceleration).
> - **Pressure Sensors:** Piezoresistive, capacitive, and resonant pressure transducers.
> - **Optical MEMS (MOEMS):** Digital micromirror devices (DLP), optical switches, tunable gratings.
> - **Microfluidic / BioMEMS:** Micropumps, microvalves, lab-on-chip, DNA microarrays.
> - **RF MEMS:** Switches, varactors, filters, resonators for high-frequency communication.
> - **Microactuators:** Micromotors, comb drives, microgrippers, micropumps (active output devices).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Scaling Laws & Fundamental MEMS Equations

> [!NOTE]
> The table below summarizes every equation a KTU 2024 student is expected to memorize for Module 1. Each equation is a **high-yield** item — at least one of these appears in nearly every past ESE paper.

| Quantity / Concept | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scaling of length | $L_{micro} = k \cdot L_{macro}$ | $k$ is the scaling factor ($0 < k \ll 1$) | Establishes the geometric miniaturization ratio. |
| Scaling of area | $A_{micro} = k^2 \cdot A_{macro}$ | — | Area scales with $k^2$. |
| Scaling of volume | $V_{micro} = k^3 \cdot V_{macro}$ | — | Volume scales with $k^3$. |
| Surface-to-volume ratio | $\frac{S}{V} \propto \frac{1}{L}$ | $S$ = surface, $V$ = volume, $L$ = length | At micro-scale, $S/V$ becomes dominant, making surface forces (van der Waals, electrostatic) overpower body forces. |
| Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $A$ in $\text{m}^2$, $d$ in $\text{m}$, $C$ in $\text{F}$ | Base equation for capacitive MEMS (accelerometers, gyros). |
| Electrostatic force | $F_e = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2} \cdot V^2$ | $V$ in volts, $F_e$ in newtons | Driving force for comb drives and parallel-plate actuators. |
| Piezoresistive gauge factor | $GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | $\varepsilon$ = strain (dimensionless) | Sensitivity measure of piezoresistive pressure sensors. |
| Spring constant of cantilever | $k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ | $E$ in Pa, $w, t, L$ in m | Mechanical stiffness of a beam-type microstructure. |
| Resonant frequency of cantilever | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{t}{L^2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{E}{12 \rho}}$ | $\rho$ in $\text{kg/m}^3$, $f_0$ in Hz | Determines sensor bandwidth and mass-sensitivity. |
| Damping coefficient (slide-film) | $c = \frac{\mu A}{d}$ | $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, $A$ = area, $d$ = gap | Critical for designing under-damped MEMS resonators. |
| Reynolds number (microfluidics) | $Re = \frac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | $v$ = velocity, $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter | Indicates laminar ($Re < 2000$) vs. turbulent flow. |
| Capillary number | $Ca = \frac{\mu v}{\gamma}$ | $\gamma$ = surface tension (N/m) | Ratio of viscous to surface-tension forces in microfluidics. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for KTU Students:** When writing the spring constant expression in a KTU answer script or in a markdown table, never use the vertical bar symbol for the absolute value of an expression. Use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` instead — for example, write $\vert \Delta R \vert$ as `$\vert \Delta R \vert$`. The unescaped pipe character breaks markdown table rendering and will cost you presentation marks in the ESE.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

The five-block microsystem architecture is the universal blueprint used by every commercial MEMS company — from **Bosch Sensortec** (smartphone accelerometers) to **STMicroelectronics** (MEMS gyroscopes) to **Cepheid / BioFire Diagnostics** (BioMEMS PCR chips). Understanding this architecture is what allows an engineer to step into any MEMS project, regardless of whether the end product is a tire-pressure sensor or a cancer-diagnostic cartridge.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Scaling of Surface-to-Volume Ratio (A Classic KTU Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **single most frequently asked derivation** in the KTU PEEBT743 Module 1 section. It is the conceptual foundation of why MEMS behaves fundamentally differently from macro-scale mechanical systems.

**Problem Statement:** A cubic mechanical structure has a characteristic side length $L$. Show analytically how the surface-to-volume ratio scales as the structure is uniformly miniaturized by a factor $k$, and explain the physical implication for MEMS design.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.**
Consider a cube with side length $L$. The surface area and volume of the cube are, respectively,

$$S_{macro} = 6 L^2$$

$$V_{macro} = L^3$$

**Step 2 — Form the surface-to-volume ratio at the macro scale.**

$$\left( \frac{S}{V} \right)_{macro} = \frac{6 L^2}{L^3} = \frac{6}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Apply uniform miniaturization.**
After miniaturization, the new side length is $L_{micro} = k \cdot L$, where $k$ is the dimensionless scaling factor ($0 < k \ll 1$, e.g., $k = 0.001$ corresponds to shrinking the structure by a factor of 1000).

**Step 4 — Compute the new surface and volume.**

$$S_{micro} = 6 (k L)^2 = 6 k^2 L^2$$

$$V_{micro} = (k L)^3 = k^3 L^3$$

**Step 5 — Form the new surface-to-volume ratio.**

$$\left( \frac{S}{V} \right)_{micro} = \frac{6 k^2 L^2}{k^3 L^3} = \frac{6}{k L}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the ratio of the two surface-to-volume ratios.**

$$\frac{\left( \frac{S}{V} \right)_{micro}}{\left( \frac{S}{V} \right)_{macro}} = \frac{\frac{6}{k L}}{\frac{6}{L}} = \frac{1}{k}$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the result.**

$$\boxed{\frac{S_{micro}}{V_{micro}} = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{S_{macro}}{V_{macro}}}$$

Since $k \ll 1$, the surface-to-volume ratio **increases dramatically** as the structure is miniaturized. For example, a $k = 0.001$ scaling causes the surface-to-volume ratio to increase by a factor of **1000×**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Implication:** At the macro-scale, **volume forces** (gravity, inertia, magnetic body forces) dominate. At the micro-scale, **surface forces** (van der Waals, electrostatic, surface tension, viscous drag) dominate. This inversion of dominant force regimes is the fundamental reason why MEMS devices can be shaken, accelerated, and chemically tested without gravity overwhelming their operation, but are extremely susceptible to **stiction**, **dielectric charging**, and **capillary collapse**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capacitive Sensitivity of a Parallel-Plate MEMS Accelerometer

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation supports a standard 7-mark sub-question in the KTU ESE on the working principle of a capacitive MEMS accelerometer.

**Problem Statement:** A MEMS accelerometer consists of a proof mass suspended by four flexure springs of combined stiffness $k$, separated from a fixed bottom electrode by an air gap $d_0$. Derive the expression for the differential capacitance change as a function of applied acceleration $a$.

**Step 1 — Establish the static equilibrium displacement.**
When the accelerometer experiences an inertial acceleration $a$, the proof mass $m$ experiences an inertial force $F = m a$. This force deflects the mass downward by a distance $x$. At equilibrium, the spring force balances the inertial force:

$$F = k x \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = \frac{m a}{k}$$

**Step 2 — Write the expression for the initial (no-acceleration) capacitance.**
With the proof mass centered, the gap is $d_0$ on both sides, and the overlap area is $A$. The nominal differential capacitance (between the two fixed electrodes and the moving mass) is zero, but the absolute capacitance to one side is:

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the capacitances when the mass is displaced.**
When the mass moves down by $x$, the upper gap becomes $d_0 - x$ and the lower gap becomes $d_0 + x$. The two individual capacitances are:

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - x}$$

$$C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 + x}$$

**Step 4 — Form the differential capacitance (the sensor output).**

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \varepsilon_0 A \left( \frac{1}{d_0 - x} - \frac{1}{d_0 + x} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Combine the fractions over a common denominator.**

$$\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 A \left( \frac{(d_0 + x) - (d_0 - x)}{(d_0 - x)(d_0 + x)} \right) = \varepsilon_0 A \cdot \frac{2x}{d_0^2 - x^2}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the small-deflection approximation.**
For typical MEMS accelerometers, $x \ll d_0$, so $x^2 \ll d_0^2$ and the denominator simplifies to $d_0^2$:

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A \, x}{d_0^2}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the displacement expression from Step 1.**

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A}{d_0^2} \cdot \frac{m a}{k}$$

**Step 8 — Define the sensitivity.**

$$\boxed{S = \frac{\Delta C}{a} = \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A \, m}{k \, d_0^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** To increase the sensitivity $S$, the designer can (a) increase the proof mass $m$, (b) increase the overlap area $A$, (c) decrease the spring stiffness $k$, or (d) decrease the air gap $d_0$. However, decreasing $d_0$ also makes the device more susceptible to pull-in instability (when the electrostatic force exceeds the mechanical restoring force), so trade-offs are essential.

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical Microsystem

The mermaid diagram below illustrates the canonical five-block architecture of a microsystem, with the data and signal flow between blocks clearly shown. This diagram is the answer to one of the most common 7-mark sub-questions in the KTU PEEBT743 exam.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef inputBlock fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef coreBlock fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef electronBlock fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef processBlock fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef outputBlock fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000

    envBlock[Physical Environment Stimulus]:::inputBlock
    sensorBlock[MEMS Sensor and Actuator Core]:::coreBlock
    condBlock[Signal Conditioning ASIC Amplifier Filter]:::electronBlock
    adcBlock[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]:::electronBlock
    procBlock[Microcontroller and DSP Data Processing]:::processBlock
    commBlock[Communication Interface I2C SPI USB BLE]:::outputBlock
    userBlock[End User Display Cloud Controller]:::outputBlock

    envBlock --> sensorBlock
    sensorBlock --> condBlock
    condBlock --> adcBlock
    adcBlock --> procBlock
    procBlock --> commBlock
    commBlock --> userBlock
    userBlock -. Feedback Control .-> sensorBlock
```

## 4.2 Functional Taxonomy of Typical MEMS Devices

The mermaid diagram below shows the major families of typical MEMS devices and their representative commercial examples. This taxonomy diagram is the most efficient way to answer the common 7-mark question *"Classify MEMS devices with examples"*.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef root fill:#0d6efd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef family fill:#198754,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef device fill:#ffc107,stroke:#000,color:#000

    memsRoot[Typical MEMS Devices]:::root

    inertialBlock[Inertial MEMS]:::family
    accelBlock[Accelerometer Example Smartphone Screen Rotation]:::device
    gyroBlock[Gyroscope Example Drone Stabilization]:::device

    pressureBlock[Pressure MEMS]:::family
    piezoPressBlock[Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor Example Tire Pressure Monitoring]:::device
    capPressBlock[Capacitive Pressure Sensor Example Blood Pressure Catheter]:::device

    opticalBlock[Optical MEMS MOEMS]:::family
    dmdBlock[Digital Micromirror Device DLP Example Projector]:::device
    optSwBlock[Optical Switch Example Telecom Cross Connect]:::device

    bioBlock[BioMEMS and Microfluidic]:::family
    labChipBlock[Lab on Chip Example DNA Amplification PCR]:::device
    microPumpBlock[Micropump Example Drug Delivery Implant]:::device

    rfBlock[RF MEMS]:::family
    rfSwitchBlock[RF Switch Example Cell Phone Antenna Tuning]:::device
    rfFilterBlock[RF Filter Example 5G Front End Module]:::device

    memsRoot --> inertialBlock
    memsRoot --> pressureBlock
    memsRoot --> opticalBlock
    memsRoot --> bioBlock
    memsRoot --> rfBlock

    inertialBlock --> accelBlock
    inertialBlock --> gyroBlock
    pressureBlock --> piezoPressBlock
    pressureBlock --> capPressBlock
    opticalBlock --> dmdBlock
    opticalBlock --> optSwBlock
    bioBlock --> labChipBlock
    bioBlock --> microPumpBlock
    rfBlock --> rfSwitchBlock
    rfBlock --> rfFilterBlock
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Capacitive MEMS Accelerometer

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a **block-level functional architecture** that maps the working sequence of a typical capacitive MEMS accelerometer, rather than a literal cross-sectional drawing. This is the format accepted in KTU answer scripts when a true cross-sectional drawing cannot be hand-sketched.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef mech fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#000
    classDef elec fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#000
    classDef out fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#000

    accIn[External Acceleration Applied to Sensor Housing]:::mech
    proofMass[Proof Mass Experiences Inertial Force F equals m times a]:::mech
    dispCalc[Mechanical Displacement x equals m times a divided by k]:::mech
    capChange[Capacitance Change Capacitor 1 and Capacitor 2 Shift in Opposite Directions]:::elec
    diffCap[Differential Capacitance Delta C equals C1 minus C2 Computed]:::elec
    sigCond[Signal Conditioning Charge Amplifier and Low Pass Filter]:::elec
    digOut[Digital Output Voltage Proportional to Applied Acceleration]:::out

    accIn --> proofMass
    proofMass --> dispCalc
    dispCalc --> capChange
    capChange --> diffCap
    diffCap --> sigCond
    sigCond --> digOut
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define MEMS and differentiate it from a microsystem.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems):** MEMS are miniaturized mechanical and electro-mechanical devices fabricated using IC-compatible batch-processing techniques, with critical dimensions typically in the **1 µm to 1000 µm** range, that integrate moving mechanical structures with electronic circuitry on a common silicon substrate.

**Microsystem:** A microsystem is the complete functional product that incorporates the MEMS sensor/actuator core, the signal-conditioning electronics, the data-processing unit, the communication interface, and the package. It is a superset of MEMS.

**Key Difference:** MEMS refers specifically to the **micro-mechanical transducer** element that physically moves or deforms, whereas a microsystem is the **integrated end-product** that includes MEMS plus all the supporting electronics, packaging, and interfaces.

---

### Question 2: List any four application areas of MEMS with one example device for each.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Application Area | Example MEMS Device |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Automotive | Piezoresistive tire-pressure sensor |
| 2 | Consumer Electronics | Capacitive accelerometer in smartphones |
| 3 | Biomedical | Lab-on-chip for DNA amplification (PCR) |
| 4 | Optical Displays | Digital Micromirror Device (DLP) in projectors |
| 5 | Telecommunications | RF MEMS switch for antenna tuning |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A: MEMS Scaling Laws and Their Physical Implications

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Sub-question (a) — 7 Marks:**  
With the help of a suitable derivation, show how the surface-to-volume ratio of a mechanical structure changes when it is uniformly miniaturized by a factor $k$. Comment on the physical implications of this scaling for MEMS design.

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to **Derivation 1 in Section 3.1** for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final result is:

$$\frac{S_{micro}}{V_{micro}} = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{S_{macro}}{V_{macro}}$$

**Implications for MEMS Design (3 additional explanation marks):**

- **Dominance of surface forces:** At the micro-scale, surface forces such as van der Waals adhesion, electrostatic attraction, and surface tension (in fluids) dominate over body forces like inertia and gravity. This makes micro-structures prone to **stiction** — the permanent adhesion of a moving structure to its substrate after contact.
- **Reduced gravity dependence:** MEMS devices can operate in any orientation and under vibration without being significantly affected by gravity, enabling applications such as implantable biomedical devices.
- **Capillary collapse:** During wet-etching or drying of micro-structures, surface-tension forces at the liquid–air interface can deform or collapse delicate beams. This is why **critical-point drying** and **super-hydrophobic coatings** are used in MEMS fabrication.
- **Heat-transfer dominance:** Heat dissipation becomes efficient at the micro-scale because of the high surface-to-volume ratio, but thermal expansion effects can cause unwanted mechanical drift.

**Sub-question (b) — 7 Marks:**  
Explain the working principle of a capacitive MEMS accelerometer with a neat sketch of its structure. Derive the expression for its differential capacitance sensitivity.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Refer to **Derivation 2 in Section 3.2** for the complete derivation. The structure consists of:

- A **proof mass** suspended by four compliant flexure springs.
- Two **fixed electrodes** on the substrate, one above and one below the proof mass.
- An air gap $d_0$ between the mass and each fixed electrode.

When the housing is accelerated, the inertial mass lags behind, displacing by $x = ma/k$. This changes the two gap capacitances in opposite directions, producing a differential output:

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A \, m a}{k \, d_0^2}$$

The differential scheme provides excellent **common-mode rejection** of temperature drift, power-supply noise, and package stress, which is why it is the preferred architecture in commercial accelerometers from Analog Devices, STMicroelectronics, and Bosch.

---

### Question B: Working Principle and Comparative Analysis of MEMS Pressure Sensors

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Sub-question (a) — 7 Marks:**  
Describe the working principle of a piezoresistive MEMS pressure sensor. Derive the expression for the gauge factor of a piezoresistor and explain how it is used to convert pressure into an electrical signal.

**Model Solution for (a):**

A piezoresistive pressure sensor consists of a thin silicon diaphragm (typically a few tens of micrometres thick) clamped at its edges, with four piezoresistors diffused into its surface in a **Wheatstone-bridge configuration**. When pressure is applied from one side, the diaphragm deforms, and the longitudinal and transverse piezoresistors experience tensile and compressive strain, respectively.

**Derivation of the Gauge Factor:**

The resistance of a uniform conductor is:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

where $\rho$ is the resistivity, $L$ is the length, and $A$ is the cross-sectional area. Taking the logarithmic differential on both sides:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

For a rectangular resistor with width $w$ and thickness $t$, the area is $A = w t$. The change in area due to axial strain $\varepsilon_L = \Delta L / L$ and lateral strain $\varepsilon_T = -\nu \varepsilon_L$ (where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio) is:

$$\frac{\Delta A}{A} = \frac{\Delta w}{w} + \frac{\Delta t}{t} = 2 \varepsilon_T = -2 \nu \varepsilon_L$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon_L + 2 \nu \varepsilon_L = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2 \nu) \varepsilon_L$$

The **gauge factor (GF)** is defined as the ratio of fractional resistance change to applied strain:

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon_L} = \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon_L} + (1 + 2 \nu)$$

For metals, $(1 + 2\nu) \approx 1.5$ to 2.0 dominates. For silicon, the **piezoresistive term** $(\Delta \rho / \rho)/\varepsilon_L$ is extremely large (on the order of 100 to 200), so the gauge factor is dominated by the resistivity change:

$$\boxed{GF_{Si} \approx 100 \text{ to } 200}$$

The four piezoresistors in a Wheatstone bridge convert the diaphragm strain into a differential output voltage:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

where $V_{ex}$ is the bridge excitation voltage.

**Sub-question (b) — 7 Marks:**  
Compare the piezoresistive and capacitive transduction methods for MEMS pressure sensors in terms of sensitivity, temperature sensitivity, power consumption, and suitability for BioMEMS applications.

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Performance Parameter | Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor | Capacitive Pressure Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensitivity** | Moderate to high; determined by gauge factor (~100–200) and diaphragm strain. | Very high; $\Delta C / C$ can exceed 50% for small gaps. |
| **Temperature Sensitivity** | Significant; the piezoresistive coefficients of silicon are temperature-dependent, requiring active compensation. | Low; capacitance is a purely geometric quantity, only weakly affected by temperature. |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher; requires constant bridge excitation current, leading to self-heating. | Very low; capacitive sensors draw power only during measurement, ideal for battery-powered implants. |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** | Lower at small signals due to Johnson noise in the bridge resistors. | Higher; capacitive readout is virtually noiseless at the sensor element. |
| **Linearity** | Good, but non-linearity appears at high strain. | Highly linear for small deflections ($x \ll d_0$). |
| **Suitability for BioMEMS** | Moderate; temperature drift is problematic for in-vivo applications. | Excellent; low power, low temperature sensitivity, and compatibility with fluidic media make it the preferred choice for blood-pressure catheters and intraocular pressure sensors. |
| **Fabrication Complexity** | Simple; standard IC doping and metallization. | More complex; requires precise gap control and sealable cavities. |
| **Long-term Drift** | Higher; piezoresistor drift over time. | Very low; no drift in the geometric sensing element. |

**Conclusion for BioMEMS:** Capacitive transduction is generally preferred for implantable and disposable BioMEMS pressure sensors because of its low power consumption, low temperature sensitivity, and high long-term stability.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 1 Questions (PEEBT743):**
> 1. **Confusing MEMS with the full microsystem:** Many students use the two terms interchangeably. The 3-mark question *"Differentiate MEMS from a microsystem"* specifically tests this distinction — failure to draw a clear contrast costs all 3 marks.
> 2. **Skipping units in derivations:** KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for every missing unit. Always state the unit of every final answer (e.g., $\Delta C$ in farads, $x$ in metres, $S$ in F·s²/m or F/g).
> 3. **Forgetting the small-deflection approximation:** In capacitive derivations, students often write $\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 A \cdot 2x / (d_0^2 - x^2)$ and stop. You must explicitly state that for $x \ll d_0$, the denominator reduces to $d_0^2$ to obtain the linearized expression. This approximation step alone is worth 2 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the physical interpretation:** A derivation without a closing paragraph on physical meaning is graded lower. Always end every derivation with a "Physical Implication" or "Design Insight" statement — even 2 lines are enough to earn the interpretation marks.
> 5. **Confusing gauge factor with strain:** The gauge factor is a **dimensionless ratio**, not a strain. Writing "GF = strain" is a common error that costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Mermaid rendering for handwritten exam:** Diagrams are accepted as **hand-sketched block diagrams** in the KTU ESE. Mermaid / digital diagrams are not required and not accepted. Practice drawing clean, labeled block diagrams on A4 sheets before the exam.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Typical MEMS and Microsystems (Module 1):**
> - **MEMS** = micro-mechanical transducer element with moving/ deformable structures (size: 1 µm to 1000 µm).
> - **Microsystem** = MEMS + signal conditioning + data processing + communication + package.
> - **Five canonical blocks:** Sensor/Actuator core, Signal conditioning (ASIC), Data processing (MCU/DSP), Communication interface, Package.
> - **Major MEMS families:** Inertial, Pressure, Optical (MOEMS), BioMEMS/Microfluidic, RF, Microactuators.
> - **Scaling law (most important):** $\dfrac{S_{micro}}{V_{micro}} = \dfrac{1}{k} \cdot \dfrac{S_{macro}}{V_{macro}}$ — surface forces dominate at micro-scale.
> - **At micro-scale, dominant forces are:** van der Waals, electrostatic, surface tension, viscous drag — **not** gravity or inertia.
> - **Capacitive sensing equation:** $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$.
> - **Electrostatic actuation force:** $F_e = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2} \cdot V^2$.
> - **Piezoresistive gauge factor of silicon:** $\approx 100$ to $200$ — extremely high due to the piezoresistive effect.
> - **Cantilever spring constant:** $k = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$.
> - **Resonant frequency of a cantilever:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{t}{L^2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{12 \rho}}$.
> - **Microfluidic flow regime:** Laminar flow dominates (Re typically < 100) — mixing must be engineered, not assumed.
> - **Capillary number** $Ca = \dfrac{\mu v}{\gamma}$ governs droplet formation in digital microfluidics.
> - **Commercial examples to remember:** Smartphone accelerometer (Bosch / ST / InvenSense), DLP projector (Texas Instruments), tire-pressure sensor (Infineon / NXP), Lab-on-chip PCR (Cepheid / BioFire).
> - **BioMEMS-specific considerations:** Biocompatibility (ISO 10993), hermetic sealing of electronics, fluidic interfacing, low power for implantables.
> - **Remember the LaTeX rule:** Never use the unescaped vertical bar `|` in markdown tables for absolute value — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compare MEMS and BioMEMS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compare MEMS and BioMEMS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is MEMS?

**MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)** are miniaturized mechanical and electromechanical elements that are fabricated using techniques originally developed for the integrated circuit (IC) industry. They integrate mechanical components, sensors, actuators, and electronics on a common silicon substrate through microfabrication technologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (MEMS):** Microsystems that integrate mechanical elements, sensors, actuators, and electronics on a common silicon substrate, typically having feature sizes ranging from **1 µm to 1000 µm**, fabricated using batch-processing techniques.

**Conceptual Analogy (MEMS):**
Think of a MEMS device as a **"tiny mechanical toolbox on a chip."** Just as a Swiss Army knife combines scissors, blades, screwdrivers, and other tools into a single compact device, MEMS combines tiny gears, springs, beams, sensors, and circuits onto a single sliver of silicon smaller than a grain of rice. When you tilt your smartphone and the screen rotates, or when your car's airbag deploys, an accelerometer (a MEMS device) is hard at work.

---

### 1.2 What is BioMEMS?

**BioMEMS (Biological Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)** is a specialized sub-class of MEMS specifically engineered for **biological, medical, biochemical, and pharmaceutical applications**. They adapt MEMS fabrication principles to manipulate, analyze, and interact with biological entities such as cells, proteins, DNA, and bodily fluids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (BioMEMS):** A class of MEMS devices that interfaces biological components (cells, proteins, nucleic acids, biomolecules) with microfabricated structures, sensors, and actuators for applications in diagnostics, drug delivery, tissue engineering, biosensing, and lab-on-a-chip platforms.

**Conceptual Analogy (BioMEMS):**
Imagine **MEMS as a precision Swiss watch**, and **BioMEMS as a watch designed to be worn inside the human body to monitor blood sugar continuously.** BioMEMS is essentially a "biological-friendly" version of MEMS, where every component is reimagined to handle fluids, cells, and chemicals safely. It is the bridge between the silicon world and the biological world.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** BioMEMS is NOT a separate technology. It is MEMS technology **re-engineered for biological environments**. The same lithography, etching, and deposition techniques are used — but the materials, surface chemistry, and packaging are tailored to be biocompatible.

---

### 1.3 Why Compare MEMS and BioMEMS?

Comparing MEMS and BioMEMS is essential for KTU 2024 students because:
- It establishes the **technological lineage** (BioMEMS descends from MEMS).
- It highlights the **unique design constraints** introduced by biological systems.
- It clarifies the **domain-specific engineering choices** in materials, fabrication, and packaging.

| Parameter | MEMS | BioMEMS |
|---|---|---|
| Core Purpose | Sense/actuate physical signals | Interface with biological matter |
| Typical Scale | **1 µm – 1 mm** | **10 nm – 1 mm** (extends to molecular) |
| Working Environment | Air, vacuum, controlled industrial settings | Aqueous, physiological (**pH 7.4**, **37 °C**) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Lineage — How BioMEMS Evolved from MEMS

The evolution follows a clear hierarchical pathway:

1. **Step 1 — Mechanical Microsystems (MEMS):** Started with silicon-based pressure sensors and accelerometers. Purely electromechanical.
2. **Step 2 — Integration of Fluidics:** Microfluidic channels and valves were introduced for handling small fluid volumes (µL to pL).
3. **Step 3 — Biological Functionalization:** Surfaces were chemically modified (e.g., with antibodies, aptamers, or PEG) to specifically bind biomolecules.
4. **Step 4 — BioMEMS Emerges:** The system can now **detect, capture, and analyze** biological entities — completing the transformation into BioMEMS.

---

### 2.2 Detailed Comparison Framework (KTU Board-Ready)

The comparison is best analyzed across **six engineering dimensions** that are consistently tested in KTU 2024 ESE papers.

#### Dimension 1: Materials
- **MEMS:** Silicon (Si), polysilicon, silicon dioxide, silicon nitride, metals (Au, Al, Pt), SU-8 polymer.
- **BioMEMS:** Same base materials **PLUS** biocompatible coatings such as **PDMS (Polydimethylsiloxane)**, **PMMA**, **PEG (Polyethylene Glycol)**, **parylene-C**, **glass**, **hydrogels**, and **titanium**.

> [!NOTE]
> The addition of **PDMS** in BioMEMS is a major differentiator. PDMS is optically transparent, gas-permeable, biocompatible, and can be rapidly prototyped via soft lithography — making it ideal for cell-culture and organ-on-chip applications.

#### Dimension 2: Operating Environment
- **MEMS:** Operates in air, vacuum, or inert gas. **Temperature range:** typically **-40 °C to +125 °C**.
- **BioMEMS:** Operates in aqueous physiological buffers, blood, saliva, urine, or cell-culture media. **Temperature:** often maintained at **37 °C (body temperature)**. **pH:** typically **7.4**.

#### Dimension 3: Fabrication Techniques
- **MEMS:** Bulk micromachining, surface micromachining, LIGA, deep reactive ion etching (DRIE), wafer bonding.
- **BioMEMS:** All MEMS techniques **PLUS** soft lithography, replica molding, micro-contact printing, and 3D bioprinting.

#### Dimension 4: Packaging and Sterilization
- **MEMS:** Hermetic wafer-level packaging, ceramic/metal housings.
- **BioMEMS:** Sterile packaging is mandatory. Methods include **autoclaving**, **UV exposure**, **ethylene oxide (EtO) treatment**, and **gamma irradiation** — each of which imposes material constraints.

#### Dimension 5: Sensing Mechanism
- **MEMS:** Piezoresistive, capacitive, piezoelectric, optical, resonant.
- **BioMEMS:** All of the above **PLUS** electrochemical (amperometric, potentiometric), bio-affinity (antigen-antibody), enzymatic (glucose oxidase), and DNA-hybridization-based detection.

#### Dimension 6: Cost and Application Domain
- **MEMS:** Cost-optimized for mass markets (smartphones, automotive, IoT). Unit cost as low as **a few cents per device**.
- **BioMEMS:** Cost is balanced against clinical validation, regulatory compliance (FDA, CE), and sterility — making per-unit cost higher, but **value per test dramatically lower** for diagnostics.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

While comparison is mostly conceptual, the following equations are used in MEMS/BioMEMS device performance analysis and frequently appear in KTU numericals:

| Concept | Equation | Variable Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacitive Sensing | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $\varepsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space, $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity, $A$ = electrode area, $d$ = gap | Farads (F) |
| Piezoresistive Output | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$ | $\pi$ = piezoresistive coefficients, $\sigma$ = stresses | Dimensionless |
| Reynolds Number (Microfluidics) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | $\rho$ = density, $v$ = velocity, $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter, $\mu$ = viscosity | Dimensionless |
| Péclet Number (BioMEMS transport) | $Pe = \dfrac{v L}{D}$ | $v$ = velocity, $L$ = characteristic length, $D$ = diffusion coefficient | Dimensionless |
| Capillary Pressure | $P_{cap} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | $\gamma$ = surface tension, $\theta$ = contact angle, $r$ = channel radius | Pascals (Pa) |
| BioMEMS Surface Binding Kinetics | $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = k_{on} C (1 - \theta) - k_{off} \theta$ | $\theta$ = surface coverage, $C$ = analyte concentration, $k_{on}, k_{off}$ = rate constants | s$^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** In BioMEMS problems, the **Reynolds number is almost always < 100**, confirming laminar flow. Students must explicitly state this assumption in their derivations to earn full marks.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MEMS Engineering Utility:** Consumer electronics (gyroscopes, accelerometers), automotive (TPMS sensors, airbag crash sensors), industrial (pressure transducers), aerospace (inertial measurement units).
- **BioMEMS Engineering Utility:** Point-of-care diagnostics (COVID-19 rapid antigen tests use microfluidic BioMEMS), implantable drug-delivery pumps (e.g., insulin micro-pumps), DNA microarrays, neural probes, organ-on-chip disease models, and single-cell analysis platforms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

Let us systematically derive the **key engineering differences** by walking through the design logic for a typical device.

#### Case Study Comparison: A Pressure Sensor

**Scenario:** Design a pressure sensor to measure **(a) automotive tire pressure (MEMS)** and **(b) intra-ocular pressure in a glaucoma patient (BioMEMS)**.

**Step 1 — Identify the working medium.**
- (a) Tire: dry air at **2–3 atm** → MEMS-compatible.
- (b) Eye: aqueous humor (salt-water solution) at body temperature → requires biocompatible, sealed, sterilizable package.

**Step 2 — Select the diaphragm material.**

The deflection $\delta$ of a circular diaphragm under pressure $P$ is given by:

$$
\delta = \dfrac{P a^4}{64 D}
$$

where $D$ is the flexural rigidity:

$$
D = \dfrac{E h^3}{12 (1 - \nu^2)}
$$

Here, $E$ is Young's modulus, $h$ is diaphragm thickness, $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio, and $a$ is the diaphragm radius.

**Step 3 — Compare material choices.**

For MEMS, silicon with $E \approx 170$ GPa is ideal — high stiffness for small deflections and high resonance frequency.

For BioMEMS, if we use the **same silicon**, the package must be hermetically sealed against fluid. Alternatively, we may switch to **parylene-C** ($E \approx 3$ GPa) for an implantable, flexible device.

**Step 4 — Compute the sensitivity trade-off.**

The capacitive sensitivity is:

$$
S = \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial P} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d^2} \cdot \dfrac{\partial A_{eff}}{\partial P}
$$

In BioMEMS, the larger allowable deformation (due to softer parylene-C diaphragm) increases $\partial A_{eff} / \partial P$, **boosting sensitivity** — but reduces mechanical robustness.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
The same physical principle (diaphragm deflection → capacitance change) is used, but **materials, packaging, sterilization, and readout electronics** differ fundamentally between MEMS and BioMEMS.

---

### 3.2 Quantitative Derivation — Why Microfluidic Flow is Always Laminar in BioMEMS

The Reynolds number characterizes the flow regime:

$$
Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}
$$

**Step 1 — Insert typical BioMEMS values.**

For a microchannel in a lab-on-chip:
- $\rho \approx 1000$ kg/m³ (water-based buffer)
- $v \approx 1$ mm/s = $10^{-3}$ m/s
- $D_h \approx 100$ µm = $10^{-4}$ m
- $\mu \approx 10^{-3}$ Pa·s (water at 37 °C)

**Step 2 — Substitute into the equation.**

$$
Re = \dfrac{(10^3)(10^{-3})(10^{-4})}{10^{-3}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
Re = \dfrac{10^{-4}}{10^{-3}} = 10^{-1} = 0.1
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

Since $Re = 0.1 \ll 2300$ (the standard pipe-flow turbulence threshold), the flow is **deeply laminar**. This is a signature feature of BioMEMS microfluidic systems — and it is why diffusion dominates mass transport, enabling predictable molecular separation and mixing strategies.

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Sensor-Selection Logic (Analytical Framework)

**Step 1 — Define the analyte.**
- Physical quantity (pressure, acceleration) → use classical **MEMS sensor**.
- Chemical/biological species (glucose, DNA, virus) → use **BioMEMS sensor** with biorecognition layer.

**Step 2 — Match the transduction principle.**

$$
\text{Signal Chain:} \quad \text{Analyte} \rightarrow \text{Biorecognition} \rightarrow \text{Transduction} \rightarrow \text{Electrical Output}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify biocompatibility.**
- The selected materials must pass **ISO 10993** cytotoxicity testing.
- The packaging must survive **at least 30 days** of immersion at 37 °C in PBS for short-term implants.

**Step 4 — Validate the limit of detection (LOD).**

$$
LOD = \dfrac{3 \sigma_{blank}}{S}
$$

where $\sigma_{blank}$ is the standard deviation of blank measurements and $S$ is the slope of the calibration curve. This is unique to BioMEMS and rarely required for plain MEMS.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Domain Relationship Block Diagram

```mermaid

```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Comparison Flow


### 4.3 Material and Environment Comparison Block

```mermaid

    ax2 --> ax2b[BioMEMS Aqueous 37 C pH 7.4]
    ax3 --> ax3a[MEMS Hermetic Ceramic]

    ax4 --> ax4a[MEMS Not Required]
    ax4 --> ax4b[BioMEMS Autoclave EtO Gamma UV]
```
