<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Gallium Arsenide

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)** is a binary III–V compound semiconductor formed by combining a Group III element (Gallium, Ga, atomic number **31**) with a Group V element (Arsenic, As, atomic number **33**). It possesses a direct bandgap of approximately **1.42 eV** at room temperature (300 K) and exhibits a zinc-blende (sphalerite) crystal structure with a lattice constant of **5.653 Å**. It is a cornerstone substrate material in BioMEMS, RF-MEMS, optoelectronics, and high-speed integrated circuits.

In the context of **PEEBT743 – Fundamentals of BioMEMS and Microfluidics**, GaAs is treated as a key *piezoelectric and semiconductor substrate* enabling mechanical-electrical coupling, optical biosensing, and high-frequency transducer fabrication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GaAs is a mandatory introductory material in Module 1 because it transitions the student from passive structural materials (Silicon, glass) to *active multifunctional semiconductors* used in transducer design.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Express Highway vs. City Road" Model

Imagine two different road networks for cars (electrons):

- **Silicon (Si)** is like a **regular city road** — bumpy, with many intersections (defects) and stop signals (phonon scattering). Cars (electrons) move, but slowly. This represents Si's lower electron mobility (~**1400 cm²/V·s**).
- **Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)** is like a **smooth multi-lane expressway** — fewer intersections, higher speed limit, less traffic. Electrons in GaAs travel at mobility up to **8500 cm²/V·s**, almost 6× faster than in Si.

This speed advantage is exactly why GaAs is used in:
- High-frequency RF BioMEMS sensors
- High-speed photodetectors in lab-on-chip systems
- Piezoelectric MEMS resonators operating in the GHz range

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Recall Card:**
> - Symbol: **GaAs**
> - Type: Direct bandgap, III–V compound semiconductor
> - Bandgap: **1.42 eV** (at 300 K)
> - Electron mobility: **~8500 cm²/V·s** (undoped)
> - Hole mobility: **~400 cm²/V·s**
> - Crystal structure: Zinc blende (FCC sublattices)
> - Density: **5.32 g/cm³**
> - Melting point: **1238 °C**

## 1.3 Classification of GaAs Within BioMEMS

| Classification Dimension | Position of GaAs |
|---|---|
| By material family | Compound (III–V) semiconductor |
| By bandgap type | **Direct bandgap** |
| By piezoelectric behaviour | **Piezoelectric** (zinc-blende family) |
| By biocompatibility | Moderate; requires passivation (Si₃N₄, SiO₂) |
| By optical activity | **Strong** — emits/absorbs light efficiently |
| By mechanical rigidity | High Young's modulus ~ **85 GPa** |

## 1.4 Why GaAs Matters in BioMEMS — A Bird's-Eye View

GaAs is selected in BioMEMS not because it is cheap (it is not) but because it offers a unique *combination* of properties unavailable in silicon:

1. **Direct bandgap → optical biosensing** (fluorescence, surface plasmon resonance, label-free detection).
2. **High electron mobility → GHz-range acoustic resonators** for mass-sensing biomolecules.
3. **Piezoelectric coupling → MEMS transducers** without external piezo layers.
4. **Semi-insulating substrate → low-loss RF MEMS** switches and antennas.
5. **Tunable surface chemistry** — GaAs can be functionalised with thiols, silanes, or biomolecules for biosensor coatings.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Hook (KTU 2024):** When asked *why GaAs over Si in BioMEMS*, always answer in terms of the *triad*: **(i) high electron mobility, (ii) direct optical bandgap, and (iii) piezoelectricity.** This single triad earns full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Atomic Structure and Bonding

GaAs is formed by sp³ hybridisation of Gallium (3s²3p¹ → 3 sp³ orbitals with one hole) and Arsenic (4s²4p³ → 4 sp³ orbitals filled). Each Ga atom shares its three valence electrons with three neighbouring As atoms, and vice-versa, producing four covalent bonds per atom in a **tetrahedral geometry**.

The bonding energy and covalent radius mismatch (Ga ≈ 1.26 Å, As ≈ 1.20 Å) introduce a slight **ionic character** (~3%) to the otherwise covalent bond, giving GaAs a small piezoelectric constant.

## 2.2 Crystal Structure: The Zinc-Blende Lattice

The zinc-blende structure consists of **two interpenetrating face-centred cubic (FCC) sublattices**:

- One FCC sublattice of Ga atoms.
- One FCC sublattice of As atoms, displaced by **¼, ¼, ¼** along the body diagonal.

Each atom is tetrahedrally coordinated to four atoms of the *opposite* species. The space group is **F-43m (No. 216)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crystallographic Identifier for KTU:**
> - Structure: **Zinc Blende (Sphalerite)**
> - Space group: **F-43m**
> - Lattice constant: **a = 5.6533 Å**
> - Nearest neighbour distance: **a√3/4 ≈ 2.448 Å**

## 2.3 Band Structure and the Direct Bandgap

The conduction band minimum (CBM) and valence band maximum (VBM) occur at the **same k-vector (Γ-point, k = 0)** in the Brillouin zone. This is the **direct bandgap** signature and is the *single most important property* for optoelectronic BioMEMS devices.

Consequence: an electron in the CBM can recombine with a hole in the VBM by emitting a **photon directly**, with no need for a phonon to conserve momentum. This gives:

- High **internal quantum efficiency** for LEDs and laser diodes.
- Strong **photoluminescence**, useful in fluorescent biosensors.
- Efficient **electro-optic modulators** for lab-on-chip optical readout.

## 2.4 Carrier Concentration and Intrinsic Statistics

For an intrinsic (undoped) semiconductor, the carrier concentration in the conduction band is given by the standard Fermi-Dirac integral:

$$n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-E_g}{2k_B T}\right)$$

where:
- $N_C$ = effective density of states in the conduction band = $2 \left( \dfrac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2} \right)^{3/2}$
- $N_V$ = effective density of states in the valence band = $2 \left( \dfrac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2} \right)^{3/2}$
- $E_g$ = bandgap energy (**1.42 eV** for GaAs at 300 K)
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant (**1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**)
- $T$ = absolute temperature
- $m_e^*, m_h^*$ = effective masses of electrons and holes

At 300 K, GaAs has $n_i \approx 2 \times 10^6 \text{ cm}^{-3}$, much lower than Si ($1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³), because of the larger bandgap.

## 2.5 Temperature Dependence of the Bandgap (Varshni's Equation)

$$E_g(T) = E_g(0) - \dfrac{\alpha T^2}{T + \beta}$$

For GaAs:
- $E_g(0) = 1.519$ eV (at 0 K)
- $\alpha = 5.405 \times 10^{-4}$ eV/K
- $\beta = 204$ K

This is critical for BioMEMS temperature sensors and for explaining drift in optical biosensor readouts.

## 2.6 Piezoelectric Coupling in GaAs

GaAs exhibits piezoelectric coefficients (in C/m²):

$$e_{14} = -0.16 \text{ C/m}^2$$

The induced polarisation under shear stress $\sigma_{ij}$ follows:

$$P_i = e_{ijk} \sigma_{jk}$$

This makes GaAs attractive for **acoustic wave BioMEMS** (FBAR, SAW) operating without an external piezoelectric film.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Typical Value (GaAs @ 300 K) | KTU Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bandgap energy | $E_g$ | **1.42 eV** | Direct bandgap justification |
| 2 | Lattice constant | $a$ | **5.653 Å** | XRD / heteroepitaxy |
| 3 | Electron mobility | $\mu_e$ | **8500 cm²/V·s** | High-frequency devices |
| 4 | Hole mobility | $\mu_h$ | **400 cm²/V·s** | p-type device design |
| 5 | Intrinsic carrier conc. | $n_i$ | $2 \times 10^6$ cm⁻³ | Semi-insulating property |
| 6 | Relative permittivity | $\varepsilon_r$ | **13.1** | Capacitive sensor design |
| 7 | Density | $\rho$ | **5.32 g/cm³** | Mass loading in resonators |
| 8 | Young's modulus | $Y$ | **85 GPa** | Mechanical stiffness |
| 9 | Melting point | $T_m$ | **1238 °C** | Thermal budget |
| 10 | Piezoelectric constant | $e_{14}$ | **−0.16 C/m²** | Piezo-MEMS design |
| 11 | Varshni equation | $E_g(T) = E_g(0) - \alpha T^2/(T+\beta)$ | $\alpha=5.4\times10^{-4}$ eV/K, $\beta=204$ K | Temperature sensors |
| 12 | Conductivity | $\sigma = q(n\mu_e + p\mu_h)$ | $\sim 10^{-7}$ S/m (semi-insulating) | RF loss estimation |
| 13 | Thermal conductivity | $k_{th}$ | **0.55 W/cm·K** | Thermal management in BioMEMS |
| 14 | Refractive index | $n$ | **3.5** (at 1.5 µm) | Waveguide / SPR biosensors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorise **rows 1, 2, 3, 7, 8, 14** — these are the most frequently tested numerical/comparative values in KTU Module 1 questions.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Specific Use of GaAs | Property Exploited |
|---|---|---|
| Optical BioMEMS | Fluorescence biosensor substrates | Direct bandgap (1.42 eV) |
| RF-MEMS | Low-loss resonators, switches | High $\mu_e$, semi-insulating |
| Acoustic BioMEMS | SAW/FBAR mass sensors for proteins | Piezoelectric $e_{14}$ |
| Lab-on-Chip | Integrated photodetectors | Efficient photon absorption |
| Implantable BioMEMS | GaAs-based neural probes | High electron mobility, miniaturisation |
| Solar-powered BioMEMS | High-efficiency cells | Direct bandgap, high absorption coefficient |

## 2.9 Why GaAs is Comparatively Less Used than Silicon

| Limitation | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|
| **High cost** (single-crystal wafer ~10× Si) | Limited to niche high-value BioMEMS |
| **Brittle & mechanically fragile** | Difficult bulk micromachining |
| **Toxicity of As** | Specialised EHS handling required |
| **Native oxide is poor** (Ga₂O₃ is unstable) | Cannot use simple SiO₂-based process flow |
| **No stable native passivation** | Requires deposited Si₃N₄/Al₂O₃ |
| **Lower mechanical Q-factor** at low freq. | Si dominates inertial BioMEMS |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** KTU answers often wrongly state that GaAs is *more* biocompatible than Si. The truth is GaAs requires **explicit surface passivation** (e.g., Si₃N₄) before any biological exposure. Always mention passivation when biocompatibility is discussed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Intrinsic Carrier Concentration for GaAs

**Goal:** Compute $n_i$ for GaAs at 300 K and compare with Silicon.

### Step 1 — Effective Density of States Expressions

The conduction band of GaAs has a single isotropic minimum at $\Gamma$, with effective mass $m_e^* = 0.067\, m_0$. The valence band has heavy-hole and light-hole bands:

$$m_{hh}^* = 0.50\, m_0, \quad m_{lh}^* = 0.087\, m_0$$

Effective density of states in the conduction band:

$$N_C = 2 \left( \dfrac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2} \right)^{3/2}$$

Effective density of states in the valence band (sum of heavy and light hole bands):

$$N_V = 2 \left( \dfrac{2\pi m_{hh}^* k_B T}{h^2} \right)^{3/2} + 2 \left( \dfrac{2\pi m_{lh}^* k_B T}{h^2} \right)^{3/2}$$

### Step 2 — Substitution of Constants

Using:
- $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $T = 300$ K
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s

Compute the prefactor:

$$\dfrac{2\pi m_0 k_B T}{h^2} = \dfrac{2\pi (9.11\times 10^{-31})(1.38\times 10^{-23})(300)}{(6.626\times 10^{-34})^2}$$

Numerical simplification:

$$= \dfrac{2.369 \times 10^{-50}}{4.39 \times 10^{-67}} = 5.40 \times 10^{16} \text{ m}^{-2}$$

### Step 3 — Conduction-Band DOS

$$N_C = 2 (0.067 \times 5.40 \times 10^{16})^{3/2} \text{ m}^{-3}$$

$$= 2 (3.62 \times 10^{15})^{3/2} = 2 (2.175 \times 10^{23}) = 4.35 \times 10^{23} \text{ m}^{-3}$$

Convert to cm⁻³:

$$N_C = 4.35 \times 10^{17} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

### Step 4 — Valence-Band DOS

$$N_V \approx 2(0.50 \times 5.40 \times 10^{16})^{3/2} + 2(0.087 \times 5.40 \times 10^{16})^{3/2}$$

$$= 2(2.70\times10^{16})^{3/2} + 2(4.70\times10^{15})^{3/2}$$

$$= 2(1.404\times10^{24}) + 2(3.22\times10^{23})$$

$$= 2.81\times10^{24} + 6.44\times10^{23} = 3.45\times10^{24} \text{ m}^{-3}$$

$$N_V = 3.45 \times 10^{18} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

### Step 5 — Intrinsic Carrier Concentration

$$n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-E_g}{2k_B T}\right)$$

Compute the geometric mean:

$$\sqrt{N_C N_V} = \sqrt{(4.35\times10^{17})(3.45\times10^{18})} = \sqrt{1.50\times10^{36}} = 1.22\times10^{18} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

Compute the exponential term:

$$\dfrac{E_g}{2k_B T} = \dfrac{1.42}{(2)(8.617\times 10^{-5})(300)} = \dfrac{1.42}{0.0517} = 27.47$$

$$\exp(-27.47) = 1.16 \times 10^{-12}$$

Final result:

$$n_i = 1.22\times 10^{18} \times 1.16\times 10^{-12} = 1.42 \times 10^{6} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

This matches the accepted GaAs intrinsic carrier concentration of $\sim 2 \times 10^6$ cm⁻³, and is **four orders of magnitude lower than Si's $n_i$**, which is the root cause of GaAs's semi-insulating behaviour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Because $E_g$ is large, very few electrons are thermally excited across the gap, leaving GaAs nearly intrinsic. This low $n_i$ is precisely why GaAs substrates are excellent electrical *insulators* in RF BioMEMS, suppressing parasitic substrate coupling.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: GaAs vs. Si vs. InP for BioMEMS Substrates

| Property | Silicon (Si) | Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) | Indium Phosphide (InP) | KTU-Most-Tested Comparison |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bandgap (eV) | 1.12 (indirect) | **1.42 (direct)** | 1.35 (direct) | GaAs > InP > Si |
| Electron mobility (cm²/V·s) | 1400 | **8500** | 5400 | GaAs is highest |
| Hole mobility (cm²/V·s) | 450 | 400 | 200 | Si slightly higher |
| Lattice constant (Å) | 5.431 | **5.653** | 5.869 | Lattice mismatch matters |
| Density (g/cm³) | 2.33 | 5.32 | 4.81 | Affects inertial BioMEMS |
| Piezoelectric | No | **Yes** ($e_{14}$ = −0.16) | Yes | Si is centrosymmetric, no piezo |
| Native oxide quality | Excellent (SiO₂) | **Poor** (Ga₂O₃ unstable) | Poor | Si dominates in CMOS |
| Cost (per wafer) | Low | High | Very high | Economic disadvantage of GaAs |
| Biocompatibility | Excellent (with coating) | Moderate (needs passivation) | Limited | Si preferred for implants |
| Optical efficiency | Poor (indirect) | **Excellent (direct)** | Excellent | GaAs/InP for optical BioMEMS |

## 3.3 Derivation: Resonant Frequency of a GaAs FBAR (Film Bulk Acoustic Resonator)

**Goal:** Estimate the resonance frequency of a GaAs piezoelectric resonator used as a mass BioMEMS sensor.

The fundamental thickness-mode resonance frequency is:

$$f_0 = \dfrac{v_a}{2 t}$$

where $v_a$ is the longitudinal acoustic velocity in GaAs and $t$ is the membrane thickness.

For GaAs, $v_a \approx 4730$ m/s along the [100] direction. For a typical BioMEMS membrane of $t = 2\,\mu$m:

$$f_0 = \dfrac{4730}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.18 \text{ GHz}$$

Such GHz resonators are highly sensitive to mass loading. The mass sensitivity is:

$$S_m = \lim_{\Delta m \to 0} \dfrac{\Delta f}{\Delta m} = -\dfrac{f_0^2}{\rho \, v_a \, A}$$

where $A$ is the active resonator area and $\rho = 5.32$ g/cm³ is GaAs density.

For a 100 µm × 100 µm device:

$$S_m = -\dfrac{(1.18 \times 10^9)^2}{(5320)(4730)(1\times 10^{-8})}$$

$$= -\dfrac{1.39\times 10^{18}}{2.52\times 10^{-1}} = -5.5 \times 10^{18} \text{ Hz/kg}$$

Equivalent surface mass sensitivity:

$$S_s = S_m \cdot A = -5.5 \times 10^{18} \times 1 \times 10^{-8} = -5.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ Hz·m}^2/\text{kg}$$

$$= -55 \text{ Hz·cm}^2/\text{ng}$$

This is comparable to the gold-standard QCM (Quartz Crystal Microbalance), confirming GaAs FBARs as viable **mass BioMEMS** transducers for protein/DNA detection.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU 2024:** Derivation problems (14-mark) often ask *"Show that GaAs is suitable for GHz BioMEMS mass sensing."* This exact derivation, with these three equations, is the canonical answer path.

## 3.4 Bandgap Engineering with AlGaAs (Conceptual)

Although pure GaAs is the focus, the BioMEMS reader must know that **AlₓGa₁₋ₓAs** is grown epitaxially on GaAs with negligible lattice mismatch for $x < 0.45$, enabling bandgap tuning:

$$E_g(\text{Al}_x\text{Ga}_{1-x}\text{As}) = 1.424 + 1.247x \quad (0 \leq x \leq 0.45)$$

This enables **quantum-well infrared photodetectors (QWIPs)** and **HEMT structures** integrated with BioMEMS, all on a GaAs platform.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Crystal Structure Block Diagram (Zinc-Blende)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[GaAs Lattice] --> B[FCC Sublattice of Gallium]

    B --> B1[Ga atoms at corner and face centres]
    C --> C1[As atoms at 1/4 1/4 1/4 displaced FCC]
    B1 --> D[Tetrahedral coordination: each Ga bonded to 4 As]
    C1 --> D
    D --> E[Lattice constant a equals 5.653 Angstrom]
    E --> F[Nearest neighbour distance equals a times sqrt 3 over 4]
    D --> G[Space group F-43m, point group Td]
    G --> H[Allows piezoelectric coefficient e14 not equal to zero]
```

    S5 --> S7[Digital Readout and Concentration Output]

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**Q: Define Gallium Arsenide. Why is it classified as a direct bandgap semiconductor? Mention two of its key electronic properties.**

**Model Answer:**

Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) is a binary **III–V compound semiconductor** formed from Gallium (Group III) and Arsenic (Group V), with the chemical formula **GaAs**. It crystallises in the **zinc-blende structure** with lattice constant **5.653 Å** and has a direct bandgap of **1.42 eV** at 300 K.

It is classified as a **direct bandgap** semiconductor because the conduction band minimum (CBM) and valence band maximum (VBM) both occur at the **Γ-point (k = 0)** of the Brillouin zone, allowing electron-hole recombination to occur with direct photon emission and no momentum-change phonon participation.

**Two key electronic properties:**
1. **High electron mobility ≈ 8500 cm²/V·s** (≈ 6× that of Si), enabling high-frequency operation.
2. **Semi-insulating behaviour** with intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i \approx 2 \times 10^6$ cm⁻³, due to its wide bandgap.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**
**Q: List four advantages of GaAs over Silicon for BioMEMS applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Advantage | Engineering Significance in BioMEMS |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Direct bandgap (1.42 eV)** | Enables integrated optical biosensors, LEDs, photodetectors on-chip |
| 2 | **High electron mobility (~8500 cm²/V·s)** | Permits GHz-range acoustic/electrical BioMEMS resonators |
| 3 | **Piezoelectricity (e₁₄ = −0.16 C/m²)** | Allows self-transduction without deposited PZT/AlN films |
| 4 | **Semi-insulating substrate** | Reduces RF losses and parasitic coupling in wireless implantable BioMEMS |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE follows strict internal choice: each question in Part B is a 14-mark problem with two 7-mark sub-parts. The student answers **either Option A or Option B**.

---

### Question B — Option A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q: (a)** With neat sketches, describe the **zinc-blende crystal structure of Gallium Arsenide**. Explain why GaAs exhibits piezoelectric behaviour whereas Silicon does not. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **intrinsic carrier concentration equation** and compute $n_i$ for GaAs at 300 K given $N_C = 4.35 \times 10^{17}$ cm⁻³, $N_V = 3.45 \times 10^{18}$ cm⁻³, and $E_g = 1.42$ eV. Comment on why this value makes GaAs suitable for semi-insulating substrates. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Crystal Structure and Piezoelectricity:**

The zinc-blende structure of GaAs consists of **two interpenetrating FCC sublattices**, one of Ga and one of As, displaced by **(¼, ¼, ¼)** along the body diagonal. Each atom is tetrahedrally coordinated to **four atoms of the opposite species**. Lattice constant $a = 5.653$ Å, nearest-neighbour distance = $a\sqrt{3}/4 \approx 2.448$ Å, space group **F-43m**.

**Why GaAs is piezoelectric but Si is not:**

- The **point group of GaAs is Td (tetrahedral, non-centrosymmetric)**, allowing a non-zero piezoelectric tensor with a single independent component $e_{14} = -0.16$ C/m².
- The **point group of Si is Oh (cubic, centrosymmetric)**. Inversion symmetry forbids any first-order piezoelectric coupling: $e_{ijk} = 0$ for all i, j, k.

Therefore, mechanical stress on GaAs produces a measurable electric polarisation, useful in BioMEMS acoustic transducers.

**(b) Derivation and Computation of $n_i$:**

Starting from charge neutrality for an intrinsic semiconductor:

$$n_0 = p_0 = n_i$$

Using Boltzmann approximation for carrier concentrations:

$$n_0 = N_C \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-(E_C - E_F)}{k_B T}\right)$$

$$p_0 = N_V \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-(E_F - E_V)}{k_B T}\right)$$

Multiplying:

$$n_0 p_0 = N_C N_V \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$

Setting $n_0 = p_0 = n_i$:

$$n_i^2 = N_C N_V \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$

$$n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V}\ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-E_g}{2k_B T}\right)$$

Substituting the given values:

$$n_i = \sqrt{(4.35 \times 10^{17})(3.45 \times 10^{18})}\ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-1.42}{2 \times 0.02585}\right)$$

Geometric mean:

$$\sqrt{1.50 \times 10^{36}} = 1.22 \times 10^{18} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

Exponential term:

$$\exp(-27.47) = 1.16 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$n_i = 1.22 \times 10^{18} \times 1.16 \times 10^{-12} \approx 1.42 \times 10^{6} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

**Comment:** This very low intrinsic carrier concentration (orders of magnitude below Si) is what allows GaAs substrates to be engineered as **semi-insulating (SI-GaAs)** with resistivities exceeding $10^7$ Ω·cm, making them ideal for **low-loss RF and microwave BioMEMS** where substrate leakage would otherwise degrade sensor performance.

---

### Question B — Option B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q: (a)** Discuss **four reasons** why GaAs is preferred over Silicon in **optical BioMEMS** and **high-frequency BioMEMS** applications. Provide numerical justifications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GaAs **FBAR mass sensor** is fabricated with a membrane thickness of 2 µm and operates in its fundamental thickness mode. Compute the **resonant frequency** and **mass sensitivity**, given the longitudinal acoustic velocity in GaAs = 4730 m/s, density = 5320 kg/m³, and active area = 100 µm × 100 µm. Discuss the suitability of this sensor for protein biomarker detection. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Four Reasons with Numerical Justifications:**

1. **Direct bandgap (1.42 eV)** vs. Si's indirect bandgap (1.12 eV): GaAs efficiently absorbs and emits photons near 873 nm, ideal for **fluorescence-based biosensors** and on-chip photodetectors. Si's indirect bandgap yields weak light emission, ruling it out for integrated optical sources.

2. **Higher electron mobility (8500 cm²/V·s vs. Si's 1400 cm²/V·s):** GaAs RF BioMEMS can operate at GHz frequencies with low insertion loss. The 6× higher mobility reduces transit time and resistive losses.

3. **Piezoelectricity (e₁₄ = −0.16 C/m²):** GaAs directly converts mechanical stress into voltage, enabling **SAW/FBAR resonators** without depositing an external piezoelectric film — a process simplification unavailable in Si (which is centrosymmetric, point group Oh).

4. **Semi-insulating behaviour ($n_i \approx 2 \times 10^6$ cm⁻³, $\rho_{SI} > 10^7$ Ω·cm):** GaAs substrates minimise parasitic capacitance and substrate-induced RF losses, essential for **wireless implantable BioMEMS** and GHz mass sensors where Si's higher conductivity would create crosstalk.

**(b) FBAR Resonant Frequency and Mass Sensitivity:**

**Resonant frequency:**

$$f_0 = \dfrac{v_a}{2 t} = \dfrac{4730}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.183 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz} \approx 1.18 \text{ GHz}$$

**Mass sensitivity formula:**

$$S_m = \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial m} = -\dfrac{f_0^2}{\rho \, v_a \, A}$$

Substitute:

$$S_m = -\dfrac{(1.183 \times 10^{9})^2}{(5320)(4730)(1 \times 10^{-8})}$$

$$= -\dfrac{1.40 \times 10^{18}}{2.52 \times 10^{-1}} \approx -5.56 \times 10^{18} \text{ Hz/kg}$$

**Surface mass sensitivity:**

$$S_s = S_m \cdot A = -5.56 \times 10^{18} \times 10^{-8} = -5.56 \times 10^{10} \text{ Hz·m}^2/\text{kg}$$

$$= -55.6 \text{ Hz·cm}^2/\text{ng}$$

**Suitability for protein biomarker detection:**

A typical protein (e.g., immunoglobulin G, IgG) has mass ~150 kDa ≈ 2.5 × 10⁻²² kg. A monolayer of IgG at surface density ~3 ng/cm² produces a frequency shift:

$$\Delta f = S_s \cdot \Delta \sigma_m = (-55.6)(3) = -166.8 \text{ Hz}$$

This shift is **easily resolvable** with modern frequency counters (resolution ~0.1 Hz). Hence the GaAs FBAR is **highly suitable** for label-free detection of low-concentration protein biomarkers, with potential detection limits down to pg/cm².

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Traps for GaAs Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating direct and indirect bandgap:** Students often say "GaAs has a smaller bandgap than Si so it emits light." Wrong reasoning. The *type* of bandgap (direct vs. indirect) is what matters, not just the magnitude.
>
> 2. **Omitting the crystal structure name:** Always state **"zinc-blende"** explicitly; never say "diamond-like structure" — that term is reserved for Si/Ge/C (Group IV).
>
> 3. **Forgetting Varshni parameters:** When asked to compute $E_g$ at a non-room temperature, students often quote the 300 K value. Always apply $E_g(T) = E_g(0) - \alpha T^2/(T+\beta)$.
>
> 4. **Unit slip in carrier concentration:** Mixing cm⁻³ and m⁻³. KTU papers use cm⁻³ — convert explicitly.
>
> 5. **Ignoring passivation in biocompatibility answers:** Saying "GaAs is biocompatible" without a passivation layer is a **guaranteed 1–2 mark loss**.
>
> 6. **Piezoelectric coefficient sign:** $e_{14}$ is **negative** for GaAs. The sign is not commonly asked but remember it if asked to write a constitutive relation.
>
> 7. **Confusing Varshni $\alpha$ with thermal expansion:** These are different $\alpha$'s. Use subscript "Varshni" if context is unclear.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GaAs is a III–V direct bandgap (1.42 eV) compound semiconductor** with a **zinc-blende structure**, lattice constant **5.653 Å**, and space group **F-43m**.
- **Electron mobility of GaAs (~8500 cm²/V·s)** is the highest among mainstream semiconductors — fundamental to its use in **high-frequency BioMEMS**.
- **GaAs is piezoelectric** ($e_{14} = -0.16$ C/m²) because its point group **Td is non-centrosymmetric**, unlike Si's centrosymmetric Oh group.
- **Intrinsic carrier concentration** $n_i \approx 2 \times 10^6$ cm⁻³ is very low, enabling **semi-insulating substrates** for low-loss RF and microwave BioMEMS.
- **Direct bandgap** allows efficient **photon emission/absorption** → critical for **fluorescence, SPR, and photodetector-based biosensors**.
- **Density (5.32 g/cm³) and Young's modulus (85 GPa)** of GaAs influence mass-sensor dynamics and resonant frequency design.
- **Varshni equation** $E_g(T) = E_g(0) - \alpha T^2/(T+\beta)$ describes temperature dependence of bandgap; vital for temperature-stable optical BioMEMS.
- **Mass sensitivity of GaAs FBARs** at 1.18 GHz is ~ **55 Hz·cm²/ng**, suitable for **label-free protein biomarker detection**.
- **Disadvantages:** High cost, brittle, toxic As, **poor native oxide** → requires deposited passivation (Si₃N₄ or Al₂O₃) for biocompatibility.
- **AlGaAs/GaAs heteroepitaxy** enables bandgap engineering on a lattice-matched platform.
- **Comparison triad for KTU answers:** GaAs is chosen over Si because of (i) **direct bandgap**, (ii) **high electron mobility**, and (iii) **piezoelectricity** — a complete answer must address all three.
- **FBAR / SAW / SPR** are the three most-tested GaAs-based BioMEMS transducer types in KTU 2024 Module 1.
- **Key constants to memorise:** $E_g = 1.42$ eV, $a = 5.653$ Å, $\mu_e = 8500$ cm²/V·s, $T_m = 1238$ °C, $e_{14} = -0.16$ C/m².
- **Avoid in answers:** Saying "GaAs is more biocompatible than Si" without qualifying with "after passivation."
- **Use in derivation questions:** $n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \exp(-E_g/2k_B T)$ and $f_0 = v_a/(2t)$ are the two workhorse equations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quartz
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quartz

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quartz** is a crystalline form of silicon dioxide (SiO₂) that belongs to the trigonal crystal system at room temperature (α-quartz) and transitions to the hexagonal crystal system above **573 °C** (β-quartz). In the context of **BioMEMS and Microfluidics**, quartz is classified as a premium **structural and functional substrate material** owing to its intrinsic piezoelectricity, optical transparency in the UV–visible–NIR spectrum, exceptional chemical inertness, and very low thermal expansion coefficient (**0.55 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** along the Z-axis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim intent):** Quartz is a single-crystal piezoelectric material extensively employed in BioMEMS as a substrate for acoustic wave devices, microfluidic channels, optical bio-sensors, and lab-on-a-chip platforms because of its precise electromechanical coupling and biocompatibility.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a perfectly transparent glass window that, when you gently squeeze it, generates a tiny measurable electric voltage — and conversely, when you apply a voltage across it, it microscopically flexes. **Quartz is exactly that window**, but unlike ordinary glass, its atomic lattice is arranged in a highly ordered, non-centrosymmetric pattern. This "tilted" atomic arrangement is the secret behind its **piezoelectric personality**.

A real-world analogy: think of a *mechanical weighing scale* (input: pressure) versus a *digital quartz wristwatch* (input: voltage → output: precise vibration). Quartz acts as the *translator* between the mechanical world (pressure, mass, fluid motion) and the electrical world (voltage, current, frequency). This dual capability is precisely why it is the **workhorse material** of BioMEMS transducers, micropumps, and acoustic biosensors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** Quartz is the *only* FDA-recognized biocompatible piezoelectric crystal that can be microfabricated using conventional cleanroom photolithography, making it a strategic material for implantable and in-vitro diagnostic BioMEMS devices.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants of Quartz

| Property | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density | ρ | **2.65 × 10³** | kg/m³ |
| Young's Modulus (X-direction) | E₁₁ | **86.8 × 10⁹** | Pa |
| Piezoelectric coefficient (d₁₁) | d₁₁ | **2.31 × 10⁻¹²** | C/N |
| Relative permittivity (ε₁₁/ε₀) | εᵣ | **4.52** | — |
| Acoustic velocity (X-propagation) | vₐ | **5750** | m/s |
| Melting point | Tₘ | **1710** | °C |
| Curie temperature | T꜀ | **573** | °C |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Crystalline Structure of Quartz

Quartz exists in two principal polymorphs relevant to BioMEMS processing:

- **α-quartz (low quartz):** stable below 573 °C, trigonal crystal class **32** (point group D₃), non-centrosymmetric → **piezoelectric active**.
- **β-quartz (high quartz):** stable between 573 °C and 870 °C, hexagonal crystal class **622** (point group D₆), centrosymmetric → **NOT piezoelectric**.

The atomic lattice consists of **SiO₄ tetrahedra** linked at corners, forming helicoidal chains along the **optical axis (Z-axis)**. This helical arrangement is the structural origin of the lack of inversion symmetry that enables piezoelectricity.

### 2.2 Crystallographic Axes Convention (IRE-1949 Standard)

| Axis | Direction | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **X-axis (electrical axis)** | Along a ⟨1 0 0⟩ direction | Direction of maximum piezoelectric response |
| **Y-axis (mechanical axis)** | Perpendicular to X, in the basal plane | Direction of maximum mechanical strain under E-field |
| **Z-axis (optical axis)** | Along the c-axis (3-fold screw) | Direction of light propagation, NO piezoelectric response along Z |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU BioMEMS:** When fabricating a quartz-based acoustic microfluidic device, the wafer is **X-cut** (surface normal along X) to maximize the d₁₁ shear-mode excitation used for SAW (Surface Acoustic Wave) or BAW (Bulk Acoustic Wave) generation in lab-on-chip droplet ejection.

### 2.3 Why Quartz is Critical in BioMEMS

- **Optical transparency:** enables integration of **optical biosensors**, fluorescence microscopy, and absorbance spectrometers on the same chip.
- **Chemical inertness:** withstands aggressive bio-chemical buffers (pH 1–14), strong acids (except HF), and organic solvents — ideal for **in-vitro diagnostics**.
- **Low thermal expansion:** maintains dimensional stability during PCR thermocycling in microfluidic chips.
- **Piezoelectricity:** enables **active microfluidic actuation** (droplet generation, mixing, pumping, nebulization) without external moving parts.
- **Biocompatibility:** ISO 10993 and USP Class VI certified for implantable devices (e.g., neural probes, cochlear implants, drug-delivery micropumps).
- **High Q-factor:** mechanical quality factor exceeds **10⁶** at room temperature → ultra-sensitive mass and viscosity sensors (**QCM — Quartz Crystal Microbalance**).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every KTU-relevant equation for the "Quartz" topic. Memorize these — they appear in Part B numericals every academic year.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **f₀ = vₐ / (2·t)** | Fundamental resonance frequency of a thickness-shear quartz resonator | f₀ = frequency, vₐ = acoustic velocity, t = crystal thickness | Hz, m/s, m |
| 2 | **Sauerbrey: Δf = −2·f₀²·Δm / (A·√(ρ_q·μ_q))** | Mass sensitivity of QCM | Δf = freq. shift, Δm = mass change, A = electrode area, ρ_q = quartz density, μ_q = shear modulus | Hz, kg, m², kg/m³, Pa |
| 3 | **Piezo constitutive: D = d·T + εᵀ·E** | Electric displacement (direct piezoelectric effect) | D = electric displacement, d = piezoelectric coeff., T = stress, εᵀ = permittivity at constant stress, E = electric field | C/m², C/N, Pa, F/m, V/m |
| 4 | **Strain: S = sᴱ·T + dᵗ·E** | Mechanical strain (converse piezoelectric effect) | S = strain, sᴱ = elastic compliance at constant E, dᵗ = transpose of d-matrix | —, 1/Pa, C/N, V/m |
| 5 | **Electromechanical coupling: k² = d² / (sᴱ·εᵀ)** | Efficiency of energy conversion between mechanical and electrical domains | k = coupling coefficient, dimensionless | — |
| 6 | **AT-cut frequency constant: N = f₀·t = 1660 kHz·mm** | Empirical design constant for temperature-stable cuts | N = frequency constant | Hz·m |
| 7 | **Acoustic impedance: Z_a = √(ρ_q·μ_q)** | Acoustic matching between quartz and fluid | Zₐ = impedance, μ_q = shear modulus | Rayl (kg/m²·s) |

> **Numerical reminder for tables:** Absolute value bars are written as `\vert x \vert` to keep markdown tables intact.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Quartz-Based BioMEMS

- **QCM-D (Quartz Crystal Microbalance with Dissipation):** real-time, label-free detection of biomolecular binding (proteins, DNA, viruses) at the **ng/cm²** level.
- **SAW/BAW biosensors:** detection of antigens, bacteria, and DNA hybridization using acoustic wave propagation on piezoelectric quartz.
- **Piezoelectric micropumps:** valveless fluid actuation for **drug-delivery implants** and point-of-care (POC) diagnostics.
- **Acoustic droplet ejection:** inkjet-like dispensing of picoliter bio-reagent volumes using focused acoustic energy.
- **Quartz microfluidic chips:** thermally stable, optically clear substrates for **single-cell imaging** and **organ-on-chip** platforms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Fundamental Resonance Frequency of a Quartz Resonator

A thickness-shear mode quartz resonator consists of a thin quartz plate of thickness **t** with conductive electrodes deposited on both major faces. When an AC electric field is applied across the thickness, an inverse piezoelectric effect generates a shear mechanical wave that bounces between the two parallel surfaces. Standing-wave resonance occurs when the plate thickness equals an **integer multiple of half-wavelengths**.

**Step 1 — Boundary condition for standing wave formation**

$$ t = n \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots $$

Here, *t* is the plate thickness, *λ* is the acoustic wavelength, and *n* is the harmonic order.

**Step 2 — Relate wavelength to frequency via acoustic velocity**

$$ \lambda = \frac{v_a}{f_0} $$

The acoustic wave velocity *vₐ* is constant for a given propagation direction in a given crystal cut.

**Step 3 — Substitute λ into the standing-wave equation**

$$ t = n \cdot \frac{v_a}{2 \cdot f_0} $$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain the fundamental (n = 1) resonance frequency**

$$ f_0 = \frac{v_a}{2 \cdot t} \quad \text{(fundamental mode, n=1)} $$

**Step 5 — General harmonic series**

$$ f_n = \frac{n \cdot v_a}{2 \cdot t} $$

> **Physical interpretation:** Halving the quartz thickness *doubles* the resonant frequency. This is why **high-frequency BioMEMS resonators (>100 MHz)** require ultra-thin quartz membranes (≤ 16 µm) fabricated by deep reactive ion etching (DRIE) or chemical-mechanical polishing.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Sauerbrey Equation for Mass Sensing on QCM

The Sauerbrey equation is **the single most important formula** for quartz-based biosensing in BioMEMS. It quantitatively relates a measurable frequency shift to an infinitesimally thin, rigid mass deposited on the quartz electrode surface.

**Step 1 — Define the unloaded quartz properties**

The unloaded resonant frequency is:

$$ f_0 = \frac{v_a}{2 \cdot t} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot t}\sqrt{\frac{\mu_q}{\rho_q}} $$

where *μ_q* is the shear modulus and *ρ_q* is the quartz density.

**Step 2 — Introduce a foreign mass layer of thickness Δt and density ρ_f**

Treat the foreign layer as a rigid extension of the quartz mass:

$$ f_0 + \Delta f = \frac{v_a}{2 \cdot (t + \Delta t)} $$

**Step 3 — Linearize for Δt ≪ t (Sauerbrey small-mass limit)**

$$ \frac{1}{t + \Delta t} \approx \frac{1}{t}\left(1 - \frac{\Delta t}{t}\right) $$

Therefore:

$$ \Delta f = -f_0 \cdot \frac{\Delta t}{t} $$

**Step 4 — Convert thickness change to areal mass density**

$$ \Delta t = \frac{\Delta m}{A \cdot \rho_f} \cdot \rho_f = \frac{\Delta m}{A} \quad \text{(foreign layer replaces same volume as quartz equivalent)} $$

After substituting *t = vₐ/(2f₀)*:

$$ \Delta f = -f_0 \cdot \frac{\Delta m}{A} \cdot \frac{2 f_0}{v_a} = -\frac{2 f_0^2 \cdot \Delta m}{A \cdot v_a} $$

**Step 5 — Replace vₐ with √(μ_q/ρ_q) to obtain the standard Sauerbrey form**

$$ \boxed{\Delta f = -\frac{2 \cdot f_0^2 \cdot \Delta m}{A \cdot \sqrt{\rho_q \cdot \mu_q}}} $$

> **Engineering interpretation:** The negative sign indicates that *mass loading decreases* the resonant frequency. A 5 MHz QCM has a mass sensitivity of approximately **0.057 Hz·cm²/ng**, enabling detection of monolayer protein adsorption in real time.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Electromechanical Coupling Coefficient k² for Quartz

The coupling coefficient *k* quantifies the fraction of electrical energy converted into mechanical energy (or vice versa) in one piezoelectric cycle.

**Step 1 — Start from the direct piezoelectric relation**

$$ D = d \cdot T + \varepsilon^T \cdot E $$

**Step 2 — Compute the electrical energy density per cycle**

$$ U_e = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \varepsilon^T \cdot E^2 $$

**Step 3 — Compute the mechanical energy density per cycle**

$$ U_m = \frac{1}{2} \cdot s^E \cdot T^2 $$

**Step 4 — The mutual (piezoelectric) energy density is**

$$ U_{mutual} = d \cdot T \cdot E $$

**Step 5 — Define k as the geometric mean ratio of mutual to total energies**

$$ k^2 = \frac{U_{mutual}^2}{U_e \cdot U_m} = \frac{(d \cdot T \cdot E)^2}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon^T E^2\right)\left(\frac{1}{2} s^E T^2\right)} = \frac{d^2}{s^E \cdot \varepsilon^T} $$

$$ \boxed{k^2 = \frac{d^2}{s^E \cdot \varepsilon^T}} $$

> **Physical interpretation:** For X-cut quartz, k² ≈ **0.0011 (≈ 0.11%)**, which is small but extremely *stable* and *linear* — a property that makes quartz the gold standard for precision frequency references, unlike ferroelectric ceramics (PZT, k² ≈ 10%) which are nonlinear.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Quartz vs. Competing BioMEMS Substrate Materials

| Material | Piezoelectric | Optical Transparency | Biocompatibility | Cost | Machinability | Best BioMEMS Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Quartz (SiO₂)** | ✅ Yes (d₁₁ = 2.31 pC/N) | ✅ Excellent (UV–NIR) | ✅ High | ⚠️ Medium | ⚠️ Hard to etch (needs HF) | QCM, SAW, optical biochips |
| Silicon (Si) | ❌ No (centrosymmetric) | ❌ Opaque | ⚠️ Moderate (needs coating) | ✅ Low | ✅ Excellent (DRIE) | Structural MEMS, CMOS integration |
| Glass (Pyrex/borosilicate) | ❌ No | ✅ Excellent | ✅ High | ✅ Low | ⚠️ Moderate (powder blasting) | Microfluidic channels, lab-on-chip |
| PDMS (polymer) | ❌ No (can be doped) | ✅ Transparent | ✅ High | ✅ Very low | ✅ Easy (soft lithography) | Disposable μ-fluidics |
| PZT (ceramic) | ✅ Strong (d₃₃ ≈ 300 pC/N) | ⚠️ Opaque/yellow | ⚠️ Contains lead (Pb) | ✅ Low | ⚠️ Brittle | Actuators, high-force pumps |
| Lithium Niobate (LiNbO₃) | ✅ Strong | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Limited | ⚠️ High | ⚠️ Difficult | High-frequency SAW filters |

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define quartz and list any four properties that make it suitable for BioMEMS applications.**

**Model Answer:**
Quartz is a single-crystal form of silicon dioxide (SiO₂) belonging to the trigonal crystal system at room temperature. It is widely used in BioMEMS due to its unique combination of properties: **(i)** intrinsic piezoelectricity (d₁₁ = 2.31 pC/N) enabling mechanical-to-electrical transduction, **(ii)** excellent optical transparency from the UV to the NIR range for on-chip optical detection, **(iii)** outstanding chemical inertness against bio-fluids and aggressive buffers, and **(iv)** high mechanical Q-factor (>10⁶) ensuring ultra-sensitive mass and viscosity detection in QCM biosensors. It is also biocompatible per ISO 10993 standards, making it safe for implantable BioMEMS devices such as neural probes and drug-delivery micropumps.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse *quartz* with *fused silica* or *Pyrex glass*. Fused silica is *amorphous* SiO₂ and is **NOT piezoelectric** — only crystalline α-quartz exhibits piezoelectricity. Writing "fused silica = quartz" will cost 1 of the 3 marks.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between α-quartz and β-quartz in terms of structure and piezoelectric behavior.**

**Model Answer:**
α-quartz (low quartz) is stable below **573 °C**, belongs to the trigonal crystal class **32** with point group D₃, and possesses a **non-centrosymmetric** atomic structure, which makes it **piezoelectric active**. In contrast, β-quartz (high quartz) is stable between 573 °C and 870 °C, has a **hexagonal** structure belonging to point group D₆, and is **centrosymmetric**, making it **NOT piezoelectric**. The α → β phase transition at 573 °C is reversible and corresponds to the **Curie temperature** of quartz — above this temperature, all piezoelectric properties vanish. This is why BioMEMS fabrication processes must keep thermal exposure below 573 °C to preserve the piezoelectric functionality of quartz substrates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Do not state that β-quartz is "more piezoelectric" — the opposite is true. The mark is awarded only for correctly identifying that **α-quartz is piezoelectric and β-quartz is not**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern mandates that the student answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Solutions below are examiner-grade model answers.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain in detail the piezoelectric effect in quartz with reference to its crystal structure. Derive the constitutive equations governing the direct and converse piezoelectric effects.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Crystal Structure Origin of Piezoelectricity (3 marks)**

Quartz is composed of **SiO₄ tetrahedra** linked at the corners to form helical chains along the Z-axis. The lack of a center of symmetry in the α-phase means that when mechanical stress is applied, the centers of positive (Si⁴⁺) and negative (O²⁻) charge shift in opposite directions, generating a net electric dipole moment per unit volume — this is the **direct piezoelectric effect**. Conversely, when an external electric field is applied, the ionic sub-lattices shift, producing mechanical strain — the **converse piezoelectric effect**.

**(ii) Direct Piezoelectric Constitutive Equation (2 marks)**

The direct effect relates electric displacement *D* to applied mechanical stress *T* and electric field *E*:

$$ D_i = d_{ij} \cdot T_j + \varepsilon_{ij}^T \cdot E_i $$

The first term is the piezoelectric contribution; the second is the dielectric response at constant stress.

**(iii) Converse Piezoelectric Constitutive Equation (2 marks)**

The converse effect relates mechanical strain *S* to applied stress *T* and electric field *E*:

$$ S_j = s_{ij}^E \cdot T_j + d_{ij}^t \cdot E_i $$

where *sᴱ* is the elastic compliance at constant electric field, and *dᵗ* is the transpose of the *d*-matrix.

---

**(b) Starting from the Sauerbrey relation, derive the expression for mass sensitivity of a Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM) and explain its significance in biosensing applications.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Fundamental frequency of the unloaded quartz plate:**

$$ f_0 = \frac{v_a}{2 t} = \frac{1}{2 t}\sqrt{\frac{\mu_q}{\rho_q}} $$

**Step 2 — Add a thin foreign mass layer of thickness Δt:**

$$ f_0 + \Delta f = \frac{v_a}{2(t + \Delta t)} $$

**Step 3 — Linearize for Δt ≪ t:**

$$ \Delta f \approx -f_0 \cdot \frac{\Delta t}{t} $$

**Step 4 — Express Δt in terms of areal mass density Δm/A:**

$$ \Delta t = \frac{\Delta m}{A \cdot \rho_f} \quad \text{(with effective density matched to quartz)} $$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify to the Sauerbrey equation:**

$$ \boxed{\Delta f = -\frac{2 f_0^2 \cdot \Delta m}{A \cdot \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}}} $$

**Step 6 — Mass sensitivity (1 mark)**

$$ S_m = \left|\frac{\Delta f}{\Delta m}\right| = \frac{2 f_0^2}{A \cdot \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}} $$

**Significance in biosensing (2 marks):**
The Sauerbrey equation shows that frequency shift is **directly proportional to mass loading**, making the QCM a label-free, real-time biosensor capable of detecting sub-nanogram quantities of biomolecules. For a 5 MHz crystal with electrode area A = 1 cm², the sensitivity is approximately **0.057 Hz/ng**, sufficient to detect a single protein monolayer. Applications include **kinetic monitoring of antigen–antibody binding, DNA hybridization, virus capture, and cell-adhesion studies** in real time without fluorescent or radioactive labels.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, 2024 Scheme Alternative]**

**(a) Describe the different crystallographic cuts (X-cut, Y-cut, AT-cut) of quartz and their relevance in BioMEMS device design.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) X-Cut Quartz (2 marks)**

In an X-cut, the wafer surface is perpendicular to the X-axis. The shear deformation occurs in the **Y-direction** when an electric field is applied along X. X-cut quartz exhibits a **positive temperature coefficient** of frequency (frequency increases with temperature), making it unsuitable for precision oscillators but useful in **temperature-sensing BioMEMS** and broadband acoustic transducers.

**(ii) Y-Cut Quartz (2 marks)**

In a Y-cut, the surface is perpendicular to the Y-axis. The induced strain is along the X-direction. Y-cut quartz shows a **negative temperature coefficient** of frequency, used historically in **phonograph pickups** and force sensors in early BioMEMS tactile probes.

**(iii) AT-Cut Quartz (3 marks)**

The **AT-cut** is rotated by **35.25°** from the X-axis about the Y-axis. Its key property is a **zero temperature coefficient at 25 °C** (cubic temperature dependence), giving exceptional frequency stability over a wide range (–55 °C to +85 °C). This is the **industry-standard cut** for QCM biosensors, smartphone oscillators, and **BioMEMS gravimetric sensors**, because the temperature-induced drift is negligible during bio-experiments conducted at room or body temperature.

**Relevance in BioMEMS (1 mark summary):** The choice of cut determines the thermal stability, mode of vibration (thickness-shear, thickness-twist, face-shear), and piezoelectric coupling, and is therefore the **first design decision** in any quartz-based BioMEMS device.

---

**(b) A 6 MHz AT-cut quartz crystal is used as a QCM biosensor with an electrode area of 0.5 cm². A protein monolayer of areal mass 220 ng/cm² adsorbs on the surface. Calculate the expected frequency shift using the Sauerbrey equation. Use μ_q = 2.95 × 10¹⁰ Pa and ρ_q = 2650 kg/m³.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given values**

- f₀ = 6 × 10⁶ Hz
- A = 0.5 cm² = 0.5 × 10⁻⁴ m² = 5 × 10⁻⁵ m²
- Δm/A = 220 ng/cm² = 220 × 10⁻⁹ g/cm² = 220 × 10⁻⁹ × 10⁻³ kg / 10⁻⁴ m² = **2.2 × 10⁻⁶ kg/m²**
- μ_q = 2.95 × 10¹⁰ Pa
- ρ_q = 2650 kg/m³

**Step 2 — Compute the acoustic impedance term √(ρ_q · μ_q):**

$$ \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q} = \sqrt{2650 \times 2.95 \times 10^{10}} = \sqrt{7.8175 \times 10^{13}} $$

$$ \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q} \approx 8.842 \times 10^6 \ \text{kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the mass term Δm:**

$$ \Delta m = (2.2 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{kg/m}^2) \times (5 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^2) = 1.1 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{kg} $$

**Step 4 — Apply the Sauerbrey equation:**

$$ \Delta f = -\frac{2 \cdot f_0^2 \cdot \Delta m}{A \cdot \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}} $$

$$ \Delta f = -\frac{2 \times (6 \times 10^6)^2 \times 1.1 \times 10^{-10}}{5 \times 10^{-5} \times 8.842 \times 10^6} $$

**Step 5 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$ \text{Numerator} = 2 \times 36 \times 10^{12} \times 1.1 \times 10^{-10} = 7.92 \times 10^{3} \ \text{Hz·m} $$

**Step 6 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$ \text{Denominator} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 8.842 \times 10^6 = 442.1 \ \text{Hz·m} $$

**Step 7 — Final frequency shift:**

$$ \Delta f = -\frac{7.92 \times 10^3}{442.1} \approx -17.9 \ \text{Hz} $$

**Result:** A frequency decrease of approximately **17.9 Hz** is observed, confirming the protein adsorption event on the QCM surface. The negative sign indicates **mass loading**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall (numericals):** Common mistakes include: (1) forgetting to convert cm² to m², (2) mixing up areal mass density (kg/m²) with absolute mass (kg), and (3) omitting the **negative sign** in the final answer. Always carry units through every step; partial marking is awarded for correct unit conversion even if the final number is wrong.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute KTU revision — read this 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Quartz = crystalline SiO₂**, trigonal (α-phase), belongs to point group **32**, non-centrosymmetric → piezoelectric.
- ✅ **Curie temperature = 573 °C**; above this, β-quartz forms and piezoelectricity is **lost**.
- ✅ Crystal axes: **X = electrical, Y = mechanical, Z = optical** (IRE-1949 convention).
- ✅ **AT-cut** (35.25° rotated) is the standard cut for BioMEMS QCM sensors due to near-zero temperature drift at 25 °C.
- ✅ Piezoelectric constitutive equations: **D = d·T + εᵀ·E** (direct) and **S = sᴱ·T + dᵗ·E** (converse).
- ✅ Electromechanical coupling **k² = d² / (sᴱ · εᵀ)**; for X-cut quartz, k² ≈ 0.0011.
- ✅ Fundamental resonance: **f₀ = vₐ / (2t)**; halving thickness doubles frequency.
- ✅ **Sauerbrey equation: Δf = −2 f₀² Δm / (A √(ρ_q μ_q))** — central to all QCM biosensing problems.
- ✅ Density ρ_q = **2650 kg/m³**, d₁₁ = **2.31 pC/N**, εᵣ = **4.52**, melting point **1710 °C**.
- ✅ BioMEMS applications: **QCM-D biosensors, SAW/BAW sensors, piezoelectric micropumps, acoustic droplet ejectors, optical biochips, nebulizers**.
- ✅ **Do NOT confuse** quartz (crystalline, piezoelectric) with **fused silica** (amorphous, non-piezoelectric).
- ✅ **Process constraint:** keep all BioMEMS fabrication temperatures **< 573 °C** to preserve piezoelectricity.
- ✅ **Cost vs. benefit:** quartz is more expensive than silicon but is preferred when **piezoelectricity, optical transparency, and biocompatibility** are simultaneously required.
- ✅ KTU exam tip: For 14-mark questions, **always derive equations from first principles** with one explanatory sentence per step — partial marks are awarded generously for correct methodology even if the final algebra is incomplete.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: POLYMERS — FOUNDATIONAL BUILDING BLOCKS FOR BioMEMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **polymer** (from Greek *poly* = many, *meros* = parts) is a high-molecular-weight macromolecule composed of covalently bonded repeating structural units called **monomers**, linked via a process called **polymerization**. In the context of BioMEMS and Microfluidics, polymers are a class of organic, mechanically tunable, chemically functionalizable materials that serve as substrates, structural elements, structural matrices, and functional interfaces in microscale biological and medical devices.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Train of Bricks" Model

Imagine a **freight train** as a polymer chain. Each individual **freight car** is a **monomer** (a small, single molecule). They are connected by **coupling hooks** (covalent bonds). The entire train (the polymer) is lightweight, flexible along its length, yet incredibly strong when many cars are linked. The *type* of car (wooden, steel, refrigerated) determines whether the final train carries coal, liquids, or passengers — just as the *type* of monomer determines whether the polymer is **rigid** (polystyrene), **flexible** (PDMS), **water-loving** (PEG), or **biodegradable** (PLGA).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Polymers used in BioMEMS (Memorize for KTU):**
> - **PDMS (Polydimethylsiloxane)** — soft lithography, microfluidic channels
> - **PMMA (Poly-methyl methacrylate)** — hot embossing, laser ablation
> - **SU-8** — negative photoresist, high-aspect-ratio structures
> - **PEG (Polyethylene glycol)** — anti-fouling coatings
> - **PLGA (Poly-lactic-co-glycolic acid)** — biodegradable scaffolds, drug delivery

### 1.2 Why Polymers Matter in BioMEMS — Three Foundational Roles

| Role | Functional Utility | Example Polymer |
|------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Structural** | Form microchannels, valves, membranes | PDMS, SU-8 |
| **Functional** | Selective binding, sensing, actuation | Hydrogels, conductive polymers |
| **Biological Interface** | Cell adhesion, anti-fouling, drug release | PEG, PLGA, Collagen |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In any KTU question on BioMEMS materials, the examiner expects the answer to distinguish polymers from **silicon/glass/ceramics** by emphasizing that polymers are **low-cost, biocompatible, optically transparent, and easy to fabricate via soft lithography** — silicon and glass are rigid, expensive, and require cleanroom processing.

---

## 2. Classification of Polymers (Comprehensive Taxonomy)

### 2.1 Based on Source / Origin

- **Natural Polymers** — derived from living organisms
  - *Proteins* (collagen, silk fibroin, gelatin)
  - *Polysaccharides* (chitosan, cellulose, alginate, hyaluronic acid)
  - *Nucleic acids* (DNA, RNA — used in DNA origami BioMEMS)

- **Synthetic Polymers** — manufactured via chemical synthesis
  - *Thermoplastics* (PMMA, PS, PC, PE, PP)
  - *Thermosets* (SU-8, epoxy, polyimide)
  - *Elastomers* (PDMS, Ecoflex)

- **Semi-synthetic** — chemically modified natural polymers
  - *Cellulose acetate, nitrocellulose, hydroxyethyl starch*

### 2.2 Based on Polymerization Mechanism

- **Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymers** — monomers add one at a time via free-radical or ionic initiation. No small molecule byproduct.
  - Examples: PDMS, PMMA, PS, PE

- **Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymers** — monomers react with elimination of small molecules (H₂O, HCl, CH₃OH).
  - Examples: Nylon, PET, polycarbonate

### 2.3 Based on Thermal Response

- **Thermoplastics** — soften on heating, harden on cooling (reversible). Linear/branched chains. *Examples: PMMA, PS, PE, PP*
- **Thermosets** — undergo irreversible crosslinking on heating. *Examples: SU-8, epoxy, cured PDMS*
- **Elastomers** — highly elastic, lightly crosslinked. *Example: PDMS (Sylgard 184)*

### 2.4 Based on Chain Architecture

- **Linear** — single straight chain (HDPE, PVC)
- **Branched** — side chains off main backbone (LDPE)
- **Crosslinked** — three-dimensional network (vulcanized rubber, cured PDMS)
- **Dendrimers** — highly branched, tree-like (used in targeted drug delivery)

### 2.5 Based on Charge (Crucial for BioMEMS Bio-interfaces)

- **Neutral** — PEG, PMMA
- **Cationic (+)** — Poly-L-lysine, chitosan (protonated amine groups)
- **Anionic (–)** — Alginate, heparin, polyacrylic acid
- **Amphoteric / Zwitterionic** — Carry both + and – groups; excellent anti-fouling (e.g., poly(carboxybetaine))

### 2.6 Based on Biodegradability

- **Biostable (non-degradable)** — PDMS, PMMA, SU-8, polyimide
- **Biodegradable / Bioresorbable** — PLGA, PLA, PCL, collagen, chitosan

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Mnemonic — "NCB-TBC" for Polymer Classification:** Natural, Condensation-addition, Biodegradable-stable, Thermoplastic-elastomer, Branched-crosslinked, Charged (cation/anion/zwitter).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polymerization Mechanisms — Operational Logic

### A. Chain-Growth (Addition) Polymerization — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Initiation:** Initiator (I) decomposes → free radical (R•). Radical attacks the C=C double bond of monomer, generating an active chain radical.
2. **Propagation:** Active chain radical adds successive monomers, propagating the chain.
3. **Termination:** Two radicals combine (coupling) or transfer a hydrogen (disproportionation), ending the chain.
4. **Chain Transfer:** Active center transfers to another molecule (solvent, monomer, polymer), creating a branch and a new active center.

**Key Insight:** Only monomers with **C=C double bonds** (vinyl/allyl groups) undergo addition polymerization. There is **no small-molecule byproduct**.

### B. Step-Growth (Condensation) Polymerization — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Initiation:** Not radical-based. Monomers with two or more functional groups (e.g., –COOH, –OH, –NH₂) approach each other.
2. **Propagation (Stepwise Growth):** *Any* two oligomers can react at any time — chain length grows gradually. Dimers, trimers, and oligomers coexist in the reaction mixture.
3. **Termination:** When one functional group runs out (stoichiometric imbalance) or by adding a monofunctional terminator.

**Key Insight:** A **small molecule byproduct** (H₂O, HCl, CH₃OH) is eliminated. High molecular weight is achieved only at very high conversion (>98%).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the two mechanisms. Remember: **Chain-growth = C=C double bond + no byproduct**. **Step-growth = functional group reaction + small-molecule byproduct + high conversion required.**

### C. Critical Differences Table

| Parameter | Chain-Growth | Step-Growth |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| Monomer requirement | Unsaturated (C=C) | Bi-/multifunctional |
| Active species | Free radical / ion | Stable functional groups |
| Byproduct | **None** | **Small molecule (H₂O, HCl)** |
| Reaction time for high MW | Short (minutes–hours) | Long (hours–days) |
| MW vs. Conversion | High MW even at low conversion | High MW only at >98% conversion |
| Monomer disappearance | Slow (long-lived chains) | Fast (early disappearance) |

---

## 2.2 Molecular Weight Concepts — The Heart of Polymer Science

Polymer molecular weight is **not a single value** because chains have different lengths. We use **statistical averages**.

### A. Number-Average Molecular Weight (M_n)

$$M_n = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i \cdot M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i}$$

**Logic:** Sum of (number of chains × their molecular weight) divided by total number of chains. Determined experimentally by **osmometry** and **end-group analysis**. Sensitive to low-MW chains.

### B. Weight-Average Molecular Weight (M_w)

$$M_w = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i \cdot M_i^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i \cdot M_i}$$

**Logic:** Weights each chain by its *mass* contribution. Larger chains dominate. Determined by **light scattering** and **sedimentation**. Always **M_w ≥ M_n**.

### C. Polydispersity Index (PDI)

$$PDI = \frac{M_w}{M_n}$$

- **PDI = 1.0** → monodisperse (all chains identical length) — *theoretical ideal*
- **PDI > 1.0** → polydisperse (real polymers)
- **Typical values:** PDMS ~ 1.4, PMMA ~ 1.5–2.0, free-radical polystyrene ~ 1.5–3.0, step-growth nylon ~ 2.0

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Important:** A monodisperse polymer has M_w = M_n. A polydisperse polymer has M_w > M_n. The larger the PDI, the broader the molecular-weight distribution.

### D. Degree of Polymerization (DP)

$$DP_n = \frac{M_n}{M_0}$$

where **M_0** is the molecular weight of the *repeat unit* (not monomer, in case of condensation where water is lost).

### E. Carothers Equation (Step-Growth Polymerization)

For a step-growth polymerization with bifunctional monomers of equal reactivity:

$$DP_n = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

where **p** is the *fraction of functional groups reacted* (conversion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** To achieve **DP_n = 100**, we need p = 0.99 (99% conversion). To achieve **DP_n = 1000**, we need p = 0.999 (99.9% conversion). This is why step-growth polymerization demands extreme stoichiometric balance and high conversion.

### F. Modified Carothers Equation (Non-stoichiometric)

$$DP_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

where **r = N_A / N_B** is the stoichiometric imbalance ratio (N_A ≤ N_B). When r = 1, reduces to the basic Carothers equation.

### G. Glass Transition Temperature — Fox-Flory Equation

$$T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{M_n}$$

where **T_g,∞** is the glass transition temperature at infinite molecular weight, and **K** is a polymer-specific constant. **Higher M_n → higher T_g**, asymptotically approaching T_g,∞.

### H. Elastic Modulus of Crosslinked Elastomer (Statistical Theory)

For a Gaussian network of N chains per unit volume, each with end-to-end vector **r**:

$$G = N k_B T = \frac{\rho R T}{M_c}$$

where:
- **G** = shear modulus
- **N** = number density of elastically active chains
- **k_B** = Boltzmann constant
- **ρ** = density
- **M_c** = molecular weight between crosslinks
- **R** = universal gas constant
- **T** = absolute temperature

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** This equation is used to **design PDMS stiffness** in BioMEMS cell-stretching devices by tuning the crosslink density (curing agent ratio).

---

## 2.4 Properties of Polymers Relevant to BioMEMS

### 2.4.1 Mechanical Properties

- **Young's Modulus (E):** 0.5–4 MPa for cured PDMS (vs. 130 GPa for silicon). Tunable via base:curing-agent ratio.
- **Elongation at break:** 100–800% for PDMS (highly stretchable).
- **Poisson's ratio:** ~0.5 for elastomers (incompressible).

### 2.4.2 Optical Properties

- PDMS: transparent down to ~280 nm (UV cutoff). Refractive index ~1.41.
- PMMA: ~92% transmission in visible range. Excellent for optofluidics.
- SU-8: transparent in visible; absorbs in UV (used as photoresist).

### 2.4.3 Chemical Properties

- **Solvent resistance:** PDMS swells in non-polar solvents (hexane, toluene); PMMA dissolves in acetone.
- **Surface chemistry:** Plasma oxidation converts PDMS surface from hydrophobic (CH₃) to hydrophilic (Si–OH, Si–O–Si). Irreversible glass-like skin forms. Hydrophilic recovery occurs within hours.

### 2.4.4 Thermal Properties

- **Glass transition temperature (T_g):** PDMS = –125 °C, PMMA = 105 °C, SU-8 (cured) ≈ 200 °C, PS = 100 °C, PEG = –67 °C.
- **PDMS usable range:** –45 °C to +200 °C (excellent for biological assays from cryogenic to body temperature).
- **Coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE):** PDMS ~ 310 µm/m/°C (very high — important for thermal expansion design).

### 2.4.5 Biocompatibility

- **PDMS:** FDA-approved for certain implants; generally biocompatible but hydrophobic surface adsorbs proteins nonspecifically.
- **PEG:** Gold standard for non-fouling surface coating; reduces protein adsorption dramatically.
- **PLGA:** Biodegradable, FDA-approved for sutures, drug-delivery microspheres.
- **Chitosan:** Biocompatible, antimicrobial, biodegradable.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The choice of polymer in BioMEMS is dictated by the **application**:
> - *Cell-stretching* → soft PDMS (E ~ 0.5 MPa)
> - *Microneedles* → rigid biodegradable PLGA
> - *Anti-fouling coating* → PEG grafting
> - *High-aspect-ratio scaffolds* → SU-8 photolithography

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Carothers Equation for Step-Growth Polymerization

### Statement
For a step-growth polymerization of bifunctional monomers A–A and B–B with **stoichiometric balance** and **equal reactivity** of functional groups, derive the relationship between the **number-average degree of polymerization (DP_n)** and the **fractional conversion (p)**.

### Setting Up the Problem

Let us define:
- **N_0** = total number of A-functional groups initially present = total number of B-functional groups initially present (equal stoichiometry).
- After conversion *p*:
  - Number of unreacted A groups = $N_0 (1 - p)$
  - Number of reacted A groups = $N_0 p$

Each reacted A-group forms **one A–B bond**, which joins **two monomer units** into a single chain. So the total number of **polymer chains (N)** present in the system is:

$$
N = \frac{\text{Total functional groups not yet reacted}}{2} = \frac{N_0(1-p) + N_0(1-p)}{2}
$$

We divide by 2 because each chain has 2 unreacted end groups (one A and one B at the two ends). Hence:

$$
N = N_0(1 - p)
$$

### Total Number of Monomer Units

Every monomer has been incorporated into some chain. The number of structural units in the polymer (counted as if monomers, not repeat units) is approximately equal to **N_0 / 2** (because each A–A monomer provides one A-group, and the corresponding B–B monomer provides the B-group; the total number of monomer molecules is N_0 — but for our purposes, the total number of repeat units effectively present is **N_0 / 2** when considering the bifunctional pairs).

To avoid this confusion, we instead define the number of **monomer units initially** as N_0 (counting each monomer molecule as one unit). At conversion p, the total number of monomer units *consumed* is N_0·p. But the total **monomer units present in all chains** is still N_0 (none are lost — only the chains grow, and small-molecule byproducts leave).

So the **number-average degree of polymerization** is:

$$
DP_n = \frac{\text{Total number of monomer units incorporated into chains}}{\text{Total number of chains}}
$$

$$
DP_n = \frac{N_0}{N}
$$

Substituting $N = N_0(1-p)$:

$$
DP_n = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}
$$

**Thus:**

$$
\boxed{DP_n = \frac{1}{1-p}}
$$

### Numerical Verification — KTU-Style Example

A nylon-6,6 batch uses hexamethylenediamine and adipic acid in 1:1 ratio. After 4 hours, the reaction mixture shows 95% conversion of the functional groups. Calculate DP_n.

**Given:** p = 0.95

$$
DP_n = \frac{1}{1 - 0.95} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20
$$

**Conclusion:** After 95% conversion, on average each nylon chain contains only 20 repeat units — still a relatively short oligomer. To reach DP_n = 100, conversion must be 99%. To reach DP_n = 1000, conversion must be 99.9%.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Modified Carothers Equation (Non-stoichiometric Case)

### Setup

Suppose we have A–A and B–B monomers, but their **stoichiometric ratio is r < 1**, where:

$$
r = \frac{N_A}{N_B} \leq 1
$$

Here N_A is the number of A-functional groups and N_B is the number of B-functional groups, with N_B ≥ N_A (B is in excess).

### Number of Reacted Groups at Conversion p of A-groups

- Reacted A-groups = $N_A \cdot p$
- The number of **bonds formed** = $N_A \cdot p$ (each A–B bond consumes one A and one B)
- **Unreacted A-groups** = $N_A(1 - p)$
- **Unreacted B-groups** = $N_B - N_A p = N_A(1/r - p)$ (in terms of N_A and r)

### Total Number of Chains

Each chain has two unreacted end-groups. The total number of unreacted end-groups is:

$$
\text{Unreacted ends} = N_A(1-p) + N_A\left(\frac{1}{r} - p\right) = N_A\left(1 + \frac{1}{r} - 2p\right)
$$

The number of chains is half of this:

$$
N = \frac{N_A}{2}\left(1 + \frac{1}{r} - 2p\right)
$$

### Total Number of Monomer Units Initially

The total structural units = $N_A + N_B$ in terms of molecules, but considering the **effective repeat unit count** for the polymer, we can use $N_A + N_B = N_A(1 + 1/r)$ monomer molecules initially.

### Degree of Polymerization

$$
DP_n = \frac{\text{Total monomer units}}{\text{Total chains}} = \frac{N_A(1 + 1/r)}{\frac{N_A}{2}(1 + 1/r - 2p)}
$$

$$
DP_n = \frac{2(1 + 1/r)}{1 + 1/r - 2p} = \frac{2(1 + r)/r}{(1 + r - 2rp)/r}
$$

$$
\boxed{DP_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}}
$$

### Limiting Case Check

When r = 1 (stoichiometric balance):

$$
DP_n = \frac{2}{2 - 2p} = \frac{1}{1-p}
$$

Which matches the basic Carothers equation. **Verified.**

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem — M_n and M_w Calculation (KTU Pattern)

### Problem
A polymer sample consists of 3 fractions:
- Fraction 1: 50% by number of chains, each of MW = 10,000 g/mol
- Fraction 2: 30% by number of chains, each of MW = 50,000 g/mol
- Fraction 3: 20% by number of chains, each of MW = 100,000 g/mol

Calculate M_n, M_w, and PDI.

### Solution

Let total number of chains N_total = 100 (for simplicity). So N_1 = 50, N_2 = 30, N_3 = 20.

**M_n Calculation:**

$$
M_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} = \frac{50 \times 10{,}000 + 30 \times 50{,}000 + 20 \times 100{,}000}{100}
$$

$$
M_n = \frac{500{,}000 + 1{,}500{,}000 + 2{,}000{,}000}{100} = \frac{4{,}000{,}000}{100}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_n = 40{,}000 \text{ g/mol}}
$$

**M_w Calculation:**

$$
M_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = \frac{50 \times (10{,}000)^2 + 30 \times (50{,}000)^2 + 20 \times (100{,}000)^2}{4{,}000{,}000}
$$

$$
M_w = \frac{50 \times 10^8 + 30 \times 25 \times 10^8 + 20 \times 100 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
M_w = \frac{5 \times 10^9 + 75 \times 10^9 + 200 \times 10^9}{4 \times 10^6} = \frac{280 \times 10^9}{4 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_w = 70{,}000 \text{ g/mol}}
$$

**PDI Calculation:**

$$
PDI = \frac{M_w}{M_n} = \frac{70{,}000}{40{,}000} = 1.75
$$

**Interpretation:** PDI = 1.75 indicates a moderately polydisperse polymer. The chains are not uniform in length; high-MW chains dominate the weight average due to the M_i² weighting.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice that M_w (70,000) > M_n (40,000). This is *always* true for any real polydisperse polymer because the squaring term in the M_w formula amplifies the contribution of heavier chains. M_w = M_n only when PDI = 1 (monodisperse — physically impossible for synthetic polymers).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Polymer Selection Decision Framework

| Application | Required Properties | Best Polymer Choice | Reason |
|------------|---------------------|---------------------|--------|
| Microfluidic channels (soft lithography) | Optical transparency, biocompatibility, gas permeability, easy molding | **PDMS (Sylgard 184)** | Replica-molded from SU-8/Si master, seals reversibly, gas exchange for cell culture |
| High-aspect-ratio microneedles | Mechanical rigidity, biocompatibility, biodegradability | **PLGA, PLA** | Bioresorbable, sufficient stiffness for skin penetration |
| Anti-fouling coating on biochips | Hydrophilicity, protein resistance, biocompatibility | **PEG** | Stealth property; reduces nonspecific adsorption |
| Point-of-care diagnostic chips | Optical clarity, low cost, mass production | **PMMA, COP, COC** | Hot embossing, injection molding, optical transparency |
| 3D scaffolds for tissue engineering | Porosity, biocompatibility, biodegradability, cell adhesion | **PLGA, collagen, chitosan** | Tunable degradation, supports cell growth |
| Micropumps / actuators | Stimulus-responsiveness | **Hydrogels, IPN polymers** | Swell/contract with pH, temperature, electric field |
| Lab-on-chip bonding | Adhesion, low temperature cure | **PDMS–PDMS plasma bonding, SU-8** | Forms irreversible Si–O–Si covalent bonds |
