---

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "polymer" in the context of BioMEMS. List any four polymers commonly used in BioMEMS device fabrication with one application each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A polymer is a high-molecular-weight macromolecule composed of a large number of repeating structural units called *monomers*, joined by covalent bonds. In BioMEMS, polymers serve as flexible, biocompatible, and easy-to-process alternatives to silicon and glass for fabricating microfluidic, biosensing, and tissue-engineering devices.

**Four Polymers with Applications (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

| Polymer | Application |
|---------|-------------|
| **PDMS (Polydimethylsiloxane)** | Soft lithography replica molding of microfluidic channels |
| **PMMA (Poly-methyl methacrylate)** | Hot embossing of lab-on-chip devices |
| **SU-8** | High-aspect-ratio photolithographic structures, microneedles |
| **PEG (Polyethylene glycol)** | Anti-fouling surface coating on biosensor chips |

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between chain-growth and step-growth polymerization mechanisms. Give two examples of polymers formed by each route.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Chain-Growth | Step-Growth |
|---------|--------------|-------------|
| Monomer type | Vinyl monomers with C=C bonds (1) | Bifunctional monomers with –OH, –COOH, –NH₂ (½) |
| Byproduct | No small molecule released (½) | Small molecule (H₂O, HCl) released (½) |
| Example | PDMS, PMMA, Polystyrene (½) | Nylon-6,6, PET, Polycarbonate (½) |

*(Marks distributed: 3 points covered above)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Pattern]**

**a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **chain-growth (addition) polymerization mechanism** in detail. List its three stages and explain each. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A polymer sample has the following distribution:

| Fraction | N_i (number) | M_i (g/mol) |
|----------|--------------|-------------|
| 1 | 20 | 20,000 |
| 2 | 50 | 50,000 |
| 3 | 30 | 100,000 |

Calculate **M_n, M_w, and PDI**. Comment on the polydispersity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Initiation (2 marks):**
- An initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide, AIBN) decomposes thermally or photochemically to generate a free radical R•.
- The radical attacks the π-bond of a vinyl monomer (CH₂=CHX), transferring the radical to the monomer, forming an active center.

$$
I \xrightarrow{\Delta \, or \, h\nu} 2 R^{\bullet}
$$

$$
R^{\bullet} + CH_2=CHX \rightarrow R-CH_2-\overset{\bullet}{C}HX
$$

**Propagation (2 marks):**
- The active chain radical sequentially adds monomers, with the radical migrating to the chain end. The chain grows rapidly (10³–10⁶ monomers in 1 second for free-radical polymerization).

$$
R-(CH_2-CHX)_n^{\bullet} + CH_2=CHX \rightarrow R-(CH_2-CHX)_{n+1}^{\bullet}
$$

**Termination (2 marks):**
- **Coupling:** Two chain radicals combine head-to-head.
- **Disproportionation:** One radical abstracts a hydrogen from another, forming one saturated and one unsaturated chain end.
- **Chain Transfer (1 mark):** Active center transfers to a solvent, monomer, or polymer molecule. Creates a branch and a new active center.

**Schematic (1 mark — already in Section 4.2 above):** Flow diagram of initiation → propagation → termination with appropriate annotations.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** N_1 = 20, M_1 = 20,000; N_2 = 50, M_2 = 50,000; N_3 = 30, M_3 = 100,000.

**Step 1: Calculate total number of chains (½ mark):**

$$
\sum N_i = 20 + 50 + 30 = 100
$$

**Step 2: Calculate $\sum N_i M_i$ (1 mark):**

$$
\sum N_i M_i = 20 \times 20{,}000 + 50 \times 50{,}000 + 30 \times 100{,}000
$$

$$
= 400{,}000 + 2{,}500{,}000 + 3{,}000{,}000 = 5{,}900{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Calculate $\sum N_i M_i^2$ (1 mark):**

$$
\sum N_i M_i^2 = 20 \times (20{,}000)^2 + 50 \times (50{,}000)^2 + 30 \times (100{,}000)^2
$$

$$
= 20 \times 4 \times 10^8 + 50 \times 25 \times 10^8 + 30 \times 100 \times 10^8
$$

$$
= 80 \times 10^8 + 1250 \times 10^8 + 3000 \times 10^8 = 4330 \times 10^8 = 4.33 \times 10^{11}
$$

**Step 4: Compute M_n (1 mark):**

$$
M_n = \frac{5{,}900{,}000}{100} = 59{,}000 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 5: Compute M_w (1 mark):**

$$
M_w = \frac{4.33 \times 10^{11}}{5{,}900{,}000} = \frac{4.33 \times 10^{11}}{5.9 \times 10^6} \approx 73{,}389.8 \text{ g/mol}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_w \approx 73{,}390 \text{ g/mol}}
$$

**Step 6: Compute PDI (1 mark):**

$$
PDI = \frac{M_w}{M_n} = \frac{73{,}390}{59{,}000} \approx 1.244
$$

**Step 7: Comment on polydispersity (1 mark):**

Since PDI = 1.244 > 1, the polymer is **polydisperse** (real polymer behavior). The value 1.244 is *moderately* polydisperse, indicating some spread in chain lengths. The weight-average is dominated by the high-MW fraction (100,000 g/mol), which constitutes only 30% by number but contributes the largest mass.

---

### **Question B [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Pattern — Internal Choice Alternative]**

**a)** What is the **degree of polymerization (DP_n)**? Derive the **Carothers equation** for a step-growth polymerization. A nylon-6,6 polymerization has reached 98% conversion. Calculate the number-average degree of polymerization. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the **glass transition temperature (T_g)** of a polymer. Describe the **Fox-Flory equation** and discuss how molecular weight affects T_g. Mention the typical T_g values of PDMS, PMMA, and SU-8. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** The number-average degree of polymerization (DP_n) is the average number of monomer (or repeat) units per polymer chain, defined as:

$$
DP_n = \frac{M_n}{M_0}
$$

where M_0 is the molecular weight of the repeat unit.

**Derivation (4 marks — see Section 3.1 for full derivation):**

- Initially, total functional groups = N_0 (for both A and B).
- After conversion p, unreacted groups = $N_0(1-p)$ on each side.
- Total number of chains N = $N_0(1-p)$ (each chain has 2 unreacted ends, so total unreacted ends = 2N).
- Total repeat units = N_0 (all monomers incorporated).

$$
DP_n = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}
$$

**Numerical calculation (2 marks):**

Given: p = 0.98

$$
DP_n = \frac{1}{1 - 0.98} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50
$$

**Interpretation:** At 98% conversion, the average chain length is only **50 repeat units**. To achieve high-MW nylon (DP_n > 100), conversion must exceed 99%.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Definition of T_g (2 marks):**
The glass transition temperature T_g is the temperature at which a polymer transitions from a hard, glassy state to a soft, rubbery state. Below T_g, polymer chain segmental motion is frozen; above T_g, large-amplitude chain motion becomes possible. T_g is **not** a sharp phase transition (unlike melting) but a second-order thermodynamic transition observed as a step change in heat capacity, expansion coefficient, and modulus.

**Fox-Flory Equation (2 marks):**

$$
T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{M_n}
$$

where T_{g,∞} is the asymptotic T_g at infinite molecular weight, and K is a polymer-specific constant related to chain-end free volume.

**Effect of Molecular Weight (1 mark):**
- As M_n increases, T_g *increases* and approaches T_{g,∞}.
- Low-MW polymers have many chain ends, which create free volume and lower T_g.
- The relationship is *non-linear* and asymptotic.

**Typical T_g values (2 marks — ⅔ mark each):**

| Polymer | T_g (°C) | Implication |
|---------|----------|-------------|
| **PDMS** | –125 | Highly flexible, rubbery at room temperature; ideal for soft BioMEMS |
| **PMMA** | +105 | Rigid at room temperature; suitable for structural microfluidic chips |
| **SU-8 (cured)** | ~+200 | Very rigid, high-T_g; suitable for high-aspect-ratio permanent structures |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The very low T_g of PDMS (–125 °C) means that at room temperature, the polymer is *far* above its glass transition — in the rubbery, elastic regime. This is why PDMS can be stretched up to 100% elongation. Conversely, SU-8 at room temperature is well below its T_g — glassy and rigid.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — Always write g/mol for molecular weight, dimensionless for PDI/DP_n, and °C or K for T_g.
> 2. **Confusing M_w and M_n formulas** — M_n uses linear weighting ($N_i M_i$), M_w uses squared weighting ($N_i M_i^2$). Many students swap the formulas.
> 3. **Carothers equation application** — Remember, the basic Carothers equation $DP_n = 1/(1-p)$ is valid *only* for **stoichiometrically balanced** systems. For imbalanced systems, use the modified equation with **r**.
> 4. **Misclassifying mechanisms** — Chain-growth requires **C=C unsaturation**, not just any monomer. Do not say "PMMA undergoes step-growth polymerization" — it is **chain-growth**.
> 5. **Ignoring the M_0 vs. monomer MW distinction** — In step-growth (e.g., nylon from hexamethylenediamine + adipic acid), the repeat unit MW ≠ monomer MW. For nylon-6,6, the repeat unit MW is 226 g/mol, but the individual monomer MWs are 116 and 146 g/mol.
> 6. **Saying "PDMS is biodegradable"** — It is *not* biodegradable. It is biostable.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

✅ **Polymer = many monomers** joined by covalent bonds. The smallest repeating unit is the **repeat unit (mer)**.

✅ **BioMEMS polymers** to remember: **PDMS, PMMA, SU-8, PEG, PLGA, Chitosan, Collagen**.

✅ **Chain-growth** = C=C + free radical + **no byproduct** + fast MW buildup. *Examples: PDMS, PMMA, PS, PE.*

✅ **Step-growth** = functional groups + **small-molecule byproduct (H₂O, HCl)** + slow MW buildup. *Examples: Nylon, PET, PC.*

✅ **M_n** = number-average molecular weight (linear weighting). **M_w** = weight-average molecular weight (squared weighting). Always **M_w ≥ M_n**.

✅ **PDI = M_w / M_n** ≥ 1. **PDI = 1** means monodisperse (ideal but impossible in real synthesis).

✅ **Carothers Equation:** $DP_n = 1/(1-p)$. To reach **DP_n = 100**, conversion p = 0.99. To reach **DP_n = 1000**, p = 0.999.

✅ **Modified Carothers (non-stoichiometric):** $DP_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$, where r = N_A/N_B.

✅ **Fox-Flory Equation:** $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K/M_n$. Higher M_n → higher T_g, asymptotically.

✅ **T_g values to memorize:** PDMS = –125 °C; PMMA = +105 °C; SU-8 ≈ +200 °C; PS = +100 °C; PEG = –67 °C.

✅ **PDMS properties** for BioMEMS: soft (E ~ 0.5–4 MPa), transparent to UV, gas-permeable, hydrophobic (becomes hydrophilic after O₂ plasma), low-cost, easy to mold, FDA-approved for limited use.

✅ **Plasma oxidation of PDMS:** Converts surface CH₃ → Si–OH; enables **irreversible PDMS–PDMS or PDMS–glass bonding** via Si–O–Si siloxane bonds.

✅ **PEG = gold standard anti-fouling polymer.** Reduces nonspecific protein adsorption when grafted as a brush.

✅ **PLGA = biodegradable, FDA-approved** for drug delivery and sutures. Degrades by hydrolysis into lactic + glycolic acid (biocompatible metabolites).

✅ **Crosslinking** converts liquid prepolymer (e.g., PDMS base) into an elastic solid. Crosslink density controls **modulus** and **swelling**.

✅ **Mechanical modulus of elastomer** = $G = \rho R T / M_c$. Lower M_c → denser network → stiffer material.

✅ **Step-growth requires ≥98% conversion** for useful MW. Stoichiometric imbalance (r ≠ 1) caps the achievable DP_n.

✅ **For any numerical KTU problem:** Show each step explicitly, include units, and state final answer in a **box**.

✅ **Difference between PDMS and SU-8 in fabrication:** PDMS = replica molding (soft lithography); SU-8 = direct photolithography of high-aspect-ratio structures.

✅ **Anti-fouling polymers:** PEG, zwitterionic poly(carboxybetaine), poly(sulfobetaine) — these resist nonspecific protein adsorption.

✅ **Biodegradable polymers:** PLGA, PLA, PCL, chitosan, collagen, alginate, gelatin, hyaluronic acid.

✅ **Biostable polymers:** PDMS, PMMA, SU-8, polyimide, poly(ethylene terephthalate), polystyrene.

✅ **KTU mantra:** When asked to *select* a polymer for a BioMEMS application, always justify the choice using **at least three properties** (e.g., biocompatibility + transparency + fabrication ease + mechanical compliance).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micromachining Techniques - Photolithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Micromachining Techniques - Photolithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Photolithography** (also called *optical lithography* or *UV lithography*) is a planar, pattern-transfer micromachining process in which a geometric pattern is transferred from a photomask onto a photosensitive polymer film (photoresist) coated on a substrate, by selective exposure to ultraviolet (UV) light, followed by chemical development to reveal the desired topographical or chemical pattern.

In the KTU 2024 BioMEMS context, photolithography is the **workhorse pattern-definition step** that defines the lateral dimensions (X–Y plane) of microfluidic channels, microelectrodes, sensor active areas, and chamber geometries in glass, silicon, and polymer substrates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):** Photolithography is grouped under *Micromachining Techniques* and is the prerequisite for subsequent processes like etching, lift-off, bonding, and soft lithography (covered later in the module).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are stamping a corporate logo onto a metal plate using a stencil:
- The **stencil** = the **photomask** (glass plate with chromium pattern).
- The **ink-sensitive sheet** you press against it = the **photoresist** on your silicon wafer.
- The **flashlight** that cures the exposed parts = the **UV light source**.
- The **rinsing step** that washes away the unwanted ink = **chemical development**.

The final engraved/raised pattern on the plate is exactly what becomes your microfluidic channel, electrode, or MEMS structure after etching or deposition.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line takeaway:** Photolithography = *Selective UV-induced chemical change in a polymer film* → enables sub-micron to centimeter-scale patterning on a substrate.

### 1.3 Why Photolithography Matters in BioMEMS

- **Resolution:** Can pattern features down to **sub-micron** (modern DUV tools) and **1–10 µm** (standard contact/proximity tools used in academic BioMEMS labs).
- **Throughput:** A single 4-inch wafer can host **hundreds** of microfluidic devices in one exposure.
- **Compatibility:** Works on **Si, SiO₂, glass, PDMS master, SU-8, and polymer substrates** — covering ~95% of BioMEMS materials.
- **Standard metric (KTU expects):** A typical contact-mode exposure resolves features near **2–5 µm**; a stepper projects features near **0.13 µm** in production fabs.

### 1.4 Sub-Topics Covered

1. Photoresists (positive vs. negative)
2. Photolithography process flow (10 steps)
3. Exposure modes (contact, proximity, projection)
4. Light sources (g-line, h-line, i-line, DUV, EUV)
5. Resolution & Depth of Focus (Rayleigh criteria)
6. Mask aligners
7. Role in BioMEMS / microfluidic device fabrication
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Photoresist — The "Photosensitive Ink"

A **photoresist** is a liquid polymer (resin + photosensitive compound + solvent) that undergoes a **chemical change** in its solubility upon UV exposure.

| Property | Positive Photoresist | Negative Photoresist |
|---|---|---|
| Exposed region | **Becomes soluble** (developer washes it away) | **Becomes insoluble / cross-linked** |
| Un-exposed region | Remains on wafer | Washed away by developer |
| Resolution | **Higher** (no swelling) | Lower (resist swells in developer) |
| Typical examples | **AZ-series (AZ 5214, AZ 9260), S1813, PMMA** | **SU-8, NR7-1500P, ma-N 400** |
| Common use in BioMEMS | Lift-off, etching mask | Thick microfluidics (e.g., SU-8 channels) |
| Image in resist | **Same** as mask | **Mirror** of mask |

> [!NOTE]
> **SU-8** is a heavily used *negative* epoxy resist in BioMEMS for fabricating **tall microchannels (50–500 µm)** for cell culture and organ-on-chip platforms.

### 2.2 The 10-Step Photolithography Process Flow

Below is the standard KTU-textbook flow executed in a **Class 100 / Class 1000 cleanroom**:

1. **Substrate Cleaning** — RCA cleaning, piranha (H₂SO₄:H₂O₂), or acetone/IPA rinse to remove organic/inorganic contaminants.
2. **Dehydration Bake** — 150–200 °C for ~10–30 min to remove adsorbed moisture.
3. **Priming (HMDS vapor or liquid)** — Hexamethyldisilazane promotes **photoresist adhesion** to the substrate; without HMDS, resist can peel during development.
4. **Photoresist Coating (Spin Coating)** — Dispense resist, spin at **500–6000 rpm** to obtain a uniform film.
5. **Soft Bake (Pre-Bake)** — 90–110 °C for 1–2 min on a hotplate; drives off solvent and stabilizes the film.
6. **Alignment & Exposure** — Mask is aligned to wafer fiducials; UV light (e.g., **i-line, 365 nm**) is shone through transparent regions of the mask.
7. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)** — Critical for chemically amplified resists (e.g., SU-8, AZ 5214-image-reversal) to drive the photoacid-catalyzed reaction.
8. **Development** — Immersion or puddle development; **TMAH (0.26 N)** for positive, **SU-8 developer** for negative.
9. **Hard Bake (Post-Develop Bake)** — 110–130 °C; improves etch resistance and mechanical stability.
10. **Inspection** — Optical microscopy, SEM, profilometry, and CD-SEM measurement of critical dimensions.

### 2.3 Exposure Modes

| Mode | Gap (mask–wafer) | Resolution | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact** | ≈ 0 µm (mask touches resist) | **~0.5–2 µm** | Academic/research labs (high defect density) |
| **Proximity** | 10–50 µm | **~2–5 µm** | Lower-defect academic work |
| **Projection (Stepper)** | Optical lenses project mask image | **<0.5 µm** (DUV) | Industrial semiconductor fabs |

### 2.4 Light Sources — Wavelength is the Resolution Lever

| Source | Wavelength (λ) | Typical NA | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **g-line (Hg i-line lamp)** | 436 nm | 0.40 – 0.55 | Older MEMS labs |
| **h-line** | 405 nm | 0.45 – 0.55 | Mid-range |
| **i-line** | 365 nm | 0.50 – 0.65 | Standard BioMEMS |
| **DUV (KrF excimer)** | 248 nm | 0.70 – 0.85 | Sub-0.25 µm CMOS |
| **DUV (ArF excimer)** | 193 nm | 0.85 – 1.35 | Sub-0.1 µm |
| **EUV** | 13.5 nm | 0.33 (catadioptric) | Leading-edge (not in academic KTU labs) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering rule of thumb (for exams):** *Shorter wavelength → smaller diffraction → higher resolution, but more expensive optics & tighter process control.*

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimum Resolution (Rayleigh) | $$R \;=\; k_{1}\,\dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$$ | $R$ = half-pitch resolution (µm), $\lambda$ = wavelength (nm), $NA$ = numerical aperture, $k_1}$ = process factor (≈ 0.4 – 0.8) | Smaller $R$ ⇒ finer features |
| 2 | Depth of Focus | $$DOF \;=\; \pm\,k_{2}\,\dfrac{\lambda}{NA^{2}}$$ | $DOF$ in µm; $k_{2} \approx 1$ | Defines the allowable resist-thickness window |
| 3 | Numerical Aperture | $$NA \;=\; n \sin\theta$$ | $n$ = refractive index of medium, $\theta$ = half-angle of cone of light | For air, $n=1$; for immersion lithography, $n \approx 1.44$ (water) |
| 4 | Exposure Dose (Energy) | $$E \;=\; I \cdot t$$ | $E$ in mJ/cm², $I$ in mW/cm², $t$ in seconds | Positive resist: ~50–100 mJ/cm² (i-line); SU-8: ~150–250 mJ/cm² |
| 5 | Spin-Coating Thickness (empirical) | $$h \;\propto\; \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\omega}}$$ | $h$ = film thickness, $\omega$ = angular velocity (rpm) | Exact: $h = K \, C^{\beta} / \omega^{1/2}$ (K, β are resist constants) |
| 6 | Modulation Transfer (contrast) | $$\gamma \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{E_{\max}}{E_{\min}}\right)}$$ | $\gamma$ = resist contrast | $\gamma \uparrow$ ⇒ sharper sidewalls |
| 7 | Line-Edge Roughness (LER) | $$LER \;=\; 3\sigma_{\text{edge}}$$ | σ in nm | Driven by photon shot noise, resist grain |

> **Board-exam tip:** Always write $R = k_{1}\lambda/NA$ with the **Rayleigh constant $k_{1}$** explicitly — KTU examiners award full marks only when the constant is named.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Microfluidic channel masters:** Photolithography defines the SU-8 master that is later replicated in PDMS for organ-on-chip devices.
- **Biosensor electrodes:** Au/Cr electrodes for glucose sensors, ISFETs, and electrochemical DNA sensors are patterned by **photolithography + lift-off**.
- **Cantilever fabrication:** Polymeric micro-cantilevers for AFM-style biosensors are released using photolithography as the mask.
- **Lab-on-Chip alignment marks:** Photolithography etches fiducials that allow bonding alignment between glass and PDMS layers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rayleigh Resolution Criterion

The Rayleigh criterion for the smallest resolvable feature in an optical projection system is derived from the diffraction of a plane wave through a circular aperture (Airy disk analysis).

**Step 1 — Single-slit diffraction pattern.**
A circular lens of diameter $D$ focuses a plane wave of wavelength $\lambda$. The first minimum of the Airy pattern occurs at an angle $\theta$ given by:

$$
\sin\theta \;=\; 1.22\,\dfrac{\lambda}{D}
$$

This $\theta$ is the half-angle subtended by the first dark ring of the Airy disk at the focal point.

**Step 2 — Numerical aperture relation.**
The numerical aperture of the lens is defined (in air, $n=1$) as:

$$
NA \;=\; \sin\theta_{\max}
$$

For a circular lens of diameter $D$ and focal length $f$, geometry gives $\sin\theta \approx D / (2f)$ for small angles. Equating with the Airy result:

$$
NA \;\approx\; \dfrac{1.22\,\lambda}{2\,f}\cdot\dfrac{1}{1}
$$

**Step 3 — Diameter of the Airy spot at the image plane.**
The radius of the first dark ring at the wafer plane is:

$$
r_{\text{Airy}} \;=\; 1.22\,\dfrac{\lambda\,f}{D}
$$

Substituting $NA = D/(2f)$:

$$
r_{\text{Airy}} \;=\; 0.61\,\dfrac{\lambda}{NA}
$$

**Step 4 — Two-point resolution (Rayleigh criterion).**
For two equal-intensity point sources to be *just resolvable*, the maximum of one Airy disk must fall on the first minimum of the other. The centre-to-centre distance is therefore the diameter of the Airy disk:

$$
R_{\min} \;=\; 2\,r_{\text{Airy}} \;=\; 1.22\,\dfrac{\lambda}{NA}
$$

**Step 5 — Inclusion of process factor $k_1$.**
Modern photolithography systems use chemically amplified resists, phase-shift masks, and off-axis illumination that change the effective constant from 1.22 to an empirical $k_1$. The general working formula is:

$$
\boxed{\,R \;=\; k_{1}\,\dfrac{\lambda}{NA}\,}
$$

where $k_1$ is typically between 0.4 (state-of-the-art) and 0.8 (older contact/proximity tools).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Depth of Focus (DOF)

**Step 1 — Wavefront curvature on defocusing.**
When the wafer is displaced by $\Delta z$ from the focal plane, the converging spherical wavefront develops a path-length difference $\delta$ at the edge of the lens aperture:

$$
\delta \;=\; \Delta z\,\bigl(1-\cos\theta\bigr)
$$

**Step 2 — For small angles, expand the cosine.**

$$
\cos\theta \;\approx\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{\theta^{2}}{2}
$$

So:

$$
\delta \;\approx\; \dfrac{\Delta z\,\theta^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\sin\theta \approx \theta = NA$ (small-angle).**

$$
\delta \;\approx\; \dfrac{\Delta z\,(NA)^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Rayleigh tolerance.**
The image is considered acceptably sharp if $\delta \le \lambda/4$ (quarter-wavelength Strehl criterion). Setting $\delta = \lambda/4$ and solving for $\Delta z$:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta z\,(NA)^{2}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{\lambda}{4}
$$

$$
\Delta z \;=\; \dfrac{\lambda}{2\,(NA)^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — General Rayleigh DOF (with process factor $k_2$).**

$$
\boxed{\,DOF \;=\; \pm\,k_{2}\,\dfrac{\lambda}{NA^{2}}\,}
$$

with $k_2 \approx 1$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A BioMEMS lab uses a contact aligner with i-line illumination ($\lambda = 365$ nm), a lens numerical aperture $NA = 0.45$, and an empirically determined $k_1 = 0.7$. Compute the **minimum line resolution** and the **depth of focus** for a **2-µm-thick AZ 5214 photoresist film**. Will the resist fit inside the DOF?

**Step 1 — Compute minimum resolution.**

$$
R \;=\; k_{1}\,\dfrac{\lambda}{NA} \;=\; 0.7 \times \dfrac{365 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}}{0.45}
$$

$$
R \;=\; 0.7 \times 8.111 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} \;=\; 5.678 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R \;\approx\; 0.57\,\mu\text{m}\,}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute DOF (with $k_2 = 1$).**

$$
DOF \;=\; \pm\,\dfrac{\lambda}{NA^{2}} \;=\; \pm\,\dfrac{365 \times 10^{-9}}{(0.45)^{2}}
$$

$$
DOF \;=\; \pm\,\dfrac{3.65 \times 10^{-7}}{0.2025} \;=\; \pm\,1.802 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,DOF \;\approx\; \pm\,1.80\,\mu\text{m}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with resist thickness.**
Resist thickness $h = 2.0\,\mu$m. The acceptable variation of focus across the film is $2 \times \vert DOF \vert = 3.60\,\mu$m, which is **larger** than $h = 2.0\,\mu$m. So the 2-µm AZ 5214 film **fits inside the DOF** — the process is feasible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** As resist thickness approaches the DOF, sidewall slopes degrade and CD (critical dimension) control is lost. For thick resists (e.g., SU-8, 100 µm), a low-NA, longer-wavelength exposure is *required* to keep DOF large.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Exposure-Mode Trade-off

| Parameter | Contact | Proximity | Projection (Stepper) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resolution $R = k_1\lambda/NA$ | $\sim 0.7\,\mu$m (best) | $\sim 2\,\mu$m | $\sim 0.13\,\mu$m (ArF) |
| DOF | very small | small | small (but compensated) |
| Defect density (mask damage) | **High** (contact) | **Low** | Mask never touches wafer |
| Mask lifetime | Short | Medium | Long |
| Cost (academic lab) | Low (₹10–50 lakh) | Low | Very high (>₹50 crore) |
| BioMEMS use | Thick SU-8 masters | Mid-res electrodes | Industrial-scale lab-on-chip |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Photolithography Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Substrate Si or Glass] --> B[RCA / Piranha Cleaning]
    B --> C[Dehydration Bake 200C]
    C --> D[HMDS Priming]
    D --> E[Photoresist Dispense]
    E --> F[Spin Coating 500 to 6000 rpm]
    F --> G[Soft Bake 100C 1 min]
    G --> H[Mask Alignment on Aligner]
    H --> I[UV Exposure i line 365 nm]
    I --> J[Post Exposure Bake]
    J --> K[Development TMAH or SU8 Dev]
    K --> L[Hard Bake 120C]
    L --> M{Quality Inspection}
    M -->|Pass| N[Patterned Wafer Ready for Etch or Lift Off]
    M -->|Fail| O[Strip Resist and Reclean]
    O --> B
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each rectangular block is a *process station*; the diamond `M` is a *decision gate* typical of process-control loops in a KTU-grade cleanroom protocol. The recycle path from `O` back to `B` represents a rework cycle when resist profile, CD, or defectivity is out of spec.

### 4.2 Functional Block — Components of a Mask Aligner

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UV[UV Lamp Source Hg or Excimer] --> COL[Collimating Optics]
    COL --> SHUTTER[Shutter and Dose Controller]
    SHUTTER --> MASK[Photomask with Cr Pattern]
    MASK --> OBJ[Objective Lens or Contact Gap]
    OBJ --> WAFF[Wafer Chuck and Stage]
    WAFF --> MON[Exposure Dose Monitor]
    MON --> SHUTTER
```

> **Block-level architecture** maps the optical path: lamp → collimation → exposure dose control → mask → projection onto wafer, with a closed-loop dose monitor feeding back to the shutter for constant energy-per-area delivery.

### 4.3 Positive vs. Negative Photoresist — Functional Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Starting Wafer with Uniform Resist] --> EXPOSURE[UV Exposure Through Mask]
    EXPOSURE --> POS[Positive Resist: Exposed Regions Soluble]
    EXPOSURE --> NEG[Negative Resist: Exposed Regions Cross Linked]
    POS --> DEVPOS[Developer Removes Exposed Areas]
    NEG --> DEVNEG[Developer Removes Un Exposed Areas]
    DEVPOS --> OUTPOS[Pattern is Copy of Mask Openings]
    DEVNEG --> OUTNEG[Pattern is Complement of Mask Openings]
```

> **Engineering insight:** The fact that a *positive* resist copies the mask and a *negative* resist gives its complement is the single most-tested concept in KTU Module 1 short-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
Differentiate between *positive* and *negative* photoresists. Give **one example** of each, and state which type is preferred for fabricating **tall (≥100 µm) microfluidic channels** in BioMEMS. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Positive photoresist:** Upon UV exposure, the illuminated regions undergo a chemical change (e.g., **photo-decomposition of DNQ inhibitor in AZ-series**) that makes them **more soluble** in the aqueous developer (e.g., 0.26 N TMAH). The unexposed regions remain on the wafer. *Example: AZ 5214, S1813, PMMA.* (1 mark)
- **Negative photoresist:** UV exposure triggers **photo-initiated cross-linking** of polymer chains (e.g., SU-8 epoxy), making the exposed regions **less soluble / insoluble**. The unexposed regions are washed away by the developer. *Example: SU-8, NR7-1500P, ma-N 400.* (1 mark)
- **For tall (≥100 µm) microfluidic channels:** **Negative photoresist — SU-8** is preferred because it can be spin-coated in multiple layers to high thickness (up to ~500 µm), offers **near-vertical sidewalls (≈ 88–90°)**, and is optically transparent, allowing exposure of thick films. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
Define **Depth of Focus (DOF)** in optical lithography. State its formula and explain **what happens to the DOF when the numerical aperture (NA) is increased** to improve resolution. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition:** The Depth of Focus is the range of axial distance (above and below the focal plane) over which the aerial image remains acceptably sharp, typically defined by a **quarter-wavelength Strehl tolerance** ($\delta = \lambda/4$). (1 mark)
- **Formula:** $DOF = \pm\,k_{2}\,\dfrac{\lambda}{NA^{2}}$, with $k_2 \approx 1$. (1 mark)
- **Effect of increasing NA:** From the formula, DOF scales as $1/NA^{2}$. Hence, **doubling NA reduces DOF to one-quarter**. This creates a *resolution–DOF trade-off* — high-NA projection steppers achieve finer features but demand ultra-thin, ultra-planar resist films, since thick resists fall outside the DOF window. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **step-by-step photolithography process flow** used to pattern a photoresist film on a silicon wafer for a BioMEMS microfluidic application. Mention the **function of HMDS priming** and the **role of soft bake**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **Rayleigh resolution criterion** $R = k_{1}\lambda/NA$ for an optical lithography system starting from the Airy-disk diffraction condition. For a stepper with $\lambda = 248$ nm, $NA = 0.75$, and $k_1 = 0.4$, compute the **minimum printable half-pitch**. Comment on whether this resolution is suitable for **sub-micrometer BioMEMS transducer arrays**. (7 marks)

---

**Model Answer — (a) (7 marks)**

1. **Substrate Cleaning (1 mark):** Si wafer is cleaned using **RCA-1** (NH₄OH:H₂O₂:H₂O, 1:1:5, 75 °C) to remove organic contaminants and particulates, followed by a **dilute HF dip** to strip native oxide if needed.
2. **Dehydration Bake (0.5 mark):** 150–200 °C for 10–30 min on a hotplate to desorb water vapour; critical for resist adhesion.
3. **HMDS Priming (1 mark):** **Hexamethyldisilazane** is vapour-deposited on the wafer. HMDS reacts with surface –OH groups to form a **hydrophobic methyl-terminated monolayer**, which improves resist wetting and prevents resist peel-off during development.
4. **Photoresist Coating (0.5 mark):** Liquid resist is dispensed and spun at 500–6000 rpm; centrifugal force spreads a uniform film. Thickness $h \propto 1/\sqrt{\omega}$.
5. **Soft Bake (Pre-Bake) (1 mark):** 90–110 °C, 1–2 min on a hotplate. **Function:** drives off residual casting solvent, **densifies the film**, improves **adhesion and uniformity**, and stabilises the resist for exposure.
6. **Alignment & Exposure (0.5 mark):** Mask aligned to wafer fiducials under a microscope; UV light is exposed through transparent regions of the mask, transferring the pattern into the resist's latent image.
7. **Post-Exposure Bake & Development (1 mark):** For chemically amplified resists, PEB drives the photoacid-catalyzed reaction. Development in TMAH (positive) or SU-8 developer (negative) reveals the pattern.
8. **Hard Bake & Inspection (0.5 mark):** 110–130 °C hard bake; optical/SEM inspection for CD and defectivity.

A neat **labelled block diagram** (as shown in Section 4.1) earns the **remaining 1 mark**.

---

**Model Answer — (b) (7 marks)**

**Derivation (5 marks):**
Starting from the Airy diffraction condition for a circular aperture, $\sin\theta = 1.22\,\lambda/D$. The radius of the first dark ring at the focal plane is $r_{\text{Airy}} = 1.22\,\lambda f / D$. Substituting $NA = D/(2f)$:

$$
r_{\text{Airy}} \;=\; 0.61\,\dfrac{\lambda}{NA}
$$

For two-point resolution (Rayleigh criterion), the centre-to-centre distance is the diameter:

$$
R_{\min} \;=\; 2\,r_{\text{Airy}} \;=\; 1.22\,\dfrac{\lambda}{NA}
$$

Including the empirical process constant $k_1$ (which absorbs resist, mask, and illumination effects):

$$
\boxed{\,R \;=\; k_{1}\,\dfrac{\lambda}{NA}\,}
$$

**Numerical computation (1.5 marks):**

$$
R \;=\; 0.4 \times \dfrac{248 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}}{0.75}
$$

$$
R \;=\; 0.4 \times 3.307 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} \;=\; 1.323 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R \;\approx\; 132\,\text{nm}\,}
$$

**Comment (0.5 mark):**
A **132-nm half-pitch** is *more than sufficient* for sub-micrometre BioMEMS transducer arrays (typical pitch: 0.5–5 µm). However, such resolution demands **DUV-grade stepper infrastructure, phase-shift masks, and tightly controlled cleanroom (Class 10 or better)**, which is generally not available in academic KTU labs.

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **three exposure modes** in photolithography — *contact*, *proximity*, and *projection* — with **neat sketches**, comparison of resolution, and comment on the **mask-damage / defectivity trade-off**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A photolithography system uses $\lambda = 365$ nm (i-line) with $NA = 0.50$. The system is used to expose a **6-µm-thick SU-8 photoresist** for a microfluidic channel. Compute the **theoretical resolution** (assume $k_1 = 0.7$) and the **depth of focus** (assume $k_2 = 1$). State whether the **6-µm SU-8 film fits inside the DOF** and justify. (7 marks)

---

**Model Answer — (a) (7 marks)**

| Mode | Gap (mask–resist) | Resolution | Mask Damage / Defectivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact** | **0 µm (mask touches resist)** | **~0.5–2 µm** (best) | **High** — mask gets scratched; particulates from mask transfer to wafer |
| **Proximity** | **10–50 µm** | **~2–5 µm** | **Low** — mask never touches wafer; some diffraction loss reduces resolution |
| **Projection** | Lens projects image; mask far from wafer | **<0.5 µm** (stepper), down to **<0.1 µm** (ArF immersion) | **Very low** — mask reusable for thousands of exposures |

**Block diagram / sketch (3 marks):** Three simple side-view sketches showing mask + resist + UV source, with gap distances clearly marked.

**Engineering discussion (1 mark):** Contact mode is the *highest-resolution low-cost* option for academic BioMEMS labs, but mask lifetime is short and **defectivity dominates yield**. Projection steppers are *low-defectivity high-throughput* industrial tools with immense capital cost. Proximity mode is a *compromise* rarely used today.

---

**Model Answer — (b) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Resolution (2 marks).**

$$
R \;=\; k_{1}\,\dfrac{\lambda}{NA} \;=\; 0.7 \times \dfrac{365 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}}{0.50}
$$

$$
R \;=\; 0.7 \times 7.30 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} \;=\; 5.11 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R \;\approx\; 0.51\,\mu\text{m}\,}
$$

**Step 2 — Depth of Focus (2 marks).**

$$
DOF \;=\; \pm\,k_{2}\,\dfrac{\lambda}{NA^{2}} \;=\; \pm\,\dfrac{365 \times 10^{-9}}{(0.50)^{2}}
$$

$$
DOF \;=\; \pm\,\dfrac{3.65 \times 10^{-7}}{0.25} \;=\; \pm\,1.46 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,DOF \;\approx\; \pm\,1.46\,\mu\text{m}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Fit-check (2 marks).**
Total usable focus window = $2 \times \vert DOF \vert = 2.92\,\mu\text{m}$. Resist thickness $h = 6.0\,\mu\text{m} > 2.92\,\mu\text{m}$. Therefore, **the 6-µm SU-8 film does NOT fit inside the DOF** for the given optical settings.

**Step 4 — Engineering remedy (1 mark).** To accommodate 6-µm resist, the lab must (i) **reduce NA** (e.g., to 0.25, raising DOF to ~5.84 µm) at the cost of coarser resolution, or (ii) use **multi-step focus-exposure (Focus-Energy Matrix, FEM)** to extend usable DOF, or (iii) thin the resist — neither desirable for a tall microfluidic channel. This illustrates the **resolution–DOF–resist-thickness triangle** that BioMEMS engineers constantly navigate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the Rayleigh constant $k_1$ in $R = k_1\lambda/NA$.** Writing only $R = 1.22\lambda/NA$ is treated as an *incomplete derivation* → **deduct 1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Unit errors:** Mixing nm with µm, or forgetting to convert $\lambda$ to consistent units before dividing. Always convert to **metres (SI)**.
> 3. **Confusing positive and negative resist behaviour** in the same sentence. State clearly *which region is washed away*.
> 4. **Skipping the role of HMDS.** KTU board expects the *adhesion-promoter* mechanism to be explained, not just named.
> 5. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-questions:** KTU awards a **dedicated 1–2 marks** for a neat, labelled schematic — omitting it costs almost a full grade level.
> 6. **Treating DOF as a single-sided quantity.** DOF is $\pm k_2\lambda/NA^2$, so the *total* focus budget is $2 \times DOF$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Photolithography = pattern transfer from a mask to a photoresist using UV light**, followed by chemical development. *(Module 1, BioMEMS)*
- **Photoresist types:** *Positive* (exposed area removed) — e.g., AZ 5214; *Negative* (exposed area retained) — e.g., **SU-8** for tall microfluidic channels.
- **Process flow (10 steps):** Cleaning → Dehydration → **HMDS priming** → Spin coating → **Soft bake** → Alignment & Exposure → PEB → Development → Hard bake → Inspection.
- **Exposure modes:** Contact (high-R, high-defect) / Proximity (medium) / Projection (best, industrial).
- **Rayleigh resolution:** $R = k_1\lambda/NA$. **DOF:** $DOF = \pm\,k_2\lambda/NA^{2}$.
- **Resolution–DOF–resist-thickness triangle:** Higher NA gives finer features but smaller DOF → thinner resists required. *This is the central trade-off question in every KTU exam.*
- **Light sources:** Shorter $\lambda$ (i-line 365 nm → DUV 248/193 nm → EUV 13.5 nm) → finer resolution, but cost and complexity rise sharply.
- **BioMEMS applications:** SU-8 masters for PDMS replication, Au/Cr electrodes via lift-off, channel definition, alignment fiducials, micro-cantilever patterning.
- **Standard numerical anchors (memorise for exam):** $\lambda = 365$ nm (i-line), $NA = 0.45$–$0.65$, $k_1 \approx 0.4$–$0.8$, $k_2 \approx 1$, SU-8 thickness range 1–500 µm.
- **Key takeaways for the 14-mark question:** Always (i) state the formula with constants, (ii) show unit-consistent numerical substitution, (iii) interpret the result in the *BioMEMS / microfluidic* context, and (iv) include a **labelled diagram** for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thin Film Deposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thin Film Deposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Thin Film Deposition** is a vacuum-based or solution-based microfabrication process in which atoms, molecules, or ions of a source material are transported onto a substrate surface to form a continuous, mechanically adhered layer whose thickness typically ranges from a few nanometers to several micrometers. In the context of **BioMEMS**, thin films are the foundational building blocks for fabricating biosensors, microfluidic channels, biocompatible coatings, electrode interfaces, and protective encapsulation layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Thin film deposition refers to the controlled physical or chemical transfer of material from a source phase (solid, liquid, or gas) onto a substrate phase, producing a film with controlled thickness, composition, microstructure, and surface properties.

The two principal families of deposition techniques are:
- **Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD)** – pure physical transport (e.g., evaporation, sputtering).
- **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)** – chemical reaction of precursor gases on the substrate (e.g., LPCVD, PECVD).

A third category, **solution-based coating** (spin coating, dip coating), is widely used in BioMEMS for polymer films (PDMS, SU-8, parylene-C, biopolymers).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **spray-painting a wall** in a sealed, dust-free room:
- The paint droplets travel from the spray can (source) through clean air (vacuum chamber) and land on the wall (substrate).
- The thickness of paint depends on how long you spray (deposition time) and how far you stand (source-to-substrate distance).
- If you heat the paint, it evaporates faster and lands more uniformly (analogous to **thermal evaporation**).
- If you bombard the paint can with energetic ions, atoms fly off in all directions and coat every surface, even the sidewalls (analogous to **sputtering**).
- If the paint chemically reacts with the wall to form a stronger bond, that mimics **CVD**.

In BioMEMS, the "wall" is a silicon wafer or polymer substrate, and the "paint" is gold (electrodes), silicon dioxide (insulation), titanium (adhesion), or parylene (biocompatible encapsulation).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constants to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.380 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$
> - **Avogadro's number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \, \text{mol}^{-1}$
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_0 = 1.013 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$
> - **Typical deposition vacuum:** $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-7}$ **Torr**
> - **Typical thin film thickness range:** $10 \, \text{nm}$ to $10 \, \mu\text{m}$
> - **Mean free path at $10^{-3}$ Torr:** $\lambda \approx 100 \, \text{cm}$ (longer than chamber)

### 1.4 Why Thin Films Matter in BioMEMS

| Application | Film Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Biosensor electrodes | Au, Pt, Ti | Electrical transduction |
| Encapsulation | Parylene-C, Si$_3$N$_4$ | Biocompatible barrier |
| Adhesion layer | Cr, Ti | Bonding Au to SiO$_2$ |
| Dielectric insulator | SiO$_2$, Si$_3$N$_4$ | Electrical isolation |
| Hydrophilic/hydrophobic tuning | Teflon, OTS | Fluid flow control |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Thin Film Deposition Techniques

The deposition landscape is organized as follows:

1. **Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD)**
   - **Thermal Evaporation** (resistive, e-beam)
   - **Sputtering** (DC, RF, magnetron, reactive)
   - **Pulsed Laser Deposition (PLD)**

2. **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)**
   - **Atmospheric Pressure CVD (APCVD)**
   - **Low Pressure CVD (LPCVD)**
   - **Plasma Enhanced CVD (PECVD)**
   - **Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD)**

3. **Solution-Based Coating**
   - **Spin Coating**
   - **Dip Coating**
   - **Electrochemical Deposition**

### 2.2 Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

#### A. Thermal Evaporation
1. Source material (e.g., Au) is placed in a **tungsten filament or crucible**.
2. High current heats the source above its **melting point** (Au: $1064^\circ\text{C}$).
3. Atoms leave the source with a **cosine distribution** (Knudsen cosine law).
4. In **high vacuum** ($\leq 10^{-5}$ Torr), mean free path $\lambda$ exceeds chamber dimensions, so atoms travel in straight lines to the substrate.
5. Atoms **condense** on the cooler substrate, nucleate, and grow into a continuous film.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Thermal evaporation produces **poor step coverage** — atoms cannot reach the sidewalls of high-aspect-ratio features.

#### B. Sputtering
1. A target of source material (e.g., Ti) is bombarded with **inert Ar$^+$ ions** at $500$–$1000$ eV.
2. Momentum transfer ejects target atoms (sputtering yield $Y \approx 0.5$–$2$).
3. Ejected atoms travel omnidirectionally, providing **excellent step coverage**.
4. **Reactive sputtering** (e.g., O$_2$ introduced) forms compounds (e.g., TiO$_2$).

#### C. CVD Process Steps
1. **Gas transport** – precursor gases (e.g., SiH$_4$, NH$_3$) flow into reactor.
2. **Gas phase reactions** – homogeneous reactions may form unwanted particles.
3. **Diffusion** – reactants diffuse to the hot substrate.
4. **Surface adsorption** – molecules stick to substrate with sticking coefficient $S$.
5. **Surface reaction** – chemical reaction forms solid film + gaseous byproducts.
6. **Byproduct desorption** – gases leave the surface.
7. **Mass transport removal** – byproducts diffuse away.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **rate-limiting step** in CVD determines whether the process is **mass-transport limited** (high T, high P) or **surface-reaction limited** (low T, low P). For BioMEMS, **low T CVD** is preferred to protect polymer components.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{N \cdot A}{\pi r^2 \cdot \rho}$ | $R$: deposition rate (cm/s), $N$: evaporation rate (atoms/s), $A$: atomic weight (g/mol), $r$: source–substrate distance (cm), $\rho$: density (g/cm³) | Evaporation rate |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^2 P}$ | $\lambda$: mean free path (m), $d$: molecular diameter (m), $P$: pressure (Pa) | Vacuum design |
| 3 | $\theta = \dfrac{K P}{1 + K P}$ | $\theta$: surface coverage, $K$: adsorption equilibrium constant, $P$: pressure | Langmuir isotherm |
| 4 | $\text{Kn} = \dfrac{\lambda}{L}$ | Kn: Knudsen number, $L$: characteristic length | Flow regime check |
| 5 | $S = \dfrac{N_{\text{ads}}}{N_{\text{inc}}}$ | $S$: sticking coefficient (0 to 1), $N_{\text{ads}}$: adsorbed atoms, $N_{\text{inc}}$: incident atoms | Film quality |
| 6 | $Y = \dfrac{N_{\text{ejected}}}{N_{\text{incident}}}$ | $Y$: sputtering yield (atoms/ion) | Sputter rate |
| 7 | $\nu = \dfrac{h \, \omega \, t}{1 - e^{-h\omega/2\pi k_B T}}$ | $\nu$: adatom vibration freq., $t$: time | Nucleation theory |
| 8 | $h_{\text{film}} = \dfrac{\eta \, \omega^2}{2 \, \rho \, g}$ | $h$: film thickness, $\eta$: viscosity, $\omega$: spin speed, $\rho$: density, $g$: gravity | Spin coating |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In tables, do NOT confuse $\lambda$ (mean free path) with $\lambda$ (wavelength). Always check context!

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microfluidic chip sealing** – Parylene-C CVD for biocompatible channel encapsulation.
- **Neural probe insulation** – Si$_3$N$_4$ LPCVD/PECVD for sub-micron isolation.
- **Lab-on-chip electrodes** – e-beam evaporated Au/Ti for electrochemical biosensors.
- **Anti-fouling coatings** – ALD of Al$_2$O$_3$ on implantable sensors.
- **Drug delivery membranes** – Solution-cast polymer films for controlled release.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean Free Path of Gas Atoms

The mean free path $\lambda$ is the average distance a gas molecule travels between successive collisions. It is **fundamental** for designing vacuum deposition systems.

**Starting from kinetic theory of gases:**

Consider a molecule moving through a gas of stationary scatterers of diameter $d$. The collision cross-section is $\sigma = \pi d^2$. The number of scatterers per unit volume at pressure $P$ and temperature $T$ is $n = P / (k_B T)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Volume swept per unit length} = \pi d^2 \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Number of scatterers in volume } \pi d^2 \text{ per unit length} = n \cdot \pi d^2 \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \lambda = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot \pi d^2} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{P}{k_B T} \cdot \pi d^2} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\pi d^2 P} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{For identical molecules, both target and source move;} \\
& \text{relative velocity factor of } \sqrt{2} \text{ enters:} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^2 P}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Equation:**

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^2 P}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** For N$_2$ at $T = 300$ K, $P = 10^{-3}$ Torr $= 0.133$ Pa, and $d \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-10}$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{\sqrt{2} \times \pi \times (3.7 \times 10^{-10})^2 \times 0.133} \\
&= \dfrac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{\sqrt{2} \times \pi \times 1.369 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.133} \\
&= \dfrac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{8.08 \times 10^{-20}} \\
&\approx 5.1 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 5.1 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> At $10^{-3}$ Torr, $\lambda \approx 5$ cm, so a $30$ cm chamber provides **molecular flow** — ideal for line-of-sight evaporation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Deposition Rate for Thermal Evaporation (Langmuir Model)

Langmuir derived the evaporation rate $N$ (atoms/second) from a source of mass $m$, area $A_s$, vapor pressure $P_v$, and temperature $T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Ideal gas flux from kinetic theory: } \Phi = \dfrac{P_v}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Total evaporation rate: } N = \Phi \cdot A_s = \dfrac{P_v \cdot A_s}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Substrate at distance } r \text{ receives flux: } J = \dfrac{N \cos\theta}{\pi r^2} \text{ (cosine distribution)} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{For } \theta = 0 \text{ (substrate above source): } J = \dfrac{N}{\pi r^2} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Deposition rate (thickness per second): } R = \dfrac{J \cdot M}{N_A \cdot \rho} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & R = \dfrac{N \cdot M}{\pi r^2 \cdot N_A \cdot \rho} = \dfrac{N \cdot A}{\pi r^2 \cdot \rho}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $A = M/N_A$ is the atomic weight in grams (numerically equal to $A$ in g/mol). The cosine distribution assumption (Knudsen law) is valid when the source is small compared to $r$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The factor $1/(\pi r^2)$ assumes a **point source** with cosine distribution. If the source is a **small area**, use $1/(\pi r^2) \cdot A_s$ and integrate.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Sticking Coefficient & Film Nucleation

The **sticking coefficient** $S$ is the probability that an incident atom remains adsorbed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Incident flux on substrate: } J = \Phi \text{ (atoms/m}^2\text{/s)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Flux of re-evaporated atoms: } J_{\text{evap}} = n_s \nu \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_d}{k_B T}\right) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & n_s: \text{ surface site density}, \nu: \text{ adatom vibration frequency}, E_d: \text{ desorption energy} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Equilibrium condition: } S \cdot J = J_{\text{evap}} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & S = \dfrac{n_s \nu \exp(-E_d/k_B T)}{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **Au on Si at 300 K**: $E_d \approx 0.8$ eV, so $S \approx 1$ (Au sticks well). For **Au on PMMA at 300 K**: $E_d \approx 0.2$ eV, so $S \approx 0.3$ (poor adhesion — hence the need for Cr/Ti adhesion layers in BioMEMS).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm for CVD

For monolayer surface adsorption in CVD:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Rate of adsorption} \propto P (1-\theta): R_a = k_a P (1-\theta) \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Rate of desorption} \propto \theta: R_d = k_d \theta \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{At equilibrium: } R_a = R_d \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & k_a P (1-\theta) = k_d \theta \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & k_a P - k_a P \theta = k_d \theta \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & k_a P = \theta (k_d + k_a P) \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \theta = \dfrac{k_a P}{k_d + k_a P} = \dfrac{(k_a/k_d) P}{1 + (k_a/k_d) P} \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \text{Defining } K = k_a/k_d: \quad \theta = \dfrac{K P}{1 + K P}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the **Langmuir isotherm**. For very low pressure ($KP \ll 1$): $\theta \approx KP$ (linear). For high pressure ($KP \gg 1$): $\theta \to 1$ (saturation monolayer).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Thin Film Deposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Thin Film Deposition] --> B1[Physical Vapor Deposition PVD]
    A1 --> B2[Chemical Vapor Deposition CVD]
    A1 --> B3[Solution Based Coating]
    
    B1 --> C1[Thermal Evaporation]
    B1 --> C2[E Beam Evaporation]
    B1 --> C3[DC Sputtering]
    B1 --> C4[RF Sputtering]
    B1 --> C5[Magnetron Sputtering]
    B1 --> C6[Pulsed Laser Deposition]
    
    B2 --> D1[APCVD Atmospheric]
    B2 --> D2[LPCVD Low Pressure]
    B2 --> D3[PECVD Plasma Enhanced]
    B2 --> D4[ALD Atomic Layer]
    
    B3 --> E1[Spin Coating]
    B3 --> E2[Dip Coating]
    B3 --> E3[Electrochemical Deposition]
    
    C3 --> F1[High Purity Films]
    C4 --> F2[Insulator Films]
    C5 --> F3[High Rate Deposition]
    
    D4 --> G1[Sub Nanometer Control]
    D4 --> G2[3D Conformal Coating]
    
    E1 --> H1[Photoresist SU8]
    E2 --> H2[Parylene Polymer]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Thermal Evaporation System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Vacuum Chamber] --> A2[Turbo Pump]
    A2 --> A3[Rotary Pump]
    A1 --> A4[Crucible Source Material]
    A4 --> A5[Resistive Heater Filament]
    A5 --> A6[High Current Power Supply]
    A1 --> A7[Substrate Holder]
    A7 --> A8[Quartz Crystal Monitor QCM]
    A8 --> A9[Film Thickness Controller]
    A1 --> A10[Shutter]
    A10 --> A11[Pre Deposition Shield]
    A1 --> A12[Vacuum Gauge]
    A12 --> A13[Pressure Controller]
    A1 --> A14[Vapor Cloud]
    A14 --> A7
    A5 -->|Heat| A14
    A9 --> A10
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: CVD Reactor Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Precursor Gas Cylinder] --> B2[Mass Flow Controller MFC]
    B3[Carrier Gas N2 H2] --> B2
    B2 --> B4[Gas Mixing Manifold]
    B5[Dopant Gas Optional] --> B4
    B4 --> B6[Reactor Chamber]
    B6 --> B7[Heated Sub stage]
    B7 --> B8[Wafer Substrate]
    B8 --> B9[Surface Adsorption]
    B9 --> B10[Surface Chemical Reaction]
    B10 --> B11[Solid Film Deposition]
    B10 --> B12[Gaseous Byproducts]
    B12 --> B13[Exhaust Line]
    B13 --> B14[Vacuum Pump]
    B14 --> B15[Scrubber Neutralizer]
    B11 --> B16[Film Thickness and Uniformity Monitor]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Sputtering Process Mechanics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Ar Gas Inlet] --> C2[Plasma Chamber at 1e-3 Torr]
    C2 --> C3[DC or RF Power Supply]
    C3 --> C4[Target Cathode Source Material]
    C4 --> C5[Ar Positive Ions]
    C5 --> C6[High Energy Ion Bombardment]
    C6 --> C7[Momentum Transfer]
    C7 --> C8[Target Atom Ejection]
    C8 --> C9[Substrate Surface]
    C9 --> C10[Film Growth]

    C8 --> C9
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 14-mark questions:** Always include a labeled block diagram of the deposition system. Examiners award 2–3 marks specifically for a clear, correctly labeled schematic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define thin film deposition. List the two main categories with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

Thin film deposition is a process of depositing a thin layer (typically nm to µm) of material onto a substrate surface using physical or chemical means to modify its surface properties for BioMEMS applications.

- **Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD):** Material is physically transferred from a solid source. *Example: Thermal evaporation of gold electrodes.*
- **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD):** Material is formed via chemical reaction of precursor gases on the substrate. *Example: LPCVD of Si$_3$N$_4$ for neural probe insulation.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Key:** Definition (1 mark), PVD explanation + example (1 mark), CVD explanation + example (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between PVD and CVD processes based on (i) pressure, (ii) step coverage, and (iii) temperature.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PVD | CVD |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Pressure | High vacuum ($10^{-5}$ to $10^{-7}$ Torr) | Low pressure to atmospheric ($0.1$ to $760$ Torr) |
| (ii) Step coverage | Poor (line-of-sight, except sputtering) | Excellent (conformal) |
| (iii) Temperature | Lower (e-beam ~ RT to 500°C) | Higher (LPCVD 600–900°C, PECVD 200–400°C) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Key:** Each correct comparison with value = 1 mark × 3 = 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **thermal evaporation technique** for thin film deposition. Discuss its advantages and limitations in BioMEMS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **mean free path** of gas molecules. Calculate the mean free path of N$_2$ molecules at $P = 10^{-5}$ Torr and $T = 300$ K. Given molecular diameter $d = 3.7 \times 10^{-10}$ m, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, and $1$ Torr $= 133.3$ Pa. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Thermal Evaporation:**

- **Principle:** Source material is heated in vacuum to its evaporation temperature; vapor travels to substrate and condenses.
- **Block Diagram:** (Chamber → Filament/Crucible → Heated Source → Vapor Cloud → Substrate → QCM → Vacuum Pump)
- **Steps:**
  1. Load source (Au, Al) in tungsten boat.
  2. Mount substrate $10$–$30$ cm above source.
  3. Evacuate to $\leq 10^{-6}$ Torr.
  4. Pass high current ($50$–$100$ A) through boat.
  5. Source melts, evaporates; shutter opened.
  6. Vapor deposits on substrate; QCM monitors thickness.
- **Advantages:** High purity, low cost, fast for low-melting metals.
- **Limitations:** Poor step coverage, alloy composition drift, high-temperature damage to polymers.

**(b) Mean Free Path Derivation:**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^2 P}
$$

Convert pressure: $P = 10^{-5} \times 133.3 = 1.333 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{\sqrt{2} \times \pi \times (3.7 \times 10^{-10})^2 \times 1.333 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \dfrac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{\sqrt{2} \times \pi \times 1.369 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.333 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \dfrac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{\sqrt{2} \times 5.732 \times 10^{-22}} \\
&= \dfrac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{8.106 \times 10^{-22}} \\
&\approx 5.1 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

At $10^{-5}$ Torr, $\lambda \approx 5.1$ m, so even a $0.5$ m chamber provides **molecular flow**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Key (a):** Diagram 2 marks, explanation 2 marks, advantages 1.5 marks, limitations 1.5 marks.
> **Marking Key (b):** Formula derivation 3 marks, numerical substitution 2 marks, final answer with units 2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **sputtering process** for thin film deposition with a block diagram. Compare **DC sputtering vs RF sputtering**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** for CVD surface adsorption. A CVD process has $K = 10 \, \text{Pa}^{-1}$ at $T = 800$ K. Calculate the surface coverage $\theta$ at $P = 0.5 \, \text{Pa}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sputtering Process:**

- **Principle:** Energetic ions (typically Ar$^+$) bombard a target, ejecting atoms via momentum transfer, which then deposit on a substrate.
- **Block Diagram:** (Ar inlet → Plasma chamber → DC/RF power → Target cathode → Ar$^+$ ions → Bombardment → Ejected atoms → Substrate)
- **Process Steps:**
  1. Chamber evacuated to $10^{-7}$ Torr, backfilled with Ar to $10^{-3}$ Torr.
  2. High voltage applied between target (cathode) and substrate (anode).
  3. Plasma ignites; Ar$^+$ ions accelerate to target.
  4. Target atoms ejected with sputtering yield $Y = 0.5$–$2$.
  5. Ejected atoms deposit on substrate with **excellent step coverage**.

**DC vs RF Sputtering Comparison:**

| Feature | DC Sputtering | RF Sputtering |
|---|---|---|
| Power | DC (constant) | RF ($13.56$ MHz) |
| Target | Conductors only | Conductors + insulators |
| Charge buildup | Problem on insulators | Prevented by AC reversal |
| Deposition rate | Higher for metals | Slightly lower |

**(b) Langmuir Isotherm Derivation:**

As derived in Section 3.4:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{K P}{1 + K P}
$$

Substituting $K = 10 \, \text{Pa}^{-1}$ and $P = 0.5 \, \text{Pa}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \dfrac{10 \times 0.5}{1 + 10 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{5}{1 + 5} = \dfrac{5}{6} \\
\theta &\approx 0.833 \text{ or } 83.3\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Key (a):** Diagram 2 marks, process steps 2 marks, DC vs RF comparison 3 marks.
> **Marking Key (b):** Equilibrium setup 2 marks, algebra 2 marks, substitution + answer 3 marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in mean free path numerical — always state "m" or "cm".
> 2. **Mixing up $\lambda$ (mean free path) and $\nu$ (vibration frequency)** — examiners penalize this heavily.
> 3. **Drawing block diagrams without labels** — every block must be labeled; arrows alone are insufficient.
> 4. **Confusing Knudsen number regimes:** $\text{Kn} > 1$ = molecular flow (PVD); $\text{Kn} < 0.01$ = continuum (CVD/APCVD).
> 5. **Using thermal evaporation formulas for sputtering** — they have different physics.
> 6. **Skipping the "why"** — examiners award marks for explaining *why* a parameter is chosen (e.g., *why* $\leq 10^{-6}$ Torr is needed).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Thin Film Deposition**

- **Thin film deposition** = controlled transfer of material from source to substrate.
- **Two main families:** PVD (physical) and CVD (chemical).
- **Thermal evaporation:** cosine distribution, line-of-sight, poor step coverage, high purity.
- **Sputtering:** momentum transfer, conformal, can deposit insulators (RF), alloys possible.
- **CVD steps:** gas transport → diffusion → adsorption → reaction → desorption → exhaust.
- **Mean free path:** $\lambda = k_B T / (\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 P)$; at $10^{-5}$ Torr $\to \lambda \sim 5$ m.
- **Knudsen number:** $\text{Kn} = \lambda / L$; determines flow regime.
- **Langmuir isotherm:** $\theta = K P / (1 + K P)$; monolayer adsorption model.
- **Sticking coefficient $S$:** probability of adsorption; high $S$ for metals on cool substrates.
- **Sputtering yield $Y$:** typically $0.5$–$2$ atoms/ion for Ar$^+$ at $500$ eV.
- **ALD** offers sub-nm thickness control, ideal for BioMEMS sensor coatings.
- **Parylene-C** is the most common biocompatible CVD polymer for BioMEMS.
- **Ti/Cr adhesion layers** are essential for Au on SiO$_2$ in biosensor electrodes.
- **Always draw a labeled block diagram** for 14-mark questions (carries 2–3 marks).
- **Units and numeric answers** must be boxed or underlined per KTU valuation norms.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Thin Film Deposition**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Doping - Diffusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Doping - Diffusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Doping** is the intentional introduction of impurity atoms (dopants) into an intrinsic semiconductor (typically Silicon, Si) in precisely controlled concentrations (parts per million to parts per billion) to modify its electrical, mechanical, or chemical properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diffusion** is a thermal equilibrium mass-transport process in which dopant atoms migrate from regions of **high concentration** to regions of **low concentration** through the crystal lattice of the host semiconductor, driven by the chemical potential gradient (Fick's Laws). It is one of the two principal KTU-listed doping techniques, the other being **Ion Implantation**.

In BioMEMS and silicon-based microfluidic chip fabrication, diffusion doping is used to fabricate:
- Conductive electrodes on insulating substrates (e.g., borosilicate glass, Pyrex).
- Heater elements for PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction) microchambers.
- p-n junction biosensors (ISFETs, chemiresistors).
- Selective surface functionalization layers.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a single drop of red ink placed carefully in a still glass of clear water.

- The **ink** = the **dopant source** (e.g., Phosphorus vapor).
- The **water** = the **silicon crystal lattice**.
- The **spreading of color** = the **diffusion process** (driven by concentration gradient).
- **Time** controls how far the color spreads.
- **Water temperature** controls how fast the molecules move (analogous to **furnace temperature**).

If you heat the water, the ink spreads much faster — exactly the same way **high-temperature diffusion** drastically accelerates dopant penetration into silicon.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** Diffusion is a **statistical, random-walk** process at the atomic level. Atoms are constantly vibrating; given enough thermal energy and a concentration gradient, statistically more atoms jump "downhill" (toward lower concentration) than "uphill" — producing a net flux.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Boltzmann Constant | $k$ | **8.617 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K** |
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³ /mol** |
| Intrinsic Si atom density | $n_i$ | **5 × 10²² atoms/cm³** |
| Typical diffusion temperature | $T$ | **800 °C – 1200 °C** |
| Typical dopant dose | $Q$ | **10¹⁴ – 10¹⁶ atoms/cm²** |
| Diffusion depth (junction depth) | $x_j$ | **0.1 µm – 10 µm** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Step Diffusion Process

Modern semiconductor fabrication uses a **two-step diffusion sequence** to gain independent control over **surface concentration** and **junction depth**:

### Step 1: Predeposition (Constant Surface Concentration)
- Wafer is exposed to a **constant dopant vapor source** (e.g., POCl₃, BBr₃).
- Surface concentration $C_s$ is held at the **solid solubility limit** of the dopant in Si.
- Time is kept **short** so that the dopant does not penetrate too deeply.
- Dopant quantity is **NOT** conserved — fresh atoms continuously enter from the gas phase.

### Step 2: Drive-in (Constant Total Dopant / Limited Source)
- External source is **removed** (wafer placed in inert N₂ or O₂ ambient).
- The predeposited dopant is **redistributed** deeper into the wafer.
- Total dopant quantity $Q$ is **conserved** (no new atoms added or removed).
- $C_s$ **decreases** with time as the dopant spreads.

## 2.2 Fick's Two Laws of Diffusion

### Fick's First Law (Steady-State Flux)
The diffusion flux $J$ is proportional to the concentration gradient.

$$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

- $J$ = flux (atoms/cm²·s)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient (cm²/s)
- $C$ = dopant concentration (atoms/cm³)
- Negative sign: atoms flow from **high to low** concentration.

### Fick's Second Law (Time-Dependent, 1-D)
Derived by combining Fick's First Law with the continuity equation ($\partial C/\partial t = -\partial J/\partial x$), assuming $D$ is independent of $C$ (constant $D$ assumption):

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **constant $D$ assumption** holds well for low concentrations. At high doping (>10¹⁹ cm⁻³), $D$ becomes concentration-dependent, requiring the more complex **Boltzmann-Matano** analysis — beyond KTU 2024 scope.

## 2.3 Arrhenius Temperature Dependence

The diffusion coefficient $D$ is **strongly temperature-dependent** (this is the heart of the process control):

$$D = D_0 \, \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{kT}\right)$$

- $D_0$ = pre-exponential factor (cm²/s) — material/dopant specific
- $E_a$ = activation energy (eV) — typically **3.5 – 5.0 eV** for substitutional diffusion
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

| Dopant in Si | $D_0$ (cm²/s) | $E_a$ (eV) | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phosphorus (P) | 3.85 | 3.66 | n-type |
| Boron (B) | 0.76 | 3.46 | p-type |
| Arsenic (As) | 0.066 | 3.44 | n-type |
| Antimony (Sb) | 0.214 | 3.65 | n-type |

> [!TIP]
> **Exponential Insight:** A mere **100 °C rise** in furnace temperature can increase $D$ by **two orders of magnitude**. This is why diffusion furnaces require **±1 °C temperature stability**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| # | Equation / Condition | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fick's 1st Law | $J = -D \, \partial C/\partial x$ | atoms/cm²·s | Flux calculation |
| 2 | Fick's 2nd Law (1-D, constant D) | $\partial C/\partial t = D \, \partial^2 C/\partial x^2$ | atoms/cm³·s | Time evolution |
| 3 | Arrhenius relation | $D = D_0 \exp(-E_a/kT)$ | cm²/s | Temperature control |
| 4 | Predeposition profile (erfc) | $C(x,t) = C_s \, \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ | atoms/cm³ | Constant $C_s$ source |
| 5 | Drive-in profile (Gaussian) | $C(x,t) = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{\pi D t}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^2}{4Dt}\right)$ | atoms/cm³ | Constant total dose $Q$ |
| 6 | Junction depth | $x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt} \, \mathrm{erfc}^{-1}(C_B/C_s)$ | µm | Predeposition |
| 7 | Sheet resistance | $R_s = \dfrac{1}{q \int_0^{x_j} \mu(x)\,C(x)\,dx}$ | Ω/□ | Process monitoring |
| 8 | Solid solubility (P in Si @ 1000°C) | $C_s$ | **~10²¹ atoms/cm³** | Upper dopant limit |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the **two analytical solutions** (#4 and #5) and the **boundary conditions** that distinguish them. Examiners frequently test whether students correctly identify **which diffusion stage** a given profile represents.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Role of Diffusion Doping |
|---|---|
| **CMOS Fabrication** | Formation of source/drain regions, well doping |
| **BioMEMS Heaters** | Boron-doped Si used as resistive heaters for PCR thermal cycling |
| **Glass Microfluidic Chips** | Selective doping of Pyrex/glass to form conductive tracks |
| **ISFET Biosensors** | Creating p-n junction reference electrodes |
| **Solar Cells** | Emitter formation via POCl₃ diffusion |
| **Power Devices** | Deep diffused layers for high-voltage handling |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Predeposition (erfc) Solution

We solve Fick's 2nd law for the **constant surface concentration** case.

**Initial & Boundary Conditions (Predeposition):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(x, 0) &= 0 && \text{(initially pure Si, no dopant)} \\
C(0, t) &= C_s && \text{(surface held at solid solubility)} \\
C(\infty, t) &= 0 && \text{(no dopant at infinite depth)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply Laplace transform in $t$:**

Let $\tilde{C}(x,s) = \int_0^\infty C(x,t) e^{-st} dt$. Fick's law becomes:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \tilde{C}}{\partial x^2} = \frac{s}{D} \tilde{C}$$

**Step 2 — Solve this ODE** (general exponential form, reject growing term to satisfy $C \to 0$ at $x \to \infty$):

$$\tilde{C}(x,s) = A(s) \exp\!\left(-x\sqrt{s/D}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary $C(0,t) = C_s$**, which in Laplace domain gives $\tilde{C}(0,s) = C_s/s$:

$$A(s) = \frac{C_s}{s}$$

**Step 4 — Invert Laplace transform** using the known result $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{s}e^{-a\sqrt{s}}\right\} = \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{a}{2\sqrt{t}}\right)$:

$$C(x,t) = C_s \, \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reasoning recap:** We transformed the PDE → ODE → exponential solution → applied boundary → inverse transformed. The complementary error function $\mathrm{erfc}(u) = 1 - \mathrm{erf}(u)$ emerges naturally from the Laplace inversion tables.

**Step 5 — Junction depth** (where $C(x_j,t) = C_B$, the substrate background concentration):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= C_s \, \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x_j}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) \\[4pt]
\frac{C_B}{C_s} &= \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x_j}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) \\[4pt]
x_j &= 2\sqrt{Dt} \cdot \mathrm{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\frac{C_B}{C_s}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Drive-in (Gaussian) Solution

Now solve with **constant total dose** $Q$ (no external source after predeposition).

**Boundary & Initial Conditions (Drive-in):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^\infty C(x,0)\,dx &= Q && \text{(total dopant is conserved)} \\
C(\infty, t) &= 0 && \text{(no dopant at infinity)} \\
\left.\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right|_{x=0} &= 0 && \text{(no flux into the surface — reflecting boundary)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Try a Gaussian ansatz** with variance $\sigma^2 = 2Dt$:

$$C(x,t) = \frac{A}{\sqrt{t}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{4Dt}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Verify it satisfies Fick's 2nd law:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} &= -\frac{A}{2t^{3/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{4Dt}\right) + \frac{A}{\sqrt{t}} \cdot \frac{x^2}{4Dt^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{4Dt}\right) \\[4pt]
D\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} &= D \cdot \frac{A}{\sqrt{t}} \cdot \frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}\!\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{4Dt}\right)\right] \\[4pt]
&= D \cdot \frac{A}{\sqrt{t}} \cdot \left[\frac{x^2}{4D^2 t^2} - \frac{1}{2Dt}\right] \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{4Dt}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{A}{\sqrt{t}}\left[\frac{x^2}{4Dt^2} - \frac{1}{2t}\right] \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{4Dt}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The two sides match exactly — the Gaussian is a self-similar solution of the diffusion equation.

**Step 3 — Determine $A$ by total dose conservation** at $t = t_0$ (assume initial dopant is at the surface, a thin sheet):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \int_0^\infty \frac{A}{\sqrt{t}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{4Dt}\right) dx \\[4pt]
Q &= \frac{A}{\sqrt{t}} \cdot \sqrt{\pi D t} \quad \left(\text{since } \int_0^\infty e^{-u^2}du = \tfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\right) \\[4pt]
A &= \frac{Q}{\sqrt{\pi D}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final drive-in profile:**

$$C(x,t) = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{\pi D t}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{4Dt}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Surface concentration** (at $x=0$):

$$C(0,t) = C_s^{\text{drive-in}} = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{\pi D t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reasoning recap:** Notice $C_s$ **decreases** as $1/\sqrt{t}$ during drive-in — this matches the physical picture of dopant being "stretched out" deeper into the wafer while the total inventory stays fixed.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Predeposition vs. Drive-in Comparison

| Feature | Predeposition | Drive-in |
|---|---|---|
| External source | **ON** (constant vapor) | **OFF** (inert ambient) |
| Boundary at $x=0$ | $C(0,t) = C_s$ = const | $\partial C/\partial x \vert_{x=0} = 0$ |
| Dopant conservation | **NO** (gain of dopant) | **YES** ($Q$ = const) |
| Profile shape | **erfc** (complementary error fn) | **Gaussian** |
| $C_s$ vs. $t$ | $C_s$ = **constant** | $C_s \propto 1/\sqrt{t}$ (decreasing) |
| Junction depth growth | Slow | Faster (square-root time) |
| Sheet resistance | Lower | Higher (dilution) |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Phosphorus is predeposited into a silicon wafer at **1000 °C** for **30 minutes**. Given $D_P(1000°C) = 3.85 \times 10^{-14} \exp(-3.66/0.0862) \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-14}$ cm²/s, $C_s = 10^{21}$ cm⁻³, and substrate doping $C_B = 10^{15}$ cm⁻³. Find the junction depth $x_j$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\sqrt{Dt}$:**

$$\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{(2.0 \times 10^{-14})(30 \times 60)} = \sqrt{3.6 \times 10^{-11}} = 6.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the argument of $\mathrm{erfc}^{-1}$:**

$$\frac{C_B}{C_s} = \frac{10^{15}}{10^{21}} = 10^{-6}$$

From standard error function tables: $\mathrm{erfc}^{-1}(10^{-6}) \approx 3.4$

**Step 3 — Junction depth:**

$$x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt} \cdot \mathrm{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\frac{C_B}{C_s}\right) = 2 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.4 = 4.08 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm} = 0.408 \, \mu\text{m}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diffusion Process Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Si Wafer RCA Clean] --> B[Native Oxide Strip in HF Dip]
    B --> C[Load into Diffusion Furnace]
    C --> D{Process Step Selection}
    D --> E[Step 1 Predeposition POCl3 Source 800 to 1000 C]
    D --> F[Step 2 Drive-in N2 or O2 Ambient Higher Temp 1100 to 1200 C]
    E --> G[erfc Dopant Profile C_s constant]
    F --> H[Gaussian Dopant Profile Q constant]
    G --> I[Sheet Resistance Measurement Four Point Probe]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Within Spec}
    J -->|Yes| K[Proceed to Next Module Photolithography]
    J -->|No| L[Recondition Wafer Reset Process]
```

## 4.2 Diffusion Furnace Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Gas Cabinet N2 O2 POCl3 Bubbler] --> node2[Mass Flow Controllers MFC]
    node2 --> node3[Quartz Liner Tube]
    node3 --> node4[Heating Coils Three Zone]
    node4 --> node5[Si Wafer Boat 100 to 150 Wafers]
    node5 --> node6[Exhaust Scrubber]
    node4 --> node7[Temperature Controller Profile Ramps and Soak]
    node7 --> node4
```

## 4.3 Dopant Profile Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Surface x equals 0] --> B[erfc Profile Predeposition]
    A --> C[Gaussian Profile Drive-in]
    B --> D[Sharp Drop at Surface, Long Tail]
    C --> E[Round Peak at Surface, Symmetric Spread]
    D --> F[Junction x_j where C equals C_B]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Bulk Si Substrate Intrinsic]
```

## 4.4 Diffusion Mechanism at Atomic Level

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dopant Atom on Surface] --> B{Mechanism Type}
```
