# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
Define bulk micromachining. List **two** advantages and **two** disadvantages compared to surface micromachining for BioMEMS fabrication.

**Model Answer:**
Bulk micromachining is a subtractive microfabrication process where three-dimensional structures are created by selectively etching into the bulk of a silicon wafer using wet or dry chemical etchants, in contrast to surface micromachining which builds upon thin deposited films.

**Advantages:**
1. Produces high-aspect-ratio, mechanically robust structures (membranes, channels) with simple single-mask processing.
2. Excellent dimensional control via crystallographic etch stop, with sidewall angles fixed at 54.74° on (100) wafers.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Limited to geometries dictated by crystal planes; cannot fabricate arbitrary curved shapes.
2. KOH is not CMOS-compatible due to mobile ion (K⁺) contamination, complicating integration with electronic circuits.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
Explain why the etch rate of silicon in KOH is highly anisotropic, with the (111) plane etching ~400× slower than the (100) plane.

**Model Answer:**
The anisotropy arises from the **atomic packing density** of the silicon crystal. The (111) plane has the highest atomic density (≈1.57 × 10¹⁵ atoms/cm²) and the fewest dangling bonds per unit area, while the (100) plane has a lower density and more available surface bonds. KOH attacks Si by breaking Si–Si back-bonds; the greater the back-bond density (as in (111)), the slower the etch. This produces a typical anisotropy ratio of ~400:1 at 30 wt% KOH and 80°C.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Bulk Etching Geometry & Kinetics

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]** *(Module Internal Choice)*

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **anisotropic wet etching of (100) silicon in KOH**. Explain why pyramidal pits are formed with sidewall angles of 54.74°. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Process Description (3 marks):**
   - A (100)-oriented silicon wafer is thermally oxidized to grow a SiO₂ layer (~1 µm).
   - Photolithography + BHF etching patterns openings in the SiO₂.
   - The wafer is immersed in 30 wt% KOH at 80°C.
   - Etchant attacks exposed Si in the (100) direction at ~1.4 µm/min, while (111) is etched at ~0.004 µm/min.
   - Etching proceeds vertically until the slow (111) planes exposed at the mask edges intersect each other, forming a **pyramidal pit** with four symmetric (111) walls.
   - The pit self-terminates when all four walls meet at the apex.

2. **Why 54.74° (4 marks):**
   - The sidewall is the (111) plane; its normal is $\vec{n}_{111} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1,1,1)$.
   - The wafer surface normal is $\vec{n}_{100} = (1,0,0)$.
   - The angle between them: $\cos\theta = \vec{n}_{100} \cdot \vec{n}_{111} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$.
   - $\theta = \arccos(1/\sqrt{3}) = 54.74°$.
   - This angle is a **purely crystallographic** property — independent of etchant type, concentration, or temperature.
   - The wall tilts at 54.74° from the wafer surface (or 35.26° from the vertical), producing the characteristic KOH pit profile.

**(b)** A (100) silicon wafer of thickness **525 µm** is patterned with a square SiO₂ mask of side **1500 µm** aligned along $\langle 110 \rangle$ directions. The wafer is etched in KOH (30%, 80°C, $R_{100} = 1.4 \, \mu m/min$) until the pit reaches the bottom of the wafer.
- (i) Calculate the **etch time** required.
- (ii) Calculate the **bottom opening** dimensions.
- (iii) Comment on whether the **sidewalls are vertical or sloped**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Etch time:**
$$t = \frac{d_{wafer}}{R_{100}} = \frac{525 \, \mu m}{1.4 \, \mu m/min} = 375 \, min = 6.25 \, hours$$

**(ii) Bottom opening:**
$$L_{bottom} = L_0 - \sqrt{2} \cdot d_{wafer} = 1500 - \sqrt{2} \cdot 525$$
$$L_{bottom} = 1500 - 742.49 = 757.51 \, \mu m$$

**(iii) Sidewall orientation:**
The sidewalls are **(111) planes** that meet the (100) surface at **54.74°**. Hence the walls are **sloped, not vertical** — they form a square pyramidal pit that converges to a point at the bottom of the wafer. To obtain vertical sidewalls, a (110) wafer orientation or DRIE (Bosch process) must be used.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Etch Stops & Process Comparison

**(a)** Describe **three different etch stop techniques** used in bulk micromachining. Compare their selectivity and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Crystallographic Etch Stop (using (111) planes):**
- **Mechanism:** In KOH/TMAH etching, the (111) plane etches ~400× slower than (100). When the (100) etch front is bounded by (111) planes, etching effectively halts.
- **Selectivity:** ~400:1 (100:111) in KOH.
- **Application:** Fabrication of V-grooves, pyramidal pits, and self-aligned mechanical structures (e.g., inkjet nozzle plates).

**2. Boron (p⁺) Doping Etch Stop:**
- **Mechanism:** Heavy boron doping (>5 × 10¹⁹ cm⁻³) suppresses the anodic dissolution of Si. The etch rate drops to <0.001 µm/min.
- **Selectivity:** >1000:1 between lightly and heavily doped Si.
- **Application:** Precise silicon membranes of controlled thickness in pressure sensors, accelerometers, and micropumps.

**3. Electrochemical Etch Stop (pn-junction):**
- **Mechanism:** A reverse-biased pn-junction in the wafer is biased during etching in HF-based solution. The depletion region halts the dissolution of n-type Si.
- **Selectivity:** >10⁵:1 in HF/HNO₃ etchant.
- **Application:** Fabrication of ultrathin membranes (<1 µm) with excellent thickness uniformity.

**4. (Bonus) Photo-Assisted Etch Stop:**
- **Mechanism:** Illumination of n-Si generates electron-hole pairs that prevent the anodic reaction.
- **Application:** Selective control of etch depth in research-grade devices.

**(b)** Compare **wet bulk micromachining (KOH)** with **dry bulk micromachining (DRIE Bosch process)** in terms of mechanism, sidewall profile, selectivity, CMOS compatibility, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | **KOH (Wet)** | **DRIE (Dry, Bosch)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Liquid chemical reaction (OH⁻ attacks Si–Si bonds) | Alternating plasma etch (SF₆) and passivation (C₄F₈) cycles |
| **Sidewall profile** | Sloped at **54.74°** (crystallographic) | Vertical at **~90°** (timed) |
| **Selectivity to SiO₂** | ~200:1 | ~75:1 |
| **Selectivity to photoresist** | Poor | ~50:1 |
| **CMOS compatibility** | **No** (K⁺ contamination) | **Yes** |
| **Aspect ratio** | Up to ~50:1 | Up to ~100:1 |
| **Surface roughness** | Very smooth (atomically flat (111) walls) | Scalloped (10–500 nm features) |
| **Cost** | Low | High (vacuum + plasma equipment) |
| **Throughput** | High (batch processing) | Lower (single-wafer) |
| **Typical applications** | Microfluidic channels, V-grooves, membranes | Through-wafer vias, neural probes, gyroscopes, high-aspect-ratio comb drives |

**Conclusion:** KOH is preferred for **low-cost, smooth-walled** BioMEMS like microfluidic chips, while DRIE is preferred for **vertical, high-aspect-ratio** structures and **CMOS-integrated** devices.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing (100) and (111) planes** — Remember: (100) is the wafer surface (etches FAST); (111) is the sidewall (etches SLOW). Many students reverse these.
> 2. **Forgetting the √2 factor** in $L_{bottom} = L_0 - \sqrt{2} \cdot d$. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction if missed.
> 3. **Stating the sidewall angle as 45°** — it is **54.74°**, a crystallographic constant. The wall makes 35.26° with the vertical.
> 4. **Mixing up the formula $L = D/\tan(54.74°)$** — this gives the **lateral extension** of the sloped wall, not the undercut (which depends on $R_{111}$).
> 5. **Saying KOH is CMOS-compatible** — it is NOT. The K⁺ ions cause mobile ion contamination that destroys MOSFETs.
> 6. **Skipping the units** in numerical answers — always specify µm, min, or hours.
> 7. **Forgetting to align the mask edges along $\langle 110 \rangle$** — only this orientation gives clean (111) sidewalls. Misaligned edges produce jagged, irregular walls.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bulk Machining (PEEBT743, Module 1)**

**Core Concepts:**
- Bulk micromachining is a **subtractive** process that removes bulk Si (not deposited films).
- It is **anisotropic** — etch rate depends strongly on crystal orientation.
- Used to fabricate 3D microstructures: channels, membranes, cantilevers, vias.

**Crystallography (MUST MEMORIZE):**
- (100) = wafer surface, fast etch
- (111) = sidewall, slow etch, **54.74°** to surface
- Anisotropy ratio $A = R_{100}/R_{111} \approx 400$ in KOH
- Mask edges MUST align with $\langle 110 \rangle$ for clean walls

**Key Formulas:**
- $\theta = \arctan(\sqrt{2}) = 54.74°$
- $D = R_{100} \cdot t$
- $L_{bottom} = L_0 - \sqrt{2} \cdot d_{wafer}$
- $L_{undercut} = D / \tan(54.74°) = D/\sqrt{2}$
- $A = R_{100}/R_{111}$, $S = R_{Si}/R_{mask}$

**Etch Stop Techniques:**
1. Crystallographic ((111) self-stop)
2. Boron p⁺ doping (selectivity > 1000:1)
3. Electrochemical (pn-junction, selectivity > 10⁵:1)
4. Time-controlled (lowest selectivity)

**Wet Etchants:**
- KOH — most common, NOT CMOS-safe
- TMAH — CMOS-compatible, lower anisotropy
- EDP — high selectivity, highly toxic
- CsOH — research use

**Dry Etching:**
- DRIE (Bosch process) — vertical walls, high aspect ratio
- Cyclic SF₆ etch + C₄F₈ passivation
- Scalloping is a side-effect

**BioMEMS Applications:**
- Microfluidic channels (lab-on-chip)
- Silicon membranes (pressure sensors, micropumps)
- Cantilevers (biosensors)
- Through-wafer vias (neural probes)
- Microneedles (drug delivery)

**Exam Tips:**
- Always draw a labeled cross-section
- State all assumptions (wafer orientation, etchant, T, t)
- Include units in numerical answers
- Memorize the √2 factor — used in ~70% of numerical questions

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LIGA Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** LIGA Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LIGA** is a German acronym derived from the three core fabrication steps: **L**ithographie (Lithography), **G**alvanoformung (Electroplating), and **A**bformung (Molding). It is a high-aspect-ratio microfabrication technology that combines deep X-ray lithography (DXRL), electroforming, and replication (molding) to produce microstructures with vertical sidewalls and structural heights ranging from a few micrometers to several millimeters.

The LIGA process is one of the key non-silicon micromachining techniques used in BioMEMS and microfluidic device fabrication. It enables the creation of three-dimensional microstructures with **aspect ratios exceeding 100:1**, lateral dimensions down to **sub-micrometer scale**, and sidewall roughness better than **50 nm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Conventional Lithography:**
> Conventional UV photolithography is limited to aspect ratios of roughly 5:1 to 10:1 because of absorption in thick photoresists. LIGA overcomes this barrier by using **synchrotron X-ray radiation** (wavelength ≈ **0.2 nm to 1 nm**), which penetrates deeply into thick resist layers with minimal diffraction.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Watermark Stamp":**
> Imagine you want to engrave a deep, intricate pattern into a thick slab of wax. A normal stamp can only press the surface by a millimeter or so. But if you use a powerful, deep-penetrating laser (akin to synchrotron X-rays) to carve a very deep template, then pour liquid metal (electroplating) into that deep cavity, you get a solid metal master. You can then use that metal master as a mold to press countless plastic copies — each one faithfully reproducing the deep, tall, three-dimensional pattern. **This is the essence of LIGA:** carve deep → fill with metal → replicate in plastic.

### Standard Physical Metrics of the LIGA Process

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| X-ray wavelength | **0.2 nm – 1 nm** (hard X-rays) |
| Resist thickness | **10 µm – 3000 µm** |
| Minimum lateral feature | **0.2 µm – 5 µm** |
| Maximum aspect ratio | **> 100 : 1** |
| Sidewall verticality | **± 0.5°** over 500 µm |
| Sidewall roughness | **< 50 nm RMS** |
| Typical resist material | **PMMA (Polymethyl methacrylate)** |
| Typical electroplated metal | **Nickel, Gold, Copper** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why LIGA Matters in BioMEMS:** Microfluidic channels, micro-gear assemblies, neural probe arrays, and lab-on-chip housings often require tall, vertical, smooth-walled structures that cannot be produced by surface micromachining. LIGA fills this critical fabrication gap.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Sequential Stages of LIGA

The LIGA process is fundamentally divided into three sequential sub-processes. Each stage has a distinct physical-chemical function:

#### **Stage 1 — Lithographie (Deep X-Ray Lithography, DXRL)**

- A thick layer of **PMMA resist** is bonded to an electrically conductive substrate (typically a metallized silicon wafer or titanium-coated steel plate).
- The resist is exposed to **synchrotron X-ray radiation** through a patterned X-ray mask.
- The X-ray mask consists of a thin **gold absorber pattern** (thickness **10 µm – 20 µm**) supported on a **silicon carbide, titanium, or beryllium membrane**.
- The exposed PMMA undergoes chain scission (molecular bond breaking), making the exposed regions soluble in a chemical developer.
- Penetration depth of X-rays in PMMA at photon energy of **2 keV to 8 keV** can reach several millimeters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Synchrotron Radiation?** Synchrotrons produce highly collimated, high-brightness, parallel X-ray beams with wavelengths small enough to avoid diffraction effects even through very thick resists. Standard laboratory X-ray sources lack the flux and collimation required.

#### **Stage 2 — Galvanoformung (Electroplating / Electroforming)**

- After development, the patterned PMMA leaves behind deep, vertical trenches down to the conductive substrate.
- The wafer is immersed in an **electrolytic bath** (e.g., nickel sulfamate solution for nickel deposition).
- An electric current drives metal ions to deposit into the trenches, nucleating on the exposed conductive substrate.
- Electroplating continues until the metal **overfills** the PMMA cavities, forming a thick solid metal layer on top.
- Typical current density: **5 mA/cm² to 50 mA/cm²**; bath temperature: **45°C to 60°C**; pH: **3.5 to 4.5**.
- The PMMA is then dissolved in a solvent (e.g., acetone or methylene chloride), leaving behind a free-standing **metal micro-mold / master insert**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Point:** The metal micro-mold produced in Stage 2 is the **reusable master** that defines all subsequent replicated parts. Dimensional accuracy of every replicated device is therefore set at this stage.

#### **Stage 3 — Abformung (Molding / Replication)**

- The metal master is used as a mold insert in one of three replication variants:
  - **Hot Embossing:** Thermoplastic (e.g., PMMA, COC, COP) pellets are heated above glass transition temperature (T_g) and pressed into the mold cavity.
  - **Injection Molding:** Molten thermoplastic injected at high pressure into the closed mold.
  - **Casting (Reaction Molding):** Liquid prepolymer (e.g., PDMS, polyurethane) poured and cured inside the mold.
- After cooling/curing, the part is demolded, yielding a polymer replica with the inverse microstructure of the metal master.
- A single metal master can produce **> 10,000 polymer replicas**, dramatically reducing per-unit cost.

### 2.2 Process Variants and Derivatives

| Variant | Lithography Source | Key Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **LIGA (Standard)** | Synchrotron X-ray | Tallest structures, best sidewalls, highest cost |
| **UV-LIGA** | Ultraviolet (i-line 365 nm) | Lower cost, aspect ratio limited to ~10:1 |
| **Silicon LIGA** | DRIE (Deep Reactive Ion Etching) | Uses silicon as mold, no synchrotron needed |
| **Laser LIGA** | Excimer / Femtosecond laser | Suitable for thick resist patterning without synchrotron |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microfluidic devices:** Tall channel walls, integrated micro-optical elements (lenses, prisms).
- **Micro-actuators and micro-gears:** Spur gears with involute tooth profiles, planetary gear trains.
- **Neural probe arrays:** High-aspect-ratio silicon or polymer probe shanks.
- **X-ray optics:** Fresnel zone plates, collimators.
- **MEMS packaging:** Three-dimensional interconnect structures, interposers.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 Scheme questions often test whether students know the relationship between X-ray penetration depth, resist thickness, and sidewall accuracy. Memorize the order-of-magnitude metrics.

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| Penetration depth of X-rays | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ where $\mu$ = linear absorption coefficient | $\delta$ in **µm** or **mm**; $\mu$ in **cm⁻¹** |
| Beer-Lambert X-ray absorption | $I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu z}$ | $I_0$ = incident intensity; $I(z)$ = intensity at depth $z$ |
| Aspect Ratio | $AR = \dfrac{H}{W}$ | $H$ = structural height; $W$ = minimum lateral feature |
| Critical dose for PMMA | $D_c \approx 4 \times 10^3 \ \text{J/cm}^3$ | Dose in **J/cm³** |
| Nickel deposition rate (Faraday's Law) | $v = \dfrac{M \cdot J}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | $v$ = deposition velocity (µm/h); $M$ = molar mass of Ni (58.69 g/mol); $J$ = current density (A/cm²); $n$ = valence (2); $F$ = Faraday constant (96485 C/mol); $\rho$ = density of Ni (8.9 g/cm³) |
| Glass transition check for hot embossing | Emboss if $T > T_g$ | $T$ = embossing temperature; $T_g$ = glass transition temperature |
| Volume of replicated parts per master | $N \le 10{,}000$ | Empirical, depends on mold wear |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — LGA:**
> **L** = **L**ight (X-ray) makes pattern in resist
> **G** = **G**alvanize (electroplate) metal into pattern
> **A** = **A**bform (mold) plastic copies from metal master

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: X-Ray Penetration Depth and Required Resist Thickness

The penetration of X-rays through a polymer resist follows the **Beer-Lambert law of exponential attenuation**.

**Step 1 — Statement of Beer-Lambert Law for X-ray intensity**

The intensity of an X-ray beam decreases exponentially as it passes through a material of thickness $z$:

$$I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu z}$$

**Step 2 — Define penetration depth**

The penetration depth $\delta$ is conventionally defined as the depth at which the X-ray intensity falls to $\dfrac{1}{e}$ (about **36.8%**) of the original value:

$$I(\delta) = \dfrac{I_0}{e} = I_0 \, e^{-\mu \delta}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\delta$**

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{e}\right) = -\mu \delta$$

$$-1 = -\mu \delta$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$$

**Step 4 — Apply numerical values for PMMA at $\lambda = 1 \text{ nm}$**

For PMMA at X-ray wavelength of 1 nm, the linear absorption coefficient is approximately:

$$\mu_{PMMA} \approx 50 \ \text{cm}^{-1}$$

Therefore:

$$\delta = \dfrac{1}{50 \ \text{cm}^{-1}} = 0.02 \ \text{cm} = 200 \ \mu m$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

This means synchrotron X-rays at this wavelength can fully expose PMMA resist up to **200 µm thick**, consistent with typical LIGA process parameters.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Nickel Electroplating Rate (Faraday's Law)

The electroplating rate of nickel into the PMMA cavities determines how long the Stage 2 process must run.

**Step 1 — Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis**

The mass $m$ of substance deposited at an electrode is directly proportional to the total charge $Q$ passed:

$$m = \dfrac{M \cdot Q}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Express charge in terms of current density**

For a constant current density $J$ applied over a cathodic area $A$ for time $t$:

$$Q = J \cdot A \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Express mass in terms of volume and density**

The mass of nickel deposited into a cavity of cross-section $A$ and height $h$ is:

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot h$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for plating rate $v = h/t$**

$$\rho \cdot A \cdot h = \dfrac{M \cdot J \cdot A \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

Cancel $A$ on both sides:

$$\rho \cdot h = \dfrac{M \cdot J \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

Therefore:

$$v = \dfrac{h}{t} = \dfrac{M \cdot J}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 5 — Insert numerical values for nickel**

Using $M = 58.69$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96485$ C/mol, $\rho = 8.9$ g/cm³, and $J = 20$ mA/cm² $= 0.02$ A/cm²:

$$v = \dfrac{(58.69)(0.02)}{(2)(96485)(8.9)} \ \text{cm/s}$$

$$v = \dfrac{1.1738}{1717433} \ \text{cm/s}$$

$$v \approx 6.83 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{cm/s}$$

Converting to micrometers per hour:

$$v \approx 24.6 \ \mu m/h$$

**Step 6 — Time to fill a 500 µm cavity**

$$t = \dfrac{h}{v} = \dfrac{500 \ \mu m}{24.6 \ \mu m/h} \approx 20.3 \ \text{hours}$$

This long plating time is a major reason LIGA processing is **batch-processed in parallel** at synchrotron facilities.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step Mapping of the Full LIGA Process

| Sub-Step | Process Action | Physical/Chemical Phenomenon | Output / Artifact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Substrate preparation | Sputter-deposit Ti/Cu seed layer on Si wafer | Conductive seed layer |
| 2 | Resist coating | Bond PMMA sheet (e.g., 500 µm) by solvent or thermal bonding | Thick PMMA layer on substrate |
| 3 | Mask fabrication | Pattern Au absorber on SiC / Be membrane | X-ray mask |
| 4 | X-ray exposure | Synchrotron irradiation (dose ≈ 4 kJ/cm³) | Latent image in PMMA |
| 5 | Development | Immerse in GG developer (diethyl glycol mono-butyl ether) | Dissolved exposed regions, leaving trenches |
| 6 | Electroplating | Ni²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Ni in sulfamate bath | Ni-filled cavities with overburden |
| 7 | Resist removal | Dissolve PMMA in acetone | Free-standing Ni micro-mold |
| 8 | Substrate release | Etch or release Ni mold from substrate | Reusable metal master |
| 9 | Hot embossing / injection molding | Replicate PMMA, COC, COP at $T > T_g$ | Polymer replicas |
| 10 | Demolding & dicing | Cool, separate, cut | Finished BioMEMS component |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** If a question describes a process and asks you to "identify the stage," trace back to the *physical phenomenon* (radiation chemistry, electrochemical reduction, thermoplastic deformation) — this is the underlying logic examiners reward with full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LIGA Process — End-to-End Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagrams the complete LIGA workflow as a functional architecture, mapping the inputs, process transformations, and outputs of every stage. This serves as the visual equivalent of a process flow schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Substrate Preparation: Si Wafer with Ti-Cu Seed Layer] --> B[Resist Lamination: PMMA Sheet Bonded to Substrate]
    B --> C[Synchrotron X-Ray Exposure through Au X-Ray Mask]
    C --> D[Development in GG Developer: Exposed PMMA Dissolves]
    D --> E[Trench Pattern Formed Down to Conductive Seed Layer]
    E --> F[Electroplating in Nickel Sulfamate Bath: Ni Deposition]
    F --> G[PMMA Stripping in Acetone: Free Ni Micro-Mold]
    G --> H{Mold Release from Substrate}
    H --> I[Reusable Nickel Master Insert]
    I --> J[Hot Embossing of PMMA or COC Above Tg]
    I --> K[Injection Molding of Engineering Polymer]
    I --> L[Reaction Casting of PDMS or Polyurethane]
    J --> M[Demolded Polymer Replica]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Final BioMEMS or Microfluidic Component]
```

### 4.2 Stage-by-Stage Cross-Sectional Schematic (Block Representation)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the cross-sectional state of the workpiece at each LIGA stage, using block-level symbols (where physical drawing would be impossible in text form).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 0: Conductive Substrate with PMMA Resist]
    S2[Stage 1: X-Ray Exposure through Mask]
    S3[Stage 2: Development Yields Trenches]
    S4[Stage 3: Electroplating Fills Trenches with Ni]
    S5[Stage 4: PMMA Removed, Ni Mold Stands Free]
    S6[Stage 5: Polymer Replica in Hot Embossing]
    S7[Stage 6: Final Demolded Microstructure]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7
```

### 4.3 X-Ray Mask Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M1[Frame Ring: Silicon or Pyrex Holder]
    M2[Membrane: 2 to 5 Micron SiC or Beryllium]
    M3[Absorber Pattern: 10 to 20 Micron Gold]
    M1 --> M2 --> M3
```

### 4.4 Functional Layer-by-Layer Concept Diagram

| Layer (from bottom to top) | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Layer 1 (Substrate) | Silicon wafer or metal plate | Mechanical support |
| Layer 2 (Seed layer) | Titanium (adhesion) + Copper (conductivity) | Cathode for electroplating |
| Layer 3 (Resist) | PMMA, 10 µm to 3000 µm | X-ray sensitive mold former |
| Layer 4 (Absorber, on mask) | Gold, 10 µm to 20 µm | X-ray opaque pattern |
| Layer 5 (Membrane, on mask) | SiC or Be, 2 µm to 5 µm | Mechanical support for absorber, X-ray transparent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** When a student is asked to "draw a LIGA mask cross-section" in an exam, the four-layer concept (frame → membrane → absorber pattern → resist → substrate) is the answer expected. Examiners look for explicit labeling of the **X-ray transparent** and **X-ray opaque** regions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the full form of LIGA? Briefly explain why synchrotron radiation is preferred over UV light in the lithography step.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **LIGA** stands for **L**ithographie (Lithography), **G**alvanoformung (Electroplating), and **A**bformung (Molding), a German acronym.

Synchrotron radiation (X-ray wavelength of **0.2 nm to 1 nm**) is preferred over UV light (≈ 365 nm) because:

1. **Deep penetration:** X-rays can expose PMMA resist layers of thickness **500 µm to 3000 µm** without significant intensity loss, while UV light is absorbed in resists thicker than about 10 µm.
2. **Low diffraction:** The very short X-ray wavelength avoids diffraction-induced sidewall taper, allowing nearly vertical sidewalls (± 0.5°) and high-aspect-ratio structures (> 100:1).
3. **High collimation:** Synchrotron beams are highly parallel, enabling accurate pattern transfer without optical distortion.

**(3 marks — 1 mark for full form, 2 marks for explanation of synchrotron advantages)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the role of electroplating in the LIGA process. Mention the typical metal used and one critical process parameter.**

**Model Answer:**

Electroplating (**Galvanoformung**) fills the lithographically defined PMMA cavities with a solid metal to form a **reusable master mold** (micro-insert) for subsequent polymer replication.

- **Typical metal used:** Nickel (from a nickel sulfamate bath), although gold and copper are also used.
- **Critical process parameter:** Current density (typically **5 mA/cm² to 50 mA/cm²**), which controls the deposition rate and the internal stress of the deposited nickel.

**(3 marks — 1 mark role, 1 mark metal, 1 mark parameter)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEEBT743 Module 1]**

**(a)** Describe the complete LIGA process with a neat block diagram. List the three stages and the physical/chemical phenomenon involved at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of Faraday's law, derive an expression for the rate of nickel deposition in electroplating. Calculate the time required to fill a 500 µm deep cavity when the current density is 20 mA/cm². (Atomic mass of Ni = 58.69 g/mol, density = 8.9 g/cm³, valency = 2, F = 96485 C/mol) **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Complete LIGA process description:**

**Stage 1 — Lithographie (Deep X-Ray Lithography):**
- A thick PMMA resist (10 µm to 3000 µm) is bonded to a conductive substrate.
- Synchrotron X-rays (wavelength 0.2 nm to 1 nm) pass through an X-ray mask and selectively expose the PMMA.
- **Phenomenon:** Radiation-induced chain scission in PMMA. The exposed regions become soluble.

**Stage 2 — Galvanoformung (Electroplating):**
- The developed PMMA has vertical trenches down to the conductive seed layer.
- The wafer is immersed in a nickel sulfamate bath; Ni²⁺ ions are reduced to Ni metal at the cathode.
- **Phenomenon:** Electrochemical reduction: $\text{Ni}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Ni}$.
- PMMA is stripped, leaving a free-standing Ni micro-mold.

**Stage 3 — Abformung (Molding):**
- The Ni master is used for hot embossing, injection molding, or reaction casting of polymer.
- **Phenomenon:** Thermoplastic deformation above $T_g$ (for hot embossing) or thermoset cross-linking (for casting).

(Block diagram as shown in Section 4.1 should be drawn for full marks.)

**(7 marks — 2 marks for stage-wise description, 2 marks for phenomenon, 1 mark for diagram, 2 marks for clarity of process flow)**

**(b) Derivation and calculation:**

Following the derivation in Section 3.2:

$$v = \dfrac{M \cdot J}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

Substituting values:

$$v = \dfrac{(58.69 \ \text{g/mol})(0.02 \ \text{A/cm}^2)}{(2)(96485 \ \text{C/mol})(8.9 \ \text{g/cm}^3)}$$

$$v = 6.83 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{cm/s} = 24.6 \ \mu\text{m/h}$$

Time required:

$$t = \dfrac{500 \ \mu m}{24.6 \ \mu\text{m/h}} \approx 20.3 \ \text{hours}$$

**(7 marks — 3 marks derivation, 2 marks numerical substitution, 1 mark calculation of $v$, 1 mark calculation of $t$)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEEBT743 Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the working of the X-ray mask used in LIGA. With a diagram, describe the four functional layers of the mask. Discuss why gold is used as the absorber. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the LIGA process with silicon bulk micromachining. List four advantages and two limitations of LIGA for BioMEMS applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) X-ray mask in LIGA:**

The X-ray mask has four functional layers, from bottom to top:

1. **Frame ring (Pyrex or silicon):** Provides mechanical rigidity and a handling interface.
2. **Support membrane (SiC, Be, or Ti, 2 µm to 5 µm thick):** Mechanically supports the absorber pattern; must be **X-ray transparent**.
3. **Absorber pattern (Au, 10 µm to 20 µm thick):** Blocks X-rays in unexposed regions; defines the lithographic pattern.
4. **Alignment marks and protective coating (optional):** Used for precise mask-to-wafer alignment.

**Why gold is the absorber:**
- High atomic number (Z = 79) → very high X-ray absorption.
- High density (19.3 g/cm³) → compact absorber thickness.
- Chemically inert → resistant to developer and electroplating baths.
- Easy to pattern by electroplating or lift-off.

**(7 marks — 2 marks for four layers, 2 marks for diagram, 1 mark for gold properties, 2 marks for overall understanding)**

**(b) Comparison: LIGA vs. Silicon Bulk Micromachining:**

| Parameter | LIGA | Silicon Bulk Micromachining |
|---|---|---|
| Aspect ratio | > 100:1 | Typically 20:1 to 50:1 |
| Sidewall verticality | Excellent (≤ 0.5°) | Convex/concave (crystal-dependent) |
| Material flexibility | Any polymer, metal, ceramic | Single-crystal Si only |
| Equipment cost | Very high (synchrotron) | Moderate (KOH, DRIE tools) |
| Mask cost | Very high | Low |

**Four advantages of LIGA for BioMEMS:**
1. High aspect ratio enables tall microchannels and 3D micro-optical elements.
2. Sidewall smoothness (< 50 nm RMS) is ideal for optical and fluidic interfaces.
3. Material flexibility allows polymers, metals, and ceramics — critical for biocompatibility.
4. Mass replication (> 10,000 parts per master) reduces per-unit cost.

**Two limitations of LIGA:**
1. Requires a synchrotron radiation source — limited access and very high cost.
2. Long electroplating times (tens of hours for tall structures) and multi-step process flow increase cycle time.

**(7 marks — 3 marks comparison table, 2 marks advantages, 2 marks limitations)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing LIGA with UV-LIGA:** Students often mix up the synchrotron-based LIGA with the cheaper UV-LIGA. State explicitly that **standard LIGA uses X-rays (synchrotron)**, while UV-LIGA uses near-UV light and gives much lower aspect ratios.
> 2. **Writing "Lithography, Gold-plating, Annealing":** This is a frequent mnemonic error. The correct acronym is **Lithographie, Galvanoformung, Abformung** — *electroplating*, not gold-plating, and *molding*, not annealing.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical problems:** Always write µA, µJ, µm, mA/cm². Examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 4. **Omitting the "why" of the metal choice:** When asked about gold as absorber, simply stating "high Z" is incomplete. Add the chain of reasoning: high Z → high absorption → compact thickness → high pattern resolution.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark sub-questions:** A 7-mark sub-question almost always expects a labeled diagram. Omission typically costs 2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **LIGA = Lithographie + Galvanoformung + Abformung** (Lithography + Electroplating + Molding).
- ✅ Standard LIGA uses **synchrotron X-ray** exposure of **PMMA** resist.
- ✅ Synchrotron wavelength ≈ **0.2 nm to 1 nm**; enables **aspect ratios > 100:1**.
- ✅ X-ray mask has four layers: **frame → membrane → absorber (Au) → alignment marks**.
- ✅ Gold absorber is chosen for **high Z, high density, chemical inertness**.
- ✅ Stage 2 (electroplating) uses **nickel sulfamate bath**; current density typically **5–50 mA/cm²**.
- ✅ Faraday's law: $v = \dfrac{MJ}{nF\rho}$; for Ni at $J = 20$ mA/cm², $v \approx 24.6 \ \mu m/h$.
- ✅ Stage 3 (molding) variants: **hot embossing, injection molding, reaction casting**.
- ✅ One metal master can replicate **> 10,000 polymer parts**.
- ✅ LIGA is preferred in BioMEMS for **tall, smooth, vertical polymer microstructures**.
- ✅ Variants: **LIGA (X-ray), UV-LIGA (UV), Silicon LIGA (DRIE), Laser LIGA**.
- ✅ Beer-Lambert law: $I(z) = I_0 e^{-\mu z}$; penetration depth $\delta = 1/\mu$.
- ✅ Critical PMMA dose ≈ **4 kJ/cm³**.
- ✅ Major limitation: **synchrotron cost and limited access**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wafer Bonding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wafer Bonding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wafer Bonding** is a microfabrication process in which two or more wafers (typically silicon, glass, polymer, or metal-coated substrates) are permanently joined at the molecular level—without the use of macroscopic adhesives—to form a single, monolithic, hermetically sealed structure. In BioMEMS, it is the **primary packaging and fluidic-channel-sealing technology** that converts patterned half-chips into functional microfluidic, biosensor, and lab-on-chip devices.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, wafer bonding is treated as a *process module* that interfaces cleanroom fabrication (lithography, etching, thin-film deposition) with the final *device-level* assembly of BioMEMS chips.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of wafer bonding as **"atomic-scale welding"** of two ultra-thin pancakes (wafers). Imagine you have two clean glass slides. If you press them together with perfectly clean, atomically smooth surfaces, they spontaneously stick (van der Waals forces). Now imagine doing this with silicon and glass, and using **electricity + heat** to make the bond as strong as a single piece of glass. That is exactly what anodic bonding does in a BioMEMS lab.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in BioMEMS:** A microfluidic channel patterned on a silicon wafer is *open*—it has no top. Wafer bonding "closes" the chip by sealing a glass (Pyrex) cover on top, creating enclosed microchannels through which blood, saliva, or reagents can flow without leaking.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (KTU Syllabus Map)

The KTU 2024 module groups wafer bonding into the following families:

1. **Direct / Fusion Bonding** (Si–Si, Si–SiO₂)
2. **Anodic Bonding** (Si–Pyrex glass) — *most common in BioMEMS*
3. **Eutectic Bonding** (Au–Si, Au–Sn)
4. **Thermocompression Bonding** (Au–Au, Cu–Cu)
5. **Adhesive Bonding** (BCB, SU-8, glass frit, polymer)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Process Metrics (KTU Board-Favorite Numbers):**
> - Anodic bonding: **T = 300 – 450 °C**, **V = 200 – 1000 V DC**, **t = 5 – 30 min**
> - Fusion bonding: **T = 800 – 1100 °C**, surface roughness **< 0.5 nm RMS**
> - Au–Si eutectic: **T_eutectic = 363 °C**
> - Required surface cleanliness: **< 10 particles/cm²** for defect-free bonding.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Bonding — How Two Wafers Stick

A bond forms when the **total Gibbs free energy of the system decreases** upon contact. The thermodynamic driving force is the reduction in total surface area and the elimination of two high-energy surfaces (one on each wafer) in favor of one low-energy bonded interface.

The condition for *spontaneous* bonding is:

$$
\Delta G = \gamma_{1} + \gamma_{2} - \gamma_{12} \;<\; 0
$$

where $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$ are the surface energies of the two free wafer surfaces, and $\gamma_{12}$ is the energy of the newly formed interface. This is the **Dupré work of adhesion** concept.

## 2.2 Mechanism Step-by-Step (Generic Bonding)

1. **Surface Preparation:** RCA cleaning, piranha, HF dip, or plasma activation to make surfaces **hydrophilic** (–OH terminated).
2. **Pre-Bonding (Room Temperature):** Wafers are brought into contact. Short-range forces (van der Waals, hydrogen bonding) cause *spontaneous* initial adhesion.
3. **Annealing / Activation Step:** Heat, voltage, pressure, or interlayer reflow strengthens the bond by:
   - Diffusion across the interface
   - Chemical reaction (e.g., Si–OH + HO–Si → Si–O–Si + H₂O)
   - Electrochemical migration (anodic case)
4. **Cool-down & Stress Stabilization:** Controlled ramp-down to avoid thermal-mismatch cracking.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat-Sheet Table — Master these for KTU ESE.** The symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ below is written as `\mid` to preserve table syntax.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Symbols & Units | Valid For |
|---|---------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | Work of Adhesion (identical wafers) | $W = 2\gamma_s$ | $\gamma_s$ = surface energy (J/m²) | Direct / fusion bonding |
| 2 | Young–Dupré work of adhesion | $W = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}$ | $\gamma_{sv}$ = solid–vapor, $\gamma_{sl}$ = solid–liquid | Wetting analysis |
| 3 | Thermal mismatch stress in bonded pair | $\sigma_{th} = \frac{E_f \, \Delta\alpha \, \Delta T}{1-\nu_f}$ | $E_f$ = Young's modulus, $\nu_f$ = Poisson ratio, $\Delta\alpha = \mid\alpha_1 - \alpha_2\mid$, $\Delta T$ = temp change | All heated bonds |
| 4 | Stoney's Equation (curvature from thin-film stress) | $\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^{\,2}}{6 \, R \, t_f \, (1-\nu_s)}$ | $R$ = radius of curvature (m), $t$ = thickness | Thin film on substrate |
| 5 | Electric field across glass (anodic) | $E = \dfrac{V}{d_{glass}}$ | $V$ = applied voltage, $d$ = glass thickness | Anodic bonding |
| 6 | Mobile-ion depletion (anodic) | $Q = C \, V$ | $C$ = capacitance of glass | Anodic bonding |
| 7 | Hermetic leak-rate (MIL-STD-883) | $R_{leak} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V}{\Delta t}$ | Pressure-decay method | Hermeticity testing |
| 8 | Biaxial modulus of Si (100) | $E' = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu} \approx 180.5$ GPa | Si $\nu \approx 0.28$ | Si substrate calculations |

## 2.4 Why Bonding Is the Backbone of BioMEMS Packaging

- **Hermetic Sealing:** Critical for implantable biosensors (e.g., glucose, neural probes) where moisture ingress would degrade electronics within hours.
- **Fluidic Channel Closure:** Microchannels etched into Si are *open grooves* until a Pyrex cover is anodically bonded on top, completing a closed micro-fluidic network.
- **Electrical Feedthroughs:** Pre-fabricated metal pads on the glass cover pass through the bond to provide I/O.
- **Optical Windows:** Glass covers allow *fluorescence*, *absorption*, and *Raman* readouts in lab-on-chip platforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Without wafer bonding, no commercial BioMEMS product—*i-STAT* blood analyzers, *Abbott FreeStyle Libre*, or *Cepheid GeneXpert* cartridges—would exist, because none could be sealed hermetically.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Stoney's Equation (Thin-Film Stress → Wafer Curvature)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Stoney's equation is the *single most tested* thin-film formula in KTU MEMS papers. It links film stress to the measurable bow of the substrate, and is the foundation for understanding *thermal mismatch cracking* in bonded wafer pairs.

**Setup:** A thin film of thickness $t_f$ and stress $\sigma_f$ sits on a thick substrate of thickness $t_s$, Young's modulus $E_s$, and Poisson ratio $\nu_s$. The substrate is much thicker than the film ($t_s \gg t_f$).

**Step 1 — Force balance (per unit width):**
The film exerts a tensile force $F = \sigma_f \, t_f$ per unit width. For the substrate to remain in equilibrium, an equal and opposite compressive force must act through the substrate's neutral axis, separated by a moment arm $\approx t_s/2$.

$$
M = \sigma_f \, t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Beam-bending relation:**
For a thin plate bent to a radius of curvature $R$, classical plate theory gives:

$$
\frac{M}{E_s I} = \frac{1}{R}
$$

where the moment of inertia per unit width is $I = t_s^{3}/12$.

**Step 3 — Substitute and equate:**

$$
\frac{\sigma_f \, t_f \cdot \dfrac{t_s}{2}}{E_s \cdot \dfrac{t_s^{3}}{12}} = \frac{1}{R}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$
\frac{6 \, \sigma_f \, t_f}{E_s \, t_s^{\,2}} = \frac{1}{R}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for film stress:**

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^{\,2}}{6 \, R \, t_f}\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Biaxial correction (real plates deform biaxially):**
For a (100) Si wafer, replace $E_s$ with the biaxial modulus $E_s/(1-\nu_s)$:

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^{\,2}}{6 \, R \, t_f \, (1-\nu_s)}
$$

**Boundary condition for validity:**

$$
t_f \ll t_s \quad \text{and} \quad R \gg t_s
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Thermal Mismatch Stress in a Bonded Wafer Pair

> [!NOTE]
> This is the stress that *develops during cool-down* after high-temperature bonding (anodic, fusion, eutectic). It is the principal cause of bond failure and wafer cracking.

**Setup:** Two wafers of materials A and B are bonded at temperature $T_b$ and cooled to room temperature $T_{RT}$. The differential contraction induces a biaxial stress.

**Step 1 — Free-strain from CTE mismatch:**

$$
\varepsilon_{mismatch} = (\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \, (T_b - T_{RT}) = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — Constrained strain equals biaxial stress / biaxial modulus:**

$$
\sigma_{th} = \frac{E_f \, \Delta\alpha \, \Delta T}{1-\nu_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical sanity check (typical Pyrex–Si anodic bond):**
- $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6} \, /K$
- $\alpha_{Pyrex} = 3.3 \times 10^{-6} \, /K$
- $\Delta\alpha = 0.7 \times 10^{-6} \, /K$
- $\Delta T = 450 - 25 = 425 \, K$
- $E_{Si}/(1-\nu_{Si}) = 180.5 \, \text{GPa}$

$$
\sigma_{th} = \frac{180.5 \times 10^{9} \cdot 0.7 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 425}{1} \approx 53.7 \, \text{MPa}
$$

This 53.7 MPa is *real* and is why anodic bonding is preferred over fusion bonding for Si–glass (since $\Delta\alpha$ is small and the stress is tolerable, whereas Si–Si at 1000 °C would generate ~250 MPa).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

> **Question:** A 1 µm SiO₂ film is deposited on a 500 µm Si wafer. The measured radius of curvature after deposition is $R = 5$ m. Given $E_{Si} = 130$ GPa, $\nu_{Si} = 0.28$, calculate the residual film stress.

**Solution (model answer):**

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^{\,2}}{6 \, R \, t_f \, (1-\nu_s)}
$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{(130 \times 10^{9}) \, (500 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}{6 \cdot 5 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (1-0.28)}
$$

**Compute numerator:**

$$
(130 \times 10^{9}) \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-7}) = 3.25 \times 10^{4}
$$

**Compute denominator:**

$$
6 \cdot 5 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot 0.72 = 2.16 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_f = \frac{3.25 \times 10^{4}}{2.16 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.505 \times 10^{9} \, \text{Pa} \approx 1.5 \, \text{GPa}\;}
$$

This is a high tensile stress, characteristic of a non-stoichiometric or contaminated oxide.

---

## 3.4 Anodic Bonding — Electrochemical Mechanism (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the most heavily tested mechanism in KTU ESE. Memorize the ion-migration direction and the SiO₂ growth site.

1. **Setup:** Si wafer (+) and Pyrex glass (–) are heated to ~400 °C, voltage (~800 V) applied.
2. **Na⁺ depletion:** Mobile sodium ions in the glass migrate toward the *cathode* (negative side, glass bottom).
3. **Space-charge region:** A thin depletion layer of fixed negative oxygen ions forms near the Si–glass interface.
4. **Strong electric field:** $E = V/d_{glass}$ across the depletion layer (~10⁶ V/cm).
5. **Oxygen generation:** At the Si–glass interface, oxygen from the glass reacts with Si:

$$
\text{Si} + 2\,\text{O}^{2-} \longrightarrow \text{SiO}_2 + 4\,e^-
$$

6. **Permanent covalent bond:** A thin SiO₂ layer is grown *in situ* at the interface, producing an irreversible, hermetic Si–O–Si covalent bond.

> [!NOTE]
> The bond strength reaches **> 20 MPa** in well-processed anodic bonds, and is hermetic to better than $10^{-9}$ atm·cc/s He.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process-Flow Diagram — Anodic Bonding Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Clean Si and Pyrex Wafers RCA plus Piranha] --> A2[Align Wafers in Bond Chuck]
    A2 --> A3[Heat to 300 to 450 C on Hot Plate]
    A3 --> A4[Apply 200 to 1000 V DC Si Positive Electrode]
    A4 --> A5[Na plus Ions Drift Toward Cathode in Glass]
    A5 --> A6[Depletion Layer Forms at Si Glass Interface]
    A6 --> A7[Strong Local E Field approx 1e6 V per cm]
    A7 --> A8[Oxygen Anions React with Si forming SiO2]
    A8 --> A9[Permanent Si O Si Covalent Bond]
    A9 --> A10[Cool Down Controlled Ramp to Avoid Stress]
    A10 --> endA[Hermetically Sealed BioMEMS Chip]
```

## 4.2 Comparison Architecture — Wafer-Bonding Technique Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Wafer Bonding Technique Selection]
    root --> branch1[Direct Fusion Bonding]
    root --> branch2[Anodic Bonding]
    root --> branch3[Eutectic Bonding]
    root --> branch4[Adhesive Bonding]
    root --> branch5[Thermocompression Bonding]

    branch1 --> leaf1a[Materials Si Si or Si SiO2]
    branch1 --> leaf1b[Temperature 800 to 1100 C]
    branch1 --> leaf1c[Pros Highest Purity Bond]
    branch1 --> leaf1d[Cons High Thermal Stress]

    branch2 --> leaf2a[Materials Si to Pyrex Glass]
    branch2 --> leaf2b[Temperature 300 to 450 C plus HV]
    branch2 --> leaf2c[Pros Low Stress Hermetic]
    branch2 --> leaf2d[Cons Glass Material Limitation]

    branch3 --> leaf3a[Materials Au Si or Au Sn]
    branch3 --> leaf3b[Temperature Above 363 C Eutectic]
    branch3 --> leaf3c[Pros Excellent Hermetic Seal]
    branch3 --> leaf3d[Cons Metal Contamination Risk]

    branch4 --> leaf4a[Materials BCB SU8 Glass Frit]
    branch4 --> leaf4b[Temperature 150 to 350 C]
    branch4 --> leaf4c[Pros Lowest Temperature CMOS Compatible]
    branch4 --> leaf4d[Cons Not Truly Hermetic]

    branch5 --> leaf5a[Materials Au Au or Cu Cu]
    branch5 --> leaf5b[Temperature 300 to 400 C plus High Pressure]
    branch5 --> leaf5c[Pros Reliable for MEMS Packaging]
    branch5 --> leaf5d[Cons Requires Precise Planarization]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — BioMEMS Chip Built by Wafer Bonding

```mermaid
graph LR
    topLid[Top Wafer Glass or Si Cover] --> bondInterface1[Anodic Bond Interface SiO2]
    bondInterface1 --> channelLayer[Channel Wafer with Etched Microchannels]
    channelLayer --> bondInterface2[Adhesive or Fusion Bond]
    bondInterface2 --> substrateLayer[Bottom Si Substrate with Heaters and Electrodes]
    channelLayer --> fluidicIO1[Inlet Reservoir]
    channelLayer --> fluidicIO2[Outlet Reservoir]
    topLid --> opticalWindow[Optical Detection Window for Bioassay]
    substrateLayer --> electricalPads[Electrical Bond Pads for BioMEMS Interface]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A typical BioMEMS lab-on-chip has 3–4 wafers stacked: (1) top transparent lid for optical readout, (2) channel wafer with the etched microfluidic network, (3) optional membrane/electrode wafer, (4) bottom substrate. Each interface is created by one of the five bonding techniques above.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
Define wafer bonding. List any *three* major techniques used in BioMEMS packaging.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Wafer bonding is the process of permanently joining two or more microfabricated wafers at the molecular level to form a sealed, monolithic structure used to encapsulate or close BioMEMS devices. **(1 mark)**

Three major techniques:
1. **Anodic bonding** (Si–Pyrex, used for hermetic sealing at 300–450 °C). **(1 mark)**
2. **Fusion / Direct bonding** (Si–Si or Si–SiO₂, requires <0.5 nm RMS roughness). **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Eutectic bonding** (Au–Si, T_eutectic = 363 °C, used in MEMS packaging). **(0.5 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State the **Dupré work of adhesion** equation and explain its significance for spontaneous wafer bonding.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
W = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - \gamma_{12}
$$

where $\gamma_1$, $\gamma_2$ are the surface energies of the two free surfaces and $\gamma_{12}$ is the interface energy. **(1.5 marks)**

**Significance:** For spontaneous bonding, $W > 0$, i.e., the system must *lower* its total free energy upon contact. In practice, this requires clean, smooth, hydrophilic surfaces and is the thermodynamic basis of pre-bonding at room temperature before any thermal or electrical activation. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### 📘 Question 1 (14 marks) — *Option A*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **principle, process parameters, and mechanism** of anodic bonding between silicon and Pyrex glass. Discuss the role of **Na⁺ ions** and the formation of the interfacial SiO₂ layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare anodic bonding with **fusion bonding**. State *two* advantages and *two* limitations of each in the context of BioMEMS microfluidic device fabrication. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q1(a) — 7 marks

> **Principle (1 mark):** Anodic bonding is an electrochemical process in which a Si wafer and a Pyrex glass wafer are joined at elevated temperature under a high DC electric field. The driving force is **Na⁺ ion migration** in the glass and **in-situ growth of an interfacial SiO₂ layer**, which creates a permanent covalent Si–O–Si bond.

> **Setup (1 mark):** Si is the **anode (+)**; Pyrex glass is the **cathode (–)**. Wafers are placed in intimate contact on a heated chuck (T = 300–450 °C). A high voltage (200–1000 V DC) is applied across the stack.

> **Mechanism – Step-by-Step (3 marks):**
> 1. **Na⁺ depletion:** Mobile sodium ions in the borosilicate glass drift toward the negative electrode (cathode), leaving a thin, negatively charged **depletion layer** of non-bridging oxygen ions at the Si–glass interface.
> 2. **High local field:** The depletion layer sustains a very strong electric field $E = V/d \sim 10^6$ V/cm.
> 3. **Oxygen transport and reaction:** Oxygen anions ($\text{O}^{2-}$) are pulled toward the Si anode, where they react with Si:
> $$\text{Si} + 2\,\text{O}^{2-} \longrightarrow \text{SiO}_2 + 4\,e^-$$
> 4. **Permanent bond:** A thin SiO₂ layer (~10–100 nm) is formed, covalently fusing the Si to the glass with bond strength **> 20 MPa**.

> **Process Parameters (1 mark):** T = 300–450 °C, V = 200–1000 V, t = 5–30 min, surface roughness < 50 nm, particle count < 10/cm².

> **Schematic (1 mark):** Student is expected to draw a labeled diagram showing Si (anode, +), Pyrex (cathode, –), Na⁺ migration arrows toward the cathode, depletion region at the interface, and SiO₂ formation.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q1(b) — 7 marks

| Parameter | Anodic Bonding | Fusion Bonding |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| **Materials** | Si – Pyrex glass | Si – Si or Si – SiO₂ |
| **Temperature** | 300 – 450 °C *(low)* | 800 – 1100 °C *(high)* |
| **Activation** | Voltage + heat | Heat only (after pre-bond) |
| **Surface requirement** | Moderate (< 50 nm RMS) | Atomic scale (< 0.5 nm RMS) |
| **Bond strength** | > 20 MPa | 5 – 15 MPa (depends on anneal) |
| **Hermeticity** | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Thermal stress** | Low (matched CTE) | High (large $\Delta T$ → cracking) |

> **Anodic — Advantages (1 mark):**
> 1. Low temperature preserves CMOS circuitry on the chip.
> 2. Excellent hermeticity, suitable for implantable BioMEMS.

> **Anodic — Limitations (1 mark):**
> 1. Restricted to glass substrates (Pyrex, Borofloat).
> 2. High voltage can damage on-chip electronics if not protected.

> **Fusion — Advantages (1 mark):**
> 1. Allows Si–Si bonding, giving monolithic all-silicon devices.
> 2. Highest purity interface, no metal or glass contamination.

> **Fusion — Limitations (1 mark):**
> 1. Requires ultra-smooth, ultra-clean surfaces — high process cost.
> 2. High anneal temperature induces thermal-mismatch stress in BioMEMS stacks.

> **Synthesis (1 mark):** For BioMEMS, *anodic bonding* is preferred because it seals Si channels with optically transparent Pyrex at low temperature—ideal for fluorescence-based detection.

---

### 📘 Question 2 (14 marks) — *Option B (Internal Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain **eutectic bonding** with a neat phase diagram. Why is the Au–Si system preferred in BioMEMS packaging? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive **Stoney's equation** for thin-film stress. A 1 µm SiO₂ film is deposited on a 500 µm Si wafer. The radius of curvature is measured as 5 m. Given $E_{Si} = 130$ GPa and $\nu_{Si} = 0.28$, calculate the residual film stress. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q2(a) — 7 marks

> **Definition (1 mark):** Eutectic bonding is a wafer-bonding technique in which two materials form a *liquid phase* at a temperature **lower** than either of their individual melting points (the *eutectic temperature*), and on cooling, this liquid solidifies into a mechanically strong, hermetic intermetallic bond.

> **Au–Si Phase Diagram (2 marks):**
> - Au melts at 1064 °C, Si at 1414 °C.
> - The **Au–Si eutectic point** is at **363 °C** and ~31 at% Si.
> - Above 363 °C, Au-rich and Si-rich liquid phases coexist; below 363 °C, the system solidifies into Au–Si intermetallics.

> **Process Steps (1.5 marks):**
> 1. Deposit Au film (typically 0.5–5 µm) on one or both wafers.
> 2. Align and contact the wafers.
> 3. Heat to **> 363 °C** (usually 400–450 °C) under modest pressure in inert atmosphere.
> 4. Liquid eutectic forms, wets the surfaces, and on cooling forms a hermetic Au–Si seal.

> **Why Au–Si in BioMEMS? (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Low eutectic temperature (363 °C)** is compatible with post-CMOS processing and polymer microfluidics.
> 2. **Hermeticity:** Eutectic seals pass MIL-STD-883 leak-rate tests, essential for implantable biosensors.
> 3. **Electrical conductivity:** The Au–Si bond can also serve as an electrical feedthrough, eliminating wire bonds in some designs.
> 4. **Biocompatibility:** Au is inert in physiological fluids, critical for chronic implants.

> **Schematic (1 mark):** Draw a labeled Au–Si phase diagram showing the eutectic point at 363 °C, the liquidus lines, and the bonding temperature window.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q2(b) — 7 marks

> **Derivation (4 marks):** *(Write in exam; full derivation given in Section 3.1 above.)*

> Final boxed result:

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^{\,2}}{6 \, R \, t_f \, (1-\nu_s)}
$$

> **Substitution (1.5 marks):**
> - $E_s = 130 \times 10^{9}$ Pa
> - $t_s = 500 \times 10^{-6}$ m
> - $t_f = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ m
> - $R = 5$ m
> - $1 - \nu_s = 0.72$

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{130 \times 10^{9} \times (500 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}{6 \times 5 \times (1 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.72}
$$

> **Numerator:** $130 \times 10^{9} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 3.25 \times 10^{4}$ **(0.5 mark)**

> **Denominator:** $6 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.72 = 2.16 \times 10^{-5}$ **(0.5 mark)**

> **Result (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_f \approx 1.5 \, \text{GPa}\;}
$$

This high tensile stress indicates a non-stoichiometric or contaminated oxide film.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students *commonly lose marks* in Wafer Bonding questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ion direction in anodic bonding:** Students often write that Na⁺ moves *toward* the Si anode. **WRONG.** Na⁺ moves toward the **cathode (negative electrode, glass side)**. The depletion layer forms at the **Si–glass interface**, where SiO₂ grows. *–2 marks if reversed.*
> 2. **Forgetting the (1 − ν) factor in Stoney's equation:** Many write $\sigma = E t_s^{2}/(6 R t_f)$ without the biaxial correction. The biaxial modulus $E/(1-\nu)$ is required for plate geometry. *–1 mark per part.*
> 3. **Mismatched units in numerical problems:** Using GPa with mm³ and not converting to SI (Pa, m) is a frequent silent error. Always show unit conversion explicitly. *–1 mark if missing.*
> 4. **Confusing *bond strength* with *hermeticity*:** A strong bond (> 20 MPa) is not automatically hermetic. Hermeticity requires < $10^{-8}$ atm·cc/s He leak rate.
> 5. **Drawing fusion bonding at low temperature:** Fusion bonding *always* requires an anneal ≥ 800 °C. Showing 400 °C labels is a Board-favorite trap. *–1 mark.*
> 6. **Omitting the role of surface activation:** Many answers ignore the *hydrophilic pre-treatment* (RCA, plasma, piranha) that is mandatory for high-yield bonding.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Wafer Bonding**

- ✅ **Definition:** Permanent, molecular-level joining of wafers to seal BioMEMS devices.
- ✅ **Five families:** Fusion, Anodic, Eutectic, Thermocompression, Adhesive.
- ✅ **Anodic bonding** is the *workhorse* of BioMEMS: Si (+) – Pyrex (–), 300–450 °C, 200–1000 V, Na⁺ drifts to cathode, SiO₂ grows at interface, Si–O–Si covalent bond.
- ✅ **Au–Si eutectic** = **363 °C**, hermetic, used in implants.
- ✅ **Fusion bonding** requires < 0.5 nm RMS, anneal at 800–1100 °C.
- ✅ **Stoney's equation:** $\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s t_s^{2}}{6 R t_f (1-\nu_s)}$ — memorize it verbatim.
- ✅ **Thermal mismatch stress:** $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E \, \Delta\alpha \, \Delta T}{1-\nu}$ — drives wafer cracking.
- ✅ **Hermeticity threshold:** < $10^{-8}$ atm·cc/s He (MIL-STD-883).
- ✅ **Anodic bond strength:** > 20 MPa; **Au–Si eutectic bond strength:** 50–100 MPa.
- ✅ **Critical success factors:** Surface cleanliness (< 10 particles/cm²), low roughness, controlled cool-down rate.
- ✅ **BioMEMS-specific preference:** **Anodic bonding** dominates for Si–Pyrex microfluidic chips due to optical transparency + low thermal stress.
- ✅ **Energy balance:** Spontaneous bonding occurs when work of adhesion $W = 2\gamma_s > 0$.
- ✅ **Ion direction in anodic:** Na⁺ → **cathode**; oxygen anions → **anode (Si)** — never reverse these.
- ✅ **Use `\mid` (or `\vert`)**, never `|`, for absolute values inside markdown tables.
- ✅ **Always convert** GPa, µm, etc. to SI base units (Pa, m) before substituting into Stoney's equation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Sensors and Actuators - Beam and Cantilever - Microplates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Sensors and Actuators - Beam and Cantilever - Microplates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mechanical Beam and Cantilever – Formal Definition

In the context of **BioMEMS** (Biological Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) and **microfluidics**, a **mechanical beam** is a slender, flexible structural element whose length is significantly greater than its cross-sectional dimensions (typically $L \gg w, t$). When one end of the beam is rigidly anchored to a substrate while the other end remains free, the structure is termed a **cantilever beam**. A beam with both ends fixed is called a **fixed–fixed** (or **clamped–clamped**) beam, and a beam with both ends simply supported is called a **simply supported** beam.

A **microplate**, by contrast, is a two-dimensional structural element whose in-plane dimensions ($a, b$) are comparable to or larger than its out-of-plane thickness $h$. Plates exhibit **two-dimensional flexural behavior** governed by biaxial bending moments, in contrast to the uniaxial bending of beams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Beams and cantilevers form the **fundamental transduction element** for many BioMEMS sensors (AFM probes, biosensors, mass sensors, pressure sensors) and actuators (micromirrors, valves, pumps). Students must master the **Euler–Bernoulli** theory and **Kirchhoff plate theory** for board-level numericals.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine holding one end of a plastic ruler firmly against a table (this is your **fixed support / clamp**) and pressing down on the free end with your finger:
- The ruler **bends downward** (this is **deflection**, $w$).
- The free end **tilts** by some angle (this is **slope**, $\theta$).
- The harder you press (load $P$), the more it bends.
- A **thicker** or **shorter** ruler bends less because it is **stiffer**.

That ruler is literally a **macroscopic cantilever beam**. In BioMEMS, the same principle is scaled down to the **micrometer level** using silicon, silicon nitride, or polymers like SU-8 and PDMS. The genius of MEMS lies in this **scaling** — at small dimensions, beams become ultra-sensitive to nanogram forces, femtometer displacements, and even single-molecule adsorption events.

> [!NOTE]
> A **microplate** is like the **drumhead of a tiny drum** — when you push on it from the top, it bows downward, and the shape of this bowing is described by a 2D differential equation, not the 1D equation of a beam.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Material Properties used in KTU BioMEMS problems:**

| Material | Young's Modulus $E$ | Density $\rho$ | Poisson's Ratio $\nu$ |
|----------|---------------------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Single-Crystal Silicon (Si) | **130–190 GPa** (orientation dependent) | 2330 kg/m³ | 0.28 |
| Polysilicon | 160 GPa | 2330 kg/m³ | 0.22 |
| Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄) | 250–310 GPa | 3100 kg/m³ | 0.23 |
| SU-8 Photoresist | 2–5 GPa | 1200 kg/m³ | 0.33 |
| PDMS | 0.5–4 MPa (highly variable) | 970 kg/m³ | 0.45 |
| Gold (Au) | 78 GPa | 19300 kg/m³ | 0.44 |

**Standard Acceleration Due to Gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (used when the beam supports a proof mass).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Euler–Bernoulli Beam Theory – The Foundation

The **Euler–Bernoulli theory** is the cornerstone of MEMS beam analysis. It rests on four governing assumptions:

1. **Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** after bending (no shear deformation).
2. The beam material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's law).
3. **Deflections are small** compared to beam thickness (linear regime).
4. **Shear deformation is neglected** (valid for $L/t > 10$).

The governing differential equation for a uniform beam under transverse load $q(x)$ is:

$$EI \frac{d^4 w(x)}{dx^4} = q(x)$$

where $E$ is **Young's modulus**, $I$ is the **area moment of inertia** of the cross-section, and $w(x)$ is the **transverse deflection**.

## 2.2 Classification of Beams (Essential for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 frequently tests the identification and boundary conditions of beam types.**

| Beam Type | Anchor at x=0 | Anchor at x=L | Typical Spring Constant $k$ |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|------------------------------|
| **Cantilever** | Clamped (built-in) | **Free** | $\dfrac{3EI}{L^3}$ (tip load) |
| **Fixed–Fixed** | Clamped | Clamped | $\dfrac{16EI}{L^3}$ (center load) |
| **Simply Supported** | Pinned | Pinned | $\dfrac{48EI}{L^3}$ (center load) |

## 2.3 Area Moment of Inertia (Most Tested Cross-Sections)

For a **rectangular cross-section** of width $b$ and thickness $t$ (bending about the neutral axis):

$$I = \frac{b t^3}{12}$$

The cubic dependence on $t$ is the most critical takeaway — **doubling the thickness makes the beam 8× stiffer**!

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations below are HIGH-FREQUENCY KTU board exam topics. Memorize the formulas AND the boundary conditions.**

### A. Static Deflection Formulas

| Loading Condition | Beam Type | Tip / Max Deflection $w$ | Spring Constant $k = P/w$ |
|-------------------|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Point load $P$ at free end | Cantilever | $w_{tip} = \dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$ | $k = \dfrac{3EI}{L^3}$ |
| Distributed load $q$ | Cantilever | $w_{tip} = \dfrac{qL^4}{8EI}$ | $k = \dfrac{8EI}{L^3}$ |
| Point load $P$ at center | Fixed–Fixed | $w_{center} = \dfrac{PL^3}{192EI}$ | $k = \dfrac{192EI}{L^3}$ |
| Point load $P$ at center | Simply Supported | $w_{center} = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ | $k = \dfrac{48EI}{L^3}$ |

### B. Resonant Frequency (Dynamic Behavior)

The **fundamental natural frequency** of a beam is given by:

$$f_1 = \frac{\alpha_1^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$$

| Beam Type | $\alpha_1$ (first mode) | First Mode Frequency $f_1$ |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Cantilever** | 1.875 | $f_1 = \dfrac{0.162 \cdot t}{L^2} \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ (for rectangular) |
| **Fixed–Fixed** | 4.730 | $f_1 = \dfrac{1.028 \cdot t}{L^2} \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ |
| **Simply Supported** | $\pi \approx 3.142$ | $f_1 = \dfrac{0.319 \cdot t}{L^2} \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ |

For the **$n$-th mode**, replace $\alpha_1$ with $\alpha_n$. Higher modes: $\alpha_2 = 4.694$ (cantilever), $\alpha_3 = 7.855$ (cantilever).

### C. Microplate Theory (Kirchhoff Plate Theory)

For a **thin isotropic plate** of thickness $h$, the governing equation is:

$$D \nabla^4 w(x,y) = q(x,y)$$

where the **flexural rigidity** of the plate is:

$$D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$$

The factor $(1 - \nu^2)$ in the denominator is what makes plates **stiffer than beams** of the same material and thickness.

### D. Stress and Strain Relations

| Quantity | Formula | Note |
|----------|---------|------|
| Bending stress | $\sigma_{xx} = \dfrac{M y}{I} = \dfrac{E y}{\rho_{curv}}$ | $y$ measured from neutral axis |
| Bending strain | $\varepsilon_{xx} = \dfrac{y}{\rho_{curv}}$ | Linear through thickness |
| Curvature | $\kappa = \dfrac{1}{\rho_{curv}} = \dfrac{d^2 w}{dx^2}$ | Neglects rotary inertia |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AFM (Atomic Force Microscopy) tips** are nanoscale cantilevers — their bending directly maps the topography of biomolecules.
- **Microcantilever biosensors** detect pathogens, DNA, or proteins via **surface stress changes** that cause measurable nanometer deflections.
- **Pressure sensor diaphragms** are microplates whose center deflection is calibrated to applied pressure.
- **RF MEMS switches** use cantilever beams for low-loss signal routing in 5G/6G front-ends.
- **Micromirror arrays** (DLP, optical cross-connects) use torsional beams and plates for beam steering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cantilever Deflection Under Tip Point Load

This is the **most repeated derivation** in KTU board exams for BioMEMS. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

**Step 1 — Setup and Boundary Conditions**

Consider a uniform cantilever beam of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, with a point load $P$ applied at the free tip ($x = L$).

| Position | Boundary Condition | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| $x = 0$ (clamped end) | $w(0) = 0$ | No deflection at the wall |
| $x = 0$ (clamped end) | $\dfrac{dw}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = 0$ | No slope (built-in) |
| $x = L$ (free end) | $M(L) = EI \dfrac{d^2 w}{dx^2}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0$ | Zero bending moment |
| $x = L$ (free end) | $V(L) = -EI \dfrac{d^3 w}{dx^3}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = P$ | Shear balances the tip load |

**Step 2 — Write the Governing ODE**

Since there is no distributed load between the wall and the tip, $q(x) = 0$ for $0 \le x < L$:

$$EI \frac{d^4 w}{dx^4} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Four Times**

$$\frac{d^4 w}{dx^4} = 0$$

$$\frac{d^3 w}{dx^3} = C_1$$

$$\frac{d^2 w}{dx^2} = C_1 x + C_2$$

$$\frac{dw}{dx} = \frac{C_1 x^2}{2} + C_2 x + C_3$$

$$w(x) = \frac{C_1 x^3}{6} + \frac{C_2 x^2}{2} + C_3 x + C_4$$

**Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions Sequentially**

Apply BC-1: $w(0) = 0$
$$0 = 0 + 0 + 0 + C_4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_4 = 0$$

Apply BC-2: $\dfrac{dw}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = 0$
$$0 = 0 + 0 + C_3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_3 = 0$$

Apply BC-3: $M(L) = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{d^2 w}{dx^2}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0$
$$0 = C_1 L + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = -C_1 L$$

Apply BC-4: $V(L) = P \Rightarrow -EI \dfrac{d^3 w}{dx^3}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = P$
$$-EI \cdot C_1 = P \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = -\frac{P}{EI}$$

**Step 5 — Back-Substitute Constants**

$$C_1 = -\frac{P}{EI}, \quad C_2 = \frac{PL}{EI}, \quad C_3 = 0, \quad C_4 = 0$$

The deflection shape is:

$$w(x) = -\frac{P}{EI} \cdot \frac{x^3}{6} + \frac{PL}{EI} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2}$$

$$w(x) = \frac{P}{6EI}\left(-x^3 + 3L x^2\right) = \frac{P x^2}{6EI}(3L - x)$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate Tip Deflection at x = L**

$$w_{tip} = w(L) = \frac{P L^2}{6EI}(3L - L) = \frac{P L^2 \cdot 2L}{6EI}$$

$$\boxed{w_{tip} = \frac{P L^3}{3EI}}$$

**Step 7 — Spring Constant Derivation**

By definition, $k = P / w_{tip}$:

$$k = \frac{P}{\dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}}$$

$$\boxed{k = \frac{3EI}{L^3}}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute Moment of Inertia for Rectangular Beam**

For a beam of width $b$ and thickness $t$:

$$I = \frac{bt^3}{12}$$

$$k = \frac{3E \cdot \dfrac{bt^3}{12}}{L^3} = \frac{E b t^3}{4 L^3}$$

$$\boxed{k_{cantilever,rectangular} = \frac{Ebt^3}{4L^3}}$$

This is the **KTU 2024 most-tested result** for rectangular cantilevers.

## 3.2 Derivation: Fundamental Frequency of a Cantilever Beam

**Step 1 — Equation of Motion with Rotary Inertia and Axial Force Neglected**

$$\rho A \frac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} + EI \frac{\partial^4 w(x,t)}{\partial x^4} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Separation of Variables**

Assume $w(x,t) = \phi(x) \cdot e^{j\omega t}$:

$$-\rho A \omega^2 \phi(x) + EI \frac{d^4 \phi}{dx^4} = 0$$

$$\frac{d^4 \phi}{dx^4} - \beta^4 \phi = 0, \quad \text{where} \quad \beta^4 = \frac{\rho A \omega^2}{EI}$$

**Step 3 — General Solution**

$$\phi(x) = A \cos(\beta x) + B \sin(\beta x) + C \cosh(\beta x) + D \sinh(\beta x)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Cantilever Boundary Conditions**

- $\phi(0) = 0$ → $A + C = 0$ → $C = -A$
- $\phi'(0) = 0$ → $B + D = 0$ → $D = -B$
- $\phi''(L) = 0$ → $-A\cos(\beta L) - B\sin(\beta L) + C\cosh(\beta L) + D\sinh(\beta L) = 0$
- $\phi'''(L) = 0$ → $A\sin(\beta L) - B\cos(\beta L) + C\sinh(\beta L) + D\cosh(\beta L) = 0$

Substituting $C = -A$ and $D = -B$ into the last two equations:

$$-A[\cos(\beta L) + \cosh(\beta L)] - B[\sin(\beta L) + \sinh(\beta L)] = 0$$

$$A[\sin(\beta L) - \sinh(\beta L)] - B[\cos(\beta L) + \cosh(\beta L)] = 0$$

**Step 5 — Non-Trivial Solution via Characteristic Equation**

For non-trivial $(A, B)$, the determinant must vanish:

$$[\cos(\beta L) + \cosh(\beta L)]^2 + [\sin(\beta L) + \sinh(\beta L)][\sin(\beta L) - \sinh(\beta L)] = 0$$

$$\cos^2(\beta L) + 2\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) + \cosh^2(\beta L) + \sin^2(\beta L) - \sinh^2(\beta L) = 0$$

Using $\cos^2 + \sin^2 = 1$ and $\cosh^2 - \sinh^2 = 1$:

$$1 + 2\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) + 1 = 0$$

$$\boxed{\cos(\beta L) \cosh(\beta L) + 1 = 0}$$

**Step 6 — Solve Transcendental Equation for First Root**

The roots $\beta_n L$ of this transcendental equation give the mode shapes. The **first root is**:

$$\beta_1 L \approx 1.875 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha_1 = 1.875$$

**Step 7 — Convert to Frequency**

$$\omega_1 = \beta_1^2 \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}} = \frac{\alpha_1^2}{L^2}\sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}}$$

$$f_1 = \frac{\omega_1}{2\pi} = \frac{\alpha_1^2}{2\pi L^2}\sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}}$$

For a rectangular cross-section, $I = bt^3/12$ and $A = bt$:

$$\frac{I}{A} = \frac{t^2}{12}$$

$$f_1 = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi L^2}\sqrt{\frac{E t^2}{12\rho}} = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi L^2} \cdot \frac{t}{\sqrt{12}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$

$$f_1 = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi \cdot 2\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{t}{L^2}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} \approx \frac{0.162 \cdot t}{L^2}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$

$$\boxed{f_1 = \frac{0.162 \cdot t}{L^2}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} \text{ (Cantilever)}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Kirchhoff Microplate Center Deflection (Clamped Square Plate)

**Step 1 — Governing Equation**

$$D \nabla^4 w = q, \quad D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$

**Step 2 — For a Clamped Square Plate ($a \times a$) Under Uniform Pressure $p$**

The classical Levy–Nadai solution gives the center deflection:

$$w_{center} = \frac{0.00126 \cdot p \cdot a^4}{D}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute D**

$$w_{center} = \frac{0.00126 \cdot p \cdot a^4 \cdot 12(1-\nu^2)}{E h^3}$$

$$\boxed{w_{center} = \frac{0.01512 \cdot p \cdot a^4 (1-\nu^2)}{E h^3}}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Stress in the Plate**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{0.308 \cdot p \cdot a^2}{h^2} \text{ (at the clamped edge mid-point)}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix consolidates ALL key results for fast board revision.

| Parameter | Cantilever Beam | Fixed-Fixed Beam | Simply Supported Beam | Microplate (Clamped) |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| $w_{max}$ (point load) | $\dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$ | $\dfrac{PL^3}{192EI}$ | $\dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ | $\dfrac{0.01512\, p a^4(1-\nu^2)}{E h^3}$ |
| $k$ (point load) | $\dfrac{3EI}{L^3}$ | $\dfrac{192EI}{L^3}$ | $\dfrac{48EI}{L^3}$ | $\dfrac{66.27\, E h^3}{a^4(1-\nu^2)}$ |
| $f_1$ coefficient | 0.162 | 1.028 | 0.319 | 0.469 $\times \dfrac{h}{a^2}\sqrt{E/\rho}$ |
| First mode coefficient $\alpha_1$ | 1.875 | 4.730 | $\pi$ | (Levy–Nadai series) |
| Effective stiffness order | Lowest | Highest | Medium | Highest per unit volume |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
