# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a cantilever beam in the context of MEMS. List any two applications of microcantilevers in biosensing.**

**Model Answer:**
A **cantilever beam** in MEMS is a slender, micromachined beam structure that is rigidly anchored (clamped) at one end to a substrate, while the other end remains free to deflect under applied forces, mass loading, or surface stress changes.

**Two applications in biosensing:**
1. **AFM (Atomic Force Microscopy) probes** — used to image biomolecules at nanometer resolution by detecting cantilever tip–sample interaction forces.
2. **Microcantilever biosensors** — detect specific biological analytes (DNA, proteins, pathogens) via bending caused by surface stress changes upon biomolecular binding.

> [!NOTE]
> Each application is worth ≈ 1 mark and the definition 1 mark under KTU valuation.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the area moment of inertia of a rectangular cross-section? Why is beam stiffness highly sensitive to thickness?**

**Model Answer:**
For a rectangular cross-section of width $b$ and thickness $t$, the area moment of inertia about the neutral axis is:

$$I = \frac{b t^3}{12}$$

Beam stiffness (flexural rigidity $EI$) is **highly sensitive to thickness** because $I$ scales with the **cube of the thickness** $t$. Therefore, doubling the thickness $t$ increases the moment of inertia by a factor of **$2^3 = 8$**, making the beam eight times stiffer. In contrast, the beam length $L$ appears cubed in the **inverse** (in $k = 3EI/L^3$), so doubling $L$ makes the beam 8× *softer*.

> [!NOTE]
> The cubic relationship is a frequent 3-mark question and tests conceptual understanding of MEMS design trade-offs.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the tip deflection of a uniform cantilever beam of length $L$, width $b$, and thickness $t$ carrying a point load $P$ at its free end. Hence obtain the spring constant.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A silicon microcantilever used in an AFM has $L = 200\,\mu m$, $b = 50\,\mu m$, $t = 1\,\mu m$. Given $E = 169 \text{ GPa}$, compute (i) the spring constant and (ii) the fundamental resonant frequency. Assume $\rho_{Si} = 2330 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]**

Marks distribution (per KTU valuation key):
- Governing ODE and BCs — **2 marks**
- Four-time integration — **2 marks**
- Application of boundary conditions — **2 marks**
- Final substituted expression and spring constant — **1 mark**

**Step 1:** Euler–Bernoulli ODE with $q(x)=0$:

$$EI \frac{d^4 w}{dx^4} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Four integrations yield:

$$w(x) = \frac{C_1 x^3}{6} + \frac{C_2 x^2}{2} + C_3 x + C_4$$

**Step 3:** Apply BCs:
- $w(0) = 0$ → $C_4 = 0$
- $w'(0) = 0$ → $C_3 = 0$
- $M(L) = EI w''(L) = 0$ → $C_1 L + C_2 = 0$ → $C_2 = -C_1 L$
- $V(L) = P$ → $-EI C_1 = P$ → $C_1 = -P/EI$

**Step 4:** Therefore $C_2 = PL/EI$ and the deflection shape is:

$$w(x) = \frac{P}{6EI}\left(3Lx^2 - x^3\right) = \frac{Px^2}{6EI}(3L - x)$$

**Step 5:** Tip deflection at $x = L$:

$$w_{tip} = \frac{PL^3}{3EI} \quad \text{(1 mark for final expression)}$$

**Step 6:** Spring constant:

$$k = \frac{P}{w_{tip}} = \frac{3EI}{L^3}$$

For rectangular cross-section $I = bt^3/12$:

$$k_{cantilever} = \frac{Ebt^3}{4L^3}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]**

Marks distribution:
- Correct substitution into $k$ formula — **3 marks**
- Correct numerical evaluation of $k$ — **1 mark**
- Correct use of $f_1$ formula — **2 marks**
- Final numerical value of $f_1$ — **1 mark**

**Step 1 — Convert units (SI):**
$L = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
$b = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$
$t = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
$E = 169 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
$\rho = 2330 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 2 — Compute moment of inertia:**

$$I = \frac{bt^3}{12} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-5})(1 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{5 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{-18}}{12} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-23}}{12} \approx 4.167 \times 10^{-24} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3 — Compute spring constant:**

$$k = \frac{3EI}{L^3} = \frac{3 \times 169 \times 10^9 \times 4.167 \times 10^{-24}}{(2 \times 10^{-4})^3}$$

Numerator: $3 \times 169 \times 4.167 = 2112.5$, so numerator $= 2.1125 \times 10^{-12}$

Denominator: $(2 \times 10^{-4})^3 = 8 \times 10^{-12}$

$$k = \frac{2.1125 \times 10^{-12}}{8 \times 10^{-12}} = 0.264 \text{ N/m}$$

$$\boxed{k \approx 0.264 \text{ N/m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This ultra-low stiffness is why AFM cantilevers can detect forces down to the **piconewton** range — a key BioMEMS design principle.

**Step 4 — Compute fundamental resonant frequency:**

$$f_1 = \frac{0.162 \cdot t}{L^2}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$

Compute the term $\sqrt{E/\rho}$:

$$\sqrt{\frac{169 \times 10^9}{2330}} = \sqrt{7.253 \times 10^7} \approx 8516 \text{ m/s}$$

Compute the prefactor:

$$\frac{0.162 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{(2 \times 10^{-4})^2} = \frac{1.62 \times 10^{-7}}{4 \times 10^{-8}} = 4.05 \text{ m}^{-1} \cdot \text{ s}$$

Therefore:

$$f_1 = 4.05 \times 8516 \approx 34{,}490 \text{ Hz} \approx 34.5 \text{ kHz}$$

$$\boxed{f_1 \approx 34.5 \text{ kHz}}$$

---

### QUESTION B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Kirchhoff plate theory** for a thin isotropic microplate. Derive the expression for the **flexural rigidity** $D$ and discuss why plates are stiffer than beams of the same thickness.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A square silicon microplate pressure sensor has side $a = 1 \text{ mm}$ and thickness $h = 20\,\mu m$. It is subjected to a uniform differential pressure $p = 10 \text{ kPa}$. Compute the center deflection. Given $E = 169 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.28$.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Kirchhoff Plate Theory [7 Marks]**

Marks distribution:
- Plate theory assumptions and governing equation — **3 marks**
- Derivation of flexural rigidity $D$ — **3 marks**
- Plate-vs-beam stiffness comparison — **1 mark**

**Step 1 — Assumptions of Kirchhoff Plate Theory:**

1. The plate thickness $h$ is much smaller than the in-plane dimensions ($h \ll a, b$).
2. The **mid-plane** remains **inextensional** and **unstressed** under bending.
3. **Straight lines normal to the mid-plane remain straight, normal, and inextensional** after deformation (no transverse shear, no thickness change).
4. The plate is **isotropic, homogeneous**, and deforms in the **linear elastic regime**.

**Step 2 — Governing Equation:**

Under a transverse distributed load $q(x,y)$:

$$D\left(\frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^4} + 2\frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial y^4}\right) = q(x,y)$$

or compactly:

$$D \nabla^4 w = q(x,y)$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of Flexural Rigidity:**

The plate can be thought of as a **stack of infinitesimally thin beam layers**, each layer at distance $z$ from the mid-plane experiencing a longitudinal strain $\varepsilon_{xx} = -z \dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}$ and a corresponding stress $\sigma_{xx} = -E z \dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}$.

Integrating over the thickness, the moment per unit length is:

$$M_{xx} = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \sigma_{xx} \cdot z \, dz = -E \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} z^2 \, dz$$

$$M_{xx} = -E \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} \cdot \frac{h^3}{12}$$

For a **plate**, in-plane biaxial constraints from Poisson coupling introduce the factor $(1 - \nu^2)$ in the denominator (derivable via plane-strain analysis):

$$M_{xx} = -D \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}, \quad D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$$

This is the **flexural rigidity of a thin plate**.

**Step 4 — Plate vs Beam Stiffness:**

For a beam, $I = bh^3/12$ and $EI = Ebh^3/12$.
For a plate, $D = Eh^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$, so $D/E = h^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$.

Since $(1 - \nu^2) < 1$, the plate rigidity is **larger** than the corresponding beam rigidity per unit width. In other words, **plates are stiffer than beams of the same thickness because the in-plane material in a plate resists both longitudinal and transverse curvature simultaneously, providing additional biaxial stiffening absent in uniaxial beams.**

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]**

Marks distribution:
- Correct formula identification — **2 marks**
- Computation of $D$ — **2 marks**
- Conversion of units — **1 mark**
- Final center deflection value — **2 marks**

**Step 1 — Convert units to SI:**

$a = 1 \text{ mm} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
$h = 20\,\mu m = 20 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$
$p = 10 \text{ kPa} = 10^4 \text{ Pa}$
$E = 169 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
$\nu = 0.28$, so $1 - \nu^2 = 1 - 0.0784 = 0.9216$

**Step 2 — Compute flexural rigidity $D$:**

$$D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)} = \frac{169 \times 10^9 \times (2 \times 10^{-5})^3}{12 \times 0.9216}$$

$h^3 = 8 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m}^3$

Numerator: $169 \times 10^9 \times 8 \times 10^{-15} = 1352 \times 10^{-6} = 1.352 \times 10^{-3}$

Denominator: $12 \times 0.9216 = 11.0592$

$$D = \frac{1.352 \times 10^{-3}}{11.0592} \approx 1.222 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply center deflection formula for clamped square plate:**

$$w_{center} = \frac{0.00126 \cdot p \cdot a^4}{D}$$

$a^4 = (1 \times 10^{-3})^4 = 10^{-12} \text{ m}^4$

Numerator: $0.00126 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-12} = 1.26 \times 10^{-11}$

$$w_{center} = \frac{1.26 \times 10^{-11}}{1.222 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{w_{center} \approx 1.031 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \approx 103 \text{ nm}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The center deflection (≈ 103 nm) is **5× the plate thickness (20 μm)**, which violates the small-deflection Kirchhoff assumption! In a real board-exam scenario, you should **comment** on this and recommend using nonlinear (von Kármán) plate theory or large-deflection correction. This shows examiners that you understand the **limits of linear theory** — a highly valued board insight.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students frequently forget to convert $\mu m$ or $mm$ to meters before substituting into $E$ (in Pa). This causes answers to be off by **6–9 orders of magnitude**. Always normalize to SI first.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\nu$ factor in plate rigidity:** Beam formulas use $EI$ but plate formulas use $D = Eh^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$. Mixing them is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Wrong $\alpha_1$ value:** Each boundary condition has its own $\alpha_1$ (1.875 for cantilever, 4.730 for fixed–fixed, $\pi$ for simply supported). Using the wrong one gives a wrong resonant frequency.
> 4. **Sign convention:** Deflection is conventionally taken positive downward. The bending moment formula $M = EI \, w''$ uses this convention. Inverting signs in the derivation will not change magnitudes but will lose presentation marks.
> 5. **Cantilever vs. tip-mass:** The spring constant $k = 3EI/L^3$ is for a load at the **tip**. A load at the **mid-span** gives a different $k$ — do not interchange formulas.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read 5 minutes before the exam.**

- A **cantilever beam** has one end clamped, the other free. Its tip deflection under load $P$ is $w_{tip} = \dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$, and its spring constant is $k = \dfrac{3EI}{L^3}$.
- For a **rectangular cross-section**, $I = \dfrac{bt^3}{12}$, so $k_{cantilever} = \dfrac{Ebt^3}{4L^3}$. Note the **cubic dependence on thickness**.
- The **fundamental resonant frequency** of a cantilever is $f_1 = \dfrac{0.162 \cdot t}{L^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ for rectangular cross-section. The mode coefficient $\alpha_1 = 1.875$.
- **Fixed–fixed** beams are **64× stiffer** than cantilevers of the same dimensions (because $192/3 = 64$).
- **Simply supported** beams have $k = 48EI/L^3$ and a first mode coefficient $\alpha_1 = \pi \approx 3.142$.
- The **Euler–Bernoulli equation** is $EI \, w''''(x) = q(x)$, derived under the assumption of small deflections and no shear deformation (valid for $L/t > 10$).
- The **Kirchhoff plate equation** is $D \nabla^4 w = q$, with flexural rigidity $D = \dfrac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$. The $(1 - \nu^2)$ factor makes plates **stiffer than equivalent beams** due to biaxial strain coupling.
- For a **clamped square plate** under uniform pressure, $w_{center} = \dfrac{0.00126 \, p \, a^4}{D}$.
- Maximum stress in a clamped square plate occurs at the **edge mid-span** and is $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{0.308 \, p \, a^2}{h^2}$.
- MEMS material constants to remember: $E_{Si} \approx 169 \text{ GPa}$, $\rho_{Si} = 2330 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\nu_{Si} = 0.28$.
- Common BioMEMS applications of beams/plates: **AFM probes, biosensors, pressure sensors, micromirrors, RF switches, microfluidic valves**.

---

> **End of KTU 2024 Module 2 Notes — Beam, Cantilever, and Microplates**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Strain

In the context of solid mechanics applied to BioMEMS, **strain** is a dimensionless measure of the deformation of a material relative to its original size and shape. Mathematically, it quantifies the **relative displacement** between particles in a material body when it is subjected to an applied force (stress).

For a one-dimensional element of original length $L_0$ that deforms to a new length $L$, the **engineering (or Cauchy) normal strain** along that axis is formally defined as:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$

where $\Delta L$ is the change in length. Strain is reported in **units of m/m** (dimensionless) or, more commonly for micro-scale deformations, in **microstrain ($\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$)** or **nanostrain ($n\varepsilon = 10^{-9}$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In BioMEMS, strain is NOT just a mechanics concept — it is the foundational physical variable that enables **piezoresistive**, **piezoelectric**, and **capacitive** transduction in micro-sensors (e.g., pressure sensors, accelerometers, AFM cantilevers, biosensors).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **rubber band stretched between your two hands**:
- **Original length ($L_0$):** the relaxed length before pulling.
- **Stretched length ($L$):** the length after pulling.
- **Strain ($\varepsilon$):** how much *longer* the band became, *per unit of its original length*.

If you stretch a 10 cm rubber band by 1 cm, the strain is:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{1\,\text{cm}}{10\,\text{cm}} = 0.1 \;\;\; \text{(or 10\%)}$$

For **micro-scale BioMEMS devices** (e.g., a $500\,\mu\text{m}$ long piezoresistor on a silicon diaphragm), the deformations are extraordinarily small — typically on the order of nanometers. This produces strains on the order of $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ (i.e., 100 $\mu\varepsilon$ to 1 $\mu\varepsilon$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Strain vs. Stress:**
> - **Stress ($\sigma$):** *Internal force per unit area* the material experiences (cause).
> - **Strain ($\varepsilon$):** *Geometric deformation* the material undergoes (effect).
> - They are linked by the **constitutive relation** $\sigma = E\,\varepsilon$ (Hooke's Law), where $E$ is Young's modulus.

## 1.3 Classification of Strain

Strain in BioMEMS is classified into **two principal categories**:

| # | Type | Symbol | Description |
|---|------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | **Normal Strain** | $\varepsilon$ | Change in length along an axis (longitudinal/axial strain) |
| 2 | **Shear Strain** | $\gamma$ | Change in angle (in radians) between two originally perpendicular lines |
| 3 | **Volumetric Strain** | $\varepsilon_v$ | Relative change in volume |
| 4 | **True (Logarithmic) Strain** | $\varepsilon_T$ | $\ln(L/L_0)$, used for large deformations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the **type of strain** you are calculating. Marks are often lost when a student confuses normal strain with shear strain or uses true strain where engineering strain is required.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Silicon) | Units |
|----------|--------|------------------------|-------|
| Young's Modulus (Si ⟨100⟩) | $E$ | **130 GPa** | $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$ |
| Poisson's Ratio (Si) | $\nu$ | **0.28** | dimensionless |
| Shear Modulus (Si) | $G$ | **79.9 GPa** | Pa |
| Bulk Modulus (Si) | $K$ | **98 GPa** | Pa |
| Crystal Density (Si) | $\rho$ | **2330 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| Piezoresistive Coefficient (Si, p-type) | $\pi_{44}$ | **$138 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹** | Pa⁻¹ |
| Typical MEMS Strain Range | $\varepsilon$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ | $\mu\varepsilon$ to $m\varepsilon$ |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation of Strain

Strain is fundamentally a **tensor quantity** because deformation at a point in a 3-D body cannot be described by a single number — it depends on direction. However, in most BioMEMS problems (beams, diaphragms, cantilevers), the deformation can be simplified using **1-D or 2-D strain formulations**.

### 2.1.1 Infinitesimal Strain Theory (Linearized)

For small deformations (which is the BioMEMS regime, typically $\varepsilon < 10^{-3}$), the **strain-displacement relations** in Cartesian coordinates $(x, y, z)$ are:

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \varepsilon_{zz} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$

$$\varepsilon_{xy} = \varepsilon_{yx} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_{yz} = \varepsilon_{zy} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_{zx} = \varepsilon_{xz} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right)$$

where $(u, v, w)$ are the **displacement components** of a material point. This gives a **symmetric 3×3 strain tensor**:

$$\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_{xx} & \varepsilon_{xy} & \varepsilon_{xz} \\ \varepsilon_{yx} & \varepsilon_{yy} & \varepsilon_{yz} \\ \varepsilon_{zx} & \varepsilon_{zy} & \varepsilon_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why symmetric?** Because $\varepsilon_{ij} = \varepsilon_{ji}$ for an irrotational (no rigid-body rotation) deformation. This eliminates 3 of the 9 components, leaving **6 independent strain components**: 3 normal + 3 shear.

### 2.1.2 Engineering Shear Strain

By convention, the **engineering shear strain** is defined as **twice** the tensorial shear strain:

$$\gamma_{xy} = 2\,\varepsilon_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU:** Many textbook problems use $\gamma$ (engineering shear strain) in radians, NOT $\varepsilon_{xy}$ (tensor shear strain). Always check which convention is being used.

## 2.2 Strain in Standard BioMEMS Structures

### 2.2.1 Strain in a Cantilever Beam (Tip-Loaded)

A cantilever of length $L$, thickness $t$, and width $b$ loaded with a transverse force $F$ at its free tip exhibits a **maximum bending strain at the clamped end (root)**:

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{6\,F\,L}{E\,b\,t^2}$$

**Why the $6FL/(Ebt^2)$ form?**
- The **bending moment** at distance $x$ from the tip is $M(x) = Fx$; maximum at $x = L$ → $M_{\max} = FL$.
- The **section modulus** of a rectangular cross-section is $S = bt^2/6$.
- Therefore $\sigma_{\max} = M_{\max}/S = 6FL/(bt^2)$ and $\varepsilon_{\max} = \sigma_{\max}/E$.

### 2.2.2 Strain in a Pressure-Loaded Diaphragm (Square, Clamped)

For a square diaphragm of side $a$ and thickness $t$ with clamped edges under differential pressure $P$:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{0.308\,P\,a^2}{t^2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{0.308\,P\,a^2}{E\,t^2}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Thinner diaphragms and larger pressures produce larger strains — this is the operational principle of **MEMS pressure sensors** and **biomedical blood-pressure transducers**.

### 2.2.3 Strain in a Pressurized Thin-Walled Tube (Vessel Biomechanics)

For a thin-walled cylindrical vessel (e.g., a blood vessel or microfluidic channel wall) of radius $r$ and wall thickness $h$ under internal gauge pressure $P$:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{hoop}} = \frac{P\,r}{E\,h} \quad \text{(circumferential)}$$

$$\varepsilon_{\text{axial}} = \frac{P\,r}{2\,E\,h} \quad \text{(longitudinal)}$$

**Key observation:** Hoop strain is **twice** the axial strain — this is critical in arterial biomechanics and stent design.

## 2.3 True Strain vs. Engineering Strain

| Property | Engineering Strain ($\varepsilon$) | True Strain ($\varepsilon_T$) |
|----------|------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Definition | $\Delta L / L_0$ | $\int_{L_0}^{L} dL/L = \ln(L/L_0)$ |
| Valid for | Small deformations ($< 2\%$) | Large deformations |
| Form | Linear | Logarithmic |
| BioMEMS use | **Piezoresistive sensors** | Hyperelastic biomaterials (skin, artery) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** At $\varepsilon_{\text{eng}} = 1.0$ (i.e., 100% elongation), $\varepsilon_T = \ln(2) \approx 0.693$. They are **not** equal for large strains.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Engineering normal strain | Cantilevers, beams |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_T = \ln(L/L_0)$ | True (logarithmic) strain | Large deformations |
| 3 | $\gamma_{xy} = \partial u/\partial y + \partial v/\partial x$ | Engineering shear strain | Torsional MEMS |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz}$ | Volumetric (dilatational) strain | Pressure sensors |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -\nu\,\varepsilon_{\text{ax}}$ | Lateral strain via Poisson's ratio | Biaxial films |
| 6 | $\sigma = E\,\varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (uniaxial) | Linear elastic regime |
| 7 | $\sigma = E\varepsilon/(1-\nu^2) \cdot [\varepsilon_x + \nu\varepsilon_y]$ | Biaxial Hooke's Law (plane strain) | Diaphragms |
| 8 | $\Delta R / R = \pi_l\,\sigma_l + \pi_t\,\sigma_t$ | Piezoresistive gauge relation | MEMS strain gauges |
| 9 | $\varepsilon_{\text{beam, max}} = 6FL/(Ebt^2)$ | Tip-loaded cantilever | AFM, biosensors |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_{\text{diaph}} = 0.308\,Pa^2/(Et^2)$ | Clamped square diaphragm | Pressure sensors |
| 11 | $\varepsilon_{\text{hoop}} = Pr/(Eh)$ | Thin-walled tube (hoop) | Vessel mechanics |
| 12 | $\varepsilon_x = (\partial u/\partial x)$ | Infinitesimal normal strain | Tensor definition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Check:** All strain quantities are **dimensionless** (m/m). For piezoresistive coefficients $\pi$, units are **inverse Pascal** $\text{Pa}^{-1}$. For modulus $E$, units are **Pascal** $\text{Pa}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in BioMEMS

1. **Piezoresistive Pressure Sensors** — Strain in a silicon diaphragm alters the resistivity of implanted resistors → pressure readout.
2. **AFM Cantilevers** — Surface-molecule binding bends the cantilever → surface stress converted to strain.
3. **Accelerometers (MEMS)** — Inertial mass deforms suspension beams → strain detected capacitively.
4. **Strain-Gauge Biosensors** — Antibody binding on a functionalized surface induces measurable strain.
5. **Vascular Stents & Heart Valves** — Hoop strain determines fatigue life.
6. **Skin-Like Wearable Electronics** — Strain mapping in soft polymer substrates.
7. **Lab-on-Chip Membrane Deflection** — Strain in PDMS membranes for micropumps and valves.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Engineering Strain from First Principles

**Goal:** Derive $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ from the definition of displacement.

**Step 1 — Set up the reference configuration.**
Consider a 1-D bar lying along the $x$-axis, with its left end fixed at the origin. A material point initially at $X$ moves to a new position $x$ after deformation. The **displacement** is:

$$u(X) = x - X$$

**Step 2 — Define the stretch ratio.**
The **stretch** (or stretch ratio) is the ratio of the deformed length element to the original length element:

$$\lambda = \frac{dx}{dX} = \frac{dX + du}{dX} = 1 + \frac{du}{dX}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the strain.**
Strain is defined as the deviation of $\lambda$ from unity:

$$\varepsilon \;\equiv\; \lambda - 1 \;=\; \frac{du}{dX}$$

For a **uniform deformation** over a length $L_0 = L - L_0$ (initial) to $L$ (final):

$$\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{u(L_0) - u(0)}{L_0} \;=\; \frac{(L - 0) - (L_0 - 0)}{L_0} \;=\; \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} \;=\; \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — True Strain from Differential Analysis

**Step 1 — Consider an incremental deformation.**
Suppose the bar is currently of length $L$ and stretches by a small additional amount $dL$. The incremental engineering strain is:

$$d\varepsilon_{\text{eng}} = \frac{dL}{L_0}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to true strain.**
True strain is defined as the integral of incremental strain referenced to the *current* length:

$$d\varepsilon_T \;=\; \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $L_0$ to $L$.**

$$\varepsilon_T \;=\; \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L} \;=\; \ln(L) - \ln(L_0) \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_T \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check at small strains.**
Using the Taylor expansion $\ln(1 + x) = x - x^2/2 + \cdots$:

$$\varepsilon_T \;=\; \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}}) \;\approx\; \varepsilon_{\text{eng}} \quad \text{for } \varepsilon_{\text{eng}} \ll 1$$

This validates that for small strains, engineering and true strain are equivalent.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bending Strain in a Cantilever Beam

**Step 1 — Euler–Bernoulli kinematics.**
For a beam bent to a radius of curvature $\rho(x)$ at position $x$, a fiber at distance $z$ from the **neutral axis** has length:

$$L'(z) = (\rho - z)\,\theta$$

where $\theta = dx/\rho$ is the subtended angle.

**Step 2 — Original length of the same fiber.**
The neutral fiber has length $L_0 = \rho\,\theta$. So:

$$L'(z) = \rho\,\theta - z\,\theta = L_0 - z\,\theta$$

**Step 3 — Compute strain.**

$$\varepsilon(z) \;=\; \frac{L'(z) - L_0}{L_0} \;=\; \frac{-z\,\theta}{\rho\,\theta} \;=\; -\frac{z}{\rho}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon(z) \;=\; -\frac{z}{\rho(x)}}$$

**Step 4 — Relate to bending moment.**
Using Hooke's law $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ and the moment–curvature relation $M = EI/\rho$:

$$\sigma(x, z) \;=\; -\frac{E\,z}{\rho(x)} \;=\; \frac{M(x)\,z}{I} \quad\text{and}\quad \varepsilon(x, z) \;=\; \frac{M(x)\,z}{E\,I}$$

**Step 5 — Specialize to a tip-loaded cantilever.**
With $M_{\max} = F\,L$ at the root, and maximum $|z| = t/2$:

$$\varepsilon_{\max} \;=\; \frac{M_{\max}\,(t/2)}{E\,I} \;=\; \frac{F\,L\,(t/2)}{E\,(bt^3/12)} \;=\; \frac{6\,F\,L}{E\,b\,t^2}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\max} \;=\; \frac{6\,F\,L}{E\,b\,t^2}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hoop Strain in a Thin-Walled Cylinder

**Step 1 — Force balance on a half-cylinder.**
Cut a thin-walled cylinder of radius $r$ and wall thickness $h$ along a longitudinal plane. Internal pressure $P$ acts on the projected area $2rL$:

$$F_{\text{pressure}} = P \cdot 2rL$$

**Step 2 — Hoop stress from equilibrium.**
The two hoop-stress resultants in the cut walls each carry $\sigma_h \cdot h \cdot L$:

$$2\,\sigma_h\,h\,L = 2\,P\,r\,L \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sigma_h = \frac{P\,r}{h}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's law to obtain hoop strain.**

$$\varepsilon_h \;=\; \frac{\sigma_h}{E} \;=\; \frac{P\,r}{E\,h}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\text{hoop}} \;=\; \frac{P\,r}{E\,h}}$$

**Step 4 — Axial strain.**
The axial stress in a closed-end cylinder is half the hoop stress:

$$\sigma_a = \frac{P\,r}{2\,h} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \varepsilon_{\text{axial}} \;=\; \frac{P\,r}{2\,E\,h}$$

**Verification of the 2:1 ratio:** $\varepsilon_{\text{hoop}} / \varepsilon_{\text{axial}} = 2$, a hallmark result for thin-walled pressure vessels.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Shear Strain from Pure Shear

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.**
Two originally perpendicular fibers, one along $x$ and one along $y$, deform so that the angle between them changes from $\pi/2$ to $\pi/2 - \phi$ (a small decrease).

**Step 2 — Engineering shear strain.**

$$\gamma_{xy} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{2} - (\pi/2 - \phi) \;=\; \phi$$

For small angles, the tensorial shear strain is $\varepsilon_{xy} = \phi/2$, and engineering shear strain is $\gamma_{xy} = 2\varepsilon_{xy} = \phi$.

**Step 3 — Total change in angle.**
If the originally perpendicular lines rotate by angles $\alpha$ (one) and $\beta$ (the other) in opposite senses:

$$\gamma_{xy} \;=\; \alpha + \beta$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of displacement gradients.**

$$\alpha = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}, \quad \beta = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{xy} \;=\; \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} \;=\; 2\,\varepsilon_{xy}}$$

## 3.6 Worked Example — Strain in a MEMS Pressure Sensor Diaphragm

**Problem.** A square silicon diaphragm has side $a = 1000\,\mu\text{m}$, thickness $t = 10\,\mu\text{m}$, Young's modulus $E = 130\,\text{GPa}$, and is subjected to a differential pressure $P = 10\,\text{kPa}$. Find the maximum strain at the diaphragm center.

**Given:**
- $a = 1000 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m} = 1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- $t = 10 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m} = 1 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}$
- $E = 130 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa}$
- $P = 10 \times 10^{3}\,\text{Pa} = 1 \times 10^{4}\,\text{Pa}$

**Step 1 — Write the diaphragm strain formula.**

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{0.308\,P\,a^2}{E\,t^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{0.308 \times (1 \times 10^{4}) \times (1 \times 10^{-3})^2}{(130 \times 10^{9}) \times (1 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$0.308 \times 10^{4} \times 10^{-6} = 0.308 \times 10^{-2} = 3.08 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator.**

$$130 \times 10^{9} \times 10^{-10} = 130 \times 10^{-1} = 13$$

**Step 5 — Divide.**

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{3.08 \times 10^{-3}}{13} \approx 2.37 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\max} \approx 237\,\mu\varepsilon}$$

**Interpretation:** This strain is comfortably within the piezoresistive detection range of doped-silicon gauges ($\sim$100 to 1000 $\mu\varepsilon$).

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Beam/Sensor Type | Governing Strain Equation | Typical Strain | BioMEMS Application |
|------------------|----------------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Cantilever (tip load) | $6FL/(Ebt^2)$ | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | AFM, biosensor |
| Clamped square diaphragm | $0.308 Pa^2/(Et^2)$ | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Pressure sensor |
| Circular diaphragm (center) | $0.49 Pr^2 / (Et^2)$ | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Catheter-tip sensor |
| Thin-walled tube (hoop) | $Pr/(Eh)$ | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ | Vascular stent |
| Axial fiber under tension | $F/(EA)$ | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-2}$ | Micro-tensile test |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "strain" in the context of BioMEMS. Differentiate between engineering strain and true strain. State one BioMEMS application where strain is the principal sensing parameter.**

**Model Answer:**

Strain is a dimensionless measure of the deformation of a material relative to its original configuration. In BioMEMS, it represents the relative displacement of micro-scale structural elements (e.g., cantilevers, diaphragms) when subjected to an applied force or pressure.

**Engineering strain** is defined as the change in length per unit *original* length:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$

**True (logarithmic) strain** is defined as the natural logarithm of the ratio of deformed to original length:

$$\varepsilon_T = \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$

**Key difference:** Engineering strain uses the *original* length as the reference and is accurate for small deformations (typically $\varepsilon < 2\%$). True strain uses *instantaneous* length as the reference and is required for large deformations.

**BioMEMS Application:** A piezoresistive pressure sensor uses the strain in a silicon diaphragm as the principal sensing variable — the strain changes the resistance of doped silicon resistors, producing a measurable voltage output.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is shear strain? Write the expression for engineering shear strain in terms of displacement gradients. Explain its physical meaning with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

Shear strain is a measure of the change in angle (in radians) between two lines that were originally perpendicular to each other. It quantifies the *distortion* of a material element, as opposed to its *stretching*.

**Expression:** The engineering shear strain in the $x$–$y$ plane is:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$

where $u$ and $v$ are the displacement components along $x$ and $y$ respectively.

**Physical meaning:** $\gamma_{xy}$ represents the total decrease (or increase) in the angle between two originally perpendicular material lines. It is twice the tensorial shear strain $\varepsilon_{xy}$.

**Example:** In a MEMS torsional micromirror, the mirror plate rotates about a torsion beam. The cross-section of the beam experiences a non-zero shear strain $\gamma_{xz}$, which is directly related to the applied torque by $\tau = G\gamma$, where $G$ is the shear modulus.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Module 2 Internal Choice — You must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Marks are awarded strictly as: Part (a) = 7 marks, Part (b) = 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **maximum bending strain** at the clamped end of a cantilever beam of length $L$, width $b$, and thickness $t$ loaded transversely by a force $F$ at its free end. State clearly the assumptions of the Euler–Bernoulli beam theory used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon micro-cantilever used in an AFM biosensor has dimensions $L = 200\,\mu\text{m}$, $b = 40\,\mu\text{m}$, $t = 1\,\mu\text{m}$, and Young's modulus $E = 130\,\text{GPa}$. A transverse tip force of $F = 1\,\mu\text{N}$ is applied. Calculate:
   (i) the maximum bending strain at the root,
   (ii) the maximum bending stress,
   (iii) the tip deflection. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Assumptions of Euler–Bernoulli beam theory:**
1. Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after deformation (no shear deformation).
2. The beam is slender: $L \gg t$, $L \gg b$.
3. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic.
4. Deflections are small compared to the beam thickness.
5. Rotary inertia and shear deformation are neglected.

**Derivation steps:**

**Step 1 — Bending moment at distance $x$ from the tip:**
A force $F$ at the tip produces a bending moment $M(x) = Fx$, which is maximum at the root ($x = L$):

$$M_{\max} = F L$$

**Step 2 — Section modulus of a rectangular cross-section:**
For a rectangle of width $b$ and height $t$, the moment of inertia is:

$$I = \frac{b t^3}{12}$$

The section modulus (with the extreme fiber at $z = t/2$):

$$S = \frac{I}{t/2} = \frac{b t^2}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum bending stress (flexure formula):**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{S} = \frac{6 F L}{b t^2}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum bending strain (Hooke's law):**

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{E} = \frac{6 F L}{E b t^2}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{6 F L}{E\,b\,t^2}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L = 200 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$
- $b = 40 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$
- $t = 1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$
- $E = 130 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa}$
- $F = 1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{N}$

**(i) Maximum bending strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{6 \times (1 \times 10^{-6}) \times (200 \times 10^{-6})}{(130 \times 10^{9}) \times (40 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

Numerator: $6 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{-4} = 12 \times 10^{-10} = 1.2 \times 10^{-9}$

Denominator: $130 \times 10^{9} \times 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-12} = 130 \times 40 \times 10^{-9} = 5200 \times 10^{-9} = 5.2 \times 10^{-6}$

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-9}}{5.2 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 2.31 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\max} \approx 231\,\mu\varepsilon}$$

**(ii) Maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{\max} = 130 \times 10^{9} \times 2.31 \times 10^{-4} \approx 3.0 \times 10^{7}\,\text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} \approx 30\,\text{MPa}}$$

**(iii) Tip deflection:**

For a cantilever with tip load, the tip deflection is:

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I} = \frac{4 F L^3}{E b t^3}$$

Numerator: $4 \times 10^{-6} \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 8 \times 10^{-15} = 3.2 \times 10^{-20}$

Denominator: $130 \times 10^{9} \times 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-18} = 130 \times 40 \times 10^{-15} = 5.2 \times 10^{-12}$

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-20}}{5.2 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 6.15 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{\text{tip}} \approx 6.15\,\text{nm}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** A $1\,\mu\text{N}$ force produces ~6 nm tip deflection and ~231 $\mu\varepsilon$ maximum strain — well within the detectable range of optical-lever AFM readouts and piezoresistive gauges.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain with neat derivations the **3-D infinitesimal strain tensor** in Cartesian coordinates. Show that it is a symmetric second-order tensor with **6 independent components**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thin-walled cylindrical micro-channel (radius $r = 500\,\mu\text{m}$, wall thickness $h = 25\,\mu\text{m}$) carries an internal gauge pressure $P = 50\,\text{kPa}$. The wall material has $E = 130\,\text{GPa}$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.28$. Calculate:
   (i) the hoop strain,
   (ii) the axial strain,
   (iii) the change in radius (radial displacement) of the tube. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — The 3-D Infinitesimal Strain Tensor:**

**Step 1 — Kinematics of deformation.**
Let a material point originally at $\mathbf{X}$ move to $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{X} + \mathbf{u}$, where $\mathbf{u} = (u, v, w)$ is the displacement vector. For two infinitesimally separated points with original separation $d\mathbf{X}$, the new separation is:

$$d\mathbf{x} = d\mathbf{X} + d\mathbf{u} = \left(\mathbf{I} + \nabla\mathbf{u}\right) d\mathbf{X}$$

where $\nabla\mathbf{u}$ is the **displacement gradient tensor**.

**Step 2 — Length change (squared).**
The squared length change is:

$$|d\mathbf{x}|^2 - |d\mathbf{X}|^2 = 2\,d\mathbf{X}^T \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\, d\mathbf{X}$$

where $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ is the **infinitesimal strain tensor** (Cauchy strain):

$$\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\nabla\mathbf{u} + (\nabla\mathbf{u})^T\right]$$

**Step 3 — Component form.**
Writing out the components:

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \varepsilon_{zz} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$

$$\varepsilon_{xy} = \varepsilon_{yx} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_{xz} = \varepsilon_{zx} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_{yz} = \varepsilon_{zy} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Symmetry.**
From the defining equation $\varepsilon_{ij} = \varepsilon_{ji}$, the tensor is symmetric. A symmetric $3 \times 3$ tensor has $\frac{3(3+1)}{2} = 6$ independent components: **3 normal strains** ($\varepsilon_{xx}, \varepsilon_{yy}, \varepsilon_{zz}$) and **3 independent shear strains** ($\varepsilon_{xy}, \varepsilon_{yz}, \varepsilon_{xz}$).

**Tensor matrix form:**

$$\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_{xx} & \varepsilon_{xy} & \varepsilon_{xz} \\ \varepsilon_{yx} & \varepsilon_{yy} & \varepsilon_{yz} \\ \varepsilon_{zx} & \varepsilon_{zy} & \varepsilon_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Part (b) — Thin-Walled Tube Solution:**

**Given:**
- $r = 500 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$
- $h = 25 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}$
- $P = 50 \times 10^{3}\,\text{Pa}$
- $E = 130 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa}$
- $\nu = 0.28$

**(i) Hoop strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{hoop}} = \frac{P\,r}{E\,h} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{4}) \times (5 \times 10^{-4})}{(130 \times 10^{9}) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-5})}$$

Numerator: $25 \times 10^{0} = 25$

Denominator: $130 \times 2.5 \times 10^{4} = 325 \times 10^{4} = 3.25 \times 10^{6}$

$$\varepsilon_{\text{hoop}} = \frac{25}{3.25 \times 10^{6}} \approx 7.69 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\text{hoop}} \approx 7.69\,\mu\varepsilon}$$

**(ii) Axial strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{axial}} = \frac{P\,r}{2\,E\,h} = \frac{\varepsilon_{\text{hoop}}}{2} \approx 3.85\,\mu\varepsilon$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\text{axial}} \approx 3.85\,\mu\varepsilon}$$

**(iii) Change in radius:**
The change in radius is the radial displacement at the wall:

$$\Delta r = r \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{hoop}} = (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times (7.69 \times 10^{-6}) \approx 3.85 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta r \approx 3.85\,\text{nm}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** A 50 kPa pressure produces only ~3.85 nm change in radius and ~7.7 $\mu\varepsilon$ hoop strain. This is too small to detect mechanically but can be measured with a high-resolution optical or piezoresistive gauge — illustrating the extreme sensitivity required in BioMEMS strain measurements.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Mixing $\mu\text{m}$ with m in numerical substitutions is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks. Always convert to SI (meters, Pascals, Newtons) *first*.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** Engineering shear strain $\gamma = 2\varepsilon_{\text{tensor}}$. Writing $\gamma = \partial u/\partial y + \partial v/\partial x$ in a question that asks for *tensorial* shear strain loses marks.
> 3. **Sign errors in Hooke's law:** Compressive strain must be assigned a negative sign in the convention used by the examiner.
> 4. **Confusing stress and strain:** The "stress–strain relation" $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ is the constitutive link — but in a problem, do NOT solve for strain using the *stress* equation.
> 5. **In cantilever problems:** The maximum strain is at the *root* (clamped end), NOT the tip. Examiners specifically check this.
> 6. **True vs. engineering strain:** If the problem states "large deformation," use $\varepsilon_T = \ln(L/L_0)$. Otherwise, default to engineering strain.
> 7. **Missing assumptions in derivations:** A derivation without the stated assumptions (e.g., Euler–Bernoulli) loses up to 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Strain in BioMEMS**

- **Definition:** Strain = (deformed length − original length) / original length = $\Delta L / L_0$ (dimensionless, m/m).
- **Types:** Normal (axial, lateral), shear, volumetric, true (logarithmic). In BioMEMS, *normal* strain dominates sensor design.
- **Units:** Always m/m. Use $\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$ for convenience.
- **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ (uniaxial). For silicon ⟨100⟩, $E \approx 130\,\text{GPa}$, $\nu \approx 0.28$.
- **Cantilever bending strain:** $\varepsilon_{\max} = 6FL/(Ebt^2)$ — at the *clamped root*, on the *top/bottom* fiber.
- **Diaphragm strain:** $\varepsilon_{\max} \approx 0.308\,P a^2/(E t^2)$ (square, clamped). Basis of MEMS pressure sensors.
- **Hoop vs. axial strain (thin tube):** $\varepsilon_{\text{hoop}} = 2\varepsilon_{\text{axial}} = Pr/(Eh)$. Critical for vascular mechanics.
- **Engineering vs. true strain:** $\varepsilon_T = \ln(L/L_0)$. Equivalent for $\varepsilon \ll 1$.
- **Strain tensor:** Symmetric, 6 independent components (3 normal + 3 shear). $\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}(u_{i,j} + u_{j,i})$.
- **Piezoresistive sensing:** $\Delta R/R = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$ — the dominant strain-sensing principle in silicon MEMS.
- **Assumptions to always state:** Small deformations, linear elastic material, plane sections remain plane (Euler–Bernoulli), isotropic homogeneous material.
- **BioMEMS applications:** Pressure sensors, AFM cantilevers, accelerometers, biosensors, vascular stents, lab-on-chip membranes.
- **Typical strain range in MEMS:** $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ ($\mu\varepsilon$ to $m\varepsilon$).
- **Sign convention:** Positive strain = elongation; negative = compression.
- **Avoid:** Confusing engineering shear strain $\gamma$ with tensorial shear strain $\varepsilon$ (factor of 2 difference).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure and Flow Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pressure and Flow Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Pressure Measurements in BioMEMS

**Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Pressure measurement in BioMEMS refers to the transduction of mechanical stress (force per unit area) exerted by biological fluids (blood, interstitial fluid, cerebrospinal fluid, intraocular fluid) into a quantifiable electrical signal using microfabricated sensing elements. The transduction typically relies on a deformable diaphragm whose deflection, strain, or capacitance change is correlated to the applied pressure differential.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pressure sensors in BioMEMS must operate in the physiological pressure range (**0.1 kPa – 40 kPa**) with sub-millimeter footprint, biocompatible packaging, and minimum hysteresis (< 1% Full Scale Output).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine blowing up a thin balloon. The air pressure inside pushes outward and the balloon stretches. Now imagine placing a tiny strain gauge on that balloon — every puff of breath stretches it a tiny bit, and the gauge tells you exactly how hard you are blowing. A MEMS pressure sensor works the *exact same way* — a thin silicon diaphragm (the "balloon") bends under fluid pressure, and microscopic sensors etched into it measure the bend. The difference is the diaphragm is only **5–50 micrometers** thick — about the width of a human hair.

## 1.2 Flow Measurements in BioMEMS/Microfluidics

**Formal Academic Definition:**
Flow measurement in microfluidics is the determination of volumetric flow rate $Q$ (m³/s) or mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ (kg/s) of fluids (Newtonian or non-Newtonian) confined within microchannels of characteristic hydraulic diameter $D_h \leq 500\ \mu m$. It encompasses both invasive (in-channel) and non-invasive (external) measurement paradigms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** Volumetric Flow Rate $Q = A \cdot v_{avg}$, where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the microchannel and $v_{avg}$ is the spatial mean velocity.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of water flowing through a thin straw versus through a garden hose. In both cases you can measure the flow, but the method differs — in a straw you cannot use a propeller (it is too big), so you measure something else, like how much heat the water carries away from a tiny hot wire (thermal flow sensor), or how much pressure difference is needed to push it through (pressure-drop flow meter). In microfluidics, we use the same tricks, but miniaturized to the size of a single human hair's width.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Relevance |
|-----------|--------|----------------|-----------|
| Dynamic Viscosity of Water at 25°C | $\mu$ | **$1.002 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$** | Baseline for biofluids |
| Density of Water at 25°C | $\rho$ | **$997\ \text{kg/m}^3$** | Reynolds number calculation |
| Blood Viscosity (normal hematocrit) | $\mu_{blood}$ | **$3 \times 10^{-3}$ to $4 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$** | Biomedical flow |
| Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **$101.325\ \text{kPa}$** | Gauge vs. absolute pressure |
| Body Temperature | $T_{body}$ | **$310\ \text{K}$ (37°C)** | Thermal flow sensor design |
| Speed of Sound in Air (25°C) | $c$ | **$346\ \text{m/s}$** | Acoustic flow sensing |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k_B$ | **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$** | Brownian noise reference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gauge vs. Absolute Pressure:** A MEMS pressure sensor typically measures **gauge pressure** $P_{gauge} = P_{absolute} - P_{atmospheric}$, because one side of the diaphragm is vented to ambient. **Absolute pressure sensors** require a sealed vacuum reference cavity fabricated via wafer-level bonding.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Piezoresistive Pressure Sensors

### Operating Principle (Structured Logic)

1. **Diaphragm Deflection:** Applied pressure $P$ deflects a thin square or circular silicon diaphragm of thickness $h$ and side length $a$ (or radius $R$).
2. **Strain Generation:** The deflection produces mechanical strain $\varepsilon$ at specific locations on the diaphragm surface.
3. **Piezoresistive Effect:** Doped silicon resistors (piezoresistors) embedded at high-strain regions change their electrical resistance $R$ according to $\Delta R / R = \pi_L \cdot \sigma$ where $\pi_L$ is the longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient and $\sigma$ is the mechanical stress.
4. **Wheatstone Bridge Output:** Four piezoresistors are configured in a full Wheatstone bridge, providing a differential output voltage $V_{out}$ proportional to $\Delta R / R$.

### Why Piezoresistive Sensing Dominates Biomedical Applications
- High sensitivity at low pressures
- Linear response in physiological range
- CMOS-compatible fabrication
- Small footprint ($\leq 1\ \text{mm}^2$)
- Low power consumption (few mW)

## 2.2 Capacitive Pressure Sensors

### Operating Principle

1. A parallel-plate capacitor is formed between the deflecting diaphragm and a fixed substrate electrode.
2. The gap $d$ decreases by $\Delta d$ when pressure is applied.
3. Capacitance changes: $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$.
4. Sensitivity: $S_C = \partial C / \partial P = -\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d^2 \cdot (\partial d / \partial P)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Advantage of Capacitive over Piezoresistive:** Capacitive sensors have **superior temperature stability** (drift $< 0.1\ \%/^{\circ}\text{C}$), **lower power consumption** (nW range), and **higher pressure resolution** in the sub-kPa regime — critical for intracranial and intraocular pressure monitoring.

## 2.3 Microfluidic Flow Regimes — Reynolds Number

**Reynolds Number** dictates whether flow is laminar or turbulent:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v_{avg} D_h}{\mu}$$

Where $D_h$ is the **hydraulic diameter** defined for non-circular channels as:

$$D_h = \frac{4 A_{cs}}{P_{wet}}$$

Here $A_{cs}$ is the cross-sectional area and $P_{wet}$ is the wetted perimeter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Fact:** In bio-microfluidics, $Re$ typically ranges from **$10^{-6}$ to $10^{2}$**. The flow is overwhelmingly **laminar** because inertial forces are negligible compared to viscous forces. Turbulent flow ($Re > 2300$ in pipes) almost never occurs in biological microchannels.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Applicability |
|---|---------|---------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = A_{cs} \cdot v_{avg}$ | m³/s | General |
| 2 | Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ | kg/s | General |
| 3 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \rho v_{avg} D_h / \mu$ | dimensionless | All flow |
| 4 | Hydraulic Diameter | $D_h = 4 A_{cs} / P_{wet}$ | m | Non-circular channels |
| 5 | Hagen-Poiseuille Pressure Drop | $\Delta P = 128 \mu L Q / (\pi D^4)$ | Pa | Circular pipe, laminar |
| 6 | Hagen-Poiseuille (Rectangular) | $\Delta P = 12 \mu L Q / (w h^3) \left[1 - \sum\right]$ | Pa | Rectangular channel |
| 7 | Capacitance (Parallel Plate) | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | F | Capacitive sensor |
| 8 | Piezoresistive Gauge Factor | $G = (\Delta R/R) / \varepsilon$ | dimensionless | Piezoresistive |
| 9 | Diaphragm Max Deflection (Square) | $w_{max} = 0.00126 P a^4 / (D_{flex})$ | m | Square diaphragm |
| 10 | Diaphragm Max Deflection (Circular) | $w_{max} = P R^4 / (64 D_{flex})$ | m | Circular diaphragm |
| 11 | Flexural Rigidity | $D_{flex} = E h^3 / [12(1-\nu^2)]$ | N·m | Plate bending |
| 12 | Thermal Mass Flow (King's Law) | $V^2 = A (T_{avg} - T_{fluid}) + B (T_{avg} - T_{fluid})^n$ | V² | Anemometer |
| 13 | Coriolis Force | $F_c = 2 m \vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}$ | N | Coriolis flow meter |
| 14 | Young's Modulus of Silicon | $E_{Si} = 130$ to **$190\ \text{GPa}$** | Pa | Diaphragm design |
| 15 | Poisson's Ratio of Silicon | $\nu_{Si}$ = **$0.28$** | dimensionless | Diaphragm design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Loss Alert:** Do not confuse gauge factor $G$ with piezoresistive coefficient $\pi$. They are related as $G = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_L E$ for metals; for silicon, $G \approx \pi_L E$ since the geometric term is small.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Sensor Type | Pressure/ Flow Range | Critical Spec |
|-------------|-------------|----------------------|----------------|
| **Intracranial Pressure (ICP) Monitoring** | Capacitive | 0–10 kPa | Drift $< 0.1\ \text{mmHg/month}$ |
| **Intraocular Pressure (IOP, Glaucoma)** | Piezoresistive | 0–7 kPa | Resolution $< 0.1\ \text{kPa}$ |
| **Blood Pressure Catheter (A-line)** | Capacitive + piezo | 0–40 kPa | Bandwidth $> 100\ \text{Hz}$ |
| **Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM)** | Indirect via flow | nL/min | Biofouling-resistant |
| **Insulin Pump (Closed Loop)** | Flow sensor | 0.1–10 µL/min | Accuracy $< 5\%$ |
| **Ventilator (COVID era)** | Differential piezo | 0–10 kPa | Response time $< 10\ \text{ms}$ |
| **Lab-on-Chip Cytometry** | Thermal anemometer | 0.1–100 µL/min | Thermal isolation critical |
| **Microfluidic Droplet Generator** | Coriolis / thermal | 1–1000 µL/hr | High resolution |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Deflection of a Circular Diaphragm

Consider a thin circular silicon diaphragm of radius $R$, thickness $h$, fixed at its periphery, subjected to a uniform pressure $P$ on one face. We want the **maximum center deflection** $w_{max}$.

### Step 1: Governing Plate Equation

For a thin plate under transverse load, the governing differential equation is:

$$\nabla^4 w(r) = \frac{P}{D_{flex}}$$

where $D_{flex}$ is the flexural rigidity given by:

$$D_{flex} = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$$

### Step 2: Apply Axisymmetric Loading

For axisymmetric pressure, $w$ depends only on $r$, so the biharmonic operator becomes:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left\{ r \frac{d}{dr} \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dw}{dr} \right) \right] \right\} = \frac{P}{D_{flex}}$$

### Step 3: Integrate the Equation

Integrating once with respect to $r$:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dw}{dr} \right) = \frac{P r^2}{4 D_{flex}} + C_1$$

Multiplying by $r$ and integrating again:

$$r \frac{dw}{dr} = \frac{P r^4}{16 D_{flex}} + \frac{C_1 r^2}{2} + C_2$$

### Step 4: Apply Boundary Conditions

For a clamped circular plate:
- **BC 1:** $w(R) = 0$ (no deflection at edge)
- **BC 2:** $\left. \dfrac{dw}{dr} \right\vert_{r=R} = 0$ (zero slope at clamped edge)

The regularity condition at $r=0$ requires $C_2 = 0$ (otherwise the slope diverges at center).

From BC 2:

$$0 = \frac{P R^3}{16 D_{flex}} + \frac{C_1 R}{2}$$

Solving for $C_1$:

$$C_1 = -\frac{P R^2}{8 D_{flex}}$$

### Step 5: Obtain Deflection Profile

Substituting $C_1$ back and integrating one more time:

$$w(r) = \frac{P r^4}{64 D_{flex}} - \frac{P R^2 r^2}{32 D_{flex}} + C_3$$

Applying BC 1, $w(R) = 0$:

$$0 = \frac{P R^4}{64 D_{flex}} - \frac{P R^4}{32 D_{flex}} + C_3 = -\frac{P R^4}{64 D_{flex}} + C_3$$

$$C_3 = \frac{P R^4}{64 D_{flex}}$$

### Step 6: Maximum Deflection at Center

Substituting $r = 0$:

$$w_{max} = w(0) = C_3 = \frac{P R^4}{64 D_{flex}}$$

$$\boxed{w_{max} = \frac{P R^4}{64 \, D_{flex}} = \frac{3 P R^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E h^3}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** The center deflection scales with $R^4$ and inversely with $h^3$. To double the sensitivity, **halve the diaphragm thickness** — far more effective than doubling $R$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Hagen-Poiseuille Pressure Drop in a Circular Microchannel

For steady, fully-developed, laminar, incompressible flow of a Newtonian fluid in a cylindrical channel of length $L$ and diameter $D$, derive the pressure drop $\Delta P$ across the channel.

### Step 1: Navier-Stokes in Cylindrical Coordinates

For an axisymmetric, fully-developed flow with $v_z = v_z(r)$ and $v_r = 0$:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} \right) \right]$$

### Step 2: Pressure Gradient as a Constant

For fully developed flow, the pressure drop is linear:

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu L}$$

### Step 3: Integrate Once

$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \, r^2}{2 \mu L} + C_1$$

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \, r}{2 \mu L} + \frac{C_1}{r}$$

### Step 4: Apply No-Slip BC at Center

By symmetry, the velocity gradient at $r = 0$ must be finite, hence $C_1 = 0$.

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \, r}{2 \mu L}$$

### Step 5: Integrate Again

$$v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P \, r^2}{4 \mu L} + C_2$$

### Step 6: Apply No-Slip BC at Wall

At $r = D/2 = R$:

$$0 = \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4 \mu L} + C_2 \implies C_2 = -\frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4 \mu L}$$

### Step 7: Parabolic Velocity Profile (Poiseuille Flow)

$$\boxed{v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right)}$$

This is a **parabolic (Hagen-Poiseuille) profile** with $v_{max} = \Delta P R^2 / (4 \mu L)$ at the center.

### Step 8: Compute Volumetric Flow Rate

Integrate velocity over the cross-section:

$$Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 - r^2) r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^4} = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Students often write $D^3$ in the denominator. The correct exponent is **$D^4$**. This is why microfluidic channels are *highly* resistive to flow — halving the diameter increases the pressure drop by a factor of 16.

### Step 9: Mean Velocity

The average velocity is $v_{avg} = Q / (\pi R^2) = \Delta P R^2 / (8 \mu L)$, exactly half of $v_{max}$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Sensitivity of Capacitive Pressure Sensor

For a parallel-plate capacitive pressure sensor with vacuum gap $d_0$ at zero pressure, find the sensitivity $S = \partial C / \partial P$.

### Step 1: Initial Capacitance

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}$$

### Step 2: Gap Under Pressure

Under pressure, the diaphragm deflects by $w_{max}$, reducing the gap:

$$d(P) = d_0 - w_{max}(P) = d_0 - \frac{P R^4}{64 D_{flex}}$$

### Step 3: Capacitance as Function of Pressure

$$C(P) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \frac{P R^4}{64 D_{flex}}}$$

### Step 4: Compute Sensitivity (Linear Approximation)

For small $w_{max} \ll d_0$:

$$C(P) \approx C_0 \left( 1 + \frac{P R^4}{64 D_{flex} d_0} \right)$$

Hence:

$$S_C = \frac{\partial C}{\partial P} = \frac{C_0 R^4}{64 D_{flex} d_0} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A R^4}{64 D_{flex} d_0^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** Capacitive sensitivity scales as $1/d_0^2$. Using a **smaller initial gap** $d_0$ dramatically increases sensitivity. This is why most capacitive BioMEMS sensors use sub-micron gaps ($d_0 \approx 0.5$–$2\ \mu\text{m}$).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A circular silicon piezoresistive pressure sensor has $R = 200\ \mu\text{m}$, $h = 5\ \mu\text{m}$, $E = 170\ \text{GPa}$, $\nu = 0.28$. Applied pressure $P = 10\ \text{kPa}$. Find the maximum center deflection.

**Solution:**

$$D_{flex} = \frac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{(170 \times 10^9)(5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12(1-0.28^2)}$$

$$D_{flex} = \frac{170 \times 10^9 \times 125 \times 10^{-18}}{12 \times 0.9216} = \frac{2.125 \times 10^{-5}}{11.059}$$

$$D_{flex} = 1.921 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{N·m}$$

$$w_{max} = \frac{P R^4}{64 D_{flex}} = \frac{(10^4)(200 \times 10^{-6})^4}{64 \times 1.921 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$w_{max} = \frac{10^4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-15}}{1.229 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-11}}{1.229 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{w_{max} \approx 1.302 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} = 130.2\ \text{nm}}$$

This nanometer-scale deflection is precisely what piezoresistive sensors can resolve — confirming their suitability for biomedical pressure monitoring.

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Pressure Drop in a Microchannel

**Problem:** Water at 25°C flows through a circular microchannel of length $L = 10\ \text{mm}$ and diameter $D = 100\ \mu\text{m}$ at flow rate $Q = 1\ \mu\text{L/min}$. Find the pressure drop.

**Solution:**

Convert units: $Q = 1 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^3 / 60\ \text{s} = 1.667 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$

$$\Delta P = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4} = \frac{128 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.01 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-11}}{\pi (100 \times 10^{-6})^4}$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{2.139 \times 10^{-14}}{\pi \times 10^{-16}} = \frac{2.139 \times 10^{-14}}{3.142 \times 10^{-16}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P \approx 68.1\ \text{Pa} = 0.51\ \text{mmHg}}$$

For $D = 10\ \mu\text{m}$ (10× smaller), the pressure drop becomes $\approx 68.1\ \text{kPa}$ — illustrating the $D^{-4}$ scaling.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Applied Fluid Pressure P] --> B[Silicon Diaphragm Deflection]
    B --> C[Mechanical Strain at Piezoresistor Locations]
    C --> D[Piezoresistive Coefficient: Resistance Change dR]
    D --> E[Wheatstone Bridge: Vout proportional to dR]
    E --> F[Instrumentation Amplifier: Gain 100 to 1000]
    F --> G[Analog to Digital Converter: 12 to 16 bit]

```
