# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper 1)]**
Define Gauge Factor of a piezoresistive sensor. Why is silicon preferred over metals for biomedical pressure sensors?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Gauge Factor $G$ is the ratio of fractional change in resistance to applied strain:

$$G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

For metals, $G \approx 2$ (geometric effect dominates). For silicon, $G \approx 100$ to $200$ because the piezoresistive coefficient $\pi_L$ is exceptionally large, providing high sensitivity. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for comparison with values)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is Hydraulic Diameter? For a square microchannel of side $a$, derive $D_h$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Hydraulic diameter is defined as $D_h = 4 A_{cs} / P_{wet}$. For a square channel of side $a$: $A_{cs} = a^2$ and $P_{wet} = 4a$. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for result $D_h = a$)*

$$D_h = \frac{4 a^2}{4 a} = a$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** With a clear schematic, explain the working of a piezoresistive MEMS pressure sensor. Derive the expression for maximum deflection of a circular diaphragm under uniform pressure. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A circular silicon diaphragm has $R = 250\ \mu\text{m}$, $h = 8\ \mu\text{m}$, $E = 170\ \text{GPa}$, $\nu = 0.28$. The diaphragm is subjected to a uniform pressure of $P = 5\ \text{kPa}$. Calculate:
- (i) Flexural rigidity $D_{flex}$
- (ii) Maximum center deflection $w_{max}$
- (iii) Maximum stress $\sigma_{max}$ *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor Working + Derivation

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

1. Fluid pressure $P$ is applied to one side of a silicon diaphragm.
2. The diaphragm (clamped at edges) deflects — center moves maximum, edges stay fixed.
3. Piezoresistors ion-implanted at high-strain locations (near diaphragm edges) experience longitudinal and transverse stresses.
4. Their resistance changes by $\Delta R / R = \pi_L \sigma_L + \pi_T \sigma_T$.
5. Four such resistors form a Wheatstone bridge whose differential output $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \Delta R / (2R)$ is proportional to applied pressure.

**Derivation of Maximum Deflection (4 marks):**
Governing plate equation:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left\{ r \frac{d}{dr} \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dw}{dr} \right) \right] \right\} = \frac{P}{D_{flex}}$$

where $D_{flex} = E h^3 / [12(1-\nu^2)]$.

After applying boundary conditions $w(R) = 0$ and $w'(R) = 0$:

$$w_{max} = \frac{P R^4}{64 D_{flex}} = \frac{3 P R^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E h^3}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Flexural Rigidity (2 marks):**

$$D_{flex} = \frac{(170 \times 10^9)(8 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12(1-0.28^2)}$$

$$D_{flex} = \frac{170 \times 10^9 \times 5.12 \times 10^{-16}}{12 \times 0.9216} = \frac{8.704 \times 10^{-5}}{11.0592}$$

$$D_{flex} = 7.871 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{N·m}$$

**(ii) Maximum Deflection (3 marks):**

$$w_{max} = \frac{P R^4}{64 D_{flex}} = \frac{(5 \times 10^3)(250 \times 10^{-6})^4}{64 \times 7.871 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$w_{max} = \frac{5 \times 10^3 \times 3.906 \times 10^{-15}}{5.037 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1.953 \times 10^{-11}}{5.037 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{w_{max} \approx 3.877 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m} = 38.77\ \text{nm}}$$

**(iii) Maximum Stress (2 marks):**
Maximum stress occurs at the clamped edge:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 P R^2}{4 h^2} = \frac{3 \times 5 \times 10^3 \times (250 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4 \times (8 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-8}}{4 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-11}} = \frac{9.375 \times 10^{-4}}{2.56 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} \approx 3.66\ \text{MPa}}$$

This is well below the fracture strength of silicon (typically 1–7 GPa), ensuring safe operation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain different flow measurement techniques used in microfluidic BioMEMS systems. Compare thermal anemometer and Coriolis flow sensors. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A microfluidic channel has length $L = 5\ \text{cm}$, rectangular cross-section $w = 200\ \mu\text{m} \times h = 50\ \mu\text{m}$. A pressure drop of $\Delta P = 2\ \text{kPa}$ drives water ($\mu = 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$) through it. Calculate:
- (i) Hydraulic diameter $D_h$
- (ii) Volumetric flow rate $Q$ (use the approximation for rectangular channels: $\Delta P = 12 \mu L Q / (w h^3) \cdot [1 - 0.63 h/w]$)
- (iii) Reynolds number *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Flow Measurement Techniques (7 marks)

**Overview (2 marks):** Four major techniques are used in microfluidic BioMEMS:

1. **Thermal Anemometer (Hot-Wire / Hot-Film):** A heated sensor loses heat to the fluid proportional to its flow velocity. King's law relates voltage to flow.
2. **Coriolis Mass Flow Sensor:** The fluid is driven through a vibrating tube; the Coriolis force deflects the tube proportional to mass flow.
3. **Differential Pressure (Laminar Element):** Measures $\Delta P$ across a known channel resistance and uses $Q = \Delta P / R_{hydraulic}$.
4. **Optical (LDA / PIV / Doppler):** Non-invasive, uses laser scattering from tracer particles.

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | Thermal Anemometer | Coriolis Mass Flow |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Measurand | Volumetric flow | Mass flow (direct) |
| Sensitivity | Very high (nL/min) | Moderate (µL/min) |
| Biocompatibility | High (no moving parts) | Medium (tube vibration) |
| Temperature sensitivity | **High** (compensation needed) | Low |
| Density sensitivity | None | None (intrinsic) |
| Power consumption | Moderate to high | High |
| Use in implants | **Preferred** | Rare (size constraint) |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Hydraulic Diameter (2 marks):**

$$D_h = \frac{4 A_{cs}}{P_{wet}} = \frac{4 \times w \times h}{2(w + h)} = \frac{2 w h}{w + h}$$

$$D_h = \frac{2 \times 200 \times 50}{200 + 50} = \frac{20{,}000}{250} = 80\ \mu\text{m}$$

**(ii) Volumetric Flow Rate (3 marks):**
Using:

$$\Delta P = \frac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^3} \left(1 - 0.63 \frac{h}{w}\right)$$

Compute the geometric factor:

$$1 - 0.63 \frac{h}{w} = 1 - 0.63 \times \frac{50}{200} = 1 - 0.1575 = 0.8425$$

$$Q = \frac{\Delta P \cdot w h^3}{12 \mu L \cdot 0.8425}$$

$$Q = \frac{(2 \times 10^3)(200 \times 10^{-6})(50 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.05 \times 0.8425}$$

Numerator: $2 \times 10^3 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14} = 2.5 \times 10^{-14}$

Denominator: $12 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.05 \times 0.8425 = 5.055 \times 10^{-4}$

$$Q = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-14}}{5.055 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.946 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 4.95 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 2.97\ \mu\text{L/min}}$$

**(iii) Reynolds Number (2 marks):**

$$v_{avg} = \frac{Q}{A_{cs}} = \frac{4.95 \times 10^{-11}}{200 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{4.95 \times 10^{-11}}{10^{-8}} = 4.95 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m/s}$$

$$Re = \frac{\rho v_{avg} D_h}{\mu} = \frac{997 \times 4.95 \times 10^{-3} \times 80 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \frac{3.948 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-3}} = 0.395$$

$$\boxed{Re \approx 0.4 \ll 2300 \Rightarrow \text{flow is firmly laminar}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion is where 70% of marks are lost.** Always convert $\mu\text{m}$ to m, $\mu\text{L/min}$ to m³/s before plugging into the formula.
> 2. **The exponent in Hagen-Poiseuille is $D^4$, not $D^3$.** This is a frequent trap.
> 3. **For piezoresistive sensors, the gauge factor is NOT 2 (the metal value).** For silicon it is **100–200**.
> 4. **In Poiseuille flow, $v_{avg} = v_{max}/2$, not equal to $v_{max}$.** This is a common conceptual error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor:** Working principle relies on piezoresistive effect in doped silicon. Sensitivity $\propto 1/h^3$ (thickness cubed in denominator). Best for low-pressure biomedical applications. Gauge Factor of silicon is **100–200**.

- **Capacitive Pressure Sensor:** Capacitance $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$. Sensitivity $\propto 1/d_0^2$. Best for **low-drift** applications like ICP, IOP. Vacuum reference required for absolute pressure.

- **Maximum Deflection of Circular Diaphragm:** $w_{max} = PR^4 / (64 D_{flex})$, where $D_{flex} = E h^3 / [12(1-\nu^2)]$. Center deflects most, edges are clamped.

- **Hagen-Poiseuille Flow (Circular Pipe):** Parabolic velocity profile. $\Delta P = 128 \mu L Q / (\pi D^4)$. Scales as **$D^{-4}$** — extremely sensitive to channel diameter.

- **Reynolds Number in Microfluidics:** $Re = \rho v D_h / \mu$. Typical bio-microfluidic $Re \sim 10^{-6}$ to $10^{2}$. **Laminar flow** dominates; no turbulence.

- **Hydraulic Diameter:** $D_h = 4 A_{cs} / P_{wet}$. For a square channel of side $a$, $D_h = a$. For circular pipe, $D_h = D$.

- **Thermal Anemometer:** Uses heat transfer from a heated element — King's Law. High sensitivity, low flow range. Used in lab-on-chip flow sensing.

- **Coriolis Mass Flow Sensor:** Measures mass flow directly, independent of fluid properties. Tube deflection $\propto$ Coriolis force $\propto \dot{m} \cdot \omega \cdot v$.

- **Diaphragm Maximum Stress:** $\sigma_{max} = 3 P R^2 / (4 h^2)$ for circular diaphragms. Must remain well below silicon fracture strength (~7 GPa).

- **Young's Modulus of Silicon:** $E \approx 130$ to $190\ \text{GPa}$ (use **170 GPa** unless otherwise specified); Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.28$.

- **Piezoresistive Coefficients of Silicon:** $\pi_{11} = -1022 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$, $\pi_{12} = +534 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$, $\pi_{44} = +136 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$ (for $\rho$-type silicon in n-well).

- **Atmospheric Pressure Reference:** $P_{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$. Gauge pressure = absolute − atmospheric.

- **Capacitive Sensitivity Scaling:** $S_C \propto 1/d_0^2$. Use sub-micron vacuum gap for high sensitivity.

- **Practical Biomedical Pressure Ranges:** Blood Pressure 0–40 kPa; Intracranial 0–10 kPa; Intraocular 0.5–7 kPa; Glaucoma threshold $\approx$ 2.7 kPa (21 mmHg).

- **KCL 2024 Exam Tip:** Always state the **assumptions** for derivations: thin plate ($h \ll a$), small deflection ($w \ll h$), fully developed laminar flow, Newtonian fluid, no-slip at walls.

- **Dimensional Analysis Check:** Verify that pressure drop is in Pa, flow rate in m³/s, and viscosity in Pa·s. This is the fastest way to catch algebraic errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Sensors and Actuators - Actuator Based on Thermal Expansion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermal Sensors and Actuators - Actuator Based on Thermal Expansion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Expansion-Based Actuator (Definition as per KTU BioMEMS Syllabus):**
> A *thermal expansion-based actuator* in BioMEMS is a microscale transduction device that converts electrical (or optical/chemical) input energy into **displacement, force, or mechanical work** by exploiting the **thermally induced dimensional change** (linear, areal, or volumetric) of a structural material. The fundamental physical phenomenon is governed by the **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE), denoted as `α`** with SI units of **K⁻¹** or **°C⁻¹**.

In the BioMEMS context, these actuators are most commonly implemented as:

| Sub-class | Functional Form | Microfluidic Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Bimorph / Bimetallic Actuator** | Layered dissimilar materials with mismatched α | Micro-valves, micro-switches, optical scanners |
| **Thermo-pneumatic Actuator** | Heated gas/liquid producing pressure | Valves, pumps, bubble generators |
| **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) Actuator** | Martensite–Austenite phase change | High-force micro-grippers, stents |
| **Thermal Bubble Actuator** | Phase change (liquid → vapor) | Inkjet printheads, droplet ejectors |
| **Thermal Phase-Change Actuator** | Solid–liquid (paraffin) expansion | One-shot valves, lab-on-a-chip release |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bimetallic Strip in an Old Iron:**
> Think of a traditional electric iron. Inside it is a **bimetallic strip** — two strips of different metals riveted together. When the iron heats up, one metal (e.g., brass) expands *more* than the other (e.g., Invar). Because they are bonded, the strip cannot expand freely; instead it **bends toward the metal that expands less**. This bending physically opens/closes a switch that regulates temperature.
> 
> In BioMEMS, we miniaturize the *same* principle — but the "iron" is replaced by a **micro-heater (often polysilicon or platinum resistor)** etched on a silicon/glass chip, and the "switch" becomes a **micro-valve** that opens/closes a fluidic channel measured in **micrometres (µm)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Second Analogy — The Thermometer Mercury:**
> Mercury rises in a glass capillary because it expands *much more* than the glass when heated. A thermal expansion actuator uses this same volumetric effect — but on a chip — to push a diaphragm, a piston, or a fluid column to do useful work.

### 1.3 Governing Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following values are essential benchmarks for KTU numerical problems:

- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (α)**:
  - Silicon (Si): **α_Si ≈ 2.6 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹**
  - Aluminum (Al): **α_Al ≈ 23 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹**
  - Copper (Cu): **α_Cu ≈ 17 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹**
  - Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS): **α_PDMS ≈ 3.1 × 10⁻⁴ K⁻¹**
  - Polysilicon: **α_poly-Si ≈ 2.8 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹**
  - Water: **α_water ≈ 2.07 × 10⁻⁴ K⁻¹**
- **Stefan–Boltzmann constant**: **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**
- **Standard reference temperature (CTE reference)**: **T₀ = 20 °C = 293.15 K**
- **Typical microscale heater resistance**: **R = 50 – 500 Ω**
- **Typical operating ΔT**: **ΔT = 1 – 200 K**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Physics — The "Why" and "How"

The actuator works in **four logical stages**, and the KTU examiner will expect you to articulate each stage:

1. **Energy Injection Stage (Input)**
   - Electrical current `I` is passed through a microfabricated resistor (e.g., Pt, polysilicon, Ti).
   - Joule heating dissipates power: **P = I²R = V²/R (Watts)**
   - The localized temperature rises, governed by the **thermal time constant** `τ_th = (ρ c_p V) / (h A_s)` where `ρ` is density, `c_p` is specific heat, `V` is volume, `h` is convective heat-transfer coefficient, and `A_s` is surface area.

2. **Thermal Expansion Stage (Energy Conversion)**
   - The heated material wants to expand by: `ΔL = α L₀ ΔT`
   - Because the active layer is mechanically constrained (bonded to a substrate of lower α, or enclosed in a sealed cavity), the *free* expansion is converted into **internal stress** `σ_th = E · α · ΔT` where **E** is Young's modulus.

3. **Mechanical Displacement Stage (Force Generation)**
   - The internal stress produces strain, which deforms the structure (bending, buckling, or diaphragm deflection).
   - The work output per cycle: **W = ½ k x²** where `k` is the structural stiffness and `x` is the tip displacement.

4. **Heat Dissipation Stage (Reset)**
   - When power is removed, the system cools primarily by **conduction** to the substrate and **convection** to the surrounding fluid.
   - The structure returns to its original state when ΔT → 0.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Thermal actuators deliver **high force (mN range) but small displacement (typically < 50 µm)**. They are therefore *complementary* to electrostatic actuators (which give large displacement but small force) and piezoelectric actuators (fast but expensive).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are high-yield — memorize the units and the sign conventions.**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Symbol Meaning | SI Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta L = \alpha \, L_0 \, \Delta T$ | Linear thermal expansion | m | Cantilever tip displacement |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \beta \, \Delta T \approx 3\alpha \, \Delta T$ | Volumetric expansion (β = bulk CTE) | m³ | Thermo-pneumatic pressure |
| 3 | $\sigma_{th} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | Thermal stress (constrained) | Pa | Bimorph force estimation |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \, \Delta T$ | Thermal strain | dimensionless | Free expansion |
| 5 | $\kappa = \dfrac{\Delta T}{P} = \dfrac{1}{G_{th}}$ | Thermal resistance analogy | K/W | Heater sizing |
| 6 | $\tau_{th} = R_{th} \, C_{th} = \dfrac{\rho \, c_p \, V}{h \, A_s}$ | Thermal time constant | s | Switching speed |
| 7 | $x_{tip} = \dfrac{3(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T \, L^2}{t \, \left(1 + \dfrac{E_1 t_1}{E_2 t_2}\right) \left(1 + \dfrac{E_2 t_2}{E_1 t_1}\right)}$ | Timoshenko bimorph tip deflection | m | Bimetallic micro-valve design |
| 8 | $P = \dfrac{n R T}{V}$ (ideal gas) | Thermo-pneumatic cavity pressure | Pa | Bubble-actuated pump |
| 9 | $Q_{joule} = I^2 R \, t$ | Joule heat generated | J | Energy budget per cycle |
| 10 | $F_{block} = \dfrac{E A \alpha \Delta T}{2}$ | Block-force of thermal actuator | N | Static load capacity |

> [!CAUTION]
> **In the formulas above, the symbols `1` and `2` refer to the two layers of a bimorph** (e.g., `α_1` = CTE of low-expansion layer, `α_2` = CTE of high-expansion layer). The `t` in the Timoshenko equation is the **total bimorph thickness** = `t_1 + t_2`.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Microfluidic Lab-on-Chip**: Thermo-pneumatic valves in integrated blood-analysis cartridges (e.g., Abaxis Piccolo system).
- **Drug Delivery**: Shape-memory alloy micro-grippers in endovascular catheters.
- **Inkjet Bio-printing**: Thermal bubble jets ejecting picolitre droplets of cell-laden bio-ink.
- **Optical BioMEMS**: Bimorph mirrors for laser-scanning confocal endoscopes.
- **Wearable Biosensors**: PDMS thermal expansion used in sweat-rate sensing patches.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linear Thermal Expansion from First Principles

We begin with the **empirical definition** of the linear coefficient of thermal expansion. The fractional change in length per unit temperature change, in the limit of infinitesimal ΔT, is defined as:

$$
\alpha(T) = \frac{1}{L_0} \frac{dL}{dT}
$$

Rearranging gives a first-order separable differential equation:

$$
dL = \alpha L_0 \, dT
$$

For an **isotropic material** and **small, uniform ΔT** (the KTU assumption for MEMS-scale problems), α is approximately constant. We integrate both sides from the initial state $(L_0, T_0)$ to the final state $(L_f, T_f)$:

$$
\int_{L_0}^{L_f} dL = \alpha L_0 \int_{T_0}^{T_f} dT
$$

Performing the integration step-by-step:

$$
L_f - L_0 = \alpha L_0 (T_f - T_0)
$$

Recognising that $\Delta L = L_f - L_0$ and $\Delta T = T_f - T_0$:

$$
\boxed{\Delta L = \alpha \, L_0 \, \Delta T}
$$

**Physical interpretation**: A 1 mm long polysilicon micro-beam (α ≈ 2.8 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹) heated by ΔT = 100 K elongates by **0.28 µm** — too small for a valve, but **enormous as a strain signal** when amplified by a long bimorph lever.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Timoshenko Bimorph Tip Deflection

Consider two bonded strips (layer 1 = low-α, layer 2 = high-α) of length `L`, widths `w`, and thicknesses `t_1`, `t_2`. The high-α layer wants to expand more than the low-α layer, so the composite **bends into an arc of radius `R`**.

The **radius of curvature** due to differential thermal expansion is given by the Timoshenko bimetallic-strip equation:

$$
\frac{1}{R} = \frac{6(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)\Delta T \, (1+m)^2}{t \, \left[ 3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn)\left(m^2 + \dfrac{1}{mn}\right) \right]}
$$

where the dimensionless parameters are:

$$
m = \frac{t_1}{t_2} \quad \text{and} \quad n = \frac{E_1}{E_2}
$$

and `t = t_1 + t_2` is the total thickness.

For a **cantilever beam clamped at one end and free at the other**, the tip deflection (small-angle approximation) is:

$$
x_{tip} = \frac{L^2}{2R}
$$

Substituting the curvature expression:

$$
\boxed{x_{tip} = \frac{3(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)\Delta T \, L^2}{t \, \left( 1 + \dfrac{E_1 t_1}{E_2 t_2} \right) \left( 1 + \dfrac{E_2 t_2}{E_1 t_1} \right)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key engineering takeaway**: Tip deflection scales as **L² / t** — doubling the beam length quadruples the deflection, while doubling the thickness halves it. KTU exam problems almost always use the **L² scaling** to test conceptual understanding.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermo-pneumatic Cavity Pressure

Consider a sealed micro-cavity of initial volume `V_0` filled with an ideal gas at pressure `P_0` and temperature `T_0`. Heat the cavity by ΔT, and assuming the **volume is mechanically constrained** (rigid walls):

From the ideal gas law, $P V = n R T$ (using the universal gas constant **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**). Differentiating at constant `n` and `V`:

$$
\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{dT}{T}
$$

Integrating from $(P_0, T_0)$ to $(P_f, T_f)$:

$$
\int_{P_0}^{P_f} \frac{dP}{P} = \int_{T_0}^{T_f} \frac{dT}{T}
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{P_f}{P_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{T_f}{T_0}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{P_f = P_0 \cdot \frac{T_f}{T_0} = P_0 \left( 1 + \frac{\Delta T}{T_0} \right)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the governing equation for thermo-pneumatic micro-valves.** A cavity at atmospheric pressure (P₀ = 101.325 kPa) and T₀ = 300 K, when heated by ΔT = 100 K, experiences a pressure rise of **ΔP = 33.8 kPa** — sufficient to deflect a thin PDMS diaphragm by tens of microns.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Device Selection Guide

| Design Requirement | Best Thermal Actuator | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Large force (> 1 mN), small displacement | **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** | Phase-change gives 4–8% strain at high stress |
| Fast response (< 1 ms) | **Bimorph / Bimetallic** | Low thermal mass, high thermal conductivity |
| Low voltage (< 5 V) operation | **Thermal Bubble (Inkjet)** | Resistive heating needs only ~1 µJ per droplet |
| Biocompatible, soft-tissue contact | **PDMS Thermo-pneumatic** | Polymer is bio-inert, large CTE |
| High force *and* high displacement | **Multi-stage cascaded bimorph** | Each stage amplifies displacement |
| Single-use, disposable | **Paraffin phase-change actuator** | Melts once, locks open/closed |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Thermal Expansion Actuators

The following Mermaid block maps the **taxonomy of thermal actuators** encountered in BioMEMS. All node IDs are alphanumeric, and labels contain only plain uppercase text (no markdown formatting inside the quotes).

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[THERMAL EXPANSION ACTUATORS]
    rootA --> typeB1[BIMORPH BIMETALLIC]
    rootA --> typeB2[THERMO PNEUMATIC]
    rootA --> typeB3[SHAPE MEMORY ALLOY]
    rootA --> typeB4[THERMAL BUBBLE JET]
    rootA --> typeB5[PHASE CHANGE PARAFFIN]

    typeB1 --> matC1[SILICON GOLD]
    typeB1 --> matC2[POLYSILICON ALUMINUM]
    typeB1 --> matC3[PDMS GLASS]

    typeB2 --> appD1[MICRO VALVE]
    typeB2 --> appD2[MICRO PUMP]

    typeB3 --> appE1[MICRO GRIPPER]
    typeB3 --> appE2[STENT ACTUATOR]

    typeB4 --> appF1[INKJET PRINTHEAD]
    typeB4 --> appF2[DROPLET DISPENSER]

    typeB5 --> appG1[ONE SHOT VALVE]
    typeB5 --> appG2[DRUG RELEASE VALVE]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bimorph Micro-Valve

The following block diagram traces the **signal flow** from electrical input to fluidic output in a representative BioMEMS micro-valve.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[DC VOLTAGE SOURCE V] --> resB[MICRO HEATER PLATINUM RESISTOR R]
    resB --> jcC[JOULE HEATING P EQUALS I SQUARED R]
    jcC --> tdD[THERMAL EXPANSION OF HIGH ALPHA LAYER]
    tdD --> bmE[BIMORPH BENDING TOWARD LOW ALPHA LAYER]
    bmE --> dispF[TIP DISPLACEMENT X TIP MICROMETRES]
    dispF --> memG[FLEXIBLE PDMS DIAPHRAGM DEFLECTS]
    memG --> valveH[MICRO VALVE OPENS OR CLOSES]
    valveH --> outI[FLUID FLOW CONTROLLED]
    resB --> cdJ[CONDUCTION TO SUBSTRATE COOLING]
    cdJ --> rtK[STRUCTURE RESETS TO INITIAL STATE]
```

### 4.3 Operational State Machine of a Thermo-pneumatic Pump

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    sClosed[CLOSED STATE T EQUALS T0]
    sHeating[HEATING STATE POWER ON]
    sExpanding[EXPANDING STATE GAS PRESSURE RISES]
    sOpen[OPEN STATE VALVE LIFTS]
    sCooling[COOLING STATE POWER OFF]
    sClosed --> sHeating : Apply voltage
    sHeating --> sExpanding : Delta T above threshold
    sExpanding --> sOpen : Pressure exceeds diaphragm stiffness
    sOpen --> sCooling : Remove voltage
    sCooling --> sClosed : Temperature returns to T0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation tip for KTU viva:** In a state diagram, *each transition arrow* is an engineering event that the examiner can question. Be ready to define the **threshold ΔT** and the **diaphragm stiffness k** for each transition.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**

**Q1. Define the Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE). Mention the CTE values of silicon and PDMS, and explain why PDMS is preferred for thermo-pneumatic micro-valves.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE), denoted α, is defined as the **fractional change in length of a material per unit change in temperature**, with SI units of K⁻¹:
>
> $$\alpha = \frac{1}{L_0} \frac{\Delta L}{\Delta T}$$
>
> Standard values at 300 K are: **α_Si ≈ 2.6 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹** and **α_PDMS ≈ 3.1 × 10⁻⁴ K⁻¹**.
>
> PDMS has a CTE that is **roughly 100× greater than silicon**. This means that for the same ΔT, a PDMS diaphragm expands approximately 100 times more than a silicon diaphragm, producing a much larger mechanical deflection. Additionally, PDMS is **biocompatible, optically transparent, and elastomeric**, making it the preferred material for thermo-pneumatic micro-valves in BioMEMS applications such as organ-on-chip devices and wearable biosensors. **(3 marks — 1 mark each for definition, values, and justification.)**

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**Q2. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a thermal bubble jet actuator used in bio-printing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A thermal bubble jet actuator operates in **four sequential stages**:
>
> 1. **Electrical input**: A short current pulse (typically 1–3 µs) is applied to a thin-film micro-heater (Pt or TaN resistor) patterned on a silicon substrate.
>
> 2. **Joule heating**: The heater temperature rises by 200–400 °C in microseconds, vaporising a thin layer of the bio-ink fluid in contact with the heater.
>
> 3. **Bubble nucleation & expansion**: A vapour bubble nucleates and expands rapidly, creating a pressure pulse of 1–10 MPa in the micro-nozzle.
>
> 4. **Droplet ejection**: The pressure pulse ejects a picolitre-to-nanolitre droplet of bio-ink through the nozzle orifice. When the pulse ends, the bubble collapses and the nozzle refills by capillary action.
>
> This mechanism is the working principle of thermal inkjet bio-printers (e.g., Hewlett-Packard DeskJet technology adapted for cell printing), enabling high-throughput, low-cost deposition of biomolecules and even living cells. **(3 marks — 1.5 marks for stages, 1.5 marks for bio-printing application context.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the governing equation for the tip deflection of a bimetallic cantilever beam of length `L` subjected to a uniform temperature rise `ΔT`, starting from the curvature formula. Clearly state the role of the **modular ratio** `n = E₁/E₂` and the **thickness ratio** `m = t₁/t₂`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bimorph cantilever is fabricated from a 200 µm long, 50 µm wide polysilicon beam (E₁ = 160 GPa, α₁ = 2.8 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹, t₁ = 1 µm) bonded to a 1 µm thick gold layer (E₂ = 78 GPa, α₂ = 14 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹, t₂ = 1 µm). Calculate the **tip deflection** when the heater raises the temperature by ΔT = 100 K. Compare the result with a pure-silicon cantilever of the same geometry. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the bimetallic strip and the Timoshenko curvature. The differential expansion `Δα = α₂ - α₁` would, in an unbonded state, produce a length mismatch of `ΔL = Δα · L · ΔT`. Because the layers are bonded, the system accommodates this mismatch by bending into an arc of radius `R`.
>
> **Step 2 (2 marks):** Using force and moment equilibrium across the bimorph cross-section, and assuming no net axial force or external moment, the curvature is derived as:
>
> $$
> \frac{1}{R} = \frac{6 \Delta\alpha \Delta T (1+m)^2}{t \left[ 3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn)\left(m^2 + \frac{1}{mn}\right) \right]}
> $$
>
> where `t = t₁ + t₂`, `m = t₁/t₂`, and `n = E₁/E₂`.
>
> **Step 3 (2 marks):** For a cantilever beam (clamped-free), the small-angle tip deflection is `x_tip = L²/(2R)`. Substituting:
>
> $$
> \boxed{x_{tip} = \frac{3 \Delta\alpha \Delta T \, L^2}{t \left( 1 + \frac{E_1 t_1}{E_2 t_2} \right) \left( 1 + \frac{E_2 t_2}{E_1 t_1} \right)}}
> $$
>
> **Step 4 (2 marks):** **Role of `m` and `n`**: The thickness ratio `m` controls how the thermal-mismatch strain is partitioned between the two layers. A larger `m` (thicker low-α layer) increases the bending stiffness asymmetrically, *reducing* the curvature. The modular ratio `n` enters because the *effective* stiffness of each layer is `E_i t_i`; if the high-α layer is much stiffer, it resists bending, *reducing* deflection. **Engineering design rule**: for maximum deflection, choose a *compliant* high-α layer bonded to a *stiff* low-α layer, with `t₁ ≈ t₂`.

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the parameters. `L = 200 µm = 2 × 10⁻⁴ m`, `t₁ = t₂ = 1 µm`, so `t = 2 µm = 2 × 10⁻⁶ m`. `m = t₁/t₂ = 1`. `n = E₁/E₂ = 160/78 ≈ 2.051`. `Δα = (14 - 2.8) × 10⁻⁶ = 11.2 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹`. `ΔT = 100 K`.
>
> **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the denominator terms:
>
> $$
> \left( 1 + \frac{E_1 t_1}{E_2 t_2} \right) = 1 + \frac{160 \times 1}{78 \times 1} = 1 + 2.051 = 3.051
> $$
>
> $$
> \left( 1 + \frac{E_2 t_2}{E_1 t_1} \right) = 1 + \frac{1}{2.051} = 1.488
> $$
>
> **Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply the bimorph tip-deflection equation:
>
> $$
> x_{tip} = \frac{3 \times (11.2 \times 10^{-6}) \times 100 \times (2 \times 10^{-4})^2}{(2 \times 10^{-6}) \times 3.051 \times 1.488}
> $$
>
> $$
> x_{tip} = \frac{3 \times 1.12 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}{9.080 \times 10^{-6}}
> $$
>
> $$
> x_{tip} = \frac{1.344 \times 10^{-10}}{9.080 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.48 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = \boxed{14.8 \text{ µm}}
> $$
>
> **Step 4 (2 marks):** **Comparison with pure-silicon cantilever**: For a pure-Si beam, `ΔL = α_Si · L · ΔT = 2.8 × 10⁻⁶ × 2 × 10⁻⁴ × 100 = 5.6 × 10⁻⁸ m = 56 nm`. This is a **straight elongation** of 56 nm, with no bending (the beam has no second layer to constrain it). The bimorph, in contrast, produces **14.8 µm of tip deflection** — a **~264× amplification** because the beam bends and the *tip* sweeps an arc. This is why bimorphs are the workhorse of BioMEMS micro-valves.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled diagram, explain the principle of operation of a **thermo-pneumatic micro-pump**. Derive the expression for the pressure rise in a sealed gas cavity subjected to a temperature rise ΔT, assuming ideal-gas behaviour. State **two advantages** and **two limitations** of thermo-pneumatic actuation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sealed PDMS micro-cavity of volume 5 mm³ initially contains air at 1 atm and 293 K. A thin-film Pt heater raises the cavity temperature uniformly to 373 K. Calculate (i) the final pressure in kPa, and (ii) the percentage change in pressure. If the cavity is connected to a 10 µm thick PDMS diaphragm of effective stiffness k = 0.5 N/m, estimate the **centre deflection** of the diaphragm assuming small-deflection linear elastic behaviour. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks

> **Step 1 (2 marks):** **Working principle and diagram description.** A thermo-pneumatic micro-pump consists of (i) a sealed gas-filled cavity bounded by a flexible diaphragm, (ii) a micro-heater embedded in or beneath the cavity wall, and (iii) inlet and outlet micro-channels with one-way valves (or diffuser/nozzle elements). When the heater is activated, the gas in the cavity expands, raising the cavity pressure and deflecting the diaphragm outward. This displaces fluid through the outlet valve. On cooling, the diaphragm relaxes, drawing in fresh fluid through the inlet valve — completing one pumping cycle.
>
> **Step 2 (3 marks):** **Derivation of pressure rise.** From the ideal gas law at constant volume and moles:
>
> $$
> \frac{P}{T} = \text{constant} \Rightarrow P_f = P_0 \cdot \frac{T_f}{T_0} = P_0 \left( 1 + \frac{\Delta T}{T_0} \right)
> $$
>
> **Step 3 (2 marks):** **Two advantages:** (1) High force output (mN range) at low voltages (3–12 V). (2) Large diaphragm displacement (10–100 µm) due to the high CTE of gases and polymers. **Two limitations:** (1) Slow response (thermal time constant typically 10 ms – 1 s) due to thermal mass. (2) Heat dissipation into the working fluid can damage temperature-sensitive biological samples.

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks

> **Step 1 (2 marks):** **Final pressure calculation.**
>
> Given: P₀ = 101.325 kPa, T₀ = 293 K, T_f = 373 K, ΔT = 80 K.
>
> $$
> P_f = 101.325 \times \frac{373}{293} = 101.325 \times 1.2730 = 128.99 \text{ kPa}
> $$
>
> **Step 2 (1 mark):** **Percentage change in pressure:**
>
> $$
> \frac{\Delta P}{P_0} \times 100\% = \frac{128.99 - 101.325}{101.325} \times 100\% = \boxed{27.3\%}
> $$
>
> **Step 3 (2 marks):** **Diaphragm deflection.** The gauge pressure driving the diaphragm is:
>
> $$
> \Delta P = P_f - P_0 = 128.99 - 101.325 = 27.66 \text{ kPa} = 27{,}660 \text{ Pa}
> $$
>
> **Step 4 (2 marks):** Using the small-deflection relation for a clamped circular diaphragm, the centre deflection is approximately:
>
> $$
> x_{centre} \approx \frac{\Delta P \cdot A_{eff}}{k}
> $$
>
> where `A_eff` is the effective diaphragm area. For a 10 µm thick PDMS diaphragm of radius `a = 500 µm` (a typical value to be assumed):
>
> $$
> A_{eff} = \pi a^2 = \pi (500 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2
> $$
>
> $$
> x_{centre} = \frac{27{,}660 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-7}}{0.5} = \frac{0.02173}{0.5} = 0.0435 \text{ m}
> $$
>
> This unrealistically large value indicates the linear approximation breaks down. For more realistic small deflections (≤ 10 µm), the gauge pressure must be lower or the diaphragm area smaller. **The KTU-marking takeaway:** Always state your assumption (small-deflection vs large-deflection regime) and check whether the result is physically consistent. **(7 marks — 2 + 1 + 2 + 2)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up α (linear CTE) and β (volumetric CTE).** The KTU paper will *not* accept `β ≈ 3α` for solids unless explicitly justified by the assumption of isotropy.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert µm to metres.** A 200 µm beam entered as `L = 200` instead of `2 × 10⁻⁴ m` in the bimorph equation produces a deflection off by 8 orders of magnitude. Always unit-check.
> 3. **Writing Timoshenko's equation without defining `m` and `n`.** Examiners deduct at least 1 mark for undefined symbols.
> 4. **Confusing thermal *time constant* with thermal *resistance*.** They are related (`τ = R_th · C_th`) but are not the same quantity.
> 5. **Omitting the energy-balance discussion.** Thermal actuators are inefficient (~1–5% energy-to-work conversion). The KTU rubric now includes an *energy efficiency* line item, as per the 2024 NEP-aligned outcome-based assessment.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Actuators Based on Thermal Expansion):**
>
> - ☐ **CTE definition**: $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{L_0} \dfrac{\Delta L}{\Delta T}$, units **K⁻¹**. Remember: Si ≈ 2.6×10⁻⁶, PDMS ≈ 3.1×10⁻⁴.
> - ☐ **Linear expansion formula**: $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ (free beam).
> - ☐ **Thermal stress (constrained)**: $\sigma = E \alpha \Delta T$.
> - ☐ **Bimorph tip deflection (Timoshenko)**: scales as **L² / t** — design rule of thumb for KTU problems.
> - ☐ **Thermo-pneumatic pressure**: $P_f = P_0 (1 + \Delta T / T_0)$ — for ideal gas at constant V.
> - ☐ **Bimorph materials pairs**: Si/Au, Poly-Si/Al, PDMS/Glass. **Mismatched α is the key.**
> - ☐ **Thermal bubble jet**: 4 stages — heat, vaporise, expand, eject. Used in bio-printing.
> - ☐ **Shape Memory Alloy**: Austenite ↔ Martensite phase change. High force, recoverable strain 4–8%.
> - ☐ **Thermal time constant** $\tau = R_{th} C_{th}$ governs switching speed; small `τ` → fast actuation.
> - ☐ **Trade-off matrix**: Thermal actuators = **high force, low displacement, slow response**.
> - ☐ **Application memory**: thermo-pneumatic valves (Abaxis), bubble jets (HP bio-printers), SMA stents (endovascular), paraffin one-shot valves (disposable LoC).
> - ☐ **Examiner hot-button**: always convert **µm → m** before plugging into the Timoshenko equation.
> - ☐ **Energy efficiency caveat**: thermal actuators dissipate most input power as heat — a design constraint in BioMEMS for biological samples.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Thermocouples

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermocouple (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A thermocouple is a **passive, contactless, solid-state temperature transducer** consisting of two dissimilar electrical conductors (thermoelements A and B) joined at one end to form a *measuring (hot) junction*, while their other ends form a *reference (cold) junction* maintained at a known temperature. The temperature difference between the two junctions generates a continuous, measurable **Seebeck electromotive force (EMF)** in the millivolt range, which is a function of the junction materials and the temperature gradient.

In the specific context of **BioMEMS and Microfluidics**, thermocouples are realized as **thin-film or wire-bonded micro-thermoelements** integrated directly onto the silicon/glass/polymer substrate of a lab-on-chip (LoC). They are used to monitor localized temperature within microchannels, reaction chambers (e.g., PCR chambers), cell-culture wells, and cryogenic bio-storage volumes.

### 1.2 The Three Foundational Thermoelectric Effects

A thermocouple is fundamentally governed by three reversible thermoelectric phenomena, all of which are interrelated through the **Kelvin (Onsager) reciprocal relations**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Thermoelectric Pillars**
>
> 1. **Seebeck Effect (1821, Thomas Johann Seebeck):** Direct conversion of a temperature gradient into an EMF.
> $$\text{EMF} = V = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} \left[S_B(T) - S_A(T)\right]\,dT$$
>
> 2. **Peltier Effect (1834, Jean Charles Athanase Peltier):** Reversible heating/cooling at a junction when an electric current is forced through two dissimilar conductors.
> $$\dot{Q}_{Peltier} = \pi_{AB}\cdot I$$
>
> 3. **Thomson Effect (1851, William Thomson / Lord Kelvin):** Reversible heating/cooling along a single current-carrying conductor subjected to a temperature gradient.
> $$\dot{Q}_{Thomson} = \tau \cdot I \cdot \Delta T$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Band Rubber-Band Thermometer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Beginners:**
> Imagine two rubber bands of different elasticity tied together at one end (the *hot junction*) and stretched across a table. If you heat the tied end, the two bands expand at *different rates* because they are made of different materials. This unequal expansion "pushes" electrons unevenly through the wires, generating a tiny voltage. The cooler end of the assembly is the *cold junction*, and the voltage measured between the free ends is proportional to how hot the tied end is.
>
> **BioMEMS Analogy:** In a microfluidic PCR chip, the reaction chamber is the *hot junction*, the bond pads at the chip edge are the *cold junction*, and the copper traces leading to the off-chip amplifier are the "rubber bands." As the thermal cycler heats the chamber, the two dissimilar metal films (e.g., Chromel–Alumel) push electrons asymmetrically, producing a millivolt signal that the controller reads to regulate the heater.

### 1.4 Why Thermocouples are Critical in BioMEMS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — BioMEMS Relevance**
> - **Fast thermal response** (time constant in microseconds for thin-film TC): essential for real-time PCR thermal cycling (20–40 cycles/min).
> - **Small junction size** (down to ~10 µm): compatible with high-density microfluidic arrays.
> - **Wide temperature range:** Type K covers **-200 °C to +1350 °C**, suitable for cryobiology to polymerase chain reactions.
> - **Self-powered:** No external excitation required (unlike RTDs or thermistors).
> - **CMOS-compatible fabrication:** Polysilicon–Aluminum, polysilicon–gold, and Chromel–Alumel thin films can be deposited via sputtering or e-beam evaporation.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Reference Junction Temp | **0** (or **25**) | °C |
| Standard Seebeck Coeff. (Type K) | **~40.45** | µV/°C |
| Standard Seebeck Coeff. (Type T) | **~38.74** | µV/°C |
| Standard Seebeck Coeff. (Type E) | **~58.66** | µV/°C |
| Standard Seebeck Coeff. (Type R/S) | **~6–10** | µV/°C |
| Standard Cold-Junction Accuracy | **±0.1 to ±0.5** | °C |
| BioMEMS Thin-Film Junction Size | **10–100** | µm |
| Typical Output EMF | **0–80** | mV |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a Thermocouple Works

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Logical Operating Steps (Exam-Worthy)**
>
> **Step 1 — Hot Junction Formation:** Two dissimilar thermoelements (A and B) are welded, soldered, or co-deposited at a point. In BioMEMS, this is typically a photolithographically defined contact pad.
>
> **Step 2 — Establishment of Thermal Gradient:** The hot junction is exposed to the unknown temperature $T_{hot}$, while the cold (reference) junction is held at $T_{ref}$ (commonly 0 °C, or electronically compensated to 25 °C).
>
> **Step 3 — Charge Carrier Diffusion:** Free electrons in the hotter end gain kinetic energy and diffuse toward the cooler end. Because materials A and B have different carrier concentrations and effective masses, the diffusion fluxes are unequal.
>
> **Step 4 — EMF Generation:** The unequal diffusion creates a net contact potential at the junctions. An equilibrium open-circuit voltage $V_{AB}$ develops.
>
> **Step 5 — Signal Conditioning:** The mV-level signal is amplified, cold-junction compensated, linearized (via polynomial NIST tables or look-up tables in firmware), and converted to a digital temperature reading.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU ESE Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Seebeck EMF (Integral form)** | $V_{AB} = \displaystyle\int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} \left[S_B(T) - S_A(T)\right]\,dT$ | V (volts); $S$ in V/K |
| 2 | **Linearized Seebeck Equation** | $V_{AB} \approx \alpha_{AB}\,\Delta T$ | $\alpha_{AB} = S_B - S_A$ in µV/°C |
| 3 | **Peltier Heat Rate** | $\dot{Q}_\pi = \pi_{AB}\cdot I$ | W; $\pi$ in V |
| 4 | **Thomson Heat Rate** | $\dot{Q}_\tau = \tau \cdot I \cdot \Delta T$ | W; $\tau$ in V/K |
| 5 | **Kelvin Relation 1 (Peltier–Seebeck)** | $\pi_{AB} = T \cdot \alpha_{AB}$ | Derivable from thermodynamics |
| 6 | **Kelvin Relation 2 (Thomson)** | $\tau_A - \tau_B = T\,\dfrac{d\alpha_{AB}}{dT}$ | Relates Thomson coeff. to $d\alpha/dT$ |
| 7 | **Law of Intermediate Metals** | $V_{ABC}(T_1, T_2) = V_{AB}(T_1, T_2)$ iff the 3rd-metal junctions are isothermal | Enables use of solder/connector metals |
| 8 | **Law of Intermediate Temperatures** | $V_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = V_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + V_{AB}(T_2, T_3)$ | Enables calibration by summation |
| 9 | **Polynomial Calibration (NIST)** | $V = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{n} c_i\,(T)^{i}$ | Typical $n = 8$ to 14 |
| 10 | **Inverse Polynomial (ITS-90)** | $T = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{n} d_i\,(V)^{i}$ | Used in cold-junction firmware |
| 11 | **Cold-Junction Compensation** | $T_{hot} = T_{measured} + (T_{ref,\text{actual}} - T_{ref,\text{nominal}})$ | Software or hardware CJC |
| 12 | **Sensitivity Limit (BioMEMS)** | $\dfrac{dV}{dT} = \alpha_{AB}$ | µV/°C |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall — Pipe Symbol Trap:** When copying the table above into your answer booklet, do **not** use the vertical bar `|` for absolute values or interval notation. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX, or write "absolute value of x" in plain text. KTU examiners often deduct marks for broken table formatting in scanned answer sheets.

## 2.3 Classification of Thermocouples (ISA / ANSI Standard Coding)

| ISA Code | + Leg | - Leg | Range (°C) | $\alpha$ (µV/°C) | BioMEMS Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type K** | Chromel (Ni-Cr) | Alumel (Ni-Al) | -200 to +1350 | ~40.45 | General lab/PCR |
| **Type J** | Iron | Constantan (Cu-Ni) | -40 to +750 | ~50.37 | Reduced atmosphere |
| **Type T** | Copper | Constantan | -250 to +350 | ~38.74 | Cryobiology |
| **Type E** | Chromel | Constantan | -200 to +900 | ~58.66 | Highest sensitivity |
| **Type N** | Nicrosil (Ni-Cr-Si) | Nisil (Ni-Si-Mg) | -200 to +1300 | ~38.61 | High-T stability |
| **Type R** | Platinum | Pt/Rh (13%) | 0 to +1480 | ~6–10 | Reference standard |
| **Type S** | Platinum | Pt/Rh (10%) | 0 to +1480 | ~6–10 | Reference standard |
| **Type B** | Pt/Rh (30%) | Pt/Rh (6%) | 0 to +1700 | ~3–8 | High-T furnaces |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial / BioMEMS Deployment Examples**
> - **Real-Time PCR Chips:** Chromel-Alumel thin-film thermocouples monitor denaturation (95 °C), annealing (55 °C), and extension (72 °C) cycles.
> - **Organ-on-Chip Platforms:** Localized temperature feedback for maintaining 37 °C ± 0.1 °C at cell-culture microchambers.
> - **Cryopreservation MEMS:** Type T (Cu-Constantan) for liquid-nitrogen range (–196 °C).
> - **Point-of-Care Diagnostics:** Disposable paper-microfluidic chips with screen-printed thermocouples for fever screening.
> - **Calibration Baths in Biomedical Labs:** Type R/S as secondary standards traceable to NIST ITS-90.

## 2.5 Why the "Why" and "How" Matter

- **Why three effects?** The Seebeck effect is the *measurement principle*, Peltier is the *thermoelectric cooler principle* (used in TECs for PCR), and Thomson is the *internal correction* that makes the Kelvin relations self-consistent.
- **How BioMEMS gains advantage?** By shrinking the junction to the micron scale, the thermal mass is reduced, the response time drops to sub-millisecond, and the temperature of a single microdroplet can be sensed without perturbing it.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Seebeck EMF Equation (Integral Form)

**Starting Principle (Seebeck Observation):**
If two dissimilar metals A and B are joined at a hot junction at temperature $T_{hot}$ and have their open ends at the reference temperature $T_{ref}$, the net EMF developed is the line integral of the differential Seebeck coefficient along the closed thermoelectric loop.

$$
V_{AB} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} S_B(T)\,dT - \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} S_A(T)\,dT
$$

**Step-by-Step Expansion:**

$$
V_{AB} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} S_B(T)\,dT + \int_{T_{hot}}^{T_{ref}} S_A(T)\,dT
$$

**Logic:** The first term represents the EMF contribution as we traverse metal B from cold to hot; the second term represents the contribution of metal A from hot back to cold (sign convention: voltage is referenced to metal A).

$$
V_{AB} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} \left[S_B(T) - S_A(T)\right]\,dT
$$

**Defining the Relative Seebeck Coefficient:**

$$
\alpha_{AB}(T) \triangleq S_B(T) - S_A(T)
$$

**Therefore:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{AB} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} \alpha_{AB}(T)\,dT\,}
$$

**Linear Approximation (valid for narrow temperature spans typical in BioMEMS, e.g., 0–100 °C):**

$$
V_{AB} \approx \alpha_{AB}^{\text{avg}} \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{ref})
$$

**Practical Example — Type K at $T_{hot}=100$ °C, $T_{ref}=0$ °C:**

$$
V_{AB} \approx 40.45\,\mu V/°C \times 100\,°C = 4045\,\mu V \approx 4.05\,mV
$$

This matches the NIST ITS-90 reference value of **4.096 mV** within 1 %, confirming the linear approximation's validity in this range.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kelvin Relation 1 (Peltier–Seebeck Linkage)

**Starting Point — Thermodynamic Reversibility:**
For a reversible thermocouple operating in equilibrium, the Seebeck voltage equals the change in Gibbs free energy per unit charge transported:

$$
V_{AB} = -\frac{\Delta G}{q}
$$

The Peltier heat absorbed at the hot junction per unit charge is:

$$
\pi_{AB} = -\frac{q_{Peltier}}{q}
$$

**Energy Balance on the Junction:**

The charge carrier absorbs Peltier heat $\pi_{AB}$ at the hot junction. The entropy change per unit charge is $\Delta S = \pi_{AB} / T_{hot}$.

**Reversibility Condition:**
For a reversible thermoelectric process, the change in Gibbs free energy equals $T \cdot \Delta S$:

$$
V_{AB} = T \cdot \frac{\pi_{AB}}{T} = \pi_{AB} \cdot \frac{1}{1}
$$

**Proper Derivation (Clausius Definition):**
Define Seebeck coefficient as entropy transported per unit charge:

$$
\alpha_{AB} = \frac{\pi_{AB}}{T}
$$

**Solving for Peltier Coefficient:**

$$
\boxed{\,\pi_{AB}(T) = T \cdot \alpha_{AB}(T)\,}
$$

**Numerical Check — Type K at 300 K:**
With $\alpha_{AB} \approx 40.45\,\mu V/K$:

$$
\pi_{AB} = 300 \times 40.45 \times 10^{-6} = 12.135\,mV
$$

This is a measurable value used in Peltier-stage modeling of thermoelectric coolers in PCR instrumentation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kelvin Relation 2 (Thomson–Seebeck Linkage)

**Starting Point — Entropy Conservation in a Continuous Conductor:**
A single homogeneous conductor carrying current $I$ in a temperature gradient absorbs/releases Thomson heat:

$$
\dot{Q}_\tau = \tau \cdot I \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Thermodynamic Argument:**
The Thomson coefficient arises from the temperature dependence of the Seebeck coefficient. If $\alpha_{AB}$ varies with $T$, the energy imbalance must be compensated by Thomson heat.

**Differential Energy Balance:**

Consider two conductors A and B in series. The rate of change of Peltier heat with temperature (per unit length) must equal the net Thomson heat:

$$
\frac{d\pi_{AB}}{dT} = \frac{\pi_{AB}}{T} + (\tau_A - \tau_B)
$$

**Substituting Relation 1** ($\pi_{AB} = T\alpha_{AB}$):

$$
\frac{d(T\alpha_{AB})}{dT} = \frac{T\alpha_{AB}}{T} + (\tau_A - \tau_B)
$$

**Expanding Left Side (Product Rule):**

$$
\alpha_{AB} + T \cdot \frac{d\alpha_{AB}}{dT} = \alpha_{AB} + (\tau_A - \tau_B)
$$

**Cancelling $\alpha_{AB}$ on Both Sides:**

$$
T \cdot \frac{d\alpha_{AB}}{dT} = \tau_A - \tau_B
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,\tau_A - \tau_B = T\,\frac{d\alpha_{AB}}{dT}\,}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Law of Intermediate Metals

**Statement:** If a third metal C is introduced into the thermocouple circuit, the net EMF is unchanged provided the two new junctions (C–A and C–B) are at the same temperature.

**Proof by Entropy Argument:**
The Seebeck EMF around any closed loop is the integral of $\alpha \, dT$. If C is isothermal, the EMF contributions from C–A and C–B at the same temperature cancel because:

$$
V_{CA}(T) + V_{CB}(T) = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} (\alpha_C - \alpha_A)\,dT + \int_{T}^{T_{ref}} (\alpha_C - \alpha_B)\,dT
$$

**Simplification:**

$$
V_{CA} + V_{CB} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \alpha_C\,dT - \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \alpha_A\,dT + \int_{T}^{T_{ref}} \alpha_C\,dT - \int_{T}^{T_{ref}} \alpha_B\,dT
$$

**Rearranging (the $\alpha_C$ integrals cancel because the limits are opposite):**

$$
V_{CA} + V_{CB} = -\int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \alpha_A\,dT - \int_{T}^{T_{ref}} \alpha_B\,dT = -\int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \alpha_A\,dT + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \alpha_B\,dT
$$

**Therefore:**

$$
V_{CA} + V_{CB} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} (\alpha_B - \alpha_A)\,dT = V_{AB}(T_{ref}, T)
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{ABC}(T_{ref}, T) = V_{AB}(T_{ref}, T)\,}
$$

**Practical Implication:** This is why solder, copper lead-wires, and connector metals do not affect the reading — the critical condition is that the intermediate-metal junctions remain isothermal.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Law of Intermediate Temperatures

**Statement:** The EMF generated by a thermocouple between temperatures $T_1$ and $T_3$ equals the sum of the EMFs generated between $T_1$–$T_2$ and $T_2$–$T_3$.

**Proof:**

$$
V_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = \int_{T_1}^{T_3} \alpha_{AB}(T)\,dT
$$

**Splitting the Integral at $T_2$:**

$$
\int_{T_1}^{T_3} \alpha_{AB}(T)\,dT = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \alpha_{AB}(T)\,dT + \int_{T_2}^{T_3} \alpha_{AB}(T)\,dT
$$

**Therefore:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = V_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + V_{AB}(T_2, T_3)\,}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
For a Type K thermocouple, if $V(0\,°C, 100\,°C) = 4.096\,mV$ and $V(100\,°C, 200\,°C) = 8.138\,mV$, then:

$$
V(0\,°C, 200\,°C) = 4.096 + 8.138 = 12.234\,mV
$$

This matches the NIST reference value of **12.209 mV** within 0.2 %, demonstrating the law's validity.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Cold-Junction Compensation (Software Method)

**Problem:** Modern data acquisition systems do not use an ice-bath cold junction. Instead, the cold junction temperature $T_{CJ}$ is measured by a separate sensor (e.g., silicon diode or thermistor), and the EMF is mathematically corrected.

**Algorithm:**

**Step 1:** Measure actual cold-junction temperature $T_{CJ}$ with secondary sensor.

**Step 2:** Convert $T_{CJ}$ to equivalent thermocouple EMF using inverse polynomial:

$$
V_{CJ} = \sum_{i=0}^{n} d_i (T_{CJ})^i
$$

**Step 3:** Measure total loop EMF $V_{measured}$ between hot junction and actual cold junction.

**Step 4:** Add the cold-junction EMF to reference the reading to 0 °C:

$$
V_{corrected} = V_{measured} + V_{CJ}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert $V_{corrected}$ to hot-junction temperature using forward polynomial:

$$
T_{hot} = \sum_{i=0}^{n} c_i (V_{corrected})^i
$$

**Example — Type K:** If $T_{CJ} = 25\,°C$, $V_{CJ}(25\,°C) = 1.000\,mV$ (from NIST tables), and measured $V_{measured} = 3.000\,mV$:

$$
V_{corrected} = 3.000 + 1.000 = 4.000\,mV \Rightarrow T_{hot} \approx 98.3\,°C
$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *KTU Part A requires concise answers in 3–5 sentences, often with one supporting equation or diagram. Model answers below are calibrated to score the full 3 marks.*

### Q1. Define the Seebeck effect. How is it exploited in a thermocouple-based BioMEMS temperature sensor? `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Seebeck effect is the direct conversion of a temperature gradient into an electromotive force (EMF) at the junction of two dissimilar conductors. In a BioMEMS temperature sensor, two thin-film thermoelements (e.g., Chromel–Alumel, ~200 nm) are patterned on a silicon substrate adjacent to a microfluidic chamber. When the chamber is heated (e.g., during PCR denaturation at 95 °C), the temperature difference between the hot junction (inside the chamber) and the cold junction (on the isothermal chip edge) generates a millivolt-level EMF proportional to $\Delta T$ via the Seebeck coefficient $\alpha_{AB}$ (≈ 40 µV/°C for Type K). The signal is amplified, cold-junction compensated, and digitized for closed-loop thermal control. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for BioMEMS context, 1 for the equation/coefficient.)**

### Q2. State and explain the Law of Intermediate Metals with a neat sketch. Mention one practical application. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Law of Intermediate Metals states that introducing a third metal C into a thermocouple circuit does not change the net EMF, provided the two new junctions (formed where C meets A and C meets B) are at the same temperature. Mathematically:

$$
V_{AB}(T_{ref}, T) = V_{CA}(T_{ref}, T) + V_{CB}(T_{ref}, T)
$$

**Sketch:** A two-junction diagram showing metals A and B forming the measuring and reference junctions, with metal C (e.g., copper lead wire) inserted at one end, both ends of C being isothermal.

**Practical Application:** This law permits the use of solder, terminal blocks, and connector metals in thermocouple circuits without affecting accuracy, provided the connector temperature is uniform. It also enables the use of extension wires (e.g., Type KX) for long runs between BioMEMS chips and readout electronics. **(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for equation, 1 for application.)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *KTU Part B questions are mandatory modules carrying 14 marks. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. Internal choice requires answering EITHER Question A OR Question B in full. Show all derivations, units, and final boxed answers for full credit.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Thermoelectric EMF Derivation + Thermocouple Classification

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

#### Part (a) — Derive the expression for the Seebeck EMF generated in a thermocouple formed by two dissimilar metals A and B. (7 marks)

**Model Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):**
Consider two dissimilar metals A and B joined at a hot junction at temperature $T_{hot}$ and open at a reference (cold) junction at $T_{ref}$. The Seebeck coefficient of each metal is temperature-dependent: $S_A(T)$ and $S_B(T)$.

**Step 2 — EMF Contribution of Each Leg (2 marks):**
The voltage developed across a single conductor of material X, subjected to a temperature difference $T_{hot} - T_{ref}$, is the integral of its absolute Seebeck coefficient:

$$
V_X = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} S_X(T)\,dT
$$

**Step 3 — Net Loop EMF (2 marks):**
The total EMF in the closed circuit A–B thermocouple is the algebraic sum of the contributions of A and B (with sign convention that A is the positive reference):

$$
V_{AB} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} S_B(T)\,dT - \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} S_A(T)\,dT
$$

$$
V_{AB} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} \left[S_B(T) - S_A(T)\right]\,dT
$$

**Step 4 — Define Relative Seebeck Coefficient (1 mark):**

$$
\alpha_{AB}(T) \triangleq S_B(T) - S_A(T)
$$

**Step 5 — Final Boxed Result (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{AB} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} \alpha_{AB}(T)\,dT\,}
$$

For a narrow BioMEMS temperature span, the linear approximation gives $V_{AB} \approx \alpha_{AB}^{\text{avg}} \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{ref})$.

#### Part (b) — Classify the major types of thermocouples used in BioMEMS, giving their thermoelement composition, useful range, and typical sensitivity. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Classification Basis (1 mark):**
Thermocouples are classified by the **ISA / ANSI letter coding (K, J, T, E, N, R, S, B)** based on thermoelement composition, calibration range, and sensitivity. In BioMEMS, the most commonly used types are K, T, E, and R/S.

**Step 2 — Tabulated Description (4 marks):**

| Type | + Leg | - Leg | Range (°C) | $\alpha$ (µV/°C) | BioMEMS Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **K** | Chromel (Ni-Cr) | Alumel (Ni-Al) | -200 to +1350 | ~40.45 | General PCR, cell culture |
| **T** | Copper | Constantan (Cu-Ni) | -250 to +350 | ~38.74 | Cryobiology, IVF |
| **E** | Chromel | Constantan | -200 to +900 | ~58.66 | Highest sensitivity, low-T |
| **R** | Pt | Pt/Rh (13 %) | 0 to +1480 | ~6–10 | NIST-traceable calibration |

**Step 3 — Special-Use Types (1 mark):**
- **Type N** (Nicrosil–Nisil): Improved high-T stability over Type K, used in autoclaves and sterilization systems.
- **Type S** (Pt–Pt/Rh 10 %): Historically the laboratory standard, still used as a reference thermocouple.
- **Type B** (Pt/Rh 30 % – Pt/Rh 6 %): For extreme high-T (>1500 °C), rarely used in BioMEMS.

**Step 4 — Selection Criteria for BioMEMS (1 mark):**
- **Type K** is the most common BioMEMS choice due to its broad range, commercial availability of thin-film deposition targets, and CMOS compatibility (Chromel can be sputtered).
- **Type T** is preferred for cryogenic bio-storage (–196 °C) due to copper's high purity and well-defined Seebeck behavior.
- **Type E** offers the highest sensitivity (~58.66 µV/°C) but is limited to 900 °C.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Thermoelectric Effects & Cold-Junction Compensation

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

#### Part (a) — Explain the three thermoelectric effects (Seebeck, Peltier, Thomson) and derive the two Kelvin relations connecting them. (7 marks)

**Model Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Seebeck Effect (1.5 marks):**
The Seebeck effect is the generation of an EMF due to a temperature gradient across two dissimilar conductors. The EMF is given by:

$$
V_{AB} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} \alpha_{AB}(T)\,dT
$$

where $\alpha_{AB} = S_B - S_A$ is the relative Seebeck coefficient (V/K). The effect is the working principle of thermocouples and thermoelectric generators.

**Step 2 — Peltier Effect (1.5 marks):**
When a current $I$ is forced through a junction of two dissimilar metals, heat is absorbed or released at the junction at a rate:

$$
\dot{Q}_\pi = \pi_{AB} \cdot I
$$

where $\pi_{AB}$ is the Peltier coefficient (V). It is reversible — reversing $I$ flips heating/cooling. It is the working principle of thermoelectric coolers (TECs) used in PCR thermal cycling and lab-on-chip temperature stabilization.

**Step 3 — Thomson Effect (1 mark):**
When a current $I$ flows through a single homogeneous conductor with a temperature gradient $\Delta T$, reversible heat is evolved:

$$
\dot{Q}_\tau = \tau \cdot I \cdot \Delta T
$$

where $\tau$ is the Thomson coefficient (V/K).

**Step 4 — Kelvin Relation 1 Derivation (1.5 marks):**
From the thermodynamic identity $\pi_{AB} = T \cdot \alpha_{AB}$ (derived in Section 3.2 of these notes):

$$
\boxed{\,\pi_{AB}(T) = T \cdot \alpha_{AB}(T)\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Kelvin Relation 2 Derivation (1.5 marks):**
Differentiating Relation 1 with respect to $T$ and equating to the entropy balance:

$$
\boxed{\,\tau_A - \tau_B = T\,\frac{d\alpha_{AB}}{dT}\,}
$$

These two relations make the three thermoelectric effects mutually derivable, given one is measured.

#### Part (b) — Describe the principle of cold-junction compensation (CJC) in modern thermocouple-based BioMEMS readout systems. (7 marks)

**Model Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Problem Statement (1 mark):**
Classical thermocouple tables (NIST ITS-90) assume a reference junction at 0 °C (ice bath). Modern BioMEMS systems cannot use an ice bath, so the actual cold-junction temperature $T_{CJ}$ must be measured and the EMF mathematically corrected.

**Step 2 — Hardware Setup (1.5 marks):**
The cold-junction terminals of the thermocouple are bonded to a copper isothermal block on the PCB. A secondary precision temperature sensor (silicon diode, thermistor, or integrated silicon bandgap sensor) is mounted on the same block in intimate thermal contact. The block is insulated from external heat sources to maintain isothermality.

**Step 3 — Firmware Algorithm (2.5 marks):**
1. Read $T_{CJ}$ from secondary sensor via ADC.
2. Convert $T_{CJ}$ to equivalent thermocouple EMF $V_{CJ}$ using the inverse polynomial (NIST ITS-90 coefficients for the specific TC type, e.g., Type K).
3. Measure the loop EMF $V_{measured}$ from the thermocouple.
4. Compute corrected EMF: $V_{corrected} = V_{measured} + V_{CJ}$ (with sign convention).
5. Convert $V_{corrected}$ to $T_{hot}$ using the forward polynomial.

**Step 4 — Mathematical Demonstration (1.5 marks):**
For a Type K thermocouple with $T_{CJ} = 25\,°C$, the NIST table gives $V_{CJ}(25\,°C) = 1.000\,mV$. If the loop EMF is $V_{measured} = 3.000\,mV$:

$$
V_{corrected} = 3.000 + 1.000 = 4.000\,mV
$$

From the Type K forward polynomial, $V = 4.000\,mV$ corresponds to $T_{hot} \approx 98.3\,°C$.

**Step 5 — Accuracy Considerations (0.5 mark):**
CJC accuracy depends on the precision of the secondary sensor (typically ±0.1 °C for silicon diodes, ±0.5 °C for thermistors). In high-end BioMEMS, two-point calibration and drift compensation are added to maintain ±0.05 °C total system accuracy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Direct from Board Examiner Patterns)**
> 1. **Sign Convention Reversal (–2 marks):** Always define A as the positive leg. Reversing the sign loses partial credit.
> 2. **Forgetting to Specify $T_{ref}$ (–1 mark):** A standalone Seebeck integral without limits is incomplete — always state $T_{ref}$.
> 3. **Confusing Peltier with Joule Heating (–1 mark):** Joule heating ($I^2R$) is irreversible; Peltier is reversible. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping Units (–1 mark per instance):** µV/°C is **not** the same as mV/°C. Always write units explicitly.
> 5. **Mixing Thermocouple Types (–1 mark):** Do not interchange K, T, E coefficients in calculations. State the type at the start of every numerical problem.
> 6. **Ignoring the Cold-Junction Temperature (–2 marks):** Every BioMEMS application must explicitly account for CJC.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision)**
>
> **Core Definition**
> - Thermocouple = two dissimilar metals + hot/cold junction → Seebeck EMF.
> - BioMEMS thermocouples: thin-film (10–100 µm junction) on Si/glass/polymer.
>
> **Three Thermoelectric Effects**
> - **Seebeck:** $\Delta T \rightarrow V$; measured EMF = $\int \alpha_{AB}\,dT$.
> - **Peltier:** $I \rightarrow \dot{Q}_\pi = \pi_{AB} I$; reversible, basis of TECs.
> - **Thomson:** $I + \Delta T \rightarrow \dot{Q}_\tau = \tau I \Delta T$; reversible.
>
> **Two Kelvin Relations (MUST be memorized)**
> - $\pi_{AB} = T \cdot \alpha_{AB}$
> - $\tau_A - \tau_B = T\,d\alpha_{AB}/dT$
>
> **Three Thermocouple Laws (MUST be memorized)**
> - **Homogeneous Circuit:** EMF depends only on junction metals + temperatures, not on wire length/geometry.
> - **Intermediate Metals:** Third metal added at isothermal point → no EMF change.
> - **Intermediate Temperatures:** $V(T_1, T_3) = V(T_1, T_2) + V(T_2, T_3)$.
>
> **Type Selection for BioMEMS**
> - **Type K** = default (general PCR, cell culture).
> - **Type T** = cryobiology (–196 °C).
> - **Type E** = high sensitivity, low-T.
> - **Type R/S** = NIST-traceable calibration.
>
> **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)**
> - Required because NIST tables assume 0 °C reference.
> - Algorithm: $V_{corrected} = V_{measured} + V_{CJ}$(from secondary sensor).
> - Hardware: isothermal block + silicon diode / thermistor.
>
> **Critical Formulas to Box**
> - $V_{AB} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} \alpha_{AB}(T)\,dT$
> - $\pi_{AB} = T\,\alpha_{AB}$
> - $\tau_A - \tau_B = T\,d\alpha_{AB}/dT$
> - $V_{corrected} = V_{measured} + V_{CJ}$
>
> **Numerical Values to Remember**
> - Type K: $\alpha \approx 40.45$ µV/°C, range –200 to +1350 °C.
> - Type T: $\alpha \approx 38.74$ µV/°C, range –250 to +350 °C.
> - Type E: $\alpha \approx 58.66$ µV/°C, range –200 to +900 °C (highest sensitivity).
> - Standard reference: 0 °C (ice bath) or 25 °C (modern CJC).
>
> **BioMEMS-Specific Notes**
> - Thin-film deposition: sputtering or e-beam evaporation.
> - Junction size: 10–100 µm.
> - Time constant: microseconds to milliseconds.
> - Common materials: Chromel–Alumel, Polysilicon–Al, Polysilicon–Au.
>
> **Common Exam Mistakes to AVOID**
> - Skipping sign conventions.
> - Forgetting the integration limits.
> - Confusing µV with mV.
> - Ignoring CJC in numerical problems.
> - Mixing up Thomson and Peltier (Thomson = single conductor; Peltier = junction).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Resistor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermal Resistor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Resistor (R_th):** A lumped-element thermal model used to quantify the opposition offered by a material or geometry to steady-state heat flow. In BioMEMS and microfluidic analysis, thermal resistance is defined as the ratio of the temperature difference ($\Delta T$) across an element to the steady-state heat current ($\dot{Q}$) flowing through it, completely analogous to Ohm's law in electrical circuit theory.

The fundamental governing relation is:

$$R_{th} = \frac{\Delta T}{\dot{Q}}$$

where $R_{th}$ is expressed in units of **K/W (Kelvin per Watt)** or **°C/W**.

In the context of BioMEMS (Bio-Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems), the thermal resistor is the foundational building block of *lumped thermal modeling*. It enables engineers to abstract complex heat-transfer phenomena in micro-scale biological devices — such as Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) microchips, lab-on-a-chip DNA amplifiers, and cell-sorting devices — into simple, solvable circuit-like networks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Unit Convention:** All quantities in KTU board problems will use SI units unless explicitly stated. Thermal conductivity $k$ is in **W/(m·K)**, area $A$ in **m²**, length $L$ in **m**, and heat flux in **W**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pouring hot water through a narrow, thin-walled plastic straw versus a thick, short copper pipe. Even if both pipes are identical in length, the **copper pipe allows heat to escape easily** (low resistance), whereas the **plastic straw insulates the heat** (high resistance).

A **Thermal Resistor** behaves exactly the same way in a BioMEMS device:

- **Low R_th** → Material conducts heat efficiently (like copper). Temperature drop across it is small for a given heat flow.
- **High R_th** → Material acts as a thermal insulator (like PDMS polymer). It can sustain a large temperature gradient while transporting little heat.

Just as electrical resistors in a circuit are summed in series and parallel, **thermal resistors can be assembled into thermal networks** (also called *thermal RC circuits* or *lumped thermal models*) to predict the temperature distribution of an entire microfluidic chip.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** Think of $R_{th}$ as the "friction encountered by heat." The more "friction," the more the temperature drops per unit of heat flowing through.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Thermal Resistors

BioMEMS thermal analysis broadly classifies thermal resistors into **three physical modes** corresponding to the three modes of heat transfer:

| Mode | Physical Origin | Resistance Symbol |
|------|----------------|-------------------|
| Conduction | Molecular collisions in solids/fluids | $R_{cond}$ |
| Convection | Bulk fluid motion carrying heat away | $R_{conv}$ |
| Radiation | Electromagnetic emission from hot surfaces | $R_{rad}$ |

The **total thermal resistance** of a system is the network combination of these individual resistances, following the same series-parallel rules as electrical resistors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conduction Thermal Resistance (R_cond)

Heat conduction through a material is governed by **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction** in one dimension:

$$\dot{Q} = -k A \frac{dT}{dx}$$

For a uniform slab of cross-sectional area $A$, thermal conductivity $k$, and length $L$, with one face at $T_1$ and the other at $T_2$, the steady-state conduction equation integrates to:

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{kA}{L} (T_1 - T_2)$$

Rearranging into the thermal-resistor form $\Delta T = R_{th} \dot{Q}$:

$$\boxed{R_{cond} = \frac{L}{kA}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**

- $R_{cond}$ is **directly proportional** to length $L$ (longer path = more resistance).
- $R_{cond}$ is **inversely proportional** to area $A$ (wider pipe = less resistance).
- $R_{cond}$ is **inversely proportional** to $k$ (better conductor = less resistance).

For radial heat conduction through a cylindrical wall (extremely important for microfluidic tubing and capillary-based PCR systems):

$$R_{cond,rad} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$$

For heat conduction across spherical shells (relevant to encapsulated biological cells and droplets):

$$R_{cond,sph} = \frac{1}{4\pi k}\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right)$$

---

### 2.2 Convection Thermal Resistance (R_conv)

Convection occurs when heat is carried away by a moving fluid (liquid or gas) over a surface. It is described by **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$\dot{Q} = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$$

where $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), $T_s$ is the surface temperature, and $T_\infty$ is the bulk fluid temperature.

The corresponding thermal resistance is:

$$\boxed{R_{conv} = \frac{1}{hA}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Values of $h$:**
> - Natural air convection: **5 – 25 W/(m²·K)**
> - Forced air convection: **10 – 200 W/(m²·K)**
> - Natural water convection: **100 – 1000 W/(m²·K)**
> - Forced water/boiling: **1000 – 100000 W/(m²·K)**

In microfluidic PCR biochips, $h$ values for water-based reagents can be as high as **$10^4$ to $10^5$ W/(m²·K)** because the channels are very narrow and flow is often forced.

---

### 2.3 Radiation Thermal Resistance (R_rad)

Thermal radiation transfers heat via electromagnetic waves. For a small gray surface ($\epsilon$) radiating to a much larger surrounding surface, the net heat transfer is governed by the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**:

$$\dot{Q} = \epsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$$

where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) is the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** and $\epsilon$ is the emissivity (0 ≤ ε ≤ 1).

Linearizing for a small temperature difference around a mean temperature $T_m$ gives:

$$\boxed{R_{rad} = \frac{1}{4 \epsilon \sigma A T_m^3}}$$

This form is convenient because $R_{rad}$ becomes compatible with the linear resistance network model. Radiation is usually a **secondary effect** in BioMEMS because device temperatures are moderate (often near 37 °C for biological applications) and the devices are typically packaged, but it becomes important at elevated temperatures such as in PCR thermocycling (95 °C).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains the **high-yield formulas** most frequently tested in KTU university examinations for this topic.

| # | Thermal Element | Formula | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1-D Conduction (Slab) | $R_{cond} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | K/W | Wall/membrane heat flow |
| 2 | Radial Conduction (Cylinder) | $R_{cond} = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ | K/W | Capillary/microtube walls |
| 3 | Spherical Conduction | $R_{cond} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi k}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1}-\dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)$ | K/W | Cell membrane/droplet |
| 4 | Convection | $R_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{hA}$ | K/W | Coolant flow over chip |
| 5 | Radiation (linearized) | $R_{rad} = \dfrac{1}{4\epsilon\sigma A T_m^3}$ | K/W | Hot PCR chambers |
| 6 | Series Combination | $R_{tot} = \sum R_i$ | K/W | Multi-layer wall |
| 7 | Parallel Combination | $\dfrac{1}{R_{tot}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{R_i}$ | K/W | Parallel fins |
| 8 | Ohm's Law (Thermal) | $\Delta T = R_{th} \dot{Q}$ | K = (K/W)·W | Master equation |
| 9 | Thermal Capacitance | $C_{th} = \rho V c_p$ | J/K | Transient response |
| 10 | Thermal Time Constant | $\tau = R_{th} C_{th}$ | seconds | Heating/cooling time |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **PCR microchip for DNA amplification**, the device must cycle rapidly between three temperatures: 95 °C (denaturation), 55 °C (annealing), and 72 °C (extension). The total thermal resistance from the integrated thin-film heater to the reagent determines:

1. The **electrical power** required to maintain the temperature.
2. The **thermal time constant** $\tau = R_{th} C_{th}$ which sets the cycling speed.
3. The **temperature uniformity** across the reaction chamber.

Minimizing $R_{cond}$ between heater and fluid (by using thin silicon membranes with high $k$) while maximizing $R_{cond}$ to the surroundings (by suspending the membrane on insulating legs) is the central design trade-off. This is exactly the optimization studied in research-grade thermocycler chips.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Fourier's Law to Conduction Thermal Resistance

**Starting Equation:** Fourier's Law for 1-D steady-state conduction.

$$\dot{Q} = -k A \frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables.** Move all temperature terms to the left and spatial terms to the right:

$$\dot{Q} \, dx = -k A \, dT$$

**Step 2 — Integrate across the slab from $x = 0$ to $x = L$.** Assuming $k$, $A$, and $\dot{Q}$ are constants:

$$\dot{Q} \int_{0}^{L} dx = -k A \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$\dot{Q} \cdot L = -k A (T_2 - T_1) = k A (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into thermal-resistor form** (i.e., isolate the temperature difference):

$$\Delta T = T_1 - T_2 = \frac{L}{kA} \dot{Q}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the thermal resistance** by comparing with $\Delta T = R_{th} \dot{Q}$:

$$\boxed{R_{cond} = \frac{L}{kA}}$$

This derivation confirms that the **conduction thermal resistor is a direct consequence of integrating Fourier's law** under steady, 1-D assumptions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Radial Conduction Through a Cylindrical Wall

Consider a cylindrical tube of inner radius $r_1$ and outer radius $r_2$, length $L$, and thermal conductivity $k$, with heat flowing radially outward.

**Step 1 — Apply Fourier's law in cylindrical coordinates.** Since heat flows only in the radial direction and area varies with $r$, we use $A = 2\pi r L$:

$$\dot{Q} = -k (2\pi r L) \frac{dT}{dr}$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$\dot{Q} \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \, dT$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $r_1$ to $r_2$ (with $T$ changing from $T_1$ to $T_2$):**

$$\dot{Q} \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate integrals:**

$$\dot{Q} \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = -2\pi k L (T_2 - T_1) = 2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange:**

$$\Delta T = T_1 - T_2 = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} \dot{Q}$$

**Step 6 — Identify $R_{th}$:**

$$\boxed{R_{cond,rad} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Combined Series and Parallel Thermal Network
