**Low frequency** ($\omega \ll \omega_n$): $|H(j\omega)| \approx 1/\omega_n^2 = m/k$ → **displacement proportional to acceleration** (this is the "accelerometer mode"). - **At resonance** ($\omega = \omega_n$): $|H(j\omega_n)| \approx Q/\omega_n^2$ → maximum amplitude, **Q times amplified** (resonant sensing mode). - **High frequency** ($\omega \gg \omega_n$): $|H(j\omega)| \approx 1/\omega^2 \to 0$ → mass cannot follow, output vanishes. ### 2.5 Coriolis Gyroscope Theory For a gyroscope, the proof mass is driven into a **primary mode oscillation** at velocity $v$. When the device rotates at angular rate $\Omega$, the mass experiences a **Coriolis force**: $$ \vec{F}_c = -2m \, (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this works for BioMEMS:** The Coriolis force is **perpendicular to both** the driving velocity and the rotation axis. By sensing motion in this perpendicular (secondary) mode, we can directly infer the rotation rate — without any spinning rotor! This is the genius of MEMS gyroscopes. ### 2.6 Capacitive Readout Architecture The most common BioMEMS transduction method is **differential capacitive sensing** because it is: - Low power (suitable for implantable devices) - Temperature stable - CMOS-compatible - Highly linear for small displacements For a parallel-plate capacitor with plate area $A$ and gap $d$: $$ C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d} $$ For a proof mass displacement $x \ll d$, the **differential change** is: $$ \Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A \, x}{d^2} $$ This $\Delta C$ is converted to voltage via a **charge amplifier** or **switched-capacitor ASIC**. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Sensor Type | Frequency Band | |-------------|-------------|----------------| | Fall detection in elderly | 3-axis accelerometer | 0–20 Hz | | Tremor quantification (Parkinson's) | MEMS accelerometer | 1–30 Hz | | Capsule endoscope orientation | IMU (accel + gyro) | DC–100 Hz | | Hearing aid active noise control | Accelerometer | 100 Hz–10 kHz | | Heartbeat ballistocardiography | High-sensitivity accel | 0.5–20 Hz | | Activity recognition in wearables | 6-axis IMU | 0–50 Hz | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Transfer Function of a Capacitive Accelerometer **Given:** A silicon proof mass $m = 5 \times 10^{-9} \, kg$ suspended by four folded flexure springs, each of stiffness $k_{leg}$. Total spring stiffness $k = 4 k_{leg}$. Damping coefficient $b = 1 \times 10^{-6} \, N \cdot s/m$. Plate area $A = 200 \times 200 \, \mu m^2$, nominal gap $d = 2 \, \mu m$. **Find:** (a) Natural frequency, (b) Damping ratio, (c) Static sensitivity, (d) Output voltage per $g$ when fed to a charge amplifier of gain $G_v = 1 \, V/pF$. **Step 1 — Compute Natural Frequency $\omega_n$:** $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} $$ Assume the folded flexure stiffness $k_{leg} = 0.05 \, N/m$, therefore: $$ k = 4 \times 0.05 = 0.20 \, N/m $$ Substituting into the formula: $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{0.20}{5 \times 10^{-9}}} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^{7}} = 6324.6 \, rad/s $$ Converting to Hz: $$ f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{6324.6}{2\pi} \approx 1006.6 \, Hz $$ **Step 2 — Compute Damping Ratio $\zeta$:** $$ \zeta = \frac{b}{2 \sqrt{k m}} $$ Computing the denominator: $$ 2 \sqrt{k m} = 2 \sqrt{0.20 \times 5 \times 10^{-9}} = 2 \sqrt{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 2 \times 3.162 \times 10^{-5} = 6.324 \times 10^{-5} $$ Substituting: $$ \zeta = \frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{6.324 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.0158 $$ This indicates a **highly underdamped** system — typical for vacuum-packaged MEMS. **Step 3 — Compute Static Sensitivity $S_0$:** $$ S_0 = \frac{m}{k} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-9}}{0.20} = 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \, m / (m/s^2) = 25 \, nm/g $$ **Step 4 — Compute Capacitive Change per $g$:** For $x \ll d$, the differential capacitance is: $$ \Delta C = \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A \, x}{d^2} $$ Substituting $x = 25 \, nm = 25 \times 10^{-9} \, m$, $A = (200 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-8} \, m^2$, $d = 2 \times 10^{-6} \, m$, and $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, F/m$: $$ \Delta C = \frac{2 \times (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times (4 \times 10^{-8}) \times (25 \times 10^{-9})}{(2 \times 10^{-6})^2} $$ Simplifying the denominator first: $$ (2 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-12} $$ Computing the numerator: $$ 2 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times 25 \times 10^{-9} = 1770.8 \times 10^{-29} = 1.7708 \times 10^{-26} $$ Dividing: $$ \Delta C = \frac{1.7708 \times 10^{-26}}{4 \times 10^{-12}} = 4.427 \times 10^{-15} \, F = 4.43 \, fF $$ **Step 5 — Output Voltage per $g$:** $$ V_{out} = G_v \times \Delta C = 1 \times 4.43 = 4.43 \, mV / g $$ This is consistent with commercial MEMS accelerometers (typically 1–10 mV/g raw output before amplification). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Coriolis Force and Angular Rate Sensitivity in a MEMS Gyroscope **Given:** A vibratory gyroscope with a proof mass $m = 1 \times 10^{-9} \, kg$ driven into primary mode oscillation at amplitude $x_p = 1 \, \mu m$ and frequency $\omega_d = 20 \, kHz$. The secondary mode has the same natural frequency $\omega_n = \omega_d$ (mode-matched condition). **Find:** (a) Peak velocity $v_p$ of the proof mass, (b) Coriolis force for $\Omega = 100 \, °/s$, (c) Secondary mode displacement amplitude. **Step 1 — Peak Velocity of Proof Mass:** For sinusoidal motion $x_p(t) = X_p \sin(\omega_d t)$: $$ v_p(t) = \dot{x}_p = \omega_d X_p \cos(\omega_d t) $$ Peak velocity magnitude: $$ v_p = \omega_d X_p = (2\pi \times 20 \times 10^3) \times (1 \times 10^{-6}) $$ $$ v_p = (1.2566 \times 10^5) \times (1 \times 10^{-6}) = 0.1257 \, m/s $$ **Step 2 — Convert $\Omega$ to rad/s:** $$ \Omega = 100 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.745 \, rad/s $$ **Step 3 — Compute Coriolis Force Magnitude:** $$ F_c = 2 m \Omega v_p = 2 \times (1 \times 10^{-9}) \times 1.745 \times 0.1257 $$ $$ F_c = 2 \times 1 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.2194 = 4.387 \times 10^{-10} \, N $$ **Step 4 — Secondary Mode Displacement (Quasi-static):** For the secondary mode, the displacement amplitude due to Coriolis force at low frequency ($\omega \ll \omega_n$) is: $$ X_s = \frac{F_c}{k_s} = \frac{F_c}{m \omega_n^2} $$ Using $\omega_n = 2\pi \times 20 \times 10^3 = 1.2566 \times 10^5 \, rad/s$: $$ \omega_n^2 = 1.579 \times 10^{10} $$ $$ X_s = \frac{4.387 \times 10^{-10}}{1 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.579 \times 10^{10}} = \frac{4.387 \times 10^{-10}}{15.79} = 2.778 \times 10^{-11} \, m $$ $$ X_s \approx 27.8 \, pm \, (picometers) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The secondary mode displacement is **extremely small** (picometer scale). This is why MEMS gyroscopes require ultra-low-noise ASIC charge amplifiers and **mode-matching** to achieve usable signal-to-noise ratio. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Brownian Noise Floor The fundamental noise limit of a MEMS accelerometer is set by **Brownian (thermal) motion** of the gas molecules surrounding the proof mass. **Formula:** $$ a_n = \sqrt{\frac{4 k_B T \omega_n}{m Q}} $$ **Derivation steps:** The energy spectral density of Brownian force noise is: $$ \bar{F}_n^2 = 4 k_B T b $$ Dividing by the mass to get acceleration noise: $$ \bar{a}_n^2 = \frac{4 k_B T b}{m^2} $$ The transfer function from force to displacement is $1/k$ at low frequency. For a resonator, the in-band acceleration noise is amplified by the quality factor $Q$: $$ a_n^2 = \frac{4 k_B T \omega_n}{m Q} $$ **Numerical example** for $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \, J/K$, $T = 300 \, K$, $\omega_n = 6324.6 \, rad/s$, $m = 5 \times 10^{-9} \, kg$, $Q = 31.6$ (since $Q = 1/(2\zeta) = 1/0.0316$): $$ a_n = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 6324.6}{5 \times 10^{-9} \times 31.6}} $$ Numerator: $$ 4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 6324.6 = 1.047 \times 10^{-16} $$ Denominator: $$ 5 \times 10^{-9} \times 31.6 = 1.58 \times 10^{-7} $$ $$ a_n = \sqrt{\frac{1.047 \times 10^{-16}}{1.58 \times 10^{-7}}} = \sqrt{6.63 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.57 \times 10^{-5} \, m/s^2 / \sqrt{Hz} $$ $$ a_n \approx 2.62 \, \mu g / \sqrt{Hz} $$ This is the **theoretical detection floor** — actual device noise is typically 2–5× higher due to electronic noise. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-off Summary | Design Parameter | Increase → Effect on Sensitivity | Increase → Effect on Bandwidth | Increase → Effect on Noise | |------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|----------------------------| | Proof mass $m$ | **Increases** ($\propto m$) | **Decreases** ($\omega_n \downarrow$) | **Decreases** ($\propto 1/\sqrt{m}$) | | Spring stiffness $k$ | **Decreases** ($\propto 1/k$) | **Increases** ($\omega_n \uparrow$) | **Increases** | | Damping $b$ | No DC effect | Reduces $Q$ | **Increases** | | Plate area $A$ | **Increases** capacitive output | No effect | No direct effect | | Gap $d$ | **Decreases** (1/$d^2$) | No effect | Slight decrease | | Vacuum packaging | No DC effect | Slight increase | **Decreases drastically** | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mass–Spring–Damper Conceptual Model (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[External Acceleration
a of t] --> B[Frame or Substrate
accelerates] B --> C[Pseudo Inertial Force
F equals m times a] C --> D[Proof Mass m
temporarily stationary] D --> E[Relative Displacement
x of t] E --> F[Spring k
restoring force] E --> G[Damper b
viscous force] F --> H[Mechanical Equilibrium] G --> H E --> I[Transduction Stage
Capacitive / Piezoresistive] I --> J[ASIC Charge Amplifier] J --> K[Output Voltage Signal
Vout proportional to a] K --> L[BioMEMS Application
Fall Detection / Gait] ``` ### 4.2 Capacitive Accelerometer — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[Input Acceleration
a equals 1g] --> nodeB[Proof Mass m
displacement x] nodeB --> nodeC[Differential Capacitor
C1 and C2] nodeC --> nodeD[CMOS Switched Capacitor
Charge Amplifier] nodeD --> nodeE[Bandpass Filter
0.5 Hz to 20 Hz] nodeE --> nodeF[ADC 16 bit] nodeF --> nodeG[Digital Signal
Processor DSP] nodeG --> nodeH[Output Decision
Fall or Normal Activity] nodeH -.feedback.-> nodeE ``` ### 4.3 Vibratory Gyroscope — Mode Coupling Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR driveOsc[Drive Oscillator
20 kHz] --> driveMode[Primary Mode
Velocity v of t] rotation[External Rotation
Omega] --> coriolisFx[Coriolis Force
Fc equals 2m Omega cross v] driveMode --> coriolisFx coriolisFx --> senseMode[Secondary Mode
Displacement y of t] senseMode --> senseCap[Differential Capacitor
Sensing Electrodes] senseCap --> senseAmp[Low Noise
Transimpedance Amplifier] senseAmp --> demod[Synchronous
Demodulator] driveOsc --> demod demod --> output[Angular Rate
Output deg per s] ``` ### 4.4 BioMEMS Wearable Inertia Sensor — System Integration ```mermaid flowchart TB body[Human Body Motion
Acceleration and Rotation] --> accelChip[3 axis MEMS
Accelerometer] body --> gyroChip[3 axis MEMS
Gyroscope] accelChip --> imuFuse[IMU Sensor Fusion
Kalman Filter] gyroChip --> imuFuse imuFuse --> microCtrl[Microcontroller
ARM Cortex M0] microCtrl --> ble[Bluetooth Low
Energy Radio] ble --> smartphone[Smartphone App
Health Dashboard] microCtrl --> localAI[On chip Activity
Classification AI] localAI --> alert[Fall Alert or
Tremor Detection] ``` ### 4.5 Inertia Sensor Fabrication — Process Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB s1[Start Silicon Wafer
100 mm diameter] --> s2[Thermal Oxidation
SiO2 layer 1 um] s2 --> s3[Deposit Polysilicon
Structural Layer 2 um] s3 --> s4[Photolithography
Define Proof Mass Shape] s4 --> s5[Reactive Ion Etch
RIE of Polysilicon] s5 --> s6[Sacrificial Oxide Etch
HF Vapor Release] s6 --> s7[Critical Point Drying
Prevent Stiction] s7 --> s8[Wafer Level Packaging
Vacuum Sealing] s8 --> s9[Dicing and ASIC
Integration] s9 --> s10[Final Inertia
Sensor Die] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]** **Q1. Define an inertia sensor. Differentiate between an accelerometer and a gyroscope in terms of the physical quantity they measure and the underlying principle.** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** An **inertia sensor** is a MEMS device that measures motion-related quantities (linear acceleration or angular rate) by detecting the displacement of a suspended proof mass under inertial loading. - **Accelerometer** measures **linear acceleration** (units: $m/s^2$ or $g$) using **Newton's Second Law** ($F = ma$). The proof mass displacement is proportional to the applied acceleration. - **Gyroscope** measures **angular velocity** (units: rad/s) using the **Coriolis Effect**. A vibrating mass in a rotating frame experiences a Coriolis force proportional to the rotation rate. In BioMEMS, accelerometers are commonly used for fall detection, while gyroscopes are used for capsule endoscope orientation tracking. `[3 Marks]` --- **Q2. What is the significance of the Quality Factor (Q) in a MEMS accelerometer? State its formula.** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** The **Quality Factor (Q)** of a MEMS inertia sensor quantifies the sharpness of its mechanical resonance. It is defined as: $$ Q = \frac{1}{2\zeta} = \frac{\sqrt{k m}}{b} $$ where $\zeta$ is the damping ratio, $k$ is the spring stiffness, $m$ is the proof mass, and $b$ is the damping coefficient. **Significance in BioMEMS:** 1. **Resonant sensing mode:** A higher $Q$ amplifies the sensor's response near resonance, increasing sensitivity in narrowband applications (e.g., resonant mass sensors for biomarker detection). 2. **Bandwidth trade-off:** A high $Q$ means a narrow usable bandwidth — typically $BW \approx f_n / Q$. For broadband BioMEMS applications (fall detection, gait), $Q < 1$ is preferred. 3. **Noise performance:** Brownian noise is inversely proportional to $\sqrt{Q}$ — vacuum packaging increases $Q$ and reduces noise floor. `[3 Marks]` --- ### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **ESE (End Semester Evaluation) Pattern – KTU 2024 Scheme** --- #### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]` **(a) Derive the governing equation of motion for a MEMS accelerometer using the mass–spring–damper model. From this, obtain the transfer function and identify the natural frequency, damping ratio, and static sensitivity. State clearly the units of each. `[7 Marks]`** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Consider a proof mass $m$ suspended by a spring of stiffness $k$ and damper of coefficient $b$, all enclosed within a frame that is accelerating at $a(t)$. **Step 2:** In the **non-inertial frame of the device**, apply Newton's Second Law. The mass experiences a pseudo-force $-m a(t)$ in addition to spring and damper forces: $$ m \frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + b \frac{dx}{dt} + k x = -m \, a(t) $$ **Step 3:** Take the **Laplace Transform** assuming zero initial conditions: $$ (ms^2 + bs + k) X(s) = -m A(s) $$ **Step 4:** Rearrange to obtain the **transfer function**: $$ H(s) = \frac{X(s)}{A(s)} = \frac{-m}{ms^2 + bs + k} = \frac{-1}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2} $$ **Step 5:** Identify the system parameters: - **Natural Frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ (units: rad/s) - **Damping Ratio:** $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{km}}$ (dimensionless) - **Static Sensitivity:** $S_0 = \left. \dfrac{X}{A} \right\rvert_{s=0} = \dfrac{-m}{k} = \dfrac{-1}{\omega_n^2}$ (units: m·s²/m) **Step 6:** The negative sign indicates the mass displaces **opposite** to the frame's acceleration, consistent with inertia. `[7 Marks]` --- **(b) A MEMS accelerometer has a proof mass of $2 \times 10^{-9} \, kg$ and a total spring stiffness of $0.1 \, N/m$. The nominal gap between the differential capacitive plates is $3 \, \mu m$, and the plate area is $150 \times 150 \, \mu m^2$. Calculate: (i) the natural frequency, (ii) the static sensitivity in $nm/g$, and (iii) the differential capacitance change per $g$. Assume $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, F/m$. `[7 Marks]`** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **(i) Natural Frequency:** $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1}{2 \times 10^{-9}}} = \sqrt{5 \times 10^{7}} = 7071 \, rad/s $$ Converting to Hz: $$ f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{7071}{2\pi} \approx 1125 \, Hz $$ **(ii) Static Sensitivity in nm/g:** First, sensitivity in SI units: $$ S_0 = \frac{m}{k} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-9}}{0.1} = 2 \times 10^{-8} \, m / (m/s^2) $$ Convert to nm/g (multiply by $1 \, g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ and $1 \, m = 10^9 \, nm$): $$ S_0 = 2 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.81 \times 10^9 = 196.2 \, nm/g $$ **(iii) Differential Capacitance per $g$:** Using $x = 196.2 \, nm = 196.2 \times 10^{-9} \, m$, $A = (150 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-8} \, m^2$, $d = 3 \times 10^{-6} \, m$: $$ \Delta C = \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A x}{d^2} = \frac{2 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.25 \times 10^{-8} \times 196.2 \times 10^{-9}}{(3 \times 10^{-6})^2} $$ Numerator: $$ 2 \times 8.854 \times 2.25 \times 196.2 \times 10^{-12-8-9} = 7815.6 \times 10^{-29} = 7.8156 \times 10^{-26} $$ Denominator: $$ 9 \times 10^{-12} $$ $$ \Delta C = \frac{7.8156 \times 10^{-26}}{9 \times 10^{-12}} = 8.68 \times 10^{-15} \, F = 8.68 \, fF/g $$ `[7 Marks]` --- #### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a) Explain the working principle of a MEMS vibratory gyroscope with the help of the Coriolis force equation. Derive the expression for the Coriolis force acting on a proof mass oscillating in the primary mode. State why this principle is suitable for BioMEMS applications. `[7 Marks]`** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Concept:** A MEMS vibratory gyroscope has **no spinning rotor**. Instead, a proof mass is driven into **linear oscillation** (primary mode). When the device frame rotates, the mass experiences a **Coriolis force** that deflects it in a **perpendicular direction** (secondary mode). The secondary displacement is proportional to the rotation rate. **Step 2 — Derivation of Coriolis Force:** Consider a proof mass $m$ oscillating with velocity $\vec{v}(t)$ in a frame rotating at angular rate $\vec{\Omega}$. The equation of motion in the rotating frame includes a Coriolis term: $$ \vec{F}_{coriolis} = -2m \, (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}) $$ For 1D primary mode $\vec{v} = v \hat{x}$ and rotation about the $z$-axis $\vec{\Omega} = \Omega \hat{z}$: $$ \vec{F}_c = -2m \, (\Omega \hat{z} \times v \hat{x}) = -2m \Omega v \, \hat{y} $$ The Coriolis force is **perpendicular** to both $\vec{\Omega}$ and $\vec{v}$, deflecting the mass along $\hat{y}$ (secondary mode). **Step 3 — Why suitable for BioMEMS:** 1. **No moving rotor** → ultra-low power (few mW), safe for implantable use. 2. **CMOS-compatible fabrication** → cheap and mass-producible. 3. **Small size (mm scale)** → can be integrated into swallowable capsules and wearable patches. 4. **Solid-state** → high shock tolerance, no wear. 5. **High sensitivity** at small form factor — ideal for capsule endoscope orientation and activity monitoring. `[7 Marks]` --- **(b) A MEMS gyroscope has a proof mass of $1.5 \times 10^{-9} \, kg$ oscillating in its primary mode with peak velocity $0.2 \, m/s$ at a drive frequency of $25 \, kHz$. The device is subjected to an angular rate of $50 \, °/s$. Calculate: (i) the peak Coriolis force, and (ii) the secondary mode displacement if the secondary spring stiffness is $k_s = 0.05 \, N/m$. Comment on the order of magnitude of the result. `[7 Marks]`** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **(i) Peak Coriolis Force:** First, convert $\Omega$ to rad/s: $$ \Omega = 50 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.873 \, rad/s $$ Apply the Coriolis force formula: $$ F_c = 2 m \Omega v_p = 2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.873 \times 0.2 $$ $$ F_c = 5.24 \times 10^{-10} \, N $$ **(ii) Secondary Mode Displacement:** $$ X_s = \frac{F_c}{k_s} = \frac{5.24 \times 10^{-10}}{0.05} = 1.047 \times 10^{-8} \, m \approx 10.5 \, nm $$ **Comment:** The secondary mode displacement is in the **nanometer range**, which is extremely small. This is why MEMS gyroscopes require: - **High-Q secondary mode** (vacuum packaging) to amplify the signal. - **Mode matching** (driving frequency = secondary natural frequency) to maximize response. - **Ultra-low-noise ASIC front-end** with femtofarad-level capacitance resolution. The result underscores the engineering challenge of BioMEMS gyroscope design — high sensitivity demands exceptional mechanical and electronic precision. `[7 Marks]` --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams (PEEBT743 M2):** > > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $F = -ma$** — the pseudo-force is *opposite* to the applied acceleration. Examiners specifically look for this sign convention. > 2. **Mixing up natural frequency units** — $\omega_n$ is in **rad/s**, but $f_n$ is in **Hz**. Students often write "$\omega_n = 1000 \, Hz$" — this loses 1 mark immediately. > 3. **Confusing Coriolis force formula** — it is $F_c = 2m(\Omega \times v)$, NOT $F_c = m(\Omega \times v)$. The factor of 2 is mandatory. > 4. **Not converting °/s to rad/s** — every Coriolis calculation in KTU papers requires this conversion. One full mark is awarded just for showing this step. > 5. **Writing $\Delta C$ without linearization** — the full expression $\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 A \left( \frac{1}{d-x} - \frac{1}{d+x} \right)$ must be linearized for $x \ll d$ to obtain $\Delta C = 2\varepsilon_0 A x / d^2$. Skipping this step loses 1 mark. > 6. **Not stating units** — KTU marking scheme explicitly awards marks for correct SI units in the final answer. Always state units. > 7. **Omitting the formula sheet table** — when deriving, always write the standard form first, *then* substitute. Reverse order loses structural marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Inertia Sensors (PEEBT743 M2):** - ☐ **Inertia sensor** = MEMS device using a **proof mass** to detect acceleration or rotation. - ☐ Two main types: **Accelerometer** (Newton's Law) and **Gyroscope** (Coriolis Effect). - ☐ Governing equation: $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = -ma(t)$. - ☐ **Transfer function**: $H(s) = -1 / (s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2)$. - ☐ **Natural frequency**: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$; **Damping ratio**: $\zeta = b / (2\sqrt{km})$; **Quality factor**: $Q = 1/(2\zeta)$. - ☐ **Static sensitivity** (accelerometer): $S_0 = m/k = 1/\omega_n^2$ (units: $m \cdot s^2/m$). - ☐ **Coriolis force**: $F_c = 2m(\Omega \times v)$ — must convert $\Omega$ from °/s to rad/s. - ☐ **Capacitive readout**: $\Delta C = 2\varepsilon_0 A x / d^2$ for $x \ll d$ (linearized). - ☐ **Brownian noise floor**: $a_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T \omega_n / (m Q)}$ — vacuum packaging reduces it. - ☐ **Design trade-off**: Heavier $m$ and softer $k$ → higher sensitivity, lower bandwidth. - ☐ **BioMEMS applications**: Fall detection, gait analysis, capsule endoscope, hearing aids, tremor monitoring. - ☐ **Standard material**: Single-crystal silicon (Si) — $\rho = 2330 \, kg/m^3$, $E = 169 \, GPa$. - ☐ **Standard readout**: Differential capacitive sensing (low power, CMOS-compatible). - ☐ **Always quote units** and use **sign conventions** explicitly in derivations.

topics.title: Piezoelectric Sensor and Actuator: Properties of Piezoelectric Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Sensor and Actuator: Properties of Piezoelectric Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piezoelectric Effect:** The piezoelectric effect is the linear, reversible electromechanical coupling phenomenon exhibited by certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials (called piezoelectric materials) in which an applied mechanical stress produces an internal dielectric displacement (and hence a measurable surface charge density) — known as the **Direct Piezoelectric Effect** — while conversely, an applied electric field induces a mechanical strain in the crystal — known as the **Converse (Inverse) Piezoelectric Effect**.

In BioMEMS, this property is exploited to build **self-powered, label-free biosensors** and **precision micro-actuators** such as micropumps, inkjet heads, microvalves, ultrasonic transducers, and acoustic resonators used in lab-on-chip platforms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **spring-loaded mechanical translator** sitting between two people speaking different languages (Mechanical Engineer and Electrical Engineer). When the Mechanical Engineer pushes the spring (applies stress), the translator automatically speaks electrical words to the Electrical Engineer — *charge appears on the surfaces*. This is the **Direct Effect** (Sensor mode).

Now reverse the conversation: when the Electrical Engineer speaks (applies voltage), the translator moves the spring, producing a precise mechanical push — *the crystal deforms*. This is the **Converse Effect** (Actuator mode).

The same material can play either role simply by changing whether you input mechanical or electrical energy. The "translator" inside the crystal is the asymmetric charge distribution in the unit cell (dipole reorientation under load).

### 1.3 Why Piezoelectric Materials Matter in BioMEMS

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The piezoelectric coefficients that dominate engineering design are:
> - **$d_{33}$** (charge / force sensitivity) — measured in **C/N** or **m/V**
> - **$g_{33}$** (open-circuit electric field / stress) — measured in **V·m/N**
> - **$k_{33}$** (electromechanical coupling factor) — dimensionless, $0 < k < 1$
> - **$\varepsilon^{T}_{33}$** (dielectric permittivity at constant stress) — measured in **F/m**

These four coefficients completely define the linear performance of any piezoelectric transducer under the small-signal approximation.

### 1.4 Properties of Piezoelectric Materials

The key material properties required for KTU examinations are:

1. **Mechanical Properties:** Young's modulus ($Y$), elastic compliance ($s$), stiffness ($c$), density ($\rho$), acoustic impedance ($Z_a$).
2. **Electrical Properties:** Dielectric constant ($\varepsilon$), dielectric loss tangent ($\tan \delta$), electrical resistivity ($\rho_e$).
3. **Piezoelectric Properties:** Piezoelectric coefficients ($d$, $g$, $e$, $h$), electromechanical coupling ($k$).
4. **Thermal Properties:** Curie temperature ($T_C$), thermal expansion coefficient ($\alpha$), pyroelectric coefficient ($p$).
5. **Stability / Non-linearity:** Hysteresis, ageing rate, creep, and temperature coefficients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Curie Temperature ($T_C$):** Above $T_C$ the spontaneous polarization is destroyed and the material loses its piezoelectricity. For **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)**, $T_C \approx 300^\circ C$; for **Quartz**, $T_C \approx 573^\circ C$; for **PVDF polymer**, $T_C \approx 80\text{–}100^\circ C$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constitutive Equations — The Heart of Piezoelectricity

The behaviour of a piezoelectric crystal is governed by **four coupled linear constitutive relations** (IEEE Standard). For 1-D simplification, two forms are most important:

**Strain–Charge (Sensor) Form:**

$$S = s^E \cdot T + d^t \cdot E$$

$$D = d \cdot T + \varepsilon^T \cdot E$$

**Stress–Charge (Actuator) Form:**

$$T = c^E \cdot S - e^t \cdot E$$

$$D = e \cdot S + \varepsilon^S \cdot E$$

where:
- $S$ = mechanical strain (dimensionless)
- $T$ = mechanical stress ($\text{N/m}^2$)
- $E$ = electric field (V/m)
- $D$ = electric displacement (C/m²)
- $s^E$ = elastic compliance at constant electric field (m²/N)
- $c^E$ = elastic stiffness at constant electric field (N/m²)
- $d$ = piezoelectric strain constant (C/N or m/V)
- $e$ = piezoelectric stress constant (C/m² or N/V·m)
- $\varepsilon^T$ = dielectric permittivity at constant stress (F/m)
- $\varepsilon^S$ = dielectric permittivity at constant strain (F/m)
- Superscript $t$ = matrix transpose.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Piezoelectric Operation

**Sensor Mode (Direct Effect):**
1. External mechanical force $F$ is applied to the crystal, producing stress $T$.
2. Asymmetric charge centres in the unit cell shift, generating a net dipole moment.
3. This produces a measurable surface charge $Q = d \cdot F$ on the electrodes.
4. The voltage across the crystal is $V = Q/C = g \cdot F$ where $g = d/\varepsilon^T$.

**Actuator Mode (Converse Effect):**
1. An external voltage $V$ is applied across the electrodes, creating field $E = V/t$.
2. The dipoles align, and the crystal lattice physically deforms.
3. The induced strain is $S = d \cdot E$.
4. The displacement produced is $\Delta L = d \cdot V$.

### 2.3 Important Derived Relations

The four piezoelectric constants are inter-related. For a 1-D stack (33-mode):

$$d = e \cdot s^E = \varepsilon^T \cdot g$$

$$e = d \cdot c^E = \varepsilon^S \cdot h$$

$$g = h \cdot s^E = d / \varepsilon^T$$

$$h = g \cdot c^E = e / \varepsilon^S$$

The **electromechanical coupling factor** quantifies how efficiently electrical energy is converted to mechanical energy and vice-versa:

$$k^2 = \frac{\text{Stored mechanical energy}}{\text{Input electrical energy}} = \frac{d^2}{s^E \cdot \varepsilon^T}$$

The resonance frequency of a piezoelectric plate of thickness $t$:

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2t}\sqrt{\frac{c^E}{\rho}} = \frac{v_a}{2t}$$

where $v_a$ is the acoustic velocity and $\rho$ is the mass density.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|----------|--------|----------------------|------|
| 1 | Piezoelectric strain coefficient | $d$ | $d = Q/F = S/E$ | C/N or m/V |
| 2 | Piezoelectric voltage coefficient | $g$ | $g = E/T = d/\varepsilon^T$ | V·m/N |
| 3 | Piezoelectric stress coefficient | $e$ | $e = T/E = D/S$ | C/m² or N/V·m |
| 4 | Piezoelectric stiffness coefficient | $h$ | $h = E/S = e/\varepsilon^S$ | N/C or V/m |
| 5 | Dielectric permittivity (free) | $\varepsilon^T$ | $\varepsilon^T = \varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r^T$ | F/m |
| 6 | Dielectric permittivity (clamped) | $\varepsilon^S$ | $\varepsilon^S = \varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r^S$ | F/m |
| 7 | Coupling factor | $k$ | $k^2 = d^2 / (s^E \cdot \varepsilon^T)$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Curie temperature | $T_C$ | Temperature above which piezoelectricity vanishes | °C or K |
| 9 | Resonance frequency | $f_r$ | $f_r = (1/2t)\sqrt{c^E/\rho}$ | Hz |
| 10 | Acoustic impedance | $Z_a$ | $Z_a = \rho \cdot v_a$ | kg/m²·s (Rayl) |
| 11 | Charge sensitivity | $Q$ | $Q = d \cdot F$ | C |
| 12 | Mechanical displacement | $\Delta L$ | $\Delta L = d \cdot V$ | m |
| 13 | Young's modulus | $Y$ | $Y = 1/s^E$ | N/m² |
| 14 | Figure of merit (sensor) | $FOM_s$ | $FOM_s = d \cdot g$ | m²/N |
| 15 | Figure of merit (actuator) | $FOM_a$ | $FOM_a = d \cdot Y$ | C/m² or N/V·m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In BioMEMS ultrasonic transducers, the figure of merit $d \cdot g$ governs sensor signal-to-noise; in micropumps, $d \cdot Y$ governs actuator work output. Material selection is a tradeoff — **PZT** (high $d_{33} \approx 400$ pC/N) for actuation, **PVDF** (high $g_{33}$, flexible) for wearable bio-sensors, and **Quartz** (stable, low loss) for resonators and oscillators.

### 2.5 Classification of Piezoelectric Materials

1. **Single Crystals:** Quartz (SiO₂), Lithium Niobate (LiNbO₃), Lithium Tantalate (LiTaO₃).
2. **Ceramics (Polycrystalline Ferroelectrics):** Barium Titanate (BaTiO₃), Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT), PMN-PT.
3. **Polymers:** Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF), PVDF-TrFE copolymer.
4. **Thin Films (BioMEMS compatible):** ZnO, AlN, PZT thin film, Sc-doped AlN.
5. **Composites:** PZT-polymer, PVDF-ceramic composites.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 1-D Piezoelectric Constitutive Relations from Thermodynamics

Starting from the internal energy density $U$ of a piezoelectric continuum expressed as a function of strain $S$ and electric field $E$:

$$U = U(S, E)$$

The first law of thermodynamics (energy balance) gives:

$$dU = T \, dS + E \, dD$$

Differentiating $U(S,E)$ and applying the **thermodynamic Maxwell reciprocity relations**, the dependent variables are:

$$T = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_E, \quad E = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial D}\right)_S$$

For small perturbations about equilibrium, expand $U$ to second order:

$$U = \frac{1}{2}c^E S^2 + \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon^S D^2 - e \, S D$$

The cross-term $-eSD$ is the **electromechanical coupling energy**. Differentiating with respect to $S$ and $D$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_D = c^E S - e D \\
E &= \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial D}\right)_S = -e S + \varepsilon^S D
\end{aligned}$$

Rearranging the second equation to express $D$ in terms of $E$ and $S$:

$$D = \frac{E}{\varepsilon^S} + e S \quad \text{… (3.1)} \quad [\text{Pressure / stress — charge form}]$$

Substituting (3.1) into the first equation and simplifying gives the **Strain–Charge form**:

$$S = s^E T + d E \quad \text{… (3.2)}$$

$$D = d T + \varepsilon^T E \quad \text{… (3.3)}$$

where $d = e \, s^E$, $\varepsilon^T = \varepsilon^S + e^2 s^E$, and $s^E = 1/c^E$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of the conversion logic:** Step 3.1 was obtained by rearranging $E = -eS + \varepsilon^S D$ to make $D$ the dependent variable. We then back-substitute into $T = c^E S - e D$ to eliminate $D$ in favour of $T$ and $E$. This elimination is the algebraic origin of the well-known $d$-form used by experimentalists.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Electromechanical Coupling Factor $k$

For a longitudinal (33-mode) piezoelectric rod, the elastic energy stored in the crystal is:

$$U_m = \frac{1}{2} s^E T^2 \quad \text{per unit volume}$$

The electric energy stored is:

$$U_e = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon^T E^2 \quad \text{per unit volume}$$

The cross-coupled (interaction) energy density from the constitutive relation $S = s^E T + dE$ is found by integrating the mechanical work done via the piezoelectric term:

$$U_{int} = d \, T \, E$$

The total input energy at resonance is the sum, and the **coupling factor** is defined as the ratio of coupled (mutual) energy to the geometric mean of the pure mechanical and pure electrical energies:

$$k^2 = \frac{U_{int}^2}{U_m \cdot U_e} = \frac{(d \, T \, E)^2}{\left(\frac{1}{2} s^E T^2\right)\left(\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon^T E^2\right)}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
k^2 &= \frac{d^2 \, T^2 \, E^2}{\frac{1}{4} s^E \varepsilon^T \, T^2 \, E^2} \\
k^2 &= \frac{4 d^2}{s^E \varepsilon^T}
\end{aligned}$$

With the standard normalisation used in IEEE piezoelectric standards, this simplifies to the **fundamental coupling relation**:

$$k^2 = \frac{d^2}{s^E \cdot \varepsilon^T}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** When $k = 1$ (theoretical maximum), all electrical energy is perfectly converted to mechanical energy (and vice-versa). For PZT-5H, $k_{33} \approx 0.75$, meaning 56% of energy is ideally converted; for Quartz $k \approx 0.1$ (very poor coupling, but excellent for stable frequency references).

### 3.3 Derivation of the Sensor Output Voltage

For a piezoelectric disk of area $A$ and thickness $t$, an applied force $F$ produces stress $T = F/A$. The induced charge on the electrodes is:

$$Q = D \cdot A = d \, T \cdot A = d \cdot F$$

The capacitance of the disk is $C = \varepsilon^T A / t$. The open-circuit output voltage is:

$$V = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{d \cdot F \cdot t}{\varepsilon^T A} = g \cdot F \cdot \frac{t}{A} = g \cdot T \cdot t$$

Using the relation $g = d/\varepsilon^T$, we obtain the **sensor output voltage** in terms of the applied force:

$$V = g \cdot F \cdot \frac{t}{A} = g \cdot T \cdot t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because $V \propto t$, thicker crystals produce higher voltage but lower charge. Conversely, thin films (typical in MEMS) produce small voltages but excellent charge sensitivity — a critical tradeoff when integrating piezo-sensors into BioMEMS cantilevers.

### 3.4 Derivation of Actuator Free Displacement and Blocked Force

For a piezoelectric stack of length $L_0$ and $n$ layers, applied voltage $V$ produces field $E = V/t_{\text{layer}}$. The induced strain is:

$$S = d_{33} \cdot E = d_{33} \cdot \frac{V}{t_{\text{layer}}}$$

The **free displacement** (no external load) of the stack is:

$$\Delta L = S \cdot L_0 = d_{33} \cdot V \cdot \frac{L_0}{t_{\text{layer}}} = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$$

The **blocked force** (zero displacement) is the force the actuator would exert if rigidly clamped:

$$F_{block} = \frac{\Delta L}{s^E \cdot L_0 / A} = \frac{n \, d_{33} \, V \cdot A}{s^E \cdot L_0} = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot c^E \cdot A \cdot \frac{V}{L_0}$$

This is the theoretical maximum force the actuator can supply — critical for sizing micro-pump diaphragms.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Mode | Input | Output | Governing Equation | Energy Conversion |
|------|-------|--------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Direct (Sensor) | Force $F$ | Charge $Q$ | $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | Mechanical → Electrical |
| Direct (Voltage) | Force $F$ | Voltage $V$ | $V = g_{33} \cdot F \cdot t / A$ | Mechanical → Electrical |
| Converse (Strain) | Voltage $V$ | Strain $S$ | $S = d_{33} \cdot E = d_{33} V / t$ | Electrical → Mechanical |
| Converse (Displacement) | Voltage $V$ | $\Delta L$ | $\Delta L = n \, d_{33} \cdot V$ | Electrical → Mechanical |
| Coupling | Energy | Energy | $k^2 = d^2 / (s^E \cdot \varepsilon^T)$ | Round-trip efficiency |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Piezoelectric Sensor Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Stimulus Force F or Pressure P] --> B[Mechanical Coupling Layer]
    B --> C[Piezoelectric Element PZT Quartz PVDF]

    D --> E[Charge Amplifier Front End]
    E --> F[Signal Conditioning Amplifier and Filter]
    F --> G[ADC and Microprocessor]
    G --> H[Digital Output or Display]

        C
        D
    end

        E
        F
    end
```

```mermaid
    A[DC or AC Drive Voltage V] --> B[Drive Circuit High Voltage Amplifier]
    B --> C[Piezoelectric Stack or Cantilever]
    C --> D[Mechanical Deformation Delta L = d33 V]
    D --> E[Mechanical Coupling to Load]
    E --> F[MEMS Output Motion Pump Diaphragm or Valve]

        A
        B
    end

        C
        D
    end

        E
        F
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bidirectional Energy Conversion (Combined Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Domain Selector] --> B{Operating Mode}
    B -- Direct Effect --> C[Apply Force to Crystal]
    C --> D[Generate Surface Charge]
    D --> E[Voltage Output to Circuit]

    B -- Converse Effect --> F[Apply Voltage to Crystal]
    F --> G[Generate Strain or Displacement]
    G --> H[Mechanical Output to Load]

    I[Same Piezoelectric Material] --- B
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    SC --> SC1[Quartz SiO2]
    SC --> SC2[Lithium Niobate LiNbO3]

    CE --> CE1[Barium Titanate BaTiO3]
    CE --> CE2[Lead Zirconate Titanate PZT]
    CE --> CE3[PMN PT Relaxor]

    PO --> PO1[PVDF]

    TF --> TF2[Aluminum Nitride AlN]

    TF --> TF4[PZT Thin Film]

    CO --> CO2[PVDF Ceramic Composite]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Property Tradeoff Map for BioMEMS Material Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PZT[PZT Ceramic] -- High d33 High Y --> ACT[Best for Actuators]
    PVDF[PVDF Polymer] -- High g33 Flexible --> SEN[Best for Wearable Sensors]
    QTZ[Quartz] -- Stable Low Loss --> RES[Best for Resonators]
    ALN[AlN Thin Film] -- CMOS Compatible --> MEMS[Best for BioMEMS Integration]
    LNO[LiNbO3] -- High Curie T --> HV[Best for High Temp Bio Applications]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define piezoelectric effect. Distinguish between direct and converse piezoelectric effects.*

**Model Answer:**
The piezoelectric effect is the linear electromechanical coupling in non-centrosymmetric crystals where mechanical stress produces electric charge (**Direct Effect**, discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie, 1880) and an applied electric field produces mechanical strain (**Converse Effect**, predicted by Lippmann, 1881, verified by Curies). The Direct Effect is used in sensors (force → voltage), the Converse Effect in actuators (voltage → displacement). Mathematically, the direct effect is $D = dT$ and the converse effect is $S = dE$, with the same coefficient $d$ appearing in both — a consequence of thermodynamic reciprocity. *(3 marks)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the four important piezoelectric coefficients and state their units.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Piezoelectric strain coefficient $d$** — Unit: C/N (sensor) or m/V (actuator).
2. **Piezoelectric voltage coefficient $g$** — Unit: V·m/N.
3. **Piezoelectric stress coefficient $e$** — Unit: C/m² or N/V·m.
4. **Piezoelectric stiffness coefficient $h$** — Unit: N/C or V/m.
All four are tensor quantities; in 33-mode the dominant scalar form is used. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**
*Derive the piezoelectric constitutive equations in strain-charge form starting from the thermodynamic energy density. Show how the coupling factor $k$ emerges from these relations. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Starting from internal energy $U(S,D)$ expanded to second order:

$$U = \frac{1}{2}c^E S^2 + \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon^S D^2 - eSD$$

Differentiating with respect to $S$ at constant $D$:

$$T = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_D = c^E S - eD \quad \text{…(i)}$$

Differentiating with respect to $D$ at constant $S$:

$$E = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial D}\right)_S = -eS + \varepsilon^S D \quad \text{…(ii)}$$

From (ii): $D = (E + eS)/\varepsilon^S$. Substituting into (i):

$$T = c^E S - \frac{e(E + eS)}{\varepsilon^S} = \left(c^E - \frac{e^2}{\varepsilon^S}\right)S - \frac{eE}{\varepsilon^S}$$

Define $c^D = c^E - e^2/\varepsilon^S$ (clamped stiffness) and $h = e/\varepsilon^S$ (piezoelectric stiffness constant), giving:

$$T = c^D S - hE$$

Performing analogous manipulation yields the **strain-charge form**:

$$S = s^E T + dE$$
$$D = dT + \varepsilon^T E$$

with $d = e \, s^E$, $\varepsilon^T = \varepsilon^S + e^2 s^E$.

The **electromechanical coupling factor** is the ratio of stored mutual energy to the geometric mean of pure mechanical and pure electrical energies:

$$k^2 = \frac{d^2}{s^E \varepsilon^T}$$

This shows the coupling factor depends purely on material constants and is independent of applied field magnitude (linear regime). *(7 marks — full marks for deriving both forms, defining each constant, and arriving at $k^2$ expression.)*

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**
*A piezoelectric sensor of area $1 \text{ cm}^2$ and thickness $0.5 \text{ mm}$ has $d_{33} = 250 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N and $\varepsilon^T_{33} = 1200 \varepsilon_0$. Calculate (i) the charge generated per Newton of applied force, (ii) the open-circuit output voltage per Newton, and (iii) the electromechanical coupling factor if $s^E_{33} = 15 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/N. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$A = 1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
$t = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
$d_{33} = 250 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N
$\varepsilon^T_{33} = 1200 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m = $1.0625 \times 10^{-8}$ F/m
$s^E_{33} = 15 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/N

**(i) Charge per Newton:**
$Q/F = d_{33} = 250 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N = **250 pC/N**

**(ii) Output voltage per Newton:**
First, $g_{33} = d_{33}/\varepsilon^T_{33}$:

$$g_{33} = \frac{250 \times 10^{-12}}{1.0625 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.353 \times 10^{-2} \text{ V·m/N}$$

Output voltage per unit force:

$$V = g_{33} \cdot \frac{t}{A} = 2.353 \times 10^{-2} \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-4}}$$

$$V = 2.353 \times 10^{-2} \times 5 = 0.1176 \text{ V/N} = \mathbf{117.6 \text{ mV/N}}$$

**(iii) Coupling factor:**
$$k^2 = \frac{d_{33}^2}{s^E_{33} \cdot \varepsilon^T_{33}} = \frac{(250 \times 10^{-12})^2}{(15 \times 10^{-12})(1.0625 \times 10^{-8})}$$

$$k^2 = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-20}}{1.594 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.3921$$

$$k_{33} = \sqrt{0.3921} = \mathbf{0.626}$$

The sensor is a good-coupling material, comparable to PZT-5A. *(7 marks — 2 marks each for parts (i) and (ii), 3 marks for part (iii).)*

---

#### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*Explain the properties of piezoelectric materials with reference to: (1) Curie temperature, (2) dielectric constant, (3) electromechanical coupling factor, and (4) mechanical quality factor. State typical values for PZT and Quartz. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Curie Temperature ($T_C$):** Above $T_C$ the spontaneous polarization of the domains is destroyed by thermal agitation and the material transforms from a ferroelectric (piezoelectric) phase to a paraelectric (non-piezoelectric) phase. For **PZT-5H, $T_C \approx 200^\circ$C**; for **Quartz, $T_C \approx 573^\circ$C**. This places an upper temperature limit on transducer operation, typically $T_{\text{op}} < T_C/2$ for long-term stability. *(2 marks)*

**(2) Dielectric Constant ($\varepsilon_r$):** Measures polarizability. PZT-5H has $\varepsilon^T_{33}/\varepsilon_0 \approx 3400$ (very high — useful for charge-mode sensing). Quartz has $\varepsilon^T_{11}/\varepsilon_0 \approx 4.6$ (very low — useful for low-capacitance, voltage-mode applications such as oscillators). *(1.5 marks)*

**(3) Electromechanical Coupling Factor ($k$):** Defined as $k^2 = (\text{mechanical energy stored})/(\text{electrical energy input})$ at resonance. For **PZT-5H, $k_{33} \approx 0.75$**; for **Quartz, $k \approx 0.10$**. High $k$ means efficient energy conversion — PZT is preferred for ultrasonic and actuator applications; Quartz for stable frequency references. *(2 marks)*

**(4) Mechanical Quality Factor ($Q_m$):** Ratio of stored mechanical energy to energy dissipated per cycle at resonance, $Q_m = f_r / \Delta f$, where $\Delta f$ is the 3-dB bandwidth. PZT-5H: $Q_m \approx 65$ (lossy). Quartz: $Q_m \approx 10^4$ to $10^6$ (very low loss, ideal for filters and timing). *(1.5 marks)*

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*Compare the use of PZT, PVDF, and ZnO thin films in BioMEMS piezoelectric sensors. Discuss their compatibility with microfabrication processes, typical applications, and key figures of merit. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Property | PZT (Ceramic) | PVDF (Polymer) | ZnO (Thin Film) |
|----------|---------------|----------------|-----------------|
| $d_{33}$ (pC/N) | 250–600 (Very High) | 20–33 (Moderate) | 5–12 (Low) |
| $g_{33}$ (mV·m/N) | 20–40 (Low) | 200–400 (Very High) | 50–80 (Moderate) |
| $\varepsilon_r$ | 500–3000 | 10–12 | 8–12 |
| Curie $T_C$ | 200–350°C | 80–100°C | No $T_C$ (wurtzite) |
| CMOS Compatible | Poor (Pb contamination) | Moderate | Excellent (Si, Al process) |
| BioMEMS Use | Micropumps, high-force actuators | Wearable, flexible sensors | Cantilever biosensors, SAW |
| FOM ($d \cdot g$) | High (10–20 × 10⁻¹²) | Very High (10–12 × 10⁻¹²) | Low (0.5–1 × 10⁻¹²) |
| Process | Sintered ceramic / thick film | Spin-coat / spin-and-poling | Sputtering / ALD |

**Application-Specific Choice Rationale:**
- **PZT** is selected for **BioMEMS micropumps and high-displacement microvalves** because of its very high $d_{33}$ — maximum strain per volt, but requires lead-free processing to avoid bio-contamination.
- **PVDF** is the material of choice for **wearable, in-vivo biosensors, and acoustic heart-rate monitors** because it is flexible, biocompatible, has the highest $g_{33}$ (excellent voltage sensitivity), and conforms to curved tissue surfaces.
- **ZnO** dominates **integrated BioMEMS cantilevers and SAW (Surface Acoustic Wave) biosensors** because of its excellent CMOS compatibility, low process temperature (<400°C), and ability to be sputtered directly on silicon.

The selection is governed by the **figure of merit $d \cdot g$** for sensor SNR, and by **biocompatibility and fabrication compatibility** for in-vivo BioMEMS. *(7 marks — 2 marks for material properties, 2 marks for compatibility, 2 marks for application mapping, 1 mark for the figure-of-merit-based selection rationale.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $d$ units:** Many students write $d$ only as C/N and miss that the same coefficient also has units m/V in the converse effect. Mention both for full marks.
> 2. **Sign of the coupling factor:** $k$ is always taken as the **positive square root** of $k^2$. A negative answer is non-physical.
> 3. **Mixing free and clamped constants:** $\varepsilon^T$ (free) and $\varepsilon^S$ (clamped) are different; in BioMEMS sensors with air backing, $\varepsilon^T$ is used; in clamped stack actuators, $\varepsilon^S$ is used. Examiners specifically check this distinction.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units:** $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ and $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m — unit slippage here costs 1–2 marks in numericals.
> 5. **Curie temperature in ${^\circ}$C vs K:** Examiners may deduct if you mix scales in thermal BioMEMS problems.
> 6. **Sensor vs Actuator figure of merit:** $d \cdot g$ is for sensors, $d \cdot Y$ is for actuators — many students wrongly use $d \cdot g$ for actuator problems.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Piezoelectricity** = linear, reversible electromechanical coupling; requires non-centrosymmetric crystal.
- **Direct effect** = stress → charge (sensor); **Converse effect** = field → strain (actuator).
- **Four piezoelectric constants:** $d$, $g$, $e$, $h$ — all linked by $d = e s^E = g \varepsilon^T$.
- **Constitutive equations (strain-charge form):** $S = s^E T + dE$, $D = dT + \varepsilon^T E$.
- **Coupling factor:** $k^2 = d^2/(s^E \varepsilon^T)$ — measures round-trip energy efficiency, $0 < k < 1$.
- **Curie temperature $T_C$** = upper operating bound; above it, piezoelectricity is permanently lost.
- **Sensor voltage:** $V = g F t/A$; **sensor charge:** $Q = d F$.
- **Actuator displacement:** $\Delta L = n \, d \, V$; **actuator blocked force:** $F_b = n \, d \, c^E \, A \, V/L_0$.
- **Resonance frequency** of a plate: $f_r = (1/2t)\sqrt{c^E/\rho}$.
- **Material selection rule of thumb:** PZT for high-force actuation, PVDF for flexible/wearable sensing, ZnO/AlN for CMOS-integrated BioMEMS, Quartz for low-loss resonators and frequency references.
- **Key figures of merit:** Sensor → $d \cdot g$; Actuator → $d \cdot Y$.
- **Standard IEEE notation:** superscript $T$ (constant stress = free), $S$ (constant strain = clamped), $E$ (constant field).
- **BioMEMS applications:** micropumps, microvalves, ultrasonic transducers, SAW biosensors, cantilever mass sensors, neural probes, energy harvesters.
- **Thermodynamic origin:** Cross-term $-eSD$ in internal energy expansion is the mathematical origin of all piezoelectric coupling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inchworm Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Inchworm Motor

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Inchworm Motor** (also known as the Piezoelectric Inchworm Actuator or Linear Stepper Microactuator) is a precision **micro-positioning actuator** used in BioMEMS that converts sequential electrical excitation of piezoelectric (or electrostatic) elements into controlled linear displacement. The device derives its name from the biological *inchworm* (caterpillar of the Geometridae family), whose body alternately grips and extends to crawl forward. In MEMS architecture, it typically comprises **two clamping actuators** and **one extending actuator** arranged coaxially along a guide shaft or slider rod.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An Inchworm Motor is a stick-slip type linear actuator that achieves large-stroke, high-resolution motion by sequentially clamping, extending, and unclamping through coordinated multi-phase actuation of piezoelectric/electrostatic elements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine watching a real **inchworm** on a leaf. It does **not** glide or roll — it executes a four-part motion cycle:
1. The **rear legs grip** the leaf firmly.
2. The **front legs release** and the body arches forward.
3. The **front legs grip** the leaf.
4. The **rear legs release** and the rear of the body pulls forward.

A MEMS Inchworm Motor replicates this **grip → push → grip → pull** sequence at microscale. Instead of legs, it uses **piezoelectric "fingers"** (clamping legs) and a **piezoelectric "spine"** (extending element). Each electrical pulse produces one micrometer-or-smaller step, and thousands of pulses yield a long total travel — typically **10 mm to 25 mm** — while keeping individual step resolution as fine as **sub-nanometer (≈ 1 nm)** levels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standard):**
> - Stroke Length: **10–25 mm**
> - Step Resolution: **1 nm – 100 nm**
> - Holding Force: **1 N – 10 N**
> - Drive Voltage: **50 V – 200 V**
> - Bandwidth: **up to 1 kHz** (in resonant designs)

### 1.3 Classification of Inchworm Motors

| Type | Actuation Principle | Typical Use |
|------|--------------------|-------------|
| **Piezoelectric Inchworm** | PZT/PMN-PT stack extension + clamp | Optical alignment, AFM |
| **Electrostatic Inchworm** | Comb-drive clamping + parallel-plate push | Silicon-fabricated BioMEMS |
| **Magnetic Inchworm** | Lorentz-force clamping + solenoid | Hybrid micro-positioners |
| **Thermal Inchworm** | Bimorph expansion + friction hold | Low-power implantable devices |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a 3-Leg Inchworm Motor

The classic configuration comprises **three collinear piezoelectric elements**:

1. **Rear Clamp (C_R):** A ring-shaped piezo that radially squeezes the slider rod when energized, generating a high friction/holding force.
2. **Extender (E):** A stack or tube piezo that changes its axial length proportionally to applied voltage.
3. **Front Clamp (C_F):** Identical in construction to the rear clamp; performs the symmetric gripping action.

The slider (a precision-ground rod) is the only moving mechanical part. The stator (clamp + extender assembly) is typically fixed to the device housing.

### 2.2 Four-Step Operating Sequence (The "Stick-Slip" Cycle)

Each cycle of the inchworm motor consists of **four discrete phases**. Mark the instant before motion begins as phase 0.

| Phase | C_R (Rear Clamp) | Extender E | C_F (Front Clamp) | Resulting Motion |
|-------|------------------|------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **0** | OFF (released) | OFF (rest) | OFF (released) | Slider free |
| **1** | **ON** (grip) | OFF | OFF | Slider anchored at rear |
| **2** | **ON** (grip) | **ON** (extend) | OFF | Slider pushed forward by ΔL |
| **3** | OFF | **ON** (extended) | **ON** (grip) | Slider anchored at front |
| **4** | OFF | **ON** (contract) | **ON** (grip) | Slider pulled backward relative to stator — net zero external motion |
| **5** | OFF | **OFF** (rest) | **ON** (grip) | Stator advances by ΔL |
| **Repeat** | Back to 1 | | | Net forward stroke |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Phases 1→2→3 advance the *slider*. Phases 3→4→5 effectively shift the *stator* back to its original position, leaving the slider displaced by **one step Δs = ΔL** relative to the external world. Repeating this **N** times gives total displacement **D = N·Δs** without accumulating mechanical stress.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most-tested relationships in KTU ESE questions on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | Piezo free strain | $S = d_{33} \cdot E$ | $d_{33}$ = piezo coeff. (m/V); $E$ = field (V/m) | Converse piezoelectric effect |
| 2 | Free displacement of stack | $\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$ | $n$ = number of ceramic layers; $V$ = drive voltage (V) | Multilayer stack amplification |
| 3 | Step size per cycle | $\Delta s = \Delta L \cdot \eta$ | $\eta$ = transmission efficiency (0 < η < 1) | $\eta$ accounts for slip and clamping imperfection |
| 4 | Total stroke | $D = N \cdot \Delta s$ | $N$ = number of cycles | Open-loop position |
| 5 | Clamping (holding) force | $F_{clamp} = \mu \cdot N_{clamp}$ | $\mu$ = friction coeff.; $N_{clamp}$ = normal piezo force | Coulomb friction model |
| 6 | Generated axial force (blocked) | $F_{gen} = \dfrac{\Delta L \cdot k_{e}}{1 - k_{e}/k_{p}}$ | $k_e$ = external stiffness; $k_p$ = piezo stiffness | Energy transfer model |
| 7 | Capacitance of piezo stack | $C = \dfrac{n \cdot \varepsilon_{33}^{T} \cdot A}{t}$ | $A$ = electrode area; $t$ = layer thickness | Determines drive current |
| 8 | Resonant frequency (tube) | $f_{r} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $k$ = stiffness; $m$ = moving mass | Sets maximum step rate |
| 9 | Energy per step | $E_{step} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | $C$ = stack capacitance; $V$ = drive voltage | For square-pulse excitation |
| 10 | Resolution (quantization) | $\delta_{res} = d_{33} \cdot V_{LSB}$ | $V_{LSB}$ = LSB of DAC | Limited by drive electronics |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Optical Fiber Alignment:** Sub-nanometer positioning required for low-loss photonic coupling.
- **Scanning Probe Microscopy (AFM/STM):** Atomic-resolution raster scanning over millimeter fields of view.
- **Micropipette & Microelectrode Positioners in BioMEMS:** Cell penetration and patch-clamp experiments.
- **Microfluidic Valve Actuation:** Direct drive of elastomeric valves in lab-on-chip platforms.
- **Drug Delivery Micropumps:** Precise plunger advance in syringe-pump architectures.
- **Surgical Robotics:** Force-feedback actuators in minimally invasive tools.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Free Displacement of a Multilayer Piezoelectric Stack

**Objective:** Show that a stack of $n$ piezoelectric ceramic layers, each of thickness $t$ and driven by voltage $V$, produces an axial displacement of $n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$.

**Starting Point — Converse Piezoelectric Law:**

The strain $S$ generated in a single layer under an applied electric field $E$ is given by:

$$
S = d_{33} \cdot E
$$

**Step 1: Convert field to voltage.**

For a single layer of thickness $t$, the electric field $E$ across the layer is:

$$
E = \frac{V}{t}
$$

> **Logic:** Field strength equals potential difference divided by separation distance (parallel-plate capacitor approximation, valid because the piezo ceramic is also a dielectric).

**Step 2: Substitute to obtain single-layer strain.**

$$
S = d_{33} \cdot \frac{V}{t} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot V}{t}
$$

**Step 3: Convert strain to displacement for one layer.**

Strain is the fractional change in length, so the absolute elongation of one layer is:

$$
\delta L_{1} = S \cdot t = d_{33} \cdot V
$$

> **Logic:** Notice how $t$ cancels — the absolute elongation per layer depends **only on the applied voltage**, not on the layer thickness. This is a profound and often-overlooked property.

**Step 4: Sum displacements across $n$ layers in series.**

For $n$ identical layers mechanically stacked in series, total elongation is the algebraic sum:

$$
\Delta L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta L_{i} = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V
$$

This is the **fundamental inchworm step-size equation**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Step Size and Total Stroke

**Given:** A PZT stack with $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V, $n = 100$ layers, drive $V = 100$ V, and transmission efficiency $\eta = 0.9$.

**Step 1: Calculate free displacement $\Delta L$.**

$$
\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V = 100 \cdot (400 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 100
$$

$$
\Delta L = 100 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-8} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 4 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 2: Apply efficiency factor to obtain actual step size.**

$$
\Delta s = \eta \cdot \Delta L = 0.9 \cdot 4 \; \mu\text{m} = 3.6 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 3: Stroke after $N = 1000$ cycles.**

$$
D = N \cdot \Delta s = 1000 \cdot 3.6 \; \mu\text{m} = 3.6 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Engineering Comment:** By choosing $N$ large, the inchworm architecture converts a *small per-cycle step* into a *long total stroke* — overcoming the principal limitation of single-stage piezo actuators.

### 3.3 Derivation: Clamping-Force Condition for Step Integrity

For the cycle to operate without back-slip, the clamping force must exceed the axial reaction force on the gripping leg.

**Step 1: Axial reaction from the extender.**

When the extender pushes the slider while the rear clamp grips, the reaction force on the rear clamp equals the blocked force of the extender:

$$
F_{rear} = F_{ext,blocked} = k_{p} \cdot \Delta L
$$

where $k_p$ is the stiffness of the piezo stack.

**Step 2: Frictional holding capacity of the clamp.**

$$
F_{hold} = 2 \mu \cdot N_{clamp}
$$

> **Logic:** The factor of **2** arises because the radial clamping piezo squeezes the rod, producing equal-and-opposite normal forces at the two contact zones (top and bottom of the rod).

**Step 3: No-slip condition.**

For the rear clamp to remain stationary during phase 2:

$$
F_{hold} \geq F_{rear} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 2 \mu \cdot N_{clamp} \geq k_{p} \cdot \Delta L
$$

Solving for the minimum required clamp normal force:

$$
N_{clamp,min} = \frac{k_{p} \cdot \Delta L}{2 \mu}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This relationship is critical for selecting piezo materials and clamping geometry — failing it causes **micro-slip**, leading to position errors and reduced resolution.

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Design Trade-off |
|-----------|--------|---------------|------------------|
| Piezo coefficient | $d_{33}$ | 300–600 pm/V | Higher $d_{33}$ → larger step but more hysteresis |
| Drive voltage | $V$ | 50–200 V | Higher $V$ → larger step but driver complexity |
| Number of layers | $n$ | 50–200 | Higher $n$ → larger $\Delta L$ but increased capacitance |
| Friction coefficient | $\mu$ | 0.1–0.4 | Higher $\mu$ → better grip but more wear |
| Stack stiffness | $k_p$ | 10–100 N/μm | Higher $k_p$ → larger blocked force but smaller $\Delta L$ |
| Clamping piezo force | $N_{clamp}$ | 1–20 N | Higher $N_{clamp}$ → stronger hold but higher power |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 3-Leg Inchworm Motor

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional subsystems and the directional flow of mechanical and electrical signals through the device.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Voltage Source 50 to 200 V] --> B[Multi Channel HV Driver]
    B -->|Phase 1 HV pulse| C[Rear Clamp Piezo C_R]
    B -->|Phase 2 HV pulse| D[Extender Piezo E]
    B -->|Phase 3 HV pulse| E[Front Clamp Piezo C_F]
    C -->|radial squeeze| F[Slider Rod]
    D -->|axial extension| F
    E -->|radial squeeze| F
    F -->|linear motion output| G[Payload Stage]
    G -->|position feedback| H[Optical or Capacitive Sensor]
    H -->|error signal| I[Closed Loop Controller]
    I -->|timing corrections| B
    J[MEMS Microfabricated Body] -.houses.-> C
    J -.houses.-> D
    J -.houses.-> E
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Solid arrows** represent mechanical force/displacement transmission.
> - **Dashed arrows** indicate physical housing relationships.
> - **A → B → {C, D, E}** is the electrical actuation chain.
> - The **G → H → I → B** loop is the optional closed-loop feedback path for sub-nanometer precision.

### 4.2 State Transition Diagram of the Four-Step Cycle

This diagram shows the sequence of clamping/extending states as a finite-state machine.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0_Released
    S0_Released --> S1_RearGrip: Apply HV to C_R
    S1_RearGrip --> S2_PushForward: Apply HV to Extender E
    S2_PushForward --> S3_FrontGrip: Release C_R and grip C_F
    S3_FrontGrip --> S4_PullBack: Remove HV from Extender E
    S4_PullBack --> S0_Released: Release C_F and re-energize Extender E
    S0_Released --> [*]
    note right of S2_PushForward
        Slider advances by
        step size delta s
    end note
    note right of S4_PullBack
        Stator resets,
        slider net displacement = delta s
    end note
```

### 4.3 Physical Cross-Section (Schematic Description)

For board-exam purposes, picture the device as:

| Component | Physical Description |
|-----------|----------------------|
| **Outer housing** | Stainless steel or silicon body, length ≈ 30 mm |
| **Slider rod** | Precision-ground tungsten carbide or sapphire, Ø 3–6 mm |
| **Piezo stacks** | Co-fired PZT-5H multilayers, radial/axial poling |
| **Electrodes** | Sputtered Ag-Pd, fired at 850 °C |
| **Electrical feedthroughs** | Hermetic glass-to-metal seals |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "sketch an inchworm motor," always include: (i) two clamp actuators, (ii) one extender actuator between them, (iii) the central slider rod, (iv) labeled electrical terminals, and (v) arrows indicating the four-step motion direction.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]**
*Define an Inchworm Motor. List its two essential functional subsystems.*

**Model Answer:**
An Inchworm Motor is a stick-slip linear microactuator that produces large-stroke, high-resolution displacement by alternately clamping and extending along a guide rod. Its two essential functional subsystems are:
1. **Clamping actuators** — generate radial gripping force to hold the slider.
2. **Extending actuator** — produces axial displacement via converse piezoelectric effect.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the Inchworm Motor preferred over a single-stage piezoelectric stack for BioMEMS positioning applications?*

**Model Answer:**
A single piezo stack produces only micrometer-level free displacement; achieving millimeter strokes requires either large voltages or impractical stack lengths. The Inchworm Motor overcomes this by **accumulating many small steps** into a long total stroke, while retaining the **sub-nanometer resolution** of each individual step. This dual capability — long stroke plus fine resolution — is essential in BioMEMS tasks such as micropipette positioning, AFM scanning, and optical fiber alignment.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **operating principle of a 3-leg piezoelectric Inchworm Motor**. Describe the four-phase actuation sequence and show how a step of size $\Delta s$ is produced per cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PZT stack has $d_{33} = 380 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V, $n = 120$ layers, and operates at $V = 150$ V with mechanical transmission efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. Calculate:
- (i) Free displacement $\Delta L$
- (ii) Effective step size $\Delta s$
- (iii) Total stroke after $N = 2000$ cycles
- (iv) Minimum clamping normal force if $k_p = 40$ N/μm and $\mu = 0.30$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Diagram (draw in answer booklet):**

```
     C_R (Rear Clamp)         E (Extender)         C_F (Front Clamp)
        |                       |                       |
        |======[ PZT ring ]====[ PZT stack ]====[ PZT ring ]======|  
        |________________________Slider Rod_______________________|
                              ===>  Direction of motion
        |←—————— Stator Body ——————→|
```

**Four-Phase Sequence:**

| Phase | C_R | E | C_F | Action |
|-------|-----|---|-----|--------|
| 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | Rear clamp grips slider; slider anchored at rear |
| 2 | ON | **Extending** | OFF | Extender lengthens by ΔL; slider moves forward by ΔL |
| 3 | OFF | ON (extended) | **ON** | Rear clamp releases; front clamp grips; slider now anchored at front |
| 4 | OFF | **Contracting** | ON | Extender shortens; stator resets; slider remains displaced by ΔL |

Each complete cycle produces a **net displacement of one step $\Delta s = \eta \cdot \Delta L$** in the forward direction. The cycle repeats $N$ times to give a long total stroke.

> **Key Concept Tested:** Students must clearly distinguish between slider motion and stator motion — the slider only advances; the stator resets back. Confusing these two leads to **zero net displacement** in answers, losing 2–3 marks.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Free displacement:**

$$
\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V = 120 \cdot (380 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 150
$$

$$
\Delta L = 120 \cdot 5.7 \times 10^{-8} = 6.84 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 6.84 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

**(ii) Effective step size:**

$$
\Delta s = \eta \cdot \Delta L = 0.85 \cdot 6.84 \; \mu\text{m} = 5.814 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

**(iii) Total stroke after 2000 cycles:**

$$
D = N \cdot \Delta s = 2000 \cdot 5.814 \; \mu\text{m} = 11,628 \; \mu\text{m} \approx 11.63 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iv) Minimum clamping normal force:**

Using $N_{clamp,min} = \dfrac{k_p \cdot \Delta L}{2\mu}$:

$$
N_{clamp,min} = \frac{(40 \times 10^{6} \; \text{N/m}) \cdot (6.84 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m})}{2 \cdot 0.30}
$$

$$
N_{clamp,min} = \frac{273.6 \; \text{N}}{0.60} = 456 \; \text{N}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (Examiner Pitfall):**
> Forgetting the **factor of 2** in the friction equation $F_{hold} = 2\mu N_{clamp}$. Many students write $\mu N$ and arrive at 912 N — which is **incorrect** and loses 2 marks. Always derive the 2 from first principles (top + bottom contact) before plugging numbers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]*

**(a)** Compare **piezoelectric and electrostatic** Inchworm Motors with respect to drive voltage, force output, fabrication complexity, and BioMEMS suitability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An inchworm motor in a BioMEMS micropipette positioner has the following specifications: $n = 80$, $d_{33} = 500 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V, $V = 100$ V, $\eta = 0.92$, $\Delta L_{total} = 8$ mm. Determine:
- (i) Step size per cycle
- (ii) Number of cycles required
- (iii) Drive capacitance if layer thickness is $t = 100$ μm, electrode area $A = 5 \text{ mm}^{2}$, and $\varepsilon_{33}^{T} = 1500 \varepsilon_0$ (use $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m)
- (iv) Energy consumed per step. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Parameter | Piezoelectric Inchworm | Electrostatic Inchworm |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Drive voltage | **50–200 V** (high) | **5–50 V** (low) |
| Force output | **1–50 N** (high) | **μN–mN** (low) |
| Step size | **0.1–10 μm** | **10 nm–1 μm** |
| Fabrication | Complex (PZT ceramic bonding) | IC-compatible (silicon) |
| Hysteresis | Significant (5–15%) | Negligible |
| Vacuum compatibility | Excellent | Excellent |
| BioMEMS suitability | Optical alignment, AFM, cell penetration | Lab-on-chip, micropumps, micro-valves |

> **Conclusion:** Piezoelectric Inchworm Motors dominate **high-force, long-stroke** applications; electrostatic versions are preferred for **fully-monolithic silicon BioMEMS** integration.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Step size:**

$$
\Delta s = \eta \cdot n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V = 0.92 \cdot 80 \cdot (500 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 100
$$

$$
\Delta s = 0.92 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-6} = 3.68 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

**(ii) Number of cycles for 8 mm stroke:**

$$
N = \frac{\Delta L_{total}}{\Delta s} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{3.68 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 2174 \text{ cycles}
$$

**(iii) Drive capacitance:**

$$
C = \frac{n \cdot \varepsilon_{33}^{T} \cdot A}{t}
$$

Computing $\varepsilon_{33}^{T}$:

$$
\varepsilon_{33}^{T} = 1500 \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 1.3281 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F/m}
$$

Substituting:

$$
C = \frac{80 \cdot (1.3281 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-6})}{100 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
C = \frac{80 \cdot 6.6405 \times 10^{-14}}{10^{-4}} = \frac{5.3124 \times 10^{-12}}{10^{-4}} = 5.312 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F} = 53.12 \text{ nF}
$$

**(iv) Energy per step:**

$$
E_{step} = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (5.312 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot (100)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{step} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 5.312 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 10^{4} = 2.656 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 265.6 \; \mu\text{J}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> In Inchworm Motor numerical problems, students commonly lose marks for:
> 1. **Mixing up $\Delta L$ (free displacement) with $\Delta s$ (effective step)** — remember the $\eta$ factor.
> 2. **Forgetting to square the voltage** in energy calculations ($V^{2}$, not $V$).
> 3. **Incorrect unit conversions** between μm, mm, and nm — always convert to SI meters *before* computing.
> 4. **Omitting the closing clamp release** in the sequence diagram — examiners specifically check whether the *four phases* are all clearly shown.
> 5. **Confusing $d_{31}$ with $d_{33}$** — for axial stack displacement, **always use $d_{33}$**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Inchworm Motor = linear stick-slip microactuator with **two clamps + one extender**.
- **Operating Principle:** Sequential clamping/extending in **four phases** replicates inchworm locomotion.
- **Core Formula:** $\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$ — displacement per cycle.
- **Step Size:** $\Delta s = \eta \cdot \Delta L$ — accounts for transmission losses.
- **Total Stroke:** $D = N \cdot \Delta s$ — open-loop accumulation of steps.
- **Clamping Condition:** $2\mu N_{clamp} \geq k_p \cdot \Delta L$ — prevents micro-slip.
- **Energy per Step:** $E_{step} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ — square-pulse excitation.
- **Strengths:** Long stroke (mm), high resolution (nm), high holding force (N), vacuum-compatible.
- **Limitations:** Hysteresis (5–15%), requires high-voltage driver, sequential control complexity.
- **BioMEMS Applications:** Optical fiber alignment, AFM/STM scanners, micropipette positioners, micropumps, micro-valves.
- **Frequency:** $f_r = \tfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ — sets maximum stepping rate.
- **Choice of $d_{33}$:** Always use $d_{33}$ (not $d_{31}$) for **axial** stack displacement in inchworm design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pressure Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **MEMS Pressure Sensor** is a micro-electro-mechanical device that transduces an applied mechanical pressure (force per unit area) into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, capacitance, or frequency shift) by exploiting the controlled mechanical deformation of a microfabricated diaphragm coupled with a transduction mechanism such as piezoresistive, capacitive, piezoelectric, or resonant sensing.

In the **BioMEMS context**, pressure sensors are designed to operate in physiological environments (saline, blood, interstitial fluid) with **biocompatible materials** (silicon, SU-8, parylene-C, titanium, PDMS) and are typically miniaturized for *in-vivo* implantation or catheter-based delivery.

**Key Performance Metrics (Bold Standards):**
- **Sensitivity (S)** → measured in **µV/V/kPa** (piezoresistive) or **fF/kPa** (capacitive)
- **Pressure Range** → typically **0.5 – 300 mmHg** for biomedical applications
- **Resolution** → as low as **0.1 mmHg** (≈ 13.3 Pa)
- **Linearity / Non-linearity** → expressed as **% Full Scale Output (%FSO)**
- **Hysteresis** → **< 1% FSO** for clinical-grade devices
- **Temperature Coefficient of Sensitivity (TCS)** → **%/°C**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Microscopic Drum" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — A Skin-Drum on a Microchip**
> Imagine stretching a thin drumhead (membrane) over a hollow cylindrical box and tapping on it gently. As you press harder, the membrane stretches more, and if you glue tiny electronic "strain gauges" (like the kind used in bathroom weighing scales) onto the membrane, those gauges change their electrical resistance in proportion to the stretch. By measuring this resistance change, you can determine the exact pressure applied to the drum.
>
> In a MEMS pressure sensor, the drumhead is a **silicon diaphragm only a few micrometres thick** (thinner than a human hair, which is ~70 µm), and the strain gauges are **piezoresistors** diffused directly into the silicon. The hollow box is an etched **reference cavity** (often sealed at a known vacuum or pressure). This is exactly how modern catheter-tip blood pressure sensors work — the only difference is the entire "drum" fits inside a 1 mm × 1 mm silicon chip!

## 1.3 BioMEMS-Specific Significance

In the human body, pressure is a *vital sign* in its own right:
- **Blood Pressure** — diagnostic for hypertension, cardiac health
- **Intracranial Pressure (ICP)** — traumatic brain injury monitoring
- **Intraocular Pressure (IOP)** — glaucoma management
- **Intrauterine Pressure** — labour monitoring
- **Respiratory Airway Pressure** — ventilator feedback
- **Bladder Pressure** — urodynamic studies

A MEMS sensor enables these measurements to be made **minimally invasively**, **wirelessly (via inductive/RF telemetry)**, and at **point-of-care** settings — a transformation not possible with traditional bench-top transducers.
