<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generic Pressure Sensor Architecture — The Four Functional Blocks

Every MEMS pressure sensor (regardless of transduction type) consists of **four functional stages**:

1. **Mechanical Element (Diaphragm)** — receives the input pressure *P* and converts it to a mechanical displacement *w(x,y)*.
2. **Transduction Element** — converts displacement into an electrical domain signal (R, C, charge *q*, or frequency *f*).
3. **Signal Conditioning Circuitry** — amplifies, linearizes, and temperature-compensates the raw output.
4. **Packaging & Interface** — provides hermetic sealing, biocompatibility, fluidic access port, and electrical feedthroughs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** When answering "Explain the working of a MEMS pressure sensor," always structure your answer in these **four blocks**. Examiners award step-marks for each block independently.

## 2.2 Operating Principle of the Diaphragm (Mechanical Stage)

When a differential pressure *P* is applied across a clamped diaphragm of thickness *t*, the diaphragm undergoes small deflection described by the **classical thin-plate equation**:

$$D \nabla^{4} w(x,y) = P$$

where *D* is the **flexural rigidity** (also called the *bending stiffness*):

$$D = \frac{E \, t^{3}}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2})}$$

- $E$ → **Young's modulus** of diaphragm material (e.g., **E ≈ 130 GPa** for Si(100), **E ≈ 169 GPa** for Si(111))
- $\nu$ → **Poisson's ratio** (≈ **0.28** for silicon)
- $t$ → diaphragm thickness (typically **2 – 50 µm**)
- $w(x,y)$ → out-of-plane deflection profile

### 2.2.1 Maximum Center Deflection (w_max)

For a **circular diaphragm** of radius *R* with clamped edges and uniform pressure *P*:

$$w_{\max} = w(0,0) = \frac{P \, R^{4}}{64 \, D}$$

> **Why the 4th power?** This is the single most important relationship in MEMS pressure sensors. A **doubling of the diaphragm radius** increases the center deflection by **16×**, while a **doubling of the thickness** reduces it by **8×**. This extreme sensitivity to geometry is why MEMS designers can tune sensor range simply by changing the diaphragm diameter.

## 2.3 Transduction Mechanism #1 — Piezoresistive Sensing

The **piezoresistive effect** (discovered by Lord Kelvin, formalized by Charles Smith in 1954) is the change in electrical resistivity of a semiconductor when subjected to mechanical strain.

The fractional resistance change is:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_{l} \, \sigma_{l} + \pi_{t} \, \sigma_{t}$$

- $\pi_l, \pi_t$ → **longitudinal and transverse piezoresistive coefficients** (for silicon, $\pi_l$ can be as high as **94 × 10⁻¹¹ Pa⁻¹** along the ⟨111⟩ direction)
- $\sigma_l, \sigma_t$ → longitudinal and transverse stress components

The resistors are arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge** to convert resistance imbalance into voltage.

## 2.4 Transduction Mechanism #2 — Capacitive Sensing

A pressure sensor diaphragm + fixed back-plate form a **parallel-plate capacitor**:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_{0} \, \varepsilon_{r} \, A}{d}$$

When pressure deflects the diaphragm, the gap *d* changes, modulating *C*:

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_{0}} = \frac{-\Delta d}{d_{0}} \quad (\text{for } \Delta d \ll d_{0})$$

> **Why capacitive for BioMEMS?** Capacitive sensors exhibit **near-zero power consumption** (critical for wireless implants), **excellent temperature stability**, **high sensitivity at low pressures**, and **compatibility with CMOS readout circuits**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Key Variables | Unit | KTU Relevance |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|------|---------------|
| 1 | Flexural Rigidity | $D = \dfrac{E t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ | E, t, ν | N·m | Diaphragm design |
| 2 | Center deflection (circular) | $w_{\max} = \dfrac{P R^{4}}{64 D}$ | P, R, D | m | Sensitivity link |
| 3 | Center deflection (square, side a) | $w_{\max} \approx \dfrac{0.0138 P a^{4}}{D}$ | P, a, D | m | Square-diaphragm design |
| 4 | Max stress (circular) | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{0.488 P R^{2}}{t^{2}}$ | P, R, t | Pa | Yield strength check |
| 5 | Capacitance | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r} A}{d}$ | A, d, ε | F | Capacitive sensor |
| 6 | Capacitive sensitivity | $S_{C} = \dfrac{\Delta C / C_{0}}{\Delta P}$ | — | /kPa | Spec sheet |
| 7 | Piezoresistive | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_{l}\sigma_{l} + \pi_{t}\sigma_{t}$ | π, σ | — | Piezoresistor design |
| 8 | Half-bridge output | $V_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{V_{s}}{2}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | V_s, R | V | Readout |
| 9 | Full-bridge output | $V_{\text{out}} = V_{s}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | V_s, R | V | 4× more sensitive |
| 10 | Resonance frequency | $f_{0} = \dfrac{10.22}{2\pi R^{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho t}}$ | D, ρ, R, t | Hz | Resonant sensor |
| 11 | Pressure conversion | $1 \text{ mmHg} = 133.322 \text{ Pa}$ | — | Pa | **Critical for BP problems** |
| 12 | Atmospheric pressure | $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 760 \text{ mmHg}$ | — | Pa | Reference standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip — 1 mmHg = 133.322 Pa** is a unit conversion that appears in **every KTU biomedical numerical**. Memorize it.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Center Deflection of a Clamped Circular Diaphragm

We derive the governing equation and boundary conditions, then state the closed-form solution that KTU expects in Part B.

**Step 1 — Governing Plate Equation**
The small-deflection bending of a thin plate under uniform pressure is:

$$D \left( \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{4}} + 2 \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{2}\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial y^{4}} \right) = P$$

In **polar coordinates** $(r,\theta)$ with axisymmetry, this reduces to:

$$D \left( \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left\{ r \frac{d}{dr} \left[ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dw}{dr}\right) \right] \right\} \right) = P$$

**Step 2 — Integrate Once**
Multiplying by $r$ and integrating:

$$D \cdot r \frac{d}{dr}\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dw}{dr}\right)\right] = \frac{P r^{2}}{2} + C_{1}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Symmetry at Centre**
At $r=0$, slope $dw/dr = 0$, so $C_{1} = 0$.

**Step 4 — Integrate Three More Times**
After integration, the general axisymmetric solution is:

$$w(r) = \frac{P r^{4}}{64 D} + A r^{2} \ln r + B r^{2} + C \ln r + F$$

**Step 5 — Apply Regularity at Centre**
Finite deflection at $r=0$ requires $A = C = 0$.

**Step 6 — Apply Clamped Edge Boundary Condition**
At $r = R$: $w(R) = 0$ and $dw/dr \mid_{r=R} = 0$.

From $dw/dr = 0$ at $r=R$:

$$\frac{P R^{3}}{16 D} + 2 B R = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = -\frac{P R^{2}}{32 D}$$

From $w(R) = 0$:

$$\frac{P R^{4}}{64 D} - \frac{P R^{2}}{32 D} \cdot R^{2} + F = 0$$

$$F = \frac{P R^{4}}{64 D} \quad \text{Wait, recheck:}\quad F = \frac{P R^{4}}{64 D} - \frac{P R^{4}}{32 D} = -\frac{P R^{4}}{64 D}$$

Substituting $A=B=C$ and $F$ back:

**Step 7 — Final Profile**

$$w(r) = \frac{P}{64 D}\left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 8 — Maximum at Centre** ($r=0$):

$$\boxed{\,w_{\max} = w(0) = \frac{P R^{4}}{64 D}\,}$$

**Step 9 — Maximum Stress at Edge** (useful for failure/yield check):

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{0.488 \, P R^{2}}{t^{2}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — A KTU Board-Style Problem

> **Problem (14 marks):** A MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor has a circular silicon diaphragm of radius *R* = 200 µm and thickness *t* = 10 µm. The piezoresistors are configured in a full Wheatstone bridge with supply $V_s = 5\text{ V}$. The longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient is $\pi_l = 50 \times 10^{-11}\text{ Pa}^{-1}$. Calculate:
> (a) The maximum center deflection when a pressure of 100 mmHg is applied.
> (b) The bridge output voltage, given that the maximum longitudinal stress is $\sigma_l = 1 \times 10^{6}$ Pa at this pressure.
> (c) The sensitivity of the sensor in mV/V/kPa.

### 3.2.1 Solution to Part (a) — Center Deflection

**Given:**
- $R = 200 \, \mu\text{m} = 200 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$
- $t = 10 \, \mu\text{m} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$
- $E = 130 \times 10^{9} \, \text{Pa}$ (silicon ⟨100⟩)
- $\nu = 0.28$
- $P = 100 \text{ mmHg} = 100 \times 133.322 = 13332.2 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1 — Compute flexural rigidity D:**

$$D = \frac{E \, t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$$

$$D = \frac{130 \times 10^{9} \times (10 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{12 \times (1 - 0.28^{2})}$$

$$D = \frac{130 \times 10^{9} \times 10^{-15}}{12 \times 0.9216}$$

$$D = \frac{1.3 \times 10^{-4}}{11.0592}$$

$$D = 1.1755 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute center deflection $w_{\max}$:**

$$w_{\max} = \frac{P R^{4}}{64 D}$$

$$w_{\max} = \frac{13332.2 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^{4}}{64 \times 1.1755 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$w_{\max} = \frac{13332.2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-15}}{7.523 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$w_{\max} = \frac{2.133 \times 10^{-11}}{7.523 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.835 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\,w_{\max} \approx 28.4 \, \text{nm}\,}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A sub-30 nm deflection of a 10-µm-thick diaphragm is enough to generate a usable electrical signal — a beautiful demonstration of the extraordinary sensitivity of MEMS.

### 3.2.2 Solution to Part (b) — Bridge Output

**Step 1 — Compute fractional resistance change:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_{l} \, \sigma_{l} = 50 \times 10^{-11} \times 1 \times 10^{6} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2 — Full-bridge output:**

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{s} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = 5 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{\text{out}} = 2.5 \, \text{mV}\,}$$

### 3.2.3 Solution to Part (c) — Sensitivity

$$S = \frac{V_{\text{out}}/V_{s}}{P} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{13.3322 \, \text{kPa}} = 3.75 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{/kPa}$$

Converting to standard units:

$$\boxed{\,S = 0.0375 \, \text{mV/V/kPa} = 37.5 \, \mu\text{V/V/kPa}\,}$$

This is consistent with commercial disposable BP sensors (typical range: 10–100 µV/V/kPa).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a MEMS Pressure Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Pressure P] --> B[Clamped Diaphragm]
    B1[Diaphragm Geometry] --> B
    B2[Young Modulus E] --> B
    B3[Poisson Ratio nu] --> B
    B --> C[Mechanical Deflection w r]
    C --> D{Transduction Stage}
    D --> E[Piezoresistive Bridge]
    D --> F[Capacitive Gap]
    D --> G[Piezoelectric Charge]
    D --> H[Resonant Frequency Shift]
    E --> I[Wheatstone Bridge]
    F --> J[Capacitance Readout IC]
    G --> K[Charge Amplifier]
    H --> L[Oscillator Circuit]
    I --> M[Signal Conditioning]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Temperature Compensation]
    N --> O[Calibrated Voltage Output Vout]
    O --> P[BioMEMS Application]
    P1[Blood Pressure BP] --> P
    P2[Intracranial Pressure ICP] --> P
    P3[Intraocular Pressure IOP] --> P
    P4[Respiratory Airway Pressure] --> P
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Piezoresistive Bridge Layout on Diaphragm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIAPH[Square Silicon Diaphragm]
        R1[Resistor R1 at Edge Max Tensile Strain]
        R2[Resistor R2 at Edge Max Compressive Strain]
        R3[Resistor R3 at Edge Max Compressive Strain]
        R4[Resistor R4 at Edge Max Tensile Strain]
        R1 --- N1((Node A))
        N1 --- R2
        R2 --- N2((Node B))
        N2 --- R3
        R3 --- N3((Node C))
        N3 --- R4
        R4 --- N1
        VS[Vsupply 5V] -.-> N1
        GND[Ground 0V] -.-> N2
        N3 --> VOPP[Vout Positive]
        N2 --> VOPN[Vout Negative]
    end
    VOPP --> AMP[Differential Amplifier]
    VOPN --> AMP
    AMP --> OUT[Calibrated Pressure Output]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Architecture (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP[Top Fluidic Port - Inlet]
    DIAPH[Silicon Diaphragm with Diffused Piezoresistors]
    SEAL[Hermetic Seal Ring - Glass Frit or Anodic Bonding]
    CAV[Sealed Reference Cavity - Vacuum or Known Pressure]
    SUB[Silicon Substrate with Bond Pads]
    PAD[Electrical Bond Pads - Wire Bond Interface]
    TOP --> DIAPH
    DIAPH --> SEAL
    SEAL --> CAV
    CAV --> SUB
    SUB --> PAD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** Pressure *P* enters the top port and deflects the diaphragm. Piezoresistors diffused on the diaphragm surface change resistance. The reference cavity (often evacuated to ~10 mTorr) provides a known pressure baseline, so the device actually measures the **differential** pressure $P - P_{\text{ref}}$. This is why absolute-pressure sensors are manufactured by sealing the cavity in a vacuum environment, while gauge-pressure sensors leave a vent hole.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEEBT743]**
**"Differentiate between piezoresistive and capacitive pressure sensing mechanisms in BioMEMS (any 3 points)."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Piezoresistive | Capacitive |
|-----------|---------------|------------|
| **Sensing element** | Diffused resistors on diaphragm | Diaphragm + back-plate capacitor |
| **Output signal** | Resistance change ΔR | Capacitance change ΔC |
| **Readout circuit** | Wheatstone bridge + amplifier | Capacitance-to-digital converter (CDC) |
| **Power consumption** | Moderate (current flow required) | **Very low** (ideal for implants) |
| **Temperature sensitivity** | **High** (needs compensation) | **Low** (intrinsically stable) |
| **Sensitivity to low pressure** | Moderate | **High** |
| **Linearity** | Good | Excellent (inversely with gap) |
| **Typical sensitivity** | 10–100 µV/V/kPa | 0.1–10 fF/kPa |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PEEBT743]**

> **(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of a MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A square silicon diaphragm (side $a = 500 \, \mu\text{m}$, thickness $t = 15 \, \mu\text{m}$) is used in a capacitive pressure sensor. The initial gap is $d_0 = 2 \, \mu\text{m}$ and the electrode area equals the full diaphragm area. Find the change in capacitance when a pressure of 60 mmHg causes a uniform center deflection equal to **15% of the gap**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Pressure → Diaphragm → Piezoresistors → Wheatstone Bridge → Differential Amplifier → Output.

**Working Principle (6 marks):**
- A thin silicon diaphragm is etched using **anisotropic wet etching** (KOH or TMAH) or **deep reactive ion etching (DRIE)**.
- Four piezoresistors are **ion-implanted or diffused** at the diaphragm edges where stress is maximum.
- Two resistors are oriented **longitudinally** (parallel to the principal stress) and two **transversely** (perpendicular).
- They are connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** with excitation $V_s$.
- Applied pressure $P$ deflects the diaphragm; tensile regions (near the clamped edge) cause one pair to increase in resistance, while compressive regions cause the other pair to decrease.
- The bridge imbalance produces $V_{\text{out}} = V_s \cdot (\Delta R / R)$.
- This small signal (~mV) is amplified by an instrumentation amplifier and temperature-compensated.

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Given:**
- $a = 500 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}, \quad t = 15 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}, \quad d_0 = 2 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$
- $\Delta d = 0.15 \times d_0 = 0.15 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} = 0.3 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$
- $A = a^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^2, \quad \varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F/m}, \quad \varepsilon_r = 1$ (air gap)

**Step 1 — Initial capacitance:**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{2.2135 \times 10^{-18}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.1067 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F} = 1.107 \, \text{pF}$$

**Step 2 — New capacitance** $C_1$ with $d_1 = d_0 - \Delta d = 1.7 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$:

$$C_1 = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{1.7 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_1 = \frac{2.2135 \times 10^{-18}}{1.7 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.3019 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F} = 1.302 \, \text{pF}$$

**Step 3 — Change in capacitance:**

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_0 = 1.302 - 1.107 = 0.195 \, \text{pF}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta C \approx 195 \, \text{fF for 60 mmHg applied pressure}\,}$$

**Sensitivity:**

$$S_C = \frac{\Delta C}{P} = \frac{0.195 \times 10^{-12}}{60 \times 133.322} = 2.44 \times 10^{-17} \, \text{F/Pa} = 24.4 \, \text{fF/kPa}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PEEBT743 Alternative]**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the maximum center deflection of a circular clamped diaphragm under uniform pressure. State all boundary conditions clearly. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A circular silicon diaphragm of radius 300 µm and thickness 8 µm is subjected to a blood-pressure equivalent of 120 mmHg. Compute (i) the maximum center deflection, (ii) the maximum bending stress at the edge, and (iii) verify the validity of the small-deflection assumption. Use $E = 130 \, \text{GPa}$ and $\nu = 0.28$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a)

The full derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. **Marks split (KTU):**
- Plate equation statement — **1 mark**
- Boundary conditions (clamped edge, finite at centre) — **2 marks**
- Integration steps and application of BCs — **3 marks**
- Final closed-form expression for $w(r)$ and $w_{\max}$ — **1 mark**

**Final result (must be reproduced):**

$$w(r) = \frac{P}{64 D}(R^2 - r^2)^2, \quad w_{\max} = \frac{P R^4}{64 D}$$

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Given:** $R = 300 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}, \; t = 8 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}, \; P = 120 \text{ mmHg} = 120 \times 133.322 = 15998.6 \, \text{Pa}$

**Step 1 — Flexural Rigidity:**

$$D = \frac{130 \times 10^{9} \times (8 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{12(1 - 0.28^{2})}$$

$$D = \frac{130 \times 10^{9} \times 5.12 \times 10^{-16}}{12 \times 0.9216} = \frac{6.656 \times 10^{-5}}{11.0592} = 6.018 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{N·m}$$

**(i) Maximum Center Deflection:**

$$w_{\max} = \frac{P R^4}{64 D} = \frac{15998.6 \times (300 \times 10^{-6})^4}{64 \times 6.018 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$w_{\max} = \frac{15998.6 \times 8.1 \times 10^{-15}}{3.852 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1.2959 \times 10^{-10}}{3.852 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\,w_{\max} = 3.364 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} = 0.336 \, \mu\text{m}\,}$$

**(ii) Maximum Edge Stress:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{0.488 \times P R^2}{t^2} = \frac{0.488 \times 15998.6 \times (300 \times 10^{-6})^2}{(8 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

$$= \frac{0.488 \times 15998.6 \times 9 \times 10^{-8}}{6.4 \times 10^{-11}} = \frac{7.025 \times 10^{-4}}{6.4 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$\boxed{\,\sigma_{\max} \approx 10.97 \, \text{MPa}\,}$$

This is well below silicon's yield strength (~7 GPa), confirming the diaphragm will not fracture.

**(iii) Validity of Small-Deflection Assumption:**
The plate theory is valid when $w_{\max} / t \le 0.2$:

$$\frac{w_{\max}}{t} = \frac{0.336 \times 10^{-6}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.042 = 4.2\%$$

Since **4.2% < 20%**, the small-deflection assumption is **valid**. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Forgetting to convert mmHg → Pa. Always multiply mmHg by **133.322**. A wrong unit typically costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing absolute vs. gauge pressure:** MEMS sensors measure *differential* pressure unless the reference cavity is sealed in vacuum. State this clearly.
> 3. **Applying square-diaphragm formula to circular diaphragm** (or vice versa). Note the coefficient: circular uses $1/64$; square uses $\approx 0.0138$.
> 4. **Forgetting flexural rigidity $D$ in deflection formula** — students sometimes write $w_{\max} = P R^4 / (64 E t^3)$, which is dimensionally wrong because the $(1-\nu^2)$ term is missing.
> 5. **Not justifying "small-deflection" validity** — always compute $w_{\max}/t$ and verify it's less than 0.2.
> 6. **Sketching the diaphragm as a rectangle or cube** — KTU examiners expect a **circular or square plate clamped at its edges** with clear indication of piezoresistor positions at the edges.
> 7. **Mixing up $C = \varepsilon A / d$ with $C = \varepsilon A / d^2$** — a classic confusion with the physics of parallel-plate capacitors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pressure Sensor (Module 2)**

- ✅ A MEMS pressure sensor is a **transducer** that converts pressure into an electrical signal via a **deformable diaphragm** + **transduction element**.
- ✅ The four universal blocks are: **Diaphragm → Transducer → Signal Conditioning → Packaging**.
- ✅ **Piezoresistive** sensors use silicon piezoresistors in a **Wheatstone bridge**; dominant in disposable BP catheters.
- ✅ **Capacitive** sensors use a **gap-change capacitor**; dominant in low-power wireless implants (ICP, IOP).
- ✅ **Piezoelectric** sensors generate charge $q = d_{ij} \sigma_{ij}$; useful for **dynamic (AC) pressure** measurement, not static.
- ✅ **Resonant** sensors detect pressure via **frequency shift**; highest stability and resolution.
- ✅ The **flexural rigidity** is $D = E t^3 / [12(1-\nu^2)]$ — must always be computed first.
- ✅ **Center deflection of circular diaphragm:** $w_{\max} = P R^4 / (64 D)$ — scales as $R^4$ and $t^{-3}$.
- ✅ **Maximum stress at edge:** $\sigma_{\max} = 0.488 P R^2 / t^2$ — used to verify against silicon's yield strength (~7 GPa).
- ✅ **Piezoresistive coefficient** $\pi_l$ for Si ranges from **10⁻¹¹ to 10⁻¹⁰ Pa⁻¹**; orientation matters (⟨111⟩ highest).
- ✅ **Capacitive sensitivity** is inversely proportional to gap: smaller gap → higher sensitivity, but reduces linearity range.
- ✅ **Bridge output:** half-bridge $\Rightarrow V_s (\Delta R / 2R)$; full-bridge $\Rightarrow V_s (\Delta R / R)$ — **full is 4× more sensitive**.
- ✅ **Unit conversion:** 1 mmHg = **133.322 Pa**; 1 atm = **101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg**; 1 bar = 100 kPa.
- ✅ **Small-deflection assumption** valid if $w_{\max} / t \le 0.2$ (20%). Always verify in numericals.
- ✅ **Biomedical applications** to remember: **BP, ICP, IOP, IUP, respiratory, bladder**.
- ✅ **Material biocompatibility:** preferred materials are **Si, SiO₂, Si₃N₄, Ti, parylene-C, SU-8, PDMS**; avoid long-term exposure of bare Si to body fluids.
- ✅ **Packaging** for implants: **hermetic sealing** (anodic bonding, glass frit, eutectic bonding); reference cavity often evacuated to ~10 mTorr for **absolute pressure** reference.
- ✅ **Advantages of MEMS over conventional sensors:** smaller size, lower power, batch fabrication (low cost), better integration with electronics, suitable for minimally invasive deployment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Dynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fluid Dynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Fluid Dynamics in BioMEMS and Microfluidics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fluid Dynamics** is the branch of continuum mechanics that describes the flow of liquids and gases under the action of internal and external forces. In the context of **BioMEMS and Microfluidics**, fluid dynamics is restricted to the study of fluid behaviour in geometries with characteristic dimensions typically in the range **1 µm to 1000 µm**, where surface forces dominate volumetric forces and the **continuum assumption** is only marginally valid.

The study of fluid dynamics at the microscale is governed by three dimensionless numbers that determine the dominant physical phenomena:

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Reynolds Number | $Re$ | Ratio of inertial to viscous forces |
| Knudsen Number | $Kn$ | Ratio of molecular mean free path to characteristic length |
| Capillary Number | $Ca$ | Ratio of viscous forces to surface tension forces |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — The Highway vs The Garden Hose:**
> Imagine driving a car on a wide multi-lane highway (macroscale fluid flow). Lanes can change freely, traffic can swirl into vortices, and small disturbances cause large chaotic effects — this is **turbulent flow**, governed by inertia (high $Re$).
>
> Now imagine pushing water through a narrow garden hose (microscale flow). The water moves in perfectly parallel, smooth layers, and the only thing that matters is the friction between the water and the inner wall of the hose — this is **laminar flow**, governed by viscosity (low $Re$).
>
> In a microfluidic lab-on-a-chip device, fluids almost always behave like the garden hose: **laminar, predictable, and viscosity-dominated**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants

The following constants appear repeatedly in BioMEMS fluid dynamics problems and must be memorized:

- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}~\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- **Kinematic viscosity of water:** $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}~\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Density of water:** $\rho = 1000~\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$
- **Surface tension of water–air at 25 °C:** $\gamma = 0.072~\text{N}/\text{m}$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_0 = 101325~\text{Pa}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these constants matter in KTU exams:**
> Numerical problems in PEEBT743 almost always use water at room temperature as the working fluid. Memorising these values saves valuable time during the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Continuum Assumption and Its Breakdown

The classical equations of fluid dynamics (Navier–Stokes, Bernoulli, continuity) assume that fluid properties such as density $\rho$ and velocity $\mathbf{v}$ are **continuous functions** of position and time. This is the **continuum hypothesis**.

The validity of this hypothesis is judged by the **Knudsen Number**:

$$
Kn = \frac{\lambda}{L_c}
$$

where $\lambda$ is the molecular mean free path and $L_c$ is the characteristic length of the flow channel.

| Regime | Knudsen Number Range | Governing Physics |
|---|---|---|
| Continuum | $Kn < 0.001$ | Classical Navier–Stokes valid |
| Slip flow | $0.001 < Kn < 0.1$ | Navier–Stokes with slip boundary condition |
| Transition | $0.1 < Kn < 10$ | Burnett / super-Burnett equations |
| Free molecular | $Kn > 10$ | Boltzmann equation / DSMC required |

> [!NOTE]
> In BioMEMS devices (channels of 10 µm), with air as the fluid, $Kn \approx 0.04$ — slip flow regime. With water, $Kn \approx 10^{-7}$ — strongly continuum. This is why **water-based biochips are easier to model** than gas-based ones.

## 2.2 The Reynolds Number — The Single Most Important Number

The Reynolds number quantifies whether flow is **laminar (smooth)** or **turbulent (chaotic)**:

$$
Re = \frac{\rho \, v \, L_c}{\mu} = \frac{v \, L_c}{\nu}
$$

where $v$ is the mean velocity and $L_c$ is the characteristic length.

### Why and How Each Term Matters

- **$\rho$ — Density:** Higher density → more inertia per unit volume → harder to redirect the flow → flow tends towards turbulence.
- **$v$ — Velocity:** Faster flow → momentum transport dominates → turbulence threshold lowered.
- **$L_c$ — Characteristic length:** Wider channels → flow has more "space" to develop disturbances.
- **$\mu$ — Viscosity:** Higher viscosity → flow layers stick together → smoother, more laminar behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical threshold for circular pipes:** Flow becomes turbulent when $Re > 2300$. For microfluidic devices, typical $Re$ values are between $10^{-6}$ and $100$, meaning flow is **almost always laminar**. This is a **favourite KTU question** — expect a 3-mark or 7-mark item asking to identify flow regime.

## 2.3 The Three Foundational Equations of Fluid Dynamics

### (a) Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

For an incompressible fluid in steady state:

$$
\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0
$$

In one-dimensional form for a pipe with variable cross-section:

$$
A_1 \, v_1 = A_2 \, v_2
$$

### (b) Navier–Stokes Equation (Conservation of Momentum)

For an incompressible Newtonian fluid with constant viscosity:

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{v} \right) = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} + \mathbf{f}_{\text{body}}
$$

The terms on the right-hand side are pressure gradient force, viscous diffusion, and body force (e.g., gravity) respectively.

### (c) Bernoulli's Equation (Energy Conservation)

For steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline:

$$
P + \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 + \rho g h = \text{constant}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bernoulli fails in microfluidics:** The "inviscid" assumption breaks down because viscosity dominates. However, Bernoulli is still used for **entrance/exit region analysis** and for estimating pressure-driven driving heads.

## 2.4 Stokes Flow — The Microfluidic Regime

When $Re \ll 1$, the **inertial term** $\rho (\mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{v})$ in Navier–Stokes becomes negligible compared to the **viscous term** $\mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v}$. The Navier–Stokes equation simplifies to the **Stokes equation**:

$$
-\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} + \mathbf{f}_{\text{body}} = 0
$$

This linear equation is the workhorse of microfluidic analysis because:
1. It is **linear** — superposition of solutions works.
2. It is **time-independent** (for steady Stokes flow).
3. Analytical solutions exist for almost all standard geometries.

## 2.5 The No-Slip Boundary Condition

At a solid wall, the fluid velocity must equal the wall velocity. For a stationary wall:

$$
\mathbf{v}\big|_{\text{wall}} = 0
$$

This produces a **boundary layer** of thickness $\delta$ next to the wall where viscous effects are significant. For laminar flow in a pipe, the boundary layer eventually fills the entire cross-section.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas in this table are examinable under PEEBT743 Module 3.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L_c}{\mu}$ | $\rho$: kg/m³, $v$: m/s, $L_c$: m, $\mu$: Pa·s |
| 2 | Knudsen Number | $Kn = \dfrac{\lambda}{L_c}$ | $\lambda$: m, $L_c$: m |
| 3 | Capillary Number | $Ca = \dfrac{\mu v}{\gamma}$ | $\gamma$: N/m |
| 4 | Continuity (1-D) | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | $A$: m², $v$: m/s |
| 5 | Navier–Stokes (Incompressible) | $\rho \left( \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{v} \right) = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} + \mathbf{f}_b$ | Pressure in Pa, body force N/m³ |
| 6 | Stokes Equation | $-\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} = 0$ | Valid when $Re < 1$ |
| 7 | Bernoulli (inviscid) | $P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 + \rho g h = \text{const}$ | $h$: m, $g$: 9.81 m/s² |
| 8 | Hagen–Poiseuille Flow Rate | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | $Q$: m³/s, $R$: m, $L$: m |
| 9 | Hagen–Poiseuille Velocity Profile | $v(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$ | $r$: radial position |
| 10 | Wall Shear Stress | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R}{2 L}$ | $\tau_w$: Pa |
| 11 | Stokes Drag (sphere) | $F_D = 6 \pi \mu R v$ | $F_D$: N |
| 12 | Hydraulic Diameter | $D_h = \dfrac{4 A}{P_w}$ | $P_w$: wetted perimeter in m |
| 13 | Mean Free Path (gas) | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^2 P}$ | $k_B$: 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K |
| 14 | Pressure Drop (general pipe) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^3}$ (rectangular) | $w$, $h$: width, height |
| 15 | Péclet Number | $Pe = Re \cdot Sc = \dfrac{v L_c}{D}$ | $D$: diffusivity in m²/s |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drug delivery microneedles:** Stokes drag determines resistance during needle insertion through tissue.
- **Lab-on-a-chip diagnostics:** Hagen–Poiseuille equations are used to design channel widths and pump pressures for PCR chips.
- **Organ-on-a-chip platforms:** Wall shear stress must be tuned to physiological values (1–10 dyn/cm²) to mimic endothelial cell environment.
- **Cell sorting:** Inertial microfluidics exploits secondary flows at intermediate $Re$ (~1–100) to focus cells by size.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Number from Navier–Stokes

**Goal:** Show that the dimensionless group $\rho v L_c / \mu$ naturally arises from non-dimensionalising the Navier–Stokes equation.

**Starting Point:** The Navier–Stokes equation for an incompressible Newtonian fluid, neglecting body forces:

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{v} \right) = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v}
$$

**Step 1 — Define dimensionless variables.** Let uppercase letters denote dimensionless quantities. We scale all lengths by $L_c$, velocities by $U$ (characteristic velocity), time by $L_c / U$, and pressure by $\rho U^2$.

$$
\mathbf{v} = U \tilde{\mathbf{v}}, \quad x = L_c \tilde{x}, \quad t = \frac{L_c}{U}\tilde{t}, \quad P = \rho U^2 \tilde{P}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term in dimensionless form.**

The convective term:

$$
\mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{v} = \frac{U^2}{L_c}\, \tilde{\mathbf{v}} \cdot \tilde{\nabla} \tilde{\mathbf{v}}
$$

The time derivative:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} = \frac{U^2}{L_c}\, \frac{\partial \tilde{\mathbf{v}}}{\partial \tilde{t}}
$$

The viscous term:

$$
\mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} = \frac{\mu U}{L_c^2}\, \tilde{\nabla}^2 \tilde{\mathbf{v}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the Navier–Stokes equation.**

$$
\rho \frac{U^2}{L_c} \left( \frac{\partial \tilde{\mathbf{v}}}{\partial \tilde{t}} + \tilde{\mathbf{v}} \cdot \tilde{\nabla} \tilde{\mathbf{v}} \right) = -\frac{\rho U^2}{L_c}\, \tilde{\nabla} \tilde{P} + \frac{\mu U}{L_c^2}\, \tilde{\nabla}^2 \tilde{\mathbf{v}}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide every term by the common factor $\rho U^2 / L_c$.**

$$
\frac{\partial \tilde{\mathbf{v}}}{\partial \tilde{t}} + \tilde{\mathbf{v}} \cdot \tilde{\nabla} \tilde{\mathbf{v}} = -\tilde{\nabla} \tilde{P} + \frac{\mu}{\rho U L_c}\, \tilde{\nabla}^2 \tilde{\mathbf{v}}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the dimensionless group.**

The coefficient of the viscous term is the reciprocal of the Reynolds number:

$$
\frac{\mu}{\rho U L_c} = \frac{1}{Re}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\frac{\partial \tilde{\mathbf{v}}}{\partial \tilde{t}} + \tilde{\mathbf{v}} \cdot \tilde{\nabla} \tilde{\mathbf{v}} = -\tilde{\nabla} \tilde{P} + \frac{1}{Re}\, \tilde{\nabla}^2 \tilde{\mathbf{v}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** When $Re \to \infty$, the viscous term vanishes and the equation becomes Euler's equation (inviscid flow). When $Re \to 0$, the convective term vanishes and we recover the Stokes equation. This confirms the meaning of $Re$ as the ratio of inertia to viscosity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Hagen–Poiseuille Velocity Profile

**Setup:** Steady, incompressible, axisymmetric, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a circular pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$, driven by a pressure drop $\Delta P$.

**Step 1 — Choose the appropriate form of Navier–Stokes.** For unidirectional flow along $z$-axis, $\mathbf{v} = (0, 0, v_z(r))$. The equation reduces to a single component:

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left[ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{d v_z}{dr} \right) \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Assume fully developed flow**, so $\partial P / \partial z$ is constant. Let $\partial P / \partial z = -\Delta P / L$ (positive pressure drop drives flow in $+z$ direction).

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \mu \cdot \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{d v_z}{dr} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply both sides by $r / \mu$ and integrate once.**

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{\mu L} \cdot \frac{r^2}{2} = r \frac{d v_z}{dr} + C_1
$$

The constant $C_1 = 0$ because $d v_z / dr$ must be finite at $r = 0$.

**Step 4 — Divide by $r$ and integrate a second time.**

$$
\frac{d v_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L}\, r
$$

$$
v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\, r^2 + C_2
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the no-slip boundary condition at the wall:** $v_z(R) = 0$.

$$
0 = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\, R^2 + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = -\frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4 \mu L}
$$

**Final Velocity Profile:**

$$
v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left( R^2 - r^2 \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The profile is **parabolic** with maximum velocity at the centreline ($r = 0$). This is the defining characteristic of Hagen–Poiseuille flow and is a **direct consequence of the no-slip condition**.

**Step 6 — Compute the volumetric flow rate by integrating the velocity profile over the cross-section.**

$$
Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r\, dr = \int_0^R \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left( R^2 - r^2 \right) 2\pi r\, dr
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_0^R \left( R^2 r - r^3 \right) dr
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}
$$

**Final Hagen–Poiseuille Equation:**

$$
Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The fourth-power dependence on $R$ explains why **doubling the channel radius increases flow by 16×**. This extreme sensitivity is exploited in microfluidic device design and is a **favourite KTU numerical question**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Continuity Equation

**Step 1 — Consider a fixed control volume $V$** enclosed by surface $S$. The total mass inside is:

$$
M = \int_V \rho \, dV
$$

**Step 2 — Conservation of mass requires that the rate of change of mass inside equals the net inflow.**

$$
\frac{d M}{d t} = -\oint_S \rho \, \mathbf{v} \cdot d\mathbf{S}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the divergence theorem** to convert the surface integral to a volume integral.

$$
-\oint_S \rho \, \mathbf{v} \cdot d\mathbf{S} = -\int_V \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) \, dV
$$

**Step 4 — Combine and apply Leibniz's rule.**

$$
\int_V \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dV = -\int_V \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) \, dV
$$

**Step 5 — Since this holds for any arbitrary control volume $V$, the integrands must be equal.**

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0
$$

**Step 6 — For an incompressible fluid, $\rho$ is constant**, so $\partial \rho / \partial t = 0$ and $\nabla \rho = 0$:

$$
\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Regime Decision Flowchart (Mermaid)

The following diagram maps the logic path used to classify a flow regime based on the calculated $Re$ and $Kn$ values.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Start: Identify Fluid and Channel Geometry"] --> B1["Calculate Characteristic Length L_c"]
    B1 --> C1["Compute Knudsen Number Kn = lambda over L_c"]
    C1 --> D1{"Kn less than 0.001?"}
    D1 -- Yes --> E1["Continuum Regime Valid"]
    D1 -- No --> F1["Slip or Molecular Regime - Special Treatment"]
    E1 --> G1["Compute Reynolds Number Re"]
    G1 --> H1{"Re less than 2300?"}
    H1 -- Yes --> I1["Laminar Flow - Use Hagen-Poiseuille or Stokes"]
    H1 -- No --> J1["Turbulent Flow - Use Empirical Correlations"]
    I1 --> K1["Identify Dominant Forces"]
    K1 --> L1{"Inertial vs Viscous?"}
    L1 -- Viscous Dominant --> M1["Stokes Flow Regime - Linear Analysis"]
    L1 -- Inertial Dominant --> N1["High Re Regime - Nonlinear Effects"]
    M1 --> O1["Apply No-Slip BC and Parabolic Profile"]
    N1 --> O1
    O1 --> P1["End: Solve Navier-Stokes with BC"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Microfluidic Flow Analysis

This block diagram maps how the major fluid dynamics components interact inside a typical BioMEMS device analysis workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1["Working Fluid Definition: density, viscosity, surface tension"] --> PROC1["Input Parameters: Channel Geometry L, W, H"]
    PROC1 --> CALC1["Compute Dimensionless Numbers: Re, Kn, Ca, Pe"]
    CALC1 --> REG["Identify Flow Regime: Laminar, Turbulent, Slip"]
    REG --> EQ1["Apply Continuity Equation: mass conservation"]
    REG --> EQ2["Apply Navier-Stokes Equation: momentum conservation"]
    REG --> EQ3["Apply Bernoulli Equation: energy conservation"]
    EQ1 --> BC["Boundary Conditions: No-Slip at Walls, Pressure Inlet-Outlet"]
    EQ2 --> BC
    EQ3 --> BC
    BC --> SOLVE["Analytical Solver: Hagen-Poiseuille, Stokes Drag, etc"]
    SOLVE --> OUT1["Output: Velocity Profile, Pressure Drop, Shear Stress, Flow Rate"]
    OUT1 --> BIO["Biological Application: Cell Sorting, Drug Delivery, Mixing"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in a KTU exam:** When asked to "explain the steps involved in microfluidic flow analysis," reproduce the decision-flow logic shown above. Examiners reward **systematic, flowchart-style answers** over scattered bullet points.

## 4.3 Comparison of Flow Regimes (Text-Based Schematic)

| Flow Regime | Velocity Profile Shape | Reynolds Number Range | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hagen–Poiseuille (Laminar Pipe) | Parabolic, max at centre | $Re < 2300$ | Straight microchannels |
| Couette Flow | Linear, max at moving wall | Very low $Re$ | Shear stress assays |
| Stokes Flow around Sphere | Symmetric, fore-aft symmetric | $Re < 1$ | Cell/cell-particle motion |
| Turbulent Pipe Flow | Flat profile, chaotic fluctuations | $Re > 4000$ | Macroscale bioreactors |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Reynolds number and explain its significance in microfluidic systems.**

**Model Answer:**
Reynolds number is a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow. It is mathematically defined as:

$$
Re = \frac{\rho \, v \, L_c}{\mu}
$$

**Significance in microfluidics:**
1. Determines whether the flow is **laminar ($Re < 2300$)** or **turbulent ($Re > 4000$)**.
2. In BioMEMS devices, $Re$ is typically between $10^{-6}$ and $100$, ensuring laminar flow.
3. Laminar flow enables **predictable mixing by diffusion only**, a foundational principle in lab-on-a-chip devices.
4. A very low $Re$ also means **viscous forces dominate**, simplifying the Navier–Stokes equation to the **Stokes equation** which is linear and analytically tractable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write only the formula. To score full marks (3/3), you **must** explain what the two forces being compared are and **why** the resulting value matters specifically for microfluidics.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is the no-slip boundary condition? Why is it important in microfluidic flow analysis?**

**Model Answer:**
The no-slip boundary condition states that at a solid wall, the velocity of the fluid relative to the wall is zero:

$$
\mathbf{v}\big|_{\text{wall}} = \mathbf{v}_{\text{wall}}
$$

For a stationary wall, $\mathbf{v}_{\text{wall}} = 0$.

**Importance:**
1. It produces the **boundary layer** where viscous stresses are transmitted into the fluid.
2. It gives rise to the **parabolic velocity profile** in Hagen–Poiseuille flow.
3. In microfluidics, the boundary layer can occupy a **significant fraction** of the channel cross-section (since $L_c$ is small), making viscous effects dominant.
4. It is the boundary condition that **couples the fluid to the device geometry**, and without it, the Navier–Stokes equation has no unique solution.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for steady, laminar, incompressible flow through a circular pipe. State all assumptions clearly. (7 Marks)**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady state ($\partial / \partial t = 0$)
2. Incompressible fluid ($\rho$ = constant)
3. Newtonian fluid (constant viscosity)
4. Laminar flow (low $Re$)
5. Fully developed, unidirectional flow: $\mathbf{v} = v_z(r) \hat{z}$
6. Axisymmetric flow
7. No body forces in the flow direction

**Derivation:**

Starting from the $z$-component of the Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates:

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left[ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{d v_z}{dr} \right) \right]
$$

With $\partial P / \partial z = -\Delta P / L$:

$$
\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{d v_z}{dr} \right) = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu L}
$$

First integration:

$$
r \frac{d v_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} r^2 + C_1
$$

By symmetry, $d v_z / dr = 0$ at $r = 0$, so $C_1 = 0$.

Second integration:

$$
v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} r^2 + C_2
$$

No-slip condition at $r = R$:

$$
v_z(R) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_2 = -\frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4 \mu L}
$$

Velocity profile:

$$
v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left( R^2 - r^2 \right)
$$

Volumetric flow rate:

$$
Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r\, dr = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}
$$

**(b) A microfluidic channel of circular cross-section has a radius of 50 µm and a length of 5 cm. Water (μ = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s) is driven through it by a pressure drop of 10 kPa. Compute: (i) the volumetric flow rate, (ii) the maximum velocity, and (iii) the wall shear stress. (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $R = 50 \, \mu\text{m} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$
- $L = 5\,\text{cm} = 0.05\,\text{m}$
- $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- $\Delta P = 10\,\text{kPa} = 10^4\,\text{Pa}$

**(i) Volumetric Flow Rate:**

$$
Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi (50 \times 10^{-6})^4 \cdot 10^4}{8 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 0.05}
$$

Computing numerator: $(50)^4 = 6.25 \times 10^6$, $(10^{-6})^4 = 10^{-24}$, so $(50 \times 10^{-6})^4 = 6.25 \times 10^{-18}$.

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-18} \cdot 10^4}{8 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 0.05}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{1.9635 \times 10^{-13}}{4 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = 4.91 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 0.491\,\mu\text{L/s}}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Velocity** (occurs at $r = 0$):

$$
v_{\max} = \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4 \mu L}
$$

$$
v_{\max} = \frac{10^4 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 0.05}
$$

$$
v_{\max} = \frac{10^4 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{\max} = 0.125\,\text{m/s}}
$$

**(iii) Wall Shear Stress:**

$$
\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \, R}{2 L}
$$

$$
\tau_w = \frac{10^4 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \cdot 0.05}
$$

$$
\tau_w = \frac{0.5}{0.1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_w = 5\,\text{Pa}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Unit conversion errors are the #1 reason for losing marks.** Convert µm to m, kPa to Pa, and cm to m **before** substituting.
> - Don't forget to mention the **7 assumptions** in part (a). Examiners specifically check this.
> - In part (b), state **where** the maximum velocity occurs ($r = 0$) and **why** the wall shear stress formula comes from $\tau = -\mu (dv/dr)|_{r=R}$.
> - Always box the final numerical answer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Starting from the general Navier–Stokes equation, derive the dimensionless form and show that the Reynolds number emerges naturally as a similarity parameter. (7 Marks)**

**Starting Equation:**

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{v} \right) = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} + \rho \mathbf{g}
$$

**Step 1:** Define dimensionless variables using characteristic scales $L_c$, $U$, $T = L_c/U$, and $\rho U^2$ for pressure:

$$
\mathbf{v} = U \tilde{\mathbf{v}}, \quad \mathbf{x} = L_c \tilde{\mathbf{x}}, \quad t = \frac{L_c}{U}\tilde{t}, \quad P = \rho U^2 \tilde{P}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute scaled operators:

$$
\nabla = \frac{1}{L_c}\tilde{\nabla}, \quad \nabla^2 = \frac{1}{L_c^2}\tilde{\nabla}^2, \quad \frac{\partial}{\partial t} = \frac{U}{L_c}\frac{\partial}{\partial \tilde{t}}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the convective term:

$$
\mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{v} = \frac{U^2}{L_c}\tilde{\mathbf{v}} \cdot \tilde{\nabla} \tilde{\mathbf{v}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the viscous term:

$$
\mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} = \frac{\mu U}{L_c^2}\tilde{\nabla}^2 \tilde{\mathbf{v}}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the pressure term:

$$
\nabla P = \frac{\rho U^2}{L_c}\tilde{\nabla}\tilde{P}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute everything back into the original Navier–Stokes equation:

$$
\rho \frac{U^2}{L_c}\left(\frac{\partial \tilde{\mathbf{v}}}{\partial \tilde{t}} + \tilde{\mathbf{v}} \cdot \tilde{\nabla}\tilde{\mathbf{v}}\right) = -\frac{\rho U^2}{L_c}\tilde{\nabla}\tilde{P} + \frac{\mu U}{L_c^2}\tilde{\nabla}^2\tilde{\mathbf{v}} + \rho \mathbf{g}
$$

**Step 7:** Divide through by $\rho U^2 / L_c$:

$$
\frac{\partial \tilde{\mathbf{v}}}{\partial \tilde{t}} + \tilde{\mathbf{v}} \cdot \tilde{\nabla}\tilde{\mathbf{v}} = -\tilde{\nabla}\tilde{P} + \frac{1}{Re}\tilde{\nabla}^2\tilde{\mathbf{v}} + \frac{1}{Fr^2}\hat{\mathbf{g}}
$$

where $Re = \rho U L_c / \mu$ and $Fr = U / \sqrt{g L_c}$.

**Conclusion:** The Reynolds number emerges as the natural dimensionless group governing the relative magnitude of inertial and viscous terms. This proves that flows with the same $Re$ are dynamically similar, regardless of their individual dimensions or fluid properties.

**(b) A rectangular microchannel has width 200 µm and height 50 µm. Blood (ρ = 1060 kg/m³, μ = 3.5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s) flows through it with an average velocity of 5 mm/s. Calculate: (i) the Reynolds number using hydraulic diameter, and (ii) the type of flow regime. (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $w = 200 \, \mu\text{m} = 200 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$
- $h = 50 \, \mu\text{m} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$
- $\rho = 1060\,\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$
- $\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- $v = 5\,\text{mm/s} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m/s}$

**(i) Hydraulic Diameter:**

$$
D_h = \frac{4 A}{P_w} = \frac{4 \, w \, h}{2(w + h)}
$$

$$
D_h = \frac{2 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 10^{-6} + 50 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
D_h = \frac{2 \cdot 10^{-8}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 8 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m} = 80 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

**Reynolds Number:**

$$
Re = \frac{\rho \, v \, D_h}{\mu}
$$

$$
Re = \frac{1060 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 8 \times 10^{-5}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
Re = \frac{4.24 \times 10^{-4}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.121
$$

**(ii) Flow Regime:**

Since $Re = 0.121 \ll 2300$, the flow is **laminar**. In fact, since $Re < 1$, the flow is in the **Stokes regime**, meaning viscous forces completely dominate over inertial forces.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Hydraulic diameter formula:** Examiners specifically check whether students correctly identify the wetted perimeter for a rectangular channel as $P_w = 2(w+h)$, not $4(w+h)$. The factor of 2 (because two sides are top and bottom, two are sides) is critical.
> - **Stokes regime vs laminar regime distinction:** Students who simply write "laminar" lose 1 mark. Mentioning **Stokes flow** because $Re < 1$ shows deeper understanding.
> - **Unit conversion:** 5 mm/s = $5 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s, not 0.005 incorrectly placed in the numerator.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3 Fluid Dynamics**

- **Continuum Assumption:** Valid when $Kn < 0.001$. Always check Kn before applying classical equations.
- **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \rho v L_c / \mu$. Critical threshold $Re = 2300$ for pipe flow. BioMEMS flows have $Re$ between $10^{-6}$ and $100$.
- **Stokes Flow:** When $Re \ll 1$, drop the inertial term $\rho (\mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{v})$. Resulting equation is linear.
- **No-Slip BC:** Fluid velocity = wall velocity. At a stationary wall, this is zero. Causes parabolic velocity profile.
- **Continuity Equation:** $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$ for incompressible flow. In 1-D: $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$.
- **Navier–Stokes Equation:** Three terms — inertia, pressure, viscosity. Plus optional body force. This is the master equation.
- **Bernoulli:** Valid only for **inviscid, steady, incompressible** flow along a streamline. **Often inapplicable** in microfluidics.
- **Hagen–Poiseuille:** $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta P / (8 \mu L)$. **Fourth-power dependence on R** is a favourite numerical.
- **Wall Shear Stress:** $\tau_w = \Delta P R / (2L)$. Important for endothelial cell biology.
- **Stokes Drag:** $F_D = 6 \pi \mu R v$. Used for cells, particles, droplets moving in microchannels.
- **Capillary Number:** $Ca = \mu v / \gamma$. Governs droplet formation in two-phase microfluidics.
- **Péclet Number:** $Pe = v L_c / D$. Compares convection to diffusion. $Pe > 1$ means convection dominates — important in mixing.
- **Hydraulic Diameter:** $D_h = 4A/P_w$. Used for non-circular channels.
- **Body Forces in Microfluidics:** Gravity is often negligible because the Bond number is small. Capillary forces dominate.
- **Numerical Values to Memorise:** $\mu_{\text{water}} = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000$ kg/m³, $\gamma_{\text{water}} = 0.072$ N/m.
- **Common Exam Trap:** Confusing **kinematic viscosity** $\nu = \mu/\rho$ (units m²/s) with **dynamic viscosity** $\mu$ (units Pa·s).
- **Examiner's Pet Topic:** Why is flow in BioMEMS always laminar? Because $Re \ll 2300$ due to small $L_c$ and low $v$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuity Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Continuity Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuity Equation (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> The **Continuity Equation** is a fundamental conservation law in fluid mechanics that mathematically expresses the principle of **conservation of mass** for a flowing fluid. In the context of BioMEMS and microfluidics, it describes how the mass of a fluid is transported through a microchannel or any defined control volume, stating that the net mass flow rate entering a control volume must equal the net mass flow rate leaving it plus the rate of change of mass stored within the volume.

The general differential form of the continuity equation for a fluid with density $\rho$ and velocity vector $\vec{v}$ is given as:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$

Where:
- $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$ represents the **local (unsteady) rate of change of density** with respect to time.
- $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v})$ is the **convective divergence term**, representing the net mass flux out of the control volume per unit volume.

For microfluidic systems where the fluid is treated as **incompressible** (constant density), the equation simplifies drastically to:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

For a **one-dimensional microchannel** of cross-sectional area $A$, this further reduces to the most commonly used form:

$$Q = A \cdot v = \text{constant}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In BioMEMS, fluids are typically **liquids** (water, blood, buffer solutions) which behave as **incompressible fluids** because the Mach number is very low and density changes are negligible. Therefore, the **incompressible continuity equation** is the primary form used in the design and analysis of microfluidic devices.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **garden hose** connected to a kitchen tap. When you partially cover the opening of the hose with your thumb, the water sprays out faster. Why? Because the **same amount of water** (same mass per unit time) must pass through every cross-section of the hose. When you reduce the cross-sectional area, the velocity **must increase** to keep the flow rate constant.

In a **microfluidic chip**, this same principle applies at the microscale. Consider a microchannel that narrows from a wide region (reservoir) to a narrow region (constriction). The fluid will flow **faster** through the narrow region to maintain a constant volumetric flow rate $Q$. This is critical in designing devices like:

- **Lab-on-a-chip** devices
- **Microfluidic mixers**
- **Droplet generators**
- **Cell sorting channels**

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Assumptions in Microfluidic Continuity Analysis:**
> - **Density** $\rho$: Assumed constant for liquids ($\approx 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for water, $\approx 1060 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for blood).
> - **Flow regime**: Laminar (Reynolds number $\ll 1$ in most microchannels).
> - **Fluid type**: Newtonian (constant viscosity).
> - **Volumetric flow rate** $Q$: Measured in $\mu \text{L/min}$ or $\text{nL/s}$ in typical BioMEMS applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

The continuity equation is derived from a single, powerful physical principle: **Mass can neither be created nor destroyed** (conservation of mass). Let us understand the logical flow:

- **Step 1: Define a Control Volume (CV)**
  A control volume is a fixed region in space through which fluid flows. In microfluidics, this is typically a segment of a microchannel of length $dx$.

- **Step 2: Apply Conservation of Mass**
  The rate of accumulation of mass inside the CV equals the net rate of mass inflow minus the net rate of mass outflow.

  $$\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}$$

- **Step 3: Express Mass Flow Rate**
  For a fluid with density $\rho$ moving with velocity $v$ through an area $A$:

  $$\dot{m} = \rho A v$$

- **Step 4: Combine and Take the Limit**
  Dividing by the volume and shrinking the CV to a point yields the differential form:

  $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$

- **Step 5: Apply the Incompressible Assumption**
  Since liquids in microchannels have constant density ($\frac{D\rho}{Dt} = 0$), the time derivative vanishes, leaving:

  $$\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$$

- **Step 6: Reduce to 1D Microchannel Flow**
  For a uniform microchannel where velocity is only along the axial direction $x$:

  $$\frac{dv}{dx} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \text{constant} \times \frac{1}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 Examinations**

| **Formula** | **Name / Form** | **Application / Condition** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$ | General Continuity Equation | Compressible, unsteady flow | $\text{kg/(m}^3\cdot\text{s)}$ |
| $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ | Incompressible Continuity | Liquid flow in microchannels | $1/\text{s}$ |
| $\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$ | Cartesian Incompressible Form | 3D microfluidic analysis | $1/\text{s}$ |
| $\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$ | Cylindrical Incompressible Form | Flow in circular microtubes | $1/\text{s}$ |
| $Q = A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | 1D Incompressible Continuity | Microchannel of varying cross-section | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| $\rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2$ | 1D Compressible Continuity | Gas flow, variable density | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| $\dot{m} = \rho A v$ | Mass Flow Rate | Any fluid in a channel | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| $v = \frac{Q}{A}$ | Velocity from Flow Rate | Calculating fluid velocity | $\text{m/s}$ |
| $A = \pi r^2$ | Cross-sectional Area (circular) | Circular microchannels | $\text{m}^2$ |
| $A = w \times h$ | Cross-sectional Area (rectangular) | Rectangular microchannels | $\text{m}^2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap:** Do **NOT** confuse the **incompressible continuity equation** ($\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$) with the **incompressible Navier-Stokes equation** ($\rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{v}$). The continuity equation is a **kinematic** constraint, while Navier-Stokes is a **dynamic** momentum balance.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The continuity equation is the **backbone** of all microfluidic device design:

- **Flow Rate Calculation**: Determining the volumetric throughput of a lab-on-a-chip.
- **Velocity Profiling**: Predicting fluid velocity at constrictions, expansions, and bifurcations.
- **Network Analysis**: Solving flow distribution in branched microchannel networks (similar to Kirchhoff's current law in electrical circuits).
- **Mass Transport Modeling**: Coupled with the convection-diffusion equation, it predicts species concentration in drug delivery and biosensor devices.
- **Design of Microfluidic Mixers**: Used to ensure proper flow ratios in T-mixers and Y-mixers.
- **Digital Microfluidics (DMF)**: Governing droplet manipulation on electrode arrays.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the General Continuity Equation from Conservation of Mass

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Goal:** Starting from the integral conservation of mass, derive the differential continuity equation applicable to microfluidic systems.

**Step 1: Consider an Arbitrary Control Volume (CV)**
Let $V$ be a fixed control volume in space, bounded by a control surface $S$ with outward unit normal $\hat{n}$.

The total mass contained within the CV at time $t$ is:

$$m_{CV} = \int_{V} \rho \, dV$$

**Step 2: Apply Conservation of Mass**
The rate of change of mass inside the CV equals the net mass flux entering the CV:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{V} \rho \, dV = -\oint_{S} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dS$$

The negative sign indicates that positive $\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}$ means outflow (mass leaving), which decreases the mass inside the CV.

**Step 3: Apply the Divergence Theorem (Gauss's Theorem)**
Transform the surface integral into a volume integral:

$$\oint_{S} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dS = \int_{V} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \, dV$$

**Step 4: Substitute Back into the Conservation Statement**

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{V} \rho \, dV = -\int_{V} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \, dV$$

**Step 5: Bring Everything to One Side**

$$\int_{V} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \right] dV = 0$$

**Step 6: Apply the Local Form (Arbitrary CV Principle)**
Since the integrand must vanish for an **arbitrary** control volume $V$, the integrand itself must be zero:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **general continuity equation**, valid for any fluid (compressible or incompressible) in any flow regime.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Incompressible Continuity Equation

**Step 1: Expand the Divergence Term Using the Product Rule**

$$\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \rho \, \nabla \cdot \vec{v} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \rho$$

**Step 2: Rewrite the Continuity Equation in Expanded Form**

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho \, \nabla \cdot \vec{v} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \rho = 0$$

**Step 3: Recognize the Material Derivative**
The material derivative of density following a fluid particle is:

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \rho$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the Continuity Equation**

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho \, \nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

**Step 5: Apply the Incompressible Assumption**
For an incompressible fluid, the density of any fluid particle does not change as it moves:

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} = 0$$

**Step 6: Conclude the Incompressible Form**

$$\rho \, \nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the 1D Continuity Equation for Microchannels

**Step 1: Start with Mass Flow Rate Conservation**
For a microchannel with two cross-sections (1 and 2), the steady-state mass flow rate must be conserved:

$$\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2$$

**Step 2: Express Mass Flow Rate in Terms of Density, Area, and Velocity**

$$\rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2$$

**Step 3: For Incompressible Flow, $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$**

$$\rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A_2 v_2$$

**Step 4: Cancel the Density on Both Sides**

$$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$$

**Step 5: Define Volumetric Flow Rate $Q = Av$**

$$Q_1 = Q_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = \text{constant}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A rectangular microchannel has a width of $w = 200 \, \mu\text{m}$ and a height of $h = 50 \, \mu\text{m}$ at the inlet. The channel narrows to a width of $w = 100 \, \mu\text{m}$ while maintaining the same height. If the inlet velocity is $v_1 = 5 \text{ mm/s}$, calculate the outlet velocity and the volumetric flow rate. Assume steady, incompressible, 1D flow with water as the working fluid ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$).

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate the Inlet Cross-Sectional Area**

$$A_1 = w_1 \times h_1 = (200 \times 10^{-6}) \times (50 \times 10^{-6}) = 1 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Outlet Cross-Sectional Area**

$$A_2 = w_2 \times h_2 = (100 \times 10^{-6}) \times (50 \times 10^{-6}) = 5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Apply the 1D Incompressible Continuity Equation**

$$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$$

**Step 4: Solve for the Outlet Velocity**

$$v_2 = \frac{A_1 v_1}{A_2} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-8})(5 \times 10^{-3})}{5 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{5 \times 10^{-11}}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.01 \text{ m/s} = 10 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the Volumetric Flow Rate**

$$Q = A_1 v_1 = (1 \times 10^{-8})(5 \times 10^{-3}) = 5 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 6: Convert to More Convenient Units**

$$Q = 5 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mL/s} = 3 \text{ mL/min} = 3000 \, \mu\text{L/min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** The velocity **doubled** (from $5 \text{ mm/s}$ to $10 \text{ mm/s}$) when the cross-sectional area was **halved**. This is a direct consequence of the continuity equation and is fundamental to the design of constriction-based flow accelerators in BioMEMS.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram: Derivation Logic Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Conservation of Mass Principle] --> B[Integral Form over Control Volume]
    B --> C[Apply Divergence Theorem]
    C --> D[General Differential Continuity Equation]
    D --> E{Is Fluid Compressible?}
    E --> F[Compressible Form: Use D rho by Dt]
    E --> G[Incompressible Assumption: D rho by Dt equals 0]
    G --> H[Incompressible Form: divergence of v equals 0]
    H --> I{Is Flow 1D?}
    I --> J[3D Cartesian or Cylindrical Form]
    I --> K[1D Microchannel Form: A1 v1 equals A2 v2]
    K --> L[Volumetric Flow Rate: Q equals constant]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Application in BioMEMS Device

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        A1[Fluid Reservoir]
        A2[Syringe Pump or Pressure Controller]
    end

    subgraph MICROCHIP[Microfluidic Chip]
        B1[Inlet Channel: Wide Cross-Section A1]
        B2[Constriction Region: Narrow Cross-Section A2]
        B3[Expansion Region: Wide Cross-Section A3]
        B4[Sensor or Detection Zone]
        B5[Outlet Channel: Wide Cross-Section A4]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage]
        C1[Waste Reservoir]
        C2[Flow Rate Measurement]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[Governing Equations]
        D1[Continuity Equation: A1v1 equals A2v2]
        D2[Navier-Stokes for Velocity Profile]
        D3[Reynolds Number: Re equals rho v Dh by mu]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> C2

    D1 -.governs.-> B1
    D1 -.governs.-> B2
    D1 -.governs.-> B3
    D2 -.governs.-> B4
    D3 -.governs.-> B5
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Continuity Equation Variants and Their Applicability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuity Equation Family] --> B[General Form: Partial rho by Partial t plus divergence of rho v equals 0]
    A --> C[Incompressible Form: divergence of v equals 0]
    A --> D[1D Form: A1v1 equals A2v2]
    A --> E[Cylindrical Form: divergence in r theta z]

    B --> B1[Compressible unsteady flow: gases in microchannels]
    C --> C1[Liquids in BioMEMS: water, blood, buffers]
    D --> D1[Microchannel design: constriction, expansion, bifurcation]
    E --> E1[Circular microtubes and capillary-based BioMEMS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This block-level architecture illustrates how the continuity equation serves as the **governing conservation law** that interlinks the input, microchannel, and output stages of any BioMEMS device. The flow rate entering the inlet must equal the flow rate exiting the outlet, and the velocity adjusts inversely with the cross-sectional area at every point along the channel.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the continuity equation for an incompressible fluid in a microchannel.**

**Model Answer:**

The continuity equation for an incompressible fluid expresses the **conservation of mass** for steady, 1D flow through a microchannel. It states that the **volumetric flow rate** $Q$ remains constant at every cross-section of the channel.

Mathematically:

$$Q = A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = \text{constant}$$

Where $A$ is the cross-sectional area and $v$ is the average fluid velocity. The underlying physical principle is that mass can neither be created nor destroyed, so the same volume of fluid must pass through every cross-section per unit time. In a narrowing channel, the velocity must increase proportionally to maintain constant flow rate.

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Differentiate between the general continuity equation and the incompressible continuity equation. State the key assumption involved.**

**Model Answer:**

The **general continuity equation** applies to all fluids (compressible and incompressible) and is given by:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$

The **incompressible continuity equation** is a simplified form applicable when the fluid density is constant:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

**Key Assumption:** The fluid is **incompressible**, meaning $\frac{D\rho}{Dt} = 0$ (the density of any fluid particle does not change as it moves with the flow). This assumption is valid for **liquids** (water, blood, buffer solutions) flowing at low Mach numbers in typical BioMEMS applications, but is not valid for **gases** at high speeds.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the general continuity equation in differential form starting from the integral form of conservation of mass. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A microfluidic channel has a circular cross-section with inlet diameter $D_1 = 500 \, \mu\text{m}$ and outlet diameter $D_2 = 250 \, \mu\text{m}$. If water flows at a volumetric flow rate of $Q = 2 \text{ mL/min}$, calculate the average velocities at the inlet and outlet. Take $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1: Define the Control Volume**
Consider a fixed region of space (control volume) $V$ bounded by a closed surface $S$. The total mass inside $V$ is:

$$m = \int_{V} \rho \, dV$$

**Step 2: Write the Conservation Statement**
The rate of change of mass inside $V$ equals the net rate of mass flowing in:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{V} \rho \, dV = -\oint_{S} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dS$$

**Step 3: Apply the Divergence Theorem**
Since $\oint_{S} \vec{F} \cdot \hat{n} \, dS = \int_{V} \nabla \cdot \vec{F} \, dV$:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{V} \rho \, dV = -\int_{V} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \, dV$$

**Step 4: Move All Terms to One Side**

$$\int_{V} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \right] dV = 0$$

**Step 5: Apply the Arbitrary CV Principle**
For the integral to vanish for an arbitrary volume, the integrand must be zero:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0}$$

**Assumptions:** (i) No mass sources or sinks inside the CV. (ii) Single-phase, single-component fluid. (iii) The control volume is fixed in space (Eulerian framework).

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1: Convert the Volumetric Flow Rate to SI Units**

$$Q = 2 \text{ mL/min} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 3.333 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Inlet Cross-Sectional Area**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (500 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Inlet Velocity**

$$v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{3.333 \times 10^{-8}}{1.963 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.1698 \text{ m/s} \approx 0.17 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Outlet Cross-Sectional Area**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (250 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5: Calculate the Outlet Velocity**

$$v_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{3.333 \times 10^{-8}}{4.909 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.679 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6: Verify Using Continuity Equation**

$$\frac{v_2}{v_1} = \frac{A_1}{A_2} = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{500}{250}\right)^2 = 4$$

$$v_2 = 4 \times v_1 = 4 \times 0.1698 = 0.679 \text{ m/s} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7: Calculate the Mass Flow Rate**

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 3.333 \times 10^{-8} = 3.333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Final Answer:** $v_1 = 0.17 \text{ m/s}$, $v_2 = 0.679 \text{ m/s}$, $\dot{m} = 3.333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Starting from the general continuity equation, derive the simplified form applicable to incompressible fluids. Explain the significance of each term in the resulting equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A T-junction microfluidic mixer mixes two streams. Stream 1 (water) enters through channel 1 of width $w_1 = 300 \, \mu\text{m}$ and height $h = 100 \, \mu\text{m}$ at velocity $v_1 = 2 \text{ mm/s}$. Stream 2 (buffer) enters through channel 2 of width $w_2 = 200 \, \mu\text{m}$ and same height at velocity $v_2 = 3 \text{ mm/s}$. Calculate the velocity of the combined stream in the downstream channel of width $w_3 = 500 \, \mu\text{m}$ and same height. Assume steady, incompressible, 1D flow. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1: Start with the General Continuity Equation**

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$

**Step 2: Expand the Divergence Term Using the Product Rule**

$$\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) + \vec{v} \cdot (\nabla \rho)$$

**Step 3: Rewrite the Equation**

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) + \vec{v} \cdot (\nabla \rho) = 0$$

**Step 4: Recognize the Material Derivative of Density**

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot (\nabla \rho)$$

**Step 5: Substitute to Obtain the Compact Form**

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) = 0$$

**Step 6: Apply the Incompressible Assumption**
For an incompressible fluid, the density of a fluid element does not change as it moves:

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} = 0$$

**Step 7: Derive the Incompressible Form**

$$\rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

**Significance of Each Term:**

- $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$: Represents the **local (unsteady)** rate of change of density at a fixed point in space. In incompressible flow, this term is **zero** because density does not change with time at any point.

- $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v})$: Represents the **net mass flux** leaving a small fluid element per unit volume. In incompressible flow, this divergence must also be zero, meaning the net volumetric flow into any infinitesimal element equals the net volumetric flow out.

- The final equation $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ is a **kinematic constraint** that restricts the allowable velocity fields in the fluid. It does not provide any information about the forces or pressures; that role is filled by the Navier-Stokes equation.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1: Identify the Governing Principle**
At a T-junction, the volumetric flow rates from the two inlet streams must combine and equal the flow rate in the downstream channel:

$$Q_1 + Q_2 = Q_3$$

**Step 2: Express Each Flow Rate as $Q = Av$**

$$A_1 v_1 + A_2 v_2 = A_3 v_3$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Inlet Areas**

$$A_1 = w_1 \times h = (300 \times 10^{-6})(100 \times 10^{-6}) = 3 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = w_2 \times h = (200 \times 10^{-6})(100 \times 10^{-6}) = 2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_3 = w_3 \times h = (500 \times 10^{-6})(100 \times 10^{-6}) = 5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Convert Velocities to SI Units**

$$v_1 = 2 \text{ mm/s} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_2 = 3 \text{ mm/s} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the Individual Flow Rates**

$$Q_1 = A_1 v_1 = (3 \times 10^{-8})(2 \times 10^{-3}) = 6 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_2 = A_2 v_2 = (2 \times 10^{-8})(3 \times 10^{-3}) = 6 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 6: Sum the Inlet Flow Rates**

$$Q_3 = Q_1 + Q_2 = 6 \times 10^{-11} + 6 \times 10^{-11} = 12 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 7: Calculate the Downstream Velocity**

$$v_3 = \frac{Q_3}{A_3} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-11}}{5 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} = 2.4 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Final Answer:** The combined stream velocity in the downstream channel is $v_3 = 2.4 \text{ mm/s}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the incompressible assumption properly:** Students often write $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ without explaining that it arises from $\frac{D\rho}{Dt} = 0$. You will lose **1 to 2 marks** if the derivation is incomplete.
>
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** Microfluidic dimensions are in **micrometers ($\mu\text{m}$)** and flow rates are often in **mL/min** or **$\mu\text{L/min}$**. Forgetting to convert to SI units (m, s) will result in a **wrong numerical answer** and loss of **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Confusing average velocity with maximum velocity:** In a 1D continuity analysis, we use the **average (mean) velocity** $v_{avg} = Q/A$, not the maximum centerline velocity. Students confuse this with the parabolic profile of laminar flow.
>
> 4. **Not specifying the control volume:** In derivation questions, students often skip the step of defining a control volume explicitly. KTU examiners specifically look for this as a logical step in the derivation. Missing this costs **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Mixing up mass and volumetric flow rates:** In the 1D form, $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ is for **incompressible** flow. If density is constant, $\rho$ cancels. For compressible flow, you must use $\rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
