> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Continuity Equation (Module 3)**

- **Core Principle:** The continuity equation is the **mathematical statement of conservation of mass** for a flowing fluid.

- **General Form:** $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$

- **Incompressible Form:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ (valid for liquids in microchannels; assumes constant density).

- **1D Microchannel Form:** $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ or equivalently $Q = Av = \text{constant}$.

- **Key Inverse Relationship:** Velocity is **inversely proportional** to cross-sectional area for a constant flow rate.

- **Material Derivative Identity:** $\frac{D\rho}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \rho$ links the general and incompressible forms.

- **BioMEMS Application:** Used in flow rate calculation, velocity profiling, network analysis, mixer design, and mass transport modeling.

- **Units to Remember:** Area in $\text{m}^2$, velocity in $\text{m/s}$, flow rate $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ (or $\text{mL/min}$), density in $\text{kg/m}^3$.

- **Physical Intuition:** Same volume of fluid must pass through every cross-section per unit time — reducing the area forces the fluid to speed up.

- **Distinction from Navier-Stokes:** Continuity is a **kinematic** (geometric/flow) constraint; Navier-Stokes is a **dynamic** (force/momentum) balance.

- **Branching Networks:** At any junction, $\sum Q_{in} = \sum Q_{out}$ (analogous to Kirchhoff's current law for electrical circuits).

- **Steady vs Unsteady:** In steady flow, $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$; in unsteady flow, the time derivative must be retained.

- **Limitation:** The continuity equation alone cannot determine the velocity field; it must be coupled with the **Navier-Stokes equation** (or Euler equation for inviscid flow) to obtain a complete solution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Momentum Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Momentum Equation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Momentum Equation**, in the context of BioMEMS and Microfluidics, is the mathematical statement of **Newton's Second Law of Motion** applied to a fluid continuum. It expresses the conservation of linear momentum for a fluid element and is universally known as the **Navier–Stokes Equation** for Newtonian fluids. For a Newtonian, incompressible fluid with constant viscosity, the vector form is: $$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$ where: - $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³) - $\vec{V}$ = velocity vector field (m/s) - $p$ = pressure (Pa) - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s or kg/(m·s)) - $\vec{g}$ = body force per unit mass (m/s²) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Momentum Equation is the **governing equation of fluid flow** at the micro-scale. In BioMEMS, it is the foundation for designing microchannels, drug-delivery devices, lab-on-chip systems, and biosensors. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **commuter train** travelling through a crowded station: - The **train (fluid element)** carries momentum — it has both **mass** and **velocity**. - The **station walls (channel walls)** push back on the train (this is the **viscous force**, $\mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V}$). - The **station escalators (pressure gradient, $-\nabla p$)** can speed the train up or slow it down. - **Gravity ($\rho \vec{g}$)** pulls the train downward. - The **conductor's voice (body forces)** may add external pushes. - The **train's own tendency to keep moving or turn (inertia, $\rho \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V}$)** is what Newton's law balances. In a **microfluidic channel**, however, the "station" is so small that the walls dominate — viscous forces crush inertial forces, which is why microfluidic flow is **slow, smooth, laminar, and predictable**. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants to Remember:** > - Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C: $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ **Pa·s** > - Density of water at 20 °C: $\rho = 998.2$ **kg/m³** > - Kinematic viscosity of water: $\nu = \mu / \rho \approx 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ **m²/s** > - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81$ **m/s²** ## 1.3 Why the Momentum Equation Matters in BioMEMS In BioMEMS devices, channels have characteristic lengths ranging from **1 µm to 1000 µm**. At this scale: | Phenomena | Macro-scale | Micro-scale | |---|---|---| | Dominant force | Inertia | **Viscosity** | | Flow regime | Turbulent possible | **Strictly laminar** | | Reynolds number ($Re$) | $10^3$ – $10^6$ | **Typically $Re < 1$** | | Mixing strategy | Turbulent diffusion | **Molecular diffusion only** | This is why the momentum equation in its **simplified, low-Re form (Stokes flow / Creeping flow)** is the workhorse of all microfluidic analysis. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Decomposition of the Momentum Equation The Navier–Stokes equation contains **four fundamental competing effects** per unit volume. Understanding each term is the key to KTU board answers. ### Term-by-Term Logical Breakdown **1. Local (Temporal) Acceleration Term** $$\rho \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$$ Represents the change in momentum at a **fixed point** in space as time progresses. Present in **unsteady flows** (e.g., pulsatile blood flow in microchannels). **2. Convective (Advective) Inertia Term** $$\rho \, (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla) \vec{V}$$ Represents momentum carried **into or out of** a fluid element by bulk flow. Dominant in high-speed flows; **negligible in micro-scale creeping flow**. **3. Pressure Gradient Force** $$-\nabla p$$ A **potential force** — fluid moves from high pressure to low pressure. This is the *driving force* in most lab-on-chip devices, often generated by syringe pumps. **4. Viscous Diffusion Term** $$\mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V}$$ Momentum diffusion caused by **internal friction** between fluid layers. Dominant at low Reynolds numbers — the regime of almost all microfluidic systems. **5. Body Force Term** $$\rho \vec{g}$$ External forces acting at a distance (gravity, electric, magnetic). In horizontal microchannels, gravity is often **negligible** compared to viscous forces. ## 2.2 The Substantial (Material) Derivative The left-hand side of the momentum equation is the **substantial derivative** of velocity, denoted $\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$: $$\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla) \vec{V}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why "substantial"?** Because it tracks the rate of change of velocity **following a moving fluid particle**, not at a fixed spatial point. The first part (local derivative) captures *unsteadiness*; the second (convective) captures *transport by flow*. ## 2.3 The Continuity Equation (Companion to Momentum) A complete microfluidic analysis requires **two coupled PDEs**: momentum + continuity. For an incompressible fluid: $$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$ This enforces **mass conservation** — what flows in must flow out. ## 2.4 Boundary Conditions (Essential for KTU) | Boundary Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | **No-slip (wall)** | $\vec{V} = 0$ at wall | Fluid sticks to solid surface | | **No-penetration** | $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = 0$ | Fluid cannot pass through wall | | **Inlet** | $\vec{V} = \vec{V}_{\text{prescribed}}$ | Velocity profile imposed at entry | | **Outlet** | $p = p_{\text{prescribed}}$ | Pressure imposed at exit | | **Symmetry** | $\partial \vec{V}/\partial n = 0$ | Mirror-image flow across axis | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — every formula here is a potential KTU exam item.** | # | Equation / Expression | Form | Domain of Validity | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$ | Full Navier–Stokes | Newtonian, incompressible, isothermal | | 2 | $\mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} = \nabla p$ | Stokes (creeping) flow | $Re \ll 1$ (micro-scale) | | 3 | $\rho \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V}$ | Unsteady Stokes | Pulsatile microflow | | 4 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | Continuity (incompressible) | All liquids, low-speed gas | | 5 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | Dimensionless inertia-to-viscosity ratio | | 6 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's law of viscosity | Newtonian fluid | | 7 | $\dfrac{D}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)$ | Substantial derivative | General fluid flow | | 8 | $P = \dfrac{p L}{\mu V}$ | Pressure scaling (creeping) | Microchannel design | | 9 | $F_{\text{viscous}} \sim \mu V L$ | Viscous force scale | Order-of-magnitude analysis | | 10 | $F_{\text{inertial}} \sim \rho V^{2} L^{2}$ | Inertial force scale | Order-of-magnitude analysis | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Drug delivery microneedles:** Predict pressure drop vs. flow rate. - **Lab-on-a-chip diagnostics:** Design channel networks ensuring uniform residence time. - **Artificial organs (e.g., artificial kidney):** Blood flow in hollow fibres obeys low-Re momentum equation. - **Inkjet bio-printing:** Droplet ejection dynamics require transient momentum balance. - **DNA electrophoresis:** Electroosmotic body force inserted into momentum equation. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Newton's Second Law to the Navier–Stokes Equation **Starting Principle:** Newton's second law for a control volume (CV) of fluid of mass $dm$: $$\sum \vec{F} = \frac{d(m\vec{V})}{dt}$$ **Step 1 — Identify the forces acting on the fluid element.** The forces per unit volume are: - Pressure force (normal stress integrated over surface) - Viscous force (shear stress integrated over surface) - Body force (gravity, electric, etc.) **Step 2 — Express the pressure force via the divergence theorem.** The net pressure force on a closed surface $S$ enclosing volume $\mathcal{V}$ is: $$\vec{F}_{\text{pressure}} = -\oint_{S} p \, \hat{n} \, dA = -\int_{\mathcal{V}} \nabla p \, dV$$ The negative sign arises because pressure acts **inward** on the surface. **Step 3 — Express the viscous force via the viscous stress tensor.** For a Newtonian fluid, the viscous stress tensor is: $$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mu \left[ \nabla \vec{V} + (\nabla \vec{V})^{T} \right] - \frac{2}{3} \mu (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) \mathbf{I}$$ The net viscous force is: $$\vec{F}_{\text{viscous}} = \oint_{S} \boldsymbol{\tau} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA = \int_{\mathcal{V}} \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} \, dV$$ **Step 4 — Sum all forces and equate to rate of change of momentum.** $$\int_{\mathcal{V}} \rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} \, dV = -\int_{\mathcal{V}} \nabla p \, dV + \int_{\mathcal{V}} \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} \, dV + \int_{\mathcal{V}} \rho \vec{g} \, dV$$ **Step 5 — Localize the integrand (since the volume is arbitrary).** $$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} + \rho \vec{g}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute the Newtonian stress tensor and apply $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ (incompressibility).** After substitution, the bulk viscosity term vanishes, leaving: $$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$ > [!NOTE] > **This is the Navier–Stokes Equation — the central result of this module.** The derivation above is a **board-exam favourite** for a 7- or 14-mark KTU question. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reduction to the Stokes (Creeping Flow) Equation In a typical microchannel of width $w \sim 100\,\mu\text{m}$, with water ($\nu \approx 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$) flowing at $V \sim 1\,\text{mm}/\text{s}$: $$Re = \frac{V w}{\nu} = \frac{(10^{-3})(10^{-4})}{10^{-6}} = 0.1 \ll 1$$ **Step 1 — Non-dimensionalize the momentum equation.** Introduce dimensionless variables using $L_{c}$ (characteristic length), $U$ (characteristic velocity), $P_{c} = \mu U / L_{c}$: $$\frac{\rho U^{2}}{L_{c}}(\vec{V}^{*} \cdot \nabla^{*} \vec{V}^{*}) = -\frac{\mu U}{L_{c}^{2}} \nabla^{*} p^{*} + \frac{\mu U}{L_{c}^{2}} \nabla^{*2} \vec{V}^{*} + \rho \vec{g}$$ **Step 2 — Divide through by $\mu U / L_{c}^{2}$ to obtain:** $$Re \, (\vec{V}^{*} \cdot \nabla^{*} \vec{V}^{*}) = -\nabla^{*} p^{*} + \nabla^{*2} \vec{V}^{*} + \frac{\rho \vec{g} L_{c}^{2}}{\mu U}$$ **Step 3 — Take the limit $Re \rightarrow 0$.** All convective-inertia terms vanish: $$\boxed{\mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} = \nabla p \quad \text{(Stokes / Creeping Flow)}}$$ This is the **dominant momentum equation in BioMEMS**. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure-Driven Flow Between Parallel Plates (Poiseuille Flow) This is the **classic microfluidic problem** and a KTU high-priority derivation. **Setup:** Two infinite parallel plates separated by gap $h$, pressure gradient $\dfrac{dp}{dx} = $ const, fully developed, steady, unidirectional flow $u(y)$. **Step 1 — Simplify the Navier–Stokes equation in the x-direction.** For fully developed, unidirectional flow: - $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$ - $v = 0, \, w = 0$ - $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 0$ - Inertia = 0 The x-momentum reduces to: $$0 = -\frac{dp}{dx} + \mu \frac{d^{2} u}{dy^{2}}$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{d^{2} u}{dy^{2}} = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{dp}{dx}$$ **Step 2 — Integrate twice with respect to $y$.** $$u(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{dp}{dx} y^{2} + C_{1} y + C_{2}$$ **Step 3 — Apply no-slip boundary conditions.** Place the plates at $y = 0$ and $y = h$. Therefore: - $u(0) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_{2} = 0$ - $u(h) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_{1} = -\dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dp}{dx} h$ **Step 4 — Final velocity profile.** $$u(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{dp}{dx} \left( y^{2} - hy \right) = -\frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{dp}{dx} \, y(h - y)$$ **Step 5 — Volumetric flow rate per unit width.** $$q = \int_{0}^{h} u(y) \, dy = -\frac{h^{3}}{12 \mu} \frac{dp}{dx}$$ **Step 6 — Maximum velocity (at $y = h/2$ and with $dp/dx < 0$ for flow in $+x$):** $$u_{\max} = -\frac{h^{2}}{8\mu} \frac{dp}{dx}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination Pearl:** The volumetric flow rate scales as $h^{3}$. **Doubling the channel height increases the flow by a factor of 8** — a critical design insight in BioMEMS. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reynolds Number from Force Ratio **Step 1 — Identify characteristic scales for inertia and viscous force.** For a fluid element of size $L$ moving at speed $V$: $$F_{\text{inertial}} \sim \rho V^{2} L^{2}$$ $$F_{\text{viscous}} \sim \mu \frac{V}{L} L^{2} = \mu V L$$ **Step 2 — Form the dimensionless ratio.** $$Re = \frac{F_{\text{inertial}}}{F_{\text{viscous}}} = \frac{\rho V^{2} L^{2}}{\mu V L} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}$$ **Step 3 — In terms of kinematic viscosity $\nu = \mu/\rho$:** $$Re = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$ **Step 4 — Physical interpretation in microfluidics.** For typical BioMEMS channels: $V \sim 10^{-3}\,\text{m/s}, \, L \sim 10^{-4}\,\text{m}, \, \nu \sim 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$ $$Re \sim \frac{(10^{-3})(10^{-4})}{10^{-6}} = 0.1$$ **Conclusion:** $Re \ll 1$ — viscous forces dominate, and the flow is **strictly laminar** with **negligible inertial effects**. The momentum equation simplifies to the **Stokes equation**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of the Momentum Equation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Newton Second Law
Sum F = d m V / dt] --> B[Identify Forces on CV
Pressure, Viscous, Body] B --> C[Apply Divergence Theorem
Surface to Volume Integral] C --> D[Substitute Newtonian
Stress Tensor] D --> E{Non-dimensionalize
and Inspect Re} E -->|Re greater than 1| F[Full Navier Stokes
Inertia Retained] E -->|Re less than 1| G[Stokes Creeping Flow
mu Laplacian V = grad p] F --> F1[Turbulent Macro Flow
e.g. Aircraft Aerodynamics] G --> G1[Laminar Micro Flow
e.g. BioMEMS Microchannels] G1 --> H1[No Slip BC
V equals 0 at wall] G1 --> H2[Continuity Equation
div V equals 0] G1 --> H3[Poiseuille Profile
q proportional h cubed] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Solving a Microfluidic Problem ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Problem Statement
Geometry and Fluid] --> P2[Identify Scales
L, V, mu, rho] P2 --> P3[Compute Re number] P3 --> P4{Re less than 1?} P4 -->|Yes| P5[Apply Stokes Eqn
mu Laplacian V = grad p] P4 -->|No| P6[Apply Full Navier Stokes] P5 --> P7[Apply Boundary Conditions
No Slip at Walls] P7 --> P8[Solve for V profile] P8 --> P9[Integrate to get Q
Volumetric Flow Rate] P9 --> P10[Compute Pressure Drop
Delta P] P10 --> P11[Validate with Experiments
or CFD Simulation] ``` ## 4.3 Force-Balance Schematic for a Fluid Element in a Microchannel ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Fluid_Element [Fluid Element inside Microchannel] direction TB F1[Pressure Force
Negative grad p] --> FB[Net Force] F2[Viscous Force
mu Laplacian V] --> FB F3[Body Force
rho g] --> FB F4[Inertial Force
rho D V over D t] --> FB end FB --> EQ[Newton Balance
F equals m a] EQ --> OUT[Momentum Equation Output
Velocity Field] ``` > [!NOTE] > The above diagrams map the **logical and mathematical flow** of deriving and applying the momentum equation in a BioMEMS context, which is a frequent KTU 14-mark question structure. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **State the Navier–Stokes equation for an incompressible Newtonian fluid. Explain the physical significance of each term.** #### Model Answer (3 marks): The Navier–Stokes equation for a Newtonian, incompressible fluid is: $$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$ **Physical significance of each term:** | Term | Physical Meaning | |---|---| | $\rho \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$ | Local (unsteady) acceleration of fluid | | $\rho (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla) \vec{V}$ | Convective inertia — momentum carried by bulk flow | | $-\nabla p$ | Force due to pressure gradient | | $\mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V}$ | Viscous diffusion of momentum | | $\rho \vec{g}$ | External body force (e.g., gravity) | --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **What is the Reynolds number? Why is it always very small in microfluidic BioMEMS devices?** #### Model Answer (3 marks): The Reynolds number is the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid: $$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$ In microfluidic BioMEMS devices, the characteristic length $L$ (channel width) is very small (typically $1\text{–}500\,\mu\text{m}$), and velocities are low. Hence, $Re \ll 1$ (often $Re < 1$, sometimes $Re < 0.01$). This means **viscous forces dominate over inertial forces**, the flow is **strictly laminar**, and mixing occurs only by **molecular diffusion** — a critical design constraint in lab-on-chip systems. --- ## PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] **(a)** Starting from Newton's second law of motion applied to a fluid element, **derive the general Navier–Stokes equation** for an incompressible Newtonian fluid. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks) **(b)** For a microfluidic device, water ($\mu = 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$, $\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$) flows steadily through a rectangular microchannel of width $h = 200\,\mu\text{m}$ and length $L = 5\,\text{cm}$ under a pressure drop of $\Delta p = 10\,\text{kPa}$. Determine: - (i) The maximum velocity $u_{\max}$ - (ii) The volumetric flow rate per unit width $q$ - (iii) The Reynolds number $Re$ and comment on the flow regime. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for (a): **Assumptions:** 1. Fluid is a continuum. 2. Fluid is Newtonian (linear stress–strain-rate relationship). 3. Fluid is incompressible ($\rho$ = constant). 4. Flow is isothermal. 5. Body force is gravity. **Derivation:** Apply Newton's second law to a control volume of fluid of mass $dm$: $$\sum \vec{F} = \frac{d}{dt}(m\vec{V})$$ For a fluid element of volume $dV$: $$\sum \vec{F}_{\text{volume}} \, dV = \rho \, dV \, \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$$ The forces are pressure, viscous, and body force: $$-\nabla p + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} + \rho \vec{g} = \rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$$ For a Newtonian fluid, the stress tensor is: $$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mu \left[ \nabla \vec{V} + (\nabla \vec{V})^{T} \right]$$ Substituting and applying $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$: $$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$ #### Model Solution for (b): **(i) Maximum velocity:** Pressure gradient: $$\frac{dp}{dx} = -\frac{\Delta p}{L} = -\frac{10 \times 10^{3}}{5 \times 10^{-2}} = -2 \times 10^{5} \, \text{Pa/m}$$ $$u_{\max} = -\frac{h^{2}}{8\mu} \frac{dp}{dx} = -\frac{(200 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}{8 \times 10^{-3}} \times (-2 \times 10^{5})$$ $$u_{\max} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-8}}{8 \times 10^{-3}} \times 2 \times 10^{5} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0 \, \text{m/s}$$ **(ii) Volumetric flow rate per unit width:** $$q = \frac{h^{3}}{12 \mu}\left(-\frac{dp}{dx}\right) = \frac{(200 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{12 \times 10^{-3}} \times 2 \times 10^{5}$$ $$q = \frac{8 \times 10^{-15}}{1.2 \times 10^{-2}} \times 2 \times 10^{5} = 1.33 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$$ **(iii) Reynolds number and flow regime:** $$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 1.0 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-3}} = 200$$ > Comment: $Re = 200$ is still within the **laminar regime** for a microchannel (transition occurs near $Re \approx 2000$ for parallel-plate flow). Inertial terms are small but **not negligible** here — a borderline case. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified] **(a)** Derive the simplified form of the Navier–Stokes equation valid in microfluidic (low-Reynolds-number) systems. Under what conditions is this simplification justified? (7 marks) **(b)** A long microchannel of rectangular cross-section $100\,\mu\text{m} \times 50\,\mu\text{m}$ carries an aqueous buffer solution. The flow is steady, fully developed, and driven by a pressure gradient $\dfrac{dp}{dx} = -1.5 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa/m}$. For water, $\mu = 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$ and $\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$. - (i) Verify that the flow is in the creeping-flow regime. - (ii) Compute the volumetric flow rate. - (iii) Determine the shear stress on the channel walls. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for (a): **Non-dimensionalization:** Introduce dimensionless variables $x^{*} = x/L_{c}, \, y^{*} = y/L_{c}, \, u^{*} = u/U, \, p^{*} = p/P_{c}$ with $P_{c} = \mu U / L_{c}$. Substituting into the Navier–Stokes x-momentum equation: $$\frac{\rho U^{2}}{L_{c}}\left(\vec{V}^{*}\cdot\nabla^{*}\vec{V}^{*}\right) = -\frac{\mu U}{L_{c}^{2}} \nabla^{*} p^{*} + \frac{\mu U}{L_{c}^{2}} \nabla^{*2} \vec{V}^{*} + \rho \vec{g}$$ Dividing by $\mu U / L_{c}^{2}$: $$Re \, (\vec{V}^{*}\cdot\nabla^{*}\vec{V}^{*}) = -\nabla^{*} p^{*} + \nabla^{*2} \vec{V}^{*} + \frac{\rho g L_{c}^{2}}{\mu U}$$ **Justification for simplification:** When $Re \ll 1$ (typical micro-scale), the convective-inertia term is **negligible** compared to the viscous term. For typical BioMEMS, $Re < 1$ and often $Re < 0.1$. Gravity is also negligible for horizontal channels of small height. **Simplified equation (Stokes / creeping flow):** $$\boxed{\mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} = \nabla p}$$ This is **linear**, allowing superposition of solutions and analytical treatment of complex geometries. #### Model Solution for (b): **(i) Verification of creeping flow regime:** Assume characteristic velocity. From Poiseuille profile (treating $h = 100\,\mu\text{m}$ as the dominant gap): $$u_{\max} = -\frac{h^{2}}{8\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} = -\frac{(10^{-4})^{2}}{8 \times 10^{-3}} \times (-1.5 \times 10^{6})$$ $$u_{\max} = \frac{10^{-8} \times 1.5 \times 10^{6}}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.015}{0.008} = 1.875 \, \text{m/s}$$ Reynolds number: $$Re = \frac{\rho u_{\max} h}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 1.875 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-3}} = 187.5$$ For microfluidic parallel-plate geometry, the **creeping-flow limit** is generally accepted for $Re \ll 200$. Here $Re = 187.5$ is at the **border** of validity; for an even smaller channel the simplification is excellent. **(ii) Volumetric flow rate (per unit width):** $$q = \frac{h^{3}}{12\mu}\left(-\frac{dp}{dx}\right) = \frac{(10^{-4})^{3}}{12 \times 10^{-3}} \times 1.5 \times 10^{6}$$ $$q = \frac{10^{-12} \times 1.5 \times 10^{6}}{1.2 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$$ Total flow rate (channel width $w = 50\,\mu\text{m}$): $$Q = q \times w = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 6.25 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} = 6.25 \, \mu\text{L/s}$$ **(iii) Shear stress on the wall:** Wall shear stress for Poiseuille flow (at $y = 0$ and $y = h$): $$\tau_{w} = \mu \left|\frac{du}{dy}\right|_{y=0} = \mu \left| -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}(2y - h)\right|_{y=0} = \frac{h}{2}\left(-\frac{dp}{dx}\right)$$ $$\tau_{w} = \frac{10^{-4}}{2} \times 1.5 \times 10^{6} = 75 \, \text{Pa}$$ This shear stress is critical for assessing **hemolysis** in blood-handling microdevices. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the substantial derivative decomposition** — Many students write only the local derivative $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t$ and miss the convective term $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$. **Both must be present** in the full Navier–Stokes. > 2. **Sign error in the pressure term** — It is $-\nabla p$, NOT $+\nabla p$. Pressure pushes from high to low. > 3. **Confusing Reynolds number limits** — In microfluidics, "small" typically means $Re < 1$ but the exact transition value depends on geometry. State the *assumption* clearly. > 4. **Dropping the continuity equation** — A momentum-only solution is incomplete. Always mention $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$. > 5. **Units inconsistency in numerical problems** — Convert $\mu\text{m}$ to $\text{m}$ before computing. KTU examiners deduct full marks for unit errors. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ The **Momentum Equation** is Newton's second law for a fluid: $\rho D\vec{V}/Dt = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2}\vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$. - ✅ The **substantial derivative** $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)$ is the rate of change following a fluid particle. - ✅ In **BioMEMS / microfluidics**, $Re \ll 1$, so the equation **simplifies to Stokes (creeping) flow**: $\mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} = \nabla p$. - ✅ The **continuity equation** $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ must always be solved together with the momentum equation. - ✅ The **no-slip boundary condition** $\vec{V} = 0$ at walls is fundamental — it makes microfluidics tractable. - ✅ For **Poiseuille flow** between parallel plates, the volumetric flow rate scales as $q \propto h^{3}$ — a critical scaling law. - ✅ The **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho V L / \mu$ quantifies the inertia-to-viscosity ratio; values $Re < 1$ are typical in BioMEMS. - ✅ **Wall shear stress** $\tau_{w} = (h/2)(-dp/dx)$ is vital for bio-compatibility and hemolysis analysis. - ✅ The Navier–Stokes equation is **non-linear** (due to $(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$), but becomes **linear** under the creeping-flow approximation. - ✅ **Newtonian fluid assumption** (constant $\mu$) holds for water, buffer solutions, blood plasma, glycerol mixtures — most BioMEMS working fluids.

topics.title: Equation of Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equation of Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equation of Motion (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The **Equation of Motion** for a fluid element is the mathematical statement of the **conservation of linear momentum** applied to a differential fluid parcel, expressed as the **Navier–Stokes equation**. For a Newtonian, incompressible fluid with constant viscosity, it is written as:
>
> $$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$
>
> In BioMEMS and microfluidics, the equation of motion governs the velocity and pressure fields inside microchannels, bioreactors, lab-on-chip devices, and drug-delivery microreservoirs.

Where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³)
- $\vec{V}$ = velocity vector field (m/s)
- $p$ = pressure (Pa)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $\vec{g}$ = body force per unit mass (m/s²)
- $\nabla p$ = pressure gradient force
- $\mu \nabla^{2}\vec{V}$ = viscous diffusion term
- $\rho \vec{g}$ = body force (gravity, etc.)

### 1.2 The Three Conservation Pillars in Microfluidics

| Conservation Law | Resulting Equation | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Mass | Continuity Equation | Fluid is neither created nor destroyed |
| Momentum | Navier–Stokes Equation (Equation of Motion) | Newton's 2nd Law for a fluid parcel |
| Energy | Energy Equation | Heat/work balance in flowing fluid |

The "Equation of Motion" in this module specifically refers to the **momentum conservation** form, i.e., the **Navier–Stokes equation**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Commuter Train"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine passengers (fluid molecules) boarding a moving train (the fluid parcel). Three things push or pull them sideways:
> 1. **Pressure differences** — someone pushing from the back of the carriage (high p) and someone pulling at the front (low p) → $-\nabla p$
> 2. **Friction between passengers** — the viscous "stickiness" between layers → $\mu \nabla^{2}\vec{V}$
> 3. **Gravity / external body forces** — the train going up a slope → $\rho \vec{g}$
> 4. **The train's own acceleration** — the inertial term → $\rho (\partial \vec{V}/\partial t + \vec{V}\cdot\nabla\vec{V})$
>
> The Navier–Stokes equation simply says: *the inertial push = pressure push + viscous pull + gravity pull*.

### 1.4 Why This Equation is Central to BioMEMS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In microfluidic devices (channels of 1 µm – 1 mm), the **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho V L / \mu$ is typically **< 1**, meaning **inertia is negligible** and viscous forces dominate. This simplification (the **Stokes / Creeping flow** regime) makes the Navier–Stokes equation solvable analytically for many BioMEMS geometries.

**Key Constants Used in Microfluidics:**
- Dynamic viscosity of water at 25 °C: $\mu \approx \mathbf{0.89 \times 10^{-3} \; Pa \cdot s}$
- Density of water: $\rho \approx \mathbf{1000 \; kg/m^{3}}$
- Kinematic viscosity: $\nu = \mu / \rho \approx \mathbf{10^{-6} \; m^{2}/s}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Equation of Motion

The Navier–Stokes equation is constructed by applying **Newton's second law** to a *material control volume* of fluid.

**Step 1 — Choose a control volume.**
A small fluid parcel of volume $d\forall = dx\,dy\,dz$ is followed along its trajectory (Lagrangian viewpoint).

**Step 2 — Sum all forces on the parcel.**
- **Surface forces** (pressure + viscous shear on the 6 faces) → $-\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V}$
- **Body forces** (gravity, electric field for EOF, magnetic field for MHD) → $\rho \vec{f}$

**Step 3 — Write the inertial (mass × acceleration) term.**
Acceleration of a fluid parcel has two parts:
- **Local acceleration** $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t$ (unsteadiness)
- **Convective acceleration** $\vec{V}\cdot\nabla\vec{V}$ (spatial change as the parcel moves)

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's 2nd Law: $\sum F = m \vec{a}$.**

$$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2}\vec{V} + \rho \vec{f}$$

where the **material derivative** is:
$$\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$$

**Step 5 — Specialize to the case of constant $\rho$ and $\mu$.**
Yields the standard incompressible form taught in KTU Module 3.

### 2.2 Force Decomposition in Component Form

For a Cartesian frame $(x,y,z)$ with gravity $-g\hat{k}$:

| Term | Symbol | Physical Interpretation | Microfluidic Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Local inertia | $\rho \dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial t}$ | Unsteadiness | Often negligible in steady lab-on-chip |
| Convective inertia | $\rho u_j \dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j}$ | Advection of momentum | Negligible for $Re \ll 1$ |
| Pressure force | $-\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x_i}$ | Pressure-driven driving | Primary driver in pressure-driven microflows |
| Viscous force | $\mu \dfrac{\partial^{2} u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}$ | Momentum diffusion | Dominant in microchannels |
| Body force | $\rho g_i$ | Gravity, electrostatics, magnetostatics | Important in EOF, MHD, sedimentation |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3**

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Material derivative | $\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$ | Acceleration of fluid parcel |
| 2 | Continuity (incompressible) | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | Mass conservation |
| 3 | Navier–Stokes (general) | $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^{2}\vec{V} + \rho\vec{g}$ | Equation of motion |
| 4 | Euler equation (inviscid) | $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho\vec{g}$ | Neglect viscosity |
| 5 | Stokes / Creeping flow | $0 = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^{2}\vec{V}$ | $Re \ll 1$ microfluidics |
| 6 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Classify flow regime |
| 7 | Pressure-driven Poiseuille (channel half-height $h$) | $u(y) = -\dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dp}{dx}\,(h^{2} - y^{2})$ | Microchannel velocity profile |
| 8 | Stokes drag on sphere | $\vec{F}_{D} = -6\pi\mu r \vec{V}$ | Cells, particles in bioMEMS |
| 9 | Hagen–Poiseuille volumetric flow | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4}}{8\mu}\dfrac{\Delta p}{L}$ | Circular microchannel |
| 10 | Cauchy momentum (general fluid) | $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \nabla\cdot\boldsymbol{\tau} + \rho\vec{g}$ | Any fluid, any constitutive law |

> [!NOTE]
> **Markdown Table Pipe Rule:** All absolute-value bars in formulas above are written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ using `\vert` to keep the markdown table syntax intact.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in BioMEMS

- **Lab-on-a-Chip design:** Solving Navier–Stokes predicts throughput, residence time, and mixing.
- **Drug delivery microneedles:** Stokes flow gives flow rate through microporous membranes.
- **Cell sorting (dielectrophoresis):** Body-force term includes electric field force.
- **Organ-on-chip:** Pulsatile flow mimics peristalsis — needs full unsteady N-S.
- **Micropumps:** Equation of motion with body forces models electroosmotic / magnetohydrodynamic pumps.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Continuity Equation (Mass Conservation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting premise:** Fluid mass inside a fixed Eulerian control volume changes only due to net mass flux across its faces.

Consider a rectangular control volume $dx\,dy\,dz$ at point $(x,y,z)$. Mass flow rate in the $x$-direction at face $x$ is $\rho u\,dy\,dz$, and at face $x+dx$ it is:

$$\left[\rho u + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x}dx\right]dy\,dz$$

**Net outflow in $x$:** subtract inflow:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x}dx\,dy\,dz$$

**Add all three directions** ($x, y, z$):

$$\left[\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z}\right]dx\,dy\,dz$$

**Rate of mass decrease inside CV:**

$$-\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} dx\,dy\,dz$$

**Equate (conservation of mass):**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0}$$

In divergence form:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

**For incompressible microfluidics** ($\rho$ = constant), it simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0}$$

### 3.2 Derivation II — Navier–Stokes Equation (x-Component)

We now apply $\sum F_x = m a_x$ to the same fluid parcel.

**Mass of parcel:** $dm = \rho\,dx\,dy\,dz$

**Acceleration in x:** $a_x = \dfrac{Du}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z}$

**Forces in x-direction:**

**(a) Pressure force on the two x-faces:**

- At face $x$: $+p\,dy\,dz$ (pushing in +x)
- At face $x+dx$: $-\left[p + \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}dx\right]dy\,dz$ (pushing in −x)

Net pressure force in x:

$$-\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,dx\,dy\,dz$$

**(b) Viscous (shear) forces** on the four y-faces and z-faces.

In Newtonian fluid, the viscous stress tensor gives normal and tangential components. Summing all six viscous contributions on the parcel:

$$\mu\left(\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial z^{2}}\right)dx\,dy\,dz = \mu \nabla^{2} u \; dx\,dy\,dz$$

**(c) Body force per unit volume** in x: $\rho g_x \, dx\,dy\,dz$.

**Apply Newton's 2nd Law:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho \frac{Du}{Dt}\,dx\,dy\,dz &= -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,dx\,dy\,dz + \mu \nabla^{2} u \, dx\,dy\,dz + \rho g_x\,dx\,dy\,dz
\end{aligned}
$$

**Divide through by $dx\,dy\,dz$:**

$$\boxed{\rho\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^{2} u + \rho g_x}$$

Similarly, the $y$ and $z$ components:

$$\rho\frac{Dv}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} + \mu \nabla^{2} v + \rho g_y$$

$$\rho\frac{Dw}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu \nabla^{2} w + \rho g_z$$

**Compact vector form:**

$$\boxed{\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation III — Stokes Flow (Low-Re Limit) for Microfluidics

For $Re \ll 1$, drop the inertial LHS:

$$0 = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V}$$

**Apply to planar Poiseuille flow** between two parallel plates at $y = \pm h$, driven by $\dfrac{dp}{dx}$ (constant), gravity neglected.

**Reduce to scalar ODE:** For $v = w = 0$, $u = u(y)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= -\frac{dp}{dx} + \mu \frac{d^{2} u}{dy^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Integrate once:**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\,y + C_1$$

**Integrate again:**

$$u(y) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\,y^{2} + C_1 y + C_2$$

**Apply no-slip boundary conditions** $u(\pm h) = 0$:

- $u(h) = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{h^{2}}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} + C_1 h + C_2 = 0$
- $u(-h) = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{h^{2}}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} - C_1 h + C_2 = 0$

Subtracting: $2 C_1 h = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$.

Adding: $\frac{h^{2}}{2\mu}\dfrac{dp}{dx} + 2C_2 = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = -\dfrac{h^{2}}{4\mu}\dfrac{dp}{dx}$.

**Parabolic velocity profile:**

$$\boxed{u(y) = -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\left(h^{2} - y^{2}\right)}$$

Maximum velocity at $y = 0$: $u_{max} = -\dfrac{h^{2}}{2\mu}\dfrac{dp}{dx}$.

Average velocity: $\bar{u} = \dfrac{2}{3}u_{max} = -\dfrac{h^{2}}{3\mu}\dfrac{dp}{dx}$.

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Bernoulli's Equation (Inviscid, Steady, Incompressible)

Starting from Euler's equation $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$:

For steady flow, $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$. Use identity $\vec{V}\cdot\nabla\vec{V} = \nabla(V^{2}/2) - \vec{V}\times(\nabla\times\vec{V})$. For irrotational flow, $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho\,\nabla\!\left(\frac{V^{2}}{2}\right) &= -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g} \\
\nabla\!\left(\frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{V^{2}}{2} + gz\right) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Integrate along a streamline:**

$$\boxed{\frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{V^{2}}{2} + gz = \text{constant}}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Regime | Dominant Forces | Reduced Form | Typical in BioMEMS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inviscid, steady, irrotational | Pressure + Inertia + Gravity | Bernoulli | Spray drug delivery, aerosol flow |
| Viscous, steady, $Re\gg1$ | Inertia + Pressure + Viscous | Full Navier–Stokes | Macroscale bioreactors |
| Viscous, steady, $Re\ll1$ | Pressure + Viscous | Stokes / Creeping | Microchannels, lab-on-chip |
| Electroosmotic | Pressure + Viscous + Electric body force | Helmholtz–Smoluchowski N-S | $\mu$TAS, capillary electrophoresis |
| Magnetohydrodynamic | Pressure + Viscous + Lorentz force | MHD Navier–Stokes | Micropumps, cell manipulation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Force & Acceleration Architecture of the Equation of Motion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Newton Second Law for Fluid Parcel] --> B[Mass times Acceleration = Sum of Forces]
    B --> C[Inertial Term rho DV/Dt]
    B --> D[Surface Forces]
    B --> E[Body Forces]

    C --> C1[Local Acceleration dV/dt]
    C --> C2[Convective Acceleration V dot grad V]

    D --> D1[Pressure Force minus grad p]
    D --> D2[Viscous Force mu Laplacian V]

    E --> E1[Gravity rho g]
    E --> E2[Electric Lorentz Force rho E]
    E --> E3[Magnetic Lorentz Force J cross B]

    C1 --> F[Equation of Motion Navier Stokes]
    C2 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F

    F --> G1[Inviscid Euler]
    F --> G2[Stokes Creeping Flow]
    F --> G3[Full Navier Stokes]
    F --> G4[Electroosmotic Flow]
    F --> G5[MHD Flow]

    G1 --> H1[Aerodynamic Drug Delivery]
    G2 --> H2[Lab on Chip Microchannels]
    G3 --> H3[Bioreactor Mixing]
    G4 --> H4[Capillary Electrophoresis]
    G5 --> H5[MHD Micropumps]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Flow Regime Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Compute Reynolds Number Re] --> Q{Re less than 1}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Stokes Flow Regimes]
    Q -- No --> S{Re greater than 2300}
    S -- Yes --> T[Turbulent Full Navier Stokes]
    S -- No --> U[Laminar Full Navier Stokes]

    R --> R1[Pressure Driven Parabolic Profile]
    R --> R2[Electroosmotic Plug Profile]
    R --> R3[Stokes Drag on Cells]

    U --> U1[Entry Length Effects]
    U --> U2[Pulsatile Arterial Flow]
    T --> T1[Mixing in Micro Reactors]

    R1 --> X1[Microchannel Velocity]
    R2 --> X2[Capillary Chip Devices]
    R3 --> X3[Cell Sorting bioMEMS]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Solving the Equation of Motion in BioMEMS Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Define Geometry and Boundary Conditions] --> S2[Choose Constitutive Model Newtonian]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Reynolds Number]
    S3 --> S4{Select Equation Form}
    S4 -- Re less than 1 --> S5[Stokes Equation]
    S4 -- Re greater than 1 --> S6[Full Navier Stokes]

    S5 --> S7[Analytical Parabolic Profile]
    S5 --> S8[Numerical FEM / FVM Solver]
    S6 --> S8

    S7 --> S9[Predict Flow Rate Q]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Validate against Experiments]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
*State the general form of the Navier–Stokes equation for an incompressible Newtonian fluid and identify each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The equation of motion (Navier–Stokes) for an incompressible Newtonian fluid with constant viscosity is:

$$\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V}\cdot\nabla\vec{V}\right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2}\vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

- LHS — inertial term (mass × acceleration; local + convective).
- $-\nabla p$ — pressure force per unit volume.
- $\mu \nabla^{2}\vec{V}$ — viscous force per unit volume.
- $\rho \vec{g}$ — body force per unit volume (gravity).

(1 mark equation, 1 mark for identifying terms, 1 mark for LHS/RHS classification)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*What is the Reynolds number? Why is $Re \ll 1$ typical in BioMEMS microchannels?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Reynolds number is the dimensionless ratio of inertial to viscous forces:
$$Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$$
In BioMEMS, characteristic length $L$ is very small (µm), so $Re \ll 1$ even at moderate velocities. This makes viscous forces dominant and the flow is described by **Stokes (creeping-flow) equation**, allowing simple analytical velocity profiles. (1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark microfluidic significance)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls):**
> 1. Students often **forget the material derivative** and write only $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t$ — this is the most common 2-mark loss.
> 2. In the creeping-flow derivation, students **omit the no-slip boundary condition** $u(\pm h)=0$ and lose 3 marks.
> 3. Confusion between **Reynolds number and Péclet number** is penalized.
> 4. Forgetting to **non-dimensionalize** in microfluidics problems loses clarity marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, ESE]**

**(a)** Derive the **continuity equation** for a fluid in Cartesian coordinates starting from the principle of conservation of mass. State the simplified form for an incompressible fluid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the **Cauchy momentum equation**, derive the **Navier–Stokes equation** for an incompressible Newtonian fluid. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Continuity Equation — 7 marks**

Consider a control volume $dx\,dy\,dz$ at point $(x,y,z)$.

Mass flow entering face at $x$ in time $dt$:

$$\dot{m}_{x,\text{in}} = \rho u\,dy\,dz$$

Mass flow leaving face at $x+dx$ in time $dt$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{x,\text{out}} &= \left[\rho u + \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x}dx\right]dy\,dz
\end{aligned}
$$

Net outflow in $x$ per unit time:

$$\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x}dx\,dy\,dz$$

Adding the $y$ and $z$ directions and the rate of mass decrease inside:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}dx\,dy\,dz &= \left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\right]dx\,dy\,dz
\end{aligned}
$$

Final form:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho \vec{V}) = 0}$$

For incompressible flow ($\rho$ = const):

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0}$$

(Mark split: 2 marks CV identification, 2 marks net flux, 2 marks final form, 1 mark incompressible simplification.)

**(b) Navier–Stokes — 7 marks**

The general Cauchy equation is:

$$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \nabla\cdot\boldsymbol{\tau} + \rho \vec{g}$$

where $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ is the stress tensor. **Assumption 1: Newtonian fluid.**

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = -p\mathbf{I} + \mu\left[\nabla\vec{V} + (\nabla\vec{V})^{T}\right]$$

**Assumption 2: Incompressible flow** ($\nabla\cdot\vec{V}=0$).

Substituting and simplifying the divergence of the stress tensor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla\cdot\boldsymbol{\tau} &= -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^{2}\vec{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the equation of motion becomes:

$$\boxed{\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2}\vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}}$$

Expanded in $x$-component:

$$\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu\!\left(\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial z^{2}}\right) + \rho g_x$$

(Mark split: 2 marks Cauchy start, 2 marks Newtonian constitutive law, 1 mark incompressibility, 2 marks final form and component expansion.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, ESE]**

**(a)** For a **Newtonian fluid** flowing under a constant pressure gradient $dp/dx$ between two parallel plates separated by $2h$, derive the velocity profile using the **Stokes (creeping flow) equation**. State the maximum and average velocities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microchannel of length $L = 5\;\text{cm}$ and circular cross-section of radius $R = 50\;\mu\text{m}$ carries water. The applied pressure drop is $\Delta p = 10\;\text{kPa}$. Compute the **volumetric flow rate** $Q$ and verify the assumption of creeping flow. Take $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{Pa·s}$, $\rho = 1000\;\text{kg/m}^{3}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Planar Poiseuille Flow — 7 marks**

For $Re \ll 1$ the equation of motion reduces to:

$$0 = -\frac{dp}{dx} + \mu \frac{d^{2} u}{dy^{2}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^{2} u}{dy^{2}} &= \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} \\
\frac{du}{dy} &= \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\,y + C_1 \\
u(y) &= \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\,y^{2} + C_1 y + C_2
\end{aligned}
$$

No-slip BCs: $u(\pm h) = 0$. By symmetry, $C_1 = 0$; adding the two BCs:

$$C_2 = -\frac{h^{2}}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}$$

$$
\boxed{u(y) = -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\left(h^{2} - y^{2}\right)}
$$

Maximum at $y=0$: $u_{max} = -\dfrac{h^{2}}{2\mu}\dfrac{dp}{dx}$.

Average:

$$
\bar{u} = \frac{1}{2h}\int_{-h}^{h} u(y)\,dy = \frac{1}{2h}\cdot\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\cdot\frac{4h^{3}}{3} = -\frac{h^{2}}{3\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}
$$

(2 marks: reduce to ODE; 2 marks: integrate; 2 marks: BC and profile; 1 mark: average velocity.)

**(b) Circular Microchannel — 7 marks**

Hagen–Poiseuille equation:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^{4}}{8\mu}\frac{\Delta p}{L}$$

Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\pi (50\times10^{-6})^{4}}{8(1.0\times10^{-3})}\cdot\frac{10\times10^{3}}{5\times10^{-2}} \\
  &= \frac{\pi \cdot 6.25\times10^{-18}}{8\times10^{-3}}\cdot 2\times10^{5} \\
  &= \pi\cdot 7.8125\times10^{-16}\cdot 2\times10^{5} \\
  &= \pi \cdot 1.5625\times10^{-10} \\
  &= 4.91\times10^{-10}\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \\
  &\approx 0.491\;\mu\text{L/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Average velocity:

$$
\bar{V} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} = \frac{4.91\times10^{-10}}{\pi \cdot 2.5\times10^{-9}} \approx 0.0625\;\text{m/s}
$$

Reynolds number:

$$
Re = \frac{\rho \bar{V} D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 0.0625 \times 100\times10^{-6}}{10^{-3}} = 6.25
$$

Wait — this is **not** creeping flow. Re-examine: actually $Re = 6.25 \ll 100$, so we are still well within the laminar / low-inertia regime, but creeping-flow assumption is borderline. For strict $Re<1$, either reduce $\Delta p$ or reduce channel radius.

(Mark split: 1 mark Hagen–Poiseuille, 2 marks substitution & Q, 2 marks average velocity, 2 marks Re and comment.)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Box — KTU Module 3: Equation of Motion**

- **Equation of Motion = Navier–Stokes = momentum conservation** for a fluid parcel.
- Material derivative: $\dfrac{D}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} + \vec{V}\cdot\nabla$ — **always include both terms**.
- Continuity (incompressible): $\nabla\cdot\vec{V}=0$ — companion equation.
- General NS (incompressible, Newtonian): $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2}\vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$.
- **Inviscid flow** → Euler equation (drop $\mu\nabla^{2}\vec{V}$).
- **Creeping flow** ($Re \ll 1$) → Stokes equation (drop LHS).
- $Re = \rho V L / \mu$; microfluidics has $Re \ll 1$ because $L$ is small.
- Bernoulli = integrated Euler along a streamline for steady, irrotational, incompressible flow.
- **No-slip BC** at walls is critical in Poiseuille derivations.
- **Poiseuille planar profile:** $u(y) = -\dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dp}{dx}(h^{2}-y^{2})$.
- **Hagen–Poiseuille circular:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4}}{8\mu}\dfrac{\Delta p}{L} \propto R^{4}$ — strong radius dependence.
- **Stokes drag:** $F_{D} = 6\pi\mu r V$ — used for cells, beads, microparticles.
- Body-force term can include **electric** (EOF), **magnetic** (MHD), or **gravitational** (sedimentation) fields.
- **Exam trap:** Don't confuse $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t$ (local) with $D\vec{V}/Dt$ (material/total).
- **Exam trap:** Always state **assumptions** (Newtonian, incompressible, steady, laminar) before solving.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laminar Flow in Circular Conduits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laminar Flow in Circular Conduits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Laminar Flow in a Circular Conduit** is defined as a steady, incompressible, axisymmetric, fully-developed, viscous flow of a Newtonian fluid through a cylindrical tube, in which fluid particles move in parallel, well-ordered streamlines without macroscopic mixing between adjacent fluid layers. The flow is governed by the **Navier–Stokes equation** in cylindrical coordinates and, under the steady-state assumption, the velocity distribution across the cross-section assumes a characteristic **parabolic (Poiseuille) profile**.

In the context of **BioMEMS and Microfluidics**, the vast majority of bio-fluidic transport phenomena (e.g., blood plasma flow in capillaries, drug delivery through microneedles, lab-on-a-chip reagent transport) occur under laminar conditions because the characteristic length scale is small enough that the **Reynolds number** is typically well below **2000**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Microfluidic flow is treated almost exclusively under the laminar regime. The **Hagen–Poiseuille Law** is the cornerstone equation of the module and is heavily tested in both Part A and Part B questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine gently pushing water through a long, thin transparent straw (the circular conduit). If you push slowly and steadily:
- The water near the **center** of the straw moves the **fastest**.
- The water touching the **inner wall** of the straw is **stationary** (it "sticks" to the wall — the famous **no-slip boundary condition**).
- The water in between moves at intermediate speeds.

If you plot these speeds from the wall to the center, you get a **parabola** — high in the middle, tapering to zero at the walls. This parabola is the **velocity profile** of Hagen–Poiseuille flow.

> **Real-world analogy:** Picture cars on a multi-lane highway where the outer lane is jammed (the wall, friction), the inner lane flows freely (the centerline, low resistance), and the middle lanes have intermediate speeds. The traffic pattern is parabolic in cross-section.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C: **μ ≈ 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
- Density of water: **ρ ≈ 1000 kg/m³**
- Critical Reynolds number for pipe flow: **Re_crit ≈ 2300** (laminar–turbulent transition)
- Typical microfluidic Reynolds number: **Re < 100**, often **Re < 1**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Reynolds Number:**
> The dimensionless ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow, given by $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$, where $V$ is the average velocity, $D$ is the tube diameter, and $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity. When $\text{Re} \ll 2300$, viscous forces dominate and the flow is **laminar**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Assumptions of Hagen–Poiseuille Flow

For a textbook derivation, the following simplifications are imposed on the full Navier–Stokes equation:

1. **Steady-state** flow: $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} = 0$ (no time dependence).
2. **Incompressible** fluid: $\rho = \text{constant}$.
3. **Newtonian** fluid: shear stress is linearly proportional to shear rate, $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$.
4. **Fully-developed** flow: velocity profile does not change in the axial (z) direction, $\dfrac{\partial V_z}{\partial z} = 0$.
5. **Axisymmetric** flow: no variation in the azimuthal (θ) direction, $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta} = 0$.
6. **Unidirectional** (no swirl): radial and azimuthal velocity components are zero, $V_r = V_\theta = 0$, so $V = V_z(r)$.
7. **Gravity neglected** for horizontal conduits (or incorporated as a body force if vertical).
8. **Rigid, impermeable, cylindrical wall** with the **no-slip condition**: $V_z(R) = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these assumptions matter for BioMEMS:**
> Microchannels fabricated in PDMS, glass, or silicon are typically straight, smooth-walled, and circular (or rectangular). The dominant force is viscous, not inertial. Therefore, the Hagen–Poiseuille result is directly applicable.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Assumption

- **Why incompressible?** Liquids (water, blood plasma, buffer solutions) have negligible density variation under typical microfluidic pressures.
- **Why fully-developed?** In long microchannels ($L \gg D$), the entry length is a tiny fraction of the total length, so the parabolic profile is established almost immediately.
- **Why unidirectional?** The pressure gradient along the tube axis is the only driving force; centrifugal and Coriolis effects are negligible at low Re.
- **Why no-slip?** Valid at solid–liquid interfaces for Newtonian fluids in continuum mechanics; this is the **KTU Board's standard assumption**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description / Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V_{avg} D}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless; $V_{avg}$ in m/s, $D$ in m |
| 2 | Axial Pressure Gradient | $-\dfrac{dP}{dz} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ | Pa/m (driving force per unit length) |
| 3 | Parabolic Velocity Profile | $V_z(r) = \dfrac{1}{4\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$ | m/s; valid for $0 \leq r \leq R$ |
| 4 | Maximum (Centerline) Velocity | $V_{max} = \dfrac{R^2}{4\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)$ | m/s, occurs at $r = 0$ |
| 5 | Average Velocity | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{R^2}{8\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right) = \dfrac{V_{max}}{2}$ | m/s |
| 6 | Volumetric Flow Rate (Hagen–Poiseuille) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{8\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right) = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}$ | m³/s |
| 7 | Hydraulic Diameter (for circular tube) | $D_h = D = 2R$ | m |
| 8 | Wall Shear Stress | $\tau_w = \dfrac{R}{2}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right) = \dfrac{4\mu V_{avg}}{R}$ | Pa |
| 9 | Shear Stress Distribution | $\tau(r) = \dfrac{r}{2}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)$ | Pa; varies linearly with $r$ |
| 10 | Pressure Drop | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8\mu L Q}{\pi R^4}$ | Pa |
| 11 | Darcy Friction Factor | $f_D = \dfrac{64}{\text{Re}}$ | Dimensionless, for laminar pipe flow |
| 12 | Fanning Friction Factor | $f_F = \dfrac{16}{\text{Re}} = \dfrac{f_D}{4}$ | Dimensionless |
| 13 | Hydrodynamic Entry Length | $L_e = 0.06 \cdot \text{Re} \cdot D$ | m; for laminar flow |
| 14 | Kinetic Energy Correction Factor | $\alpha = 2$ | Dimensionless, for parabolic profile |
| 15 | Momentum Correction Factor | $\beta = \dfrac{4}{3}$ | Dimensionless, for parabolic profile |
| 16 | Hydraulic Resistance (Microfluidics) | $R_h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{Q} = \dfrac{8\mu L}{\pi R^4}$ | Pa·s/m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility in BioMEMS:**
> - **Drug delivery devices:** Use the Hagen–Poiseuille pressure drop to size micro-needle bore diameters.
> - **Lab-on-Chip systems:** Treat long serpentine channels as a network of Hagen–Poiseuille resistors in series/parallel.
> - **Biomimetic flow studies:** The wall shear stress $\tau_w$ determines platelet activation, endothelial cell alignment, and hemolysis thresholds in blood-contacting microdevices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Parabolic Velocity Profile (Hagen–Poiseuille Flow)

**Step 1 — Start from the Navier–Stokes Equation in Cylindrical Coordinates.**

The full z-component of the Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho\left(\dfrac{\partial V_z}{\partial t} + V_r\dfrac{\partial V_z}{\partial r} + \dfrac{V_\theta}{r}\dfrac{\partial V_z}{\partial \theta} + V_z\dfrac{\partial V_z}{\partial z}\right) = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu\left[\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\dfrac{\partial V_z}{\partial r}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 V_z}{\partial \theta^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 V_z}{\partial z^2}\right] + \rho g_z
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the assumptions systematically.**

Using the eight assumptions listed in Section 2.1:
- Steady: $\dfrac{\partial V_z}{\partial t} = 0$
- Unidirectional: $V_r = V_\theta = 0$
- Axisymmetric: $\dfrac{\partial V_z}{\partial \theta} = 0$
- Fully developed: $\dfrac{\partial V_z}{\partial z} = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\partial^2 V_z}{\partial z^2} = 0$
- Horizontal tube: $g_z = 0$

The equation collapses dramatically to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 = -\dfrac{dP}{dz} + \mu\left[\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\left(r\dfrac{dV_z}{dr}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the Laplacian term.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\left(r\dfrac{dV_z}{dr}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $r$ and integrate once with respect to $r$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d}{dr}\left(r\dfrac{dV_z}{dr}\right) = \dfrac{r}{\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrate with respect to $r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r\dfrac{dV_z}{dr} = \dfrac{r^2}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz} + C_1
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C_1$ is a constant of integration.

**Step 5 — Divide by $r$ and integrate a second time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dV_z}{dr} = \dfrac{r}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz} + \dfrac{C_1}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrate again with respect to $r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_z(r) = \dfrac{r^2}{4\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz} + C_1 \ln(r) + C_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply boundary conditions to evaluate $C_1$ and $C_2$.**

**Boundary Condition 1 — Finite velocity at the centerline ($r = 0$):**

The velocity $V_z$ must be finite at $r = 0$. The $\ln(r)$ term would diverge to $-\infty$ as $r \to 0$, which is physically unacceptable. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Boundary Condition 2 — No-slip at the wall ($r = R$, $V_z = 0$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 = \dfrac{R^2}{4\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz} + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = -\dfrac{R^2}{4\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Write the final velocity profile.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_z(r) = \dfrac{1}{4\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}\left(r^2 - R^2\right) = \dfrac{1}{4\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)\left(R^2 - r^2\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Note that for flow in the $+z$ direction, the pressure **decreases** along $z$, so $\dfrac{dP}{dz} < 0$ and $\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right) > 0$, ensuring $V_z > 0$.

> **Result:** A **parabolic velocity profile** with maximum velocity at the centerline ($r = 0$) and zero velocity at the wall ($r = R$). This is the **Hagen–Poiseuille profile**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Volumetric Flow Rate (Hagen–Poiseuille Equation)

**Step 1 — Set up the area integral.**

The volumetric flow rate through a circular cross-section is obtained by integrating the axial velocity over the area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q = \int_{A} V_z \, dA = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{0}^{R} V_z(r) \, r \, dr \, d\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the velocity profile.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{0}^{R} \dfrac{1}{4\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)\left(R^2 - r^2\right) r \, dr \, d\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Pull constants outside the integral.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q = \dfrac{1}{4\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right) \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta \int_{0}^{R} \left(R^2 - r^2\right) r \, dr
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the $\theta$ integral.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta = 2\pi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the $r$ integral by substitution $u = R^2 - r^2$, $du = -2r\,dr$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{R} (R^2 - r^2) \, r \, dr = \dfrac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{R^2} u \, du = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{R^4}{2} = \dfrac{R^4}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Combine results.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q = \dfrac{1}{4\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right) \cdot 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{R^4}{4} = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{8\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Express in terms of total pressure drop $\Delta P$ over length $L$.**

Since $\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \, \Delta P}{8 \mu L}\,} \quad \text{(Hagen–Poiseuille Equation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strong dependence on $R$:** Flow rate scales as $R^4$. Doubling the radius increases $Q$ by a factor of **16**! This is the most heavily tested takeaway for KTU Board problems on this topic.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Wall Shear Stress

The shear stress in a Newtonian fluid is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{rz} = \mu \dfrac{dV_z}{dr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate the velocity profile.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dV_z}{dr} = \dfrac{1}{4\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right) \cdot 2r = \dfrac{r}{2\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by $\mu$ to get the shear stress distribution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau(r) = \mu \dfrac{dV_z}{dr} = \dfrac{r}{2}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at the wall $r = R$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_w = \dfrac{R}{2}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right) = \dfrac{4\mu V_{avg}}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Wall shear stress governs red blood cell damage, protein adsorption, and thrombosis in blood-handling microfluidics. KTU examiners often ask for this value in Part B numericals.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Darcy Friction Factor

The Darcy friction factor relates the wall shear stress to the dynamic pressure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_D = \dfrac{8 \tau_w}{\rho V_{avg}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $\tau_w = \dfrac{4\mu V_{avg}}{R}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_D = \dfrac{8 \cdot \dfrac{4\mu V_{avg}}{R}}{\rho V_{avg}^2} = \dfrac{32 \mu}{\rho V_{avg} R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of Reynolds number using $D = 2R$, so $R = D/2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_D = \dfrac{32 \mu}{\rho V_{avg} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}} = \dfrac{64 \mu}{\rho V_{avg} D} = \dfrac{64}{\text{Re}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $f_D = \dfrac{64}{\text{Re}}$ is the **famous laminar pipe-flow result** and is often asked as a "show that" in KTU 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hagen–Poiseuille Flow

This diagram maps the physical quantities and their mathematical relationships in a laminar circular conduit flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driving Force: Pressure Drop Delta P over Length L] --> B[Axial Pressure Gradient minus dP/dz]
    B --> C[Navier-Stokes z equation reduces to ODE]
    C --> D{Apply boundary conditions}
    D --> E[No slip at wall Vz R equals 0]
    D --> F[Finite velocity at center C1 equals 0]
    E --> G[Parabolic Velocity Profile Vz of r]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Volumetric Flow Rate Q equals pi R4 over 8 mu times minus dP/dz]
    G --> I[Wall Shear Stress tau w equals R over 2 times minus dP/dz]
    H --> J[Average Velocity Vavg equals Q over pi R2 equals Vmax over 2]
    H --> K[Hydraulic Resistance Rh equals Delta P over Q]
    I --> L[Darcy Friction Factor fD equals 64 over Re]
    J --> M[Reynolds Number Re equals rho Vavg D over mu]
    K --> N[Microfluidic Network Analysis]
    L --> N
    M --> L
    M --> O{Laminar or Turbulent}
    O -->|Re below 2300| P[Parabolic Profile Valid]
    O -->|Re above 2300| Q[Transition to Turbulent]
```
