## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**State the assumptions made in deriving the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for laminar flow in a circular pipe.**

**Model Answer:**

The following assumptions are made in the derivation of the Hagen–Poiseuille equation:

1. **Steady-state flow:** The flow parameters do not change with time.
2. **Incompressible fluid:** The fluid density is constant (typical for liquids).
3. **Newtonian fluid:** The shear stress is linearly proportional to the rate of shear strain.
4. **Fully-developed flow:** The velocity profile is invariant in the axial direction.
5. **Axisymmetric flow:** No variation in the azimuthal (circumferential) direction.
6. **Unidirectional flow:** Only the axial velocity component is non-zero.
7. **No-slip boundary condition:** Fluid velocity is zero at the tube wall.
8. **Rigid, impermeable wall:** The wall does not deform or absorb fluid.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget the **no-slip condition** and the **fully-developed** assumption. These two are mandatory in the list — losing either costs 0.5 marks each.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Define the Reynolds number. What is its critical value for flow in a circular pipe, and why is it particularly important in microfluidic systems?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Reynolds number is the dimensionless ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow, given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V_{avg} D}{\mu}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\rho$ is fluid density (kg/m³), $V_{avg}$ is the average velocity (m/s), $D$ is the tube diameter (m), and $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity (Pa·s).

**Critical value for circular pipes:** $\text{Re}_{crit} \approx 2300$.

- For $\text{Re} < 2300$: **Laminar flow** (parabolic profile, well-ordered streamlines).
- For $\text{Re} > 4000$: **Turbulent flow** (chaotic, mixing-dominated).
- For $2300 < \text{Re} < 4000$: **Transitional regime**.

**Importance in Microfluidics:** In bioMEMS devices, characteristic dimensions are typically in the range of 10–500 μm. Even at flow velocities of several mm/s, the Reynolds number remains well below 100, often below 1. This means **viscous forces overwhelmingly dominate** inertial forces, ensuring laminar flow, predictable Hagen–Poiseuille behavior, and reliable microfluidic circuit analysis using hydraulic resistance analogies.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "Reynolds number is the ratio of viscous to inertial forces" — this is **backwards**. The correct order is **inertial / viscous**. Deduct 1 mark for this inversion.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates, derive the **velocity profile** for steady, fully-developed, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid in a horizontal circular pipe. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A microfluidic biochip has 50 parallel circular microchannels, each of diameter $D = 200\,\mu m$ and length $L = 5\,cm$. The chip delivers a buffer solution (viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,Pa\cdot s$) at a total volumetric flow rate of $Q_{total} = 10\,\mu L/min$. Calculate:
   (i) The average velocity in each channel.
   (ii) The pressure drop across the chip.
   (iii) The wall shear stress in each channel.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Refer to the **complete derivation in Section 3.1** above. The final result is the **parabolic velocity profile**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_z(r) = \dfrac{1}{4\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)\left(R^2 - r^2\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

All 8 assumptions (steady, incompressible, Newtonian, fully-developed, axisymmetric, unidirectional, horizontal, no-slip) must be stated clearly. The integration constants $C_1 = 0$ (from finite velocity at centerline) and $C_2 = -R^2/(4\mu)(dP/dz)$ (from no-slip) must be evaluated explicitly.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given data:**
- $N = 50$ channels (in parallel)
- $D = 200\,\mu m = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,m$, so $R = 1 \times 10^{-4}\,m$
- $L = 5\,cm = 0.05\,m$
- $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,Pa\cdot s$
- $Q_{total} = 10\,\mu L/min = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-9}}{60}\,m^3/s = 1.667 \times 10^{-10}\,m^3/s$

**Step 1 — Flow rate per channel:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{N} = \dfrac{1.667 \times 10^{-10}}{50} = 3.333 \times 10^{-12}\,m^3/s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area per channel:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times (1 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 3.142 \times 10^{-8}\,m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (i) — Average velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{avg} = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{3.333 \times 10^{-12}}{3.142 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.061 \times 10^{-4}\,m/s \approx 106\,\mu m/s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (ii) — Pressure drop using Hagen–Poiseuille:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^4} = \dfrac{8 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.05 \times 3.333 \times 10^{-12}}{\pi \times (1 \times 10^{-4})^4}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P = \dfrac{1.333 \times 10^{-15}}{3.142 \times 10^{-16}} \approx 4.24\,Pa
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (iii) — Wall shear stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_w = \dfrac{4\mu V_{avg}}{R} = \dfrac{4 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.061 \times 10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 4.24 \times 10^{-3}\,Pa = 4.24\,mPa
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answers:**
- (i) $V_{avg} \approx 106\,\mu m/s$
- (ii) $\Delta P \approx 4.24\,Pa$
- (iii) $\tau_w \approx 4.24\,mPa$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> - **Unit conversion errors:** $10\,\mu L/min$ MUST be converted to $m^3/s$ before substituting. The most common mistake is leaving flow rate in $\mu L/min$ and obtaining a numerically wrong (but unit-inconsistent) answer. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Forgetting parallel distribution:** $Q$ per channel is $Q_{total}/N$, not $Q_{total}$ itself. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Mixing up $R$ and $D$:** $R = D/2$. The Hagen–Poiseuille equation uses $R^4$, so this is a factor-of-16 error. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** for volumetric flow rate of a Newtonian fluid through a circular pipe. Discuss the variation of wall shear stress with radial position.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Newtonian fluid ($\rho = 1000\,kg/m^3$, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,Pa\cdot s$) flows through a circular tube of diameter $2\,mm$ with a Reynolds number of $1000$. Calculate:
   (i) The Darcy friction factor.
   (ii) The hydrodynamic entry length.
   (iii) The pressure drop per unit length of the tube, if the wall shear stress is $0.5\,Pa$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Refer to the **complete derivation in Section 3.2** above. The result is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{8\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Variation of wall shear stress with radial position:**

From Section 3.3, the shear stress distribution across the pipe is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau(r) = \dfrac{r}{2}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **linear function of $r$**:
- At the **centerline** ($r = 0$): $\tau = 0$
- At the **wall** ($r = R$): $\tau = \tau_w = \dfrac{R}{2}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)$ (maximum)

The shear stress varies linearly from zero at the center to a maximum at the wall, which is consistent with the physical picture that adjacent fluid layers in the center move together at nearly the same velocity (low relative motion), while at the wall the velocity gradient is steepest (highest relative motion).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given data:**
- $\rho = 1000\,kg/m^3$
- $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,Pa\cdot s$
- $D = 2\,mm = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,m$, so $R = 1 \times 10^{-3}\,m$
- $\text{Re} = 1000$
- $\tau_w = 0.5\,Pa$

**Step 1 (i) — Darcy friction factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_D = \dfrac{64}{\text{Re}} = \dfrac{64}{1000} = 0.064
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (ii) — Hydrodynamic entry length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_e = 0.06 \cdot \text{Re} \cdot D = 0.06 \times 1000 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.12\,m = 12\,cm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (iii) — Pressure gradient from wall shear stress:**

From $\tau_w = \dfrac{R}{2}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\dfrac{dP}{dz} = \dfrac{2 \tau_w}{R} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.5}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 1000\,Pa/m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answers:**
- (i) $f_D = 0.064$
- (ii) $L_e = 12\,cm$
- (iii) $-\dfrac{dP}{dz} = 1000\,Pa/m$ (i.e., $\Delta P$ per meter = **1 kPa/m**)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> - **Friction factor formula mix-up:** Some students confuse the **Darcy** ($f_D = 64/\text{Re}$) and **Fanning** ($f_F = 16/\text{Re}$) friction factors, leading to a 4× error. The KTU 2024 scheme expects the **Darcy** form unless otherwise specified. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Entry length formula:** Use $L_e = 0.06 \cdot \text{Re} \cdot D$, NOT $L_e = 0.05 \cdot \text{Re} \cdot D$ (which is the thermal entry length, not the hydrodynamic one). **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Inverting the relation:** Some students write $-\dfrac{dP}{dz} = \dfrac{R}{2\tau_w}$ (inverted). Check the algebraic manipulation carefully. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** Hagen–Poiseuille flow = steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of a Newtonian fluid in a circular conduit.
- [x] **Velocity profile:** $V_z(r) = \dfrac{1}{4\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$ — **parabolic**.
- [x] **Maximum velocity:** $V_{max} = \dfrac{R^2}{4\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)$ at $r = 0$.
- [x] **Average velocity:** $V_{avg} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{2} = \dfrac{R^2}{8\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)$.
- [x] **Hagen–Poiseuille equation:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}$ — flow rate $\propto R^4$.
- [x] **Wall shear stress:** $\tau_w = \dfrac{4\mu V_{avg}}{R} = \dfrac{R}{2}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)$.
- [x] **Shear stress distribution:** $\tau(r) = \dfrac{r}{2}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)$ — **linear** with $r$.
- [x] **Darcy friction factor:** $f_D = \dfrac{64}{\text{Re}}$ — laminar flow in a circular pipe.
- [x] **Fanning friction factor:** $f_F = \dfrac{16}{\text{Re}} = f_D/4$.
- [x] **Entry length:** $L_e = 0.06 \cdot \text{Re} \cdot D$ (hydrodynamic, laminar).
- [x] **Hydraulic resistance:** $R_h = \dfrac{8\mu L}{\pi R^4}$ (used in microfluidic circuit analogy).
- [x] **Critical Reynolds number:** $\text{Re}_{crit} \approx 2300$ (laminar–turbulent transition).
- [x] **Microfluidic Re:** Typically $\ll 100$, often $\ll 1$ — laminar flow is **guaranteed**.
- [x] **Key assumptions:** Steady + Incompressible + Newtonian + Fully-developed + Axisymmetric + Unidirectional + No-slip.
- [x] **Kinetic energy correction factor:** $\alpha = 2$ (parabolic profile).
- [x] **Momentum correction factor:** $\beta = \dfrac{4}{3}$ (parabolic profile).
- [x] **No-slip BC:** $V_z(R) = 0$ — mandatory boundary condition in every KTU derivation.
- [x] **Centerline finiteness BC:** $V_z(0) = \text{finite}$ — eliminates the $\ln(r)$ singularity.
- [x] **Biological relevance:** $\tau_w$ governs hemolysis, platelet activation, endothelial health in blood-microfluidics.
- [x] **Engineering utility:** Hagen–Poiseuille is the building block of microfluidic resistor networks for lab-on-chip design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top Three Things That Will Cost You Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** (especially $\mu m$ to $m$ and $\mu L/min$ to $m^3/s$).
> 2. **Mixing up $R$ and $D$** in the $R^4$ dependence (factor-of-16 error).
> 3. **Inverting the Reynolds number definition** (inertial / viscous, not the other way around).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Flow in Micro Conduits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fluid Flow in Micro Conduits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fluid flow in micro conduits** refers to the study of fluid dynamics phenomena occurring when liquids or gases are transported through channels whose characteristic dimensions (typically diameter or hydraulic diameter $D_h$) are on the order of **1 μm to 1000 μm**. At these length scales, the conventional macroscale Navier–Stokes equations must be critically re-examined because dimensionless numbers such as the **Knudsen number ($Kn$)** and the **Reynolds number ($Re$)** take on values that invalidate several assumptions valid at the macroscale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3 Highlight):**
> Fluid flow in micro conduits is governed by the interplay of viscous forces, surface tension, electrokinetic phenomena, and the breakdown of the **continuum hypothesis**. The flow regime is classified into continuum ($Kn < 0.001$), slip flow ($0.001 < Kn < 0.1$), transition ($0.1 < Kn < 10$), and free molecular ($Kn > 10$).

The hydraulic diameter used as the characteristic length is defined as:

$$D_h = \frac{4 \times \text{Cross-sectional Area}}{\text{Wetted Perimeter}}$$

For a rectangular microchannel of width $w$ and height $h$:

$$D_h = \frac{2wh}{w + h}$$

For a circular microchannel of diameter $D$:

$$D_h = D$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to drink a thick milkshake (high viscosity fluid) through a normal straw versus through a thin coffee stirrer. The thinner straw requires much more sucking effort, even though the milkshake is the same. Now imagine scaling the straw down to the width of a human hair (about 100 μm). At this scale, several new effects dominate that you never noticed before:

- **The walls "feel" different:** The fluid no longer behaves as a smooth, continuous sheet; instead, the molecules bouncing off the wall directly influence the bulk flow (this is the **Knudsen effect**).
- **Surface forces dominate volume forces:** Gravity, which normally drives rivers, becomes negligible. Instead, the **stickiness of the fluid to the wall (surface tension and capillarity)** takes over.
- **Electric charges on the wall start to "push" the fluid:** The glass wall of a microchannel acquires a charge, and when you apply an electric field, the fluid moves on its own — this is **electroosmotic flow**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Ant Swimming in Honey":**
> Think of an ant (the fluid molecule) trying to walk through a thin film of honey inside a tiny tube. Compared to the ant's size, the tube walls feel like giant slippery cliffs. The honey's resistance (viscosity) is enormous compared to the inertia of the ant. The ant can also "feel" the texture of the wall directly. This is exactly the regime of microconduit flow — viscous forces dominate, inertia is negligible, and wall-molecular interactions matter.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard constants and parameters are essential for fluid flow analysis in microconduits (SI units):

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Dynamic viscosity of water at 25°C | $\mu$ | **$0.001$ Pa·s (or $1$ cP)** |
| Density of water at 25°C | $\rho$ | **$997$ kg/m³** |
| Surface tension of water at 25°C | $\gamma$ | **$0.072$ N/m** |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | **$8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m** |
| Zeta potential of glass-water interface | $\zeta$ | **$-25$ mV to $-100$ mV** |
| Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** |
| Elementary charge | $e$ | **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C** |
| Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | **$6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why these values matter in KTU exams:** Most numerical problems in this module use water as the working fluid with $T = 25°C$. Memorizing the four primary constants (viscosity, density, surface tension, and permittivity) at room temperature will allow you to solve over 80% of numerical questions without re-deriving.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Continuum Assumption and Its Breakdown

The **continuum hypothesis** assumes that fluid properties (velocity, pressure, density) vary continuously in space and time, and that macroscopic quantities represent averages over sufficiently large numbers of molecules. This assumption is valid when the **mean free path** $\lambda$ of the fluid molecules is much smaller than the characteristic dimension $L$ of the conduit.

The **Knudsen number** is the dimensionless parameter that quantifies the validity of the continuum assumption:

$$Kn = \frac{\lambda}{L} = \frac{\lambda}{D_h}$$

Where the mean free path $\lambda$ for an ideal gas is given by:

$$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 p}$$

Where:
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant (**$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $d$ = effective molecular diameter (m)
- $p$ = absolute pressure (Pa)

#### 2.1.1 Flow Regime Classification

The Knudsen number classifies fluid flow into four primary regimes:

- **Continuum regime ($Kn < 0.001$):** Classical Navier–Stokes equations with no-slip boundary condition are valid. This is the typical regime for liquid flow in microchannels.
- **Slip flow regime ($0.001 < Kn < 0.1$):** Navier–Stokes equations are still applicable, but the **no-slip boundary condition must be replaced by a slip boundary condition**.
- **Transition regime ($0.1 < Kn < 10$):** Continuum assumption breaks down. The **Burnett equations** or **direct simulation Monte Carlo (DSMC)** methods are required.
- **Free molecular regime ($Kn > 10$):** Molecules collide more with the walls than with each other. The flow is described by **molecular dynamics** approaches.

> [!NOTE]
> **Liquids vs. Gases:** For **liquids**, the molecular mean free path is extremely short (on the order of Angstroms), so $Kn \ll 0.001$ always holds. The continuum assumption is essentially always valid for liquids. Slip flow is **primarily a gas-phase micro-conduit phenomenon** and a critical topic for KTU 2024 exams.

### 2.2 The Reynolds Number in Microchannels

The Reynolds number represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³)
- $v$ = mean flow velocity (m/s)
- $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)

**Key observation for KTU 2024:** Because $D_h$ is very small (micro-scale), $Re$ in microchannels is typically **less than 100**, and almost always **less than 1** for most lab-on-a-chip applications. This means:

- Flow is almost always **laminar**.
- **Inertia can be neglected** compared to viscous forces.
- The parabolic velocity profile (Hagen–Poiseuille) is the dominant flow structure.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes all high-yield formulas for fluid flow in micro conduits:

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Applicable Regime |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Knudsen Number | $Kn = \lambda / D_h$ | dimensionless | All regimes |
| 2 | Mean Free Path (gas) | $\lambda = k_B T / (\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 p)$ | m | Gas microflow |
| 3 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \rho v D_h / \mu$ | dimensionless | Continuum |
| 4 | Hydraulic Diameter | $D_h = 4A / P$ | m | All geometries |
| 5 | Hagen–Poiseuille (circular) | $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta p / (8 \mu L)$ | m³/s | Circular, $Re < 2300$ |
| 6 | Mean velocity (circular) | $v_{mean} = R^2 \Delta p / (8 \mu L)$ | m/s | Circular |
| 7 | Wall shear stress | $\tau_w = R \Delta p / (2 L)$ | Pa | Circular Poiseuille |
| 8 | Friction factor (laminar) | $f = 64 / Re$ | dimensionless | Circular laminar |
| 9 | Capillary number | $Ca = \mu v / \gamma$ | dimensionless | Free surface flows |
| 10 | Capillary pressure | $p_c = 2 \gamma \cos \theta / R$ | Pa | Circular capillary |
| 11 | Washburn equation | $L^2 = \gamma R t \cos \theta / (2 \mu)$ | m² | Capillary filling |
| 12 | Slip length | $u_s = L_s (\partial u / \partial n)$ | m | Slip flow |
| 13 | Electroosmotic velocity (Helmholtz–Smoluchowski) | $v_{eo} = - \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \zeta E / \mu$ | m/s | Electroosmotic |
| 14 | Electric double layer thickness | $\lambda_D = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 k_B T / (2 n_\infty z^2 e^2)}$ | m | EDL theory |
| 15 | Pressure drop (rectangular) | $\Delta p = 12 \mu L Q / (w h^3) [1 - 0.63 (h/w)]$ | Pa | Rectangular, $h < w$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP — Avoid the pipe symbol `|`:**
> When writing $\vert \Delta p \vert$ in exam scripts, always use the standard notation with parentheses or write "$\text{abs}(\Delta p)$". Inside markdown tables (and in your answer sheets), use the LaTeX command $\vert$ for vertical bars to avoid breaking the table structure.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The theory of fluid flow in micro conduits underpins virtually every modern biomedical and chemical engineering application:

- **Lab-on-a-chip diagnostics:** Blood sample flow through microfluidic channels for point-of-care disease detection (e.g., glucose monitoring, COVID-19 antigen tests).
- **Drug delivery systems:** Controlled-release micro-needle patches that deliver precise doses via capillary-driven flow.
- **DNA sequencing devices:** Electroosmotic flow is used to transport DNA fragments through nanopores (Illumina, Oxford Nanopore platforms).
- **Micro-heat exchangers:** Used in spacecraft thermal management and high-performance electronics cooling.
- **Inkjet printing:** Droplet ejection physics in printheads rely on capillary and Reynolds number analysis at micro-nozzles.
- **Soft robotics and microfluidics:** Pneumatic microactuators in soft robots use Hagen–Poiseuille flow principles.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hagen–Poiseuille Flow in a Circular Microchannel

The Hagen–Poiseuille equation is the foundational result for pressure-driven flow in microconduits. We derive it from the **Navier–Stokes equation** in cylindrical coordinates under steady, fully-developed, laminar, incompressible flow assumptions.

#### 3.1.1 Governing Assumptions

- Steady-state: $\partial / \partial t = 0$
- Axisymmetric: $\partial / \partial \theta = 0$
- Fully-developed: $\partial v_z / \partial z = 0$
- Incompressible: $\rho = $ constant
- Unidirectional flow: $v_r = 0$, $v_\theta = 0$, $v_z = v_z(r)$
- No-slip at wall: $v_z(R) = 0$
- Axisymmetry at center: $\partial v_z / \partial r \vert_{r=0} = 0$

#### 3.1.2 Starting Equation

The $z$-component of the Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates reduces to:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} \right) \right]$$

This is because all other terms vanish under the assumptions above.

#### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Integration

**Step 1:** Rearrange the equation to isolate the radial derivative.

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} \right) = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}$$

**Step 2:** Define the constant pressure gradient. Since $v_z = v_z(r)$ only and the equation must be consistent for all $r$, the right-hand side must be a constant. Define $G = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{dp}{dz}$ (assuming linear pressure drop).

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = G$$

**Step 3:** Multiply both sides by $r$.

$$\frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = G r$$

**Step 4:** Integrate with respect to $r$ once.

$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = \int G r \, dr = \frac{G r^2}{2} + C_1$$

**Step 5:** Apply the axisymmetry boundary condition at $r = 0$: $dv_z/dr = 0$ (finite velocity at center). This gives $C_1 = 0$.

$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{G r^2}{2}$$

**Step 6:** Divide by $r$ to isolate the derivative.

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{G r}{2}$$

**Step 7:** Integrate again to obtain the velocity profile.

$$v_z(r) = \int \frac{G r}{2} \, dr = \frac{G r^2}{4} + C_2$$

**Step 8:** Apply the no-slip boundary condition: $v_z(R) = 0$.

$$0 = \frac{G R^2}{4} + C_2 \implies C_2 = -\frac{G R^2}{4}$$

**Step 9:** Substitute $C_2$ back into the velocity equation.

$$v_z(r) = \frac{G}{4} (r^2 - R^2)$$

**Step 10:** Substitute back $G = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{dp}{dz}$ and recognize that $\frac{dp}{dz} = -\frac{\Delta p}{L}$ (negative because pressure decreases in the flow direction).

$$v_z(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu} \left( -\frac{\Delta p}{L} \right) (r^2 - R^2) = \frac{\Delta p}{4 \mu L} (R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 11:** Compute the volumetric flow rate by integrating the velocity over the cross-section.

$$Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr = \int_0^R \frac{\Delta p}{4 \mu L} (R^2 - r^2) \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr$$

**Step 12:** Pull constants out of the integral.

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta p}{2 \mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) \, dr$$

**Step 13:** Evaluate the integral term by term.

$$\int_0^R R^2 r \, dr = R^2 \cdot \frac{R^2}{2} = \frac{R^4}{2}$$

$$\int_0^R r^3 \, dr = \frac{R^4}{4}$$

**Step 14:** Subtract and combine.

$$\int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) \, dr = \frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{R^4}{4}$$

**Step 15:** Final form of the Hagen–Poiseuille equation.

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta p}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8 \mu L}$$

The mean velocity is obtained by dividing by the cross-sectional area $A = \pi R^2$:

$$v_{mean} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{R^2 \Delta p}{8 \mu L}$$

The maximum velocity (at centerline, $r = 0$) is:

$$v_{max} = \frac{R^2 \Delta p}{4 \mu L} = 2 \, v_{mean}$$

This confirms the **parabolic velocity profile** characteristic of laminar microchannel flow.

### 3.2 Derivation: Knudsen Number and Flow Regime Boundaries

The Knudsen number is derived from the kinetic theory of gases. Consider a gas molecule traveling with average thermal velocity $v_{th}$. The mean free path is the average distance traveled between successive collisions.

The mean thermal velocity from the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution is:

$$v_{th} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}}$$

The collision cross-section for hard-sphere molecules of diameter $d$ is:

$$\sigma = \pi d^2$$

The number density of molecules $n$ is related to pressure and temperature by the ideal gas law:

$$n = \frac{p}{k_B T}$$

The mean free path is the ratio of the average distance traveled per unit time to the collision frequency:

$$\lambda = \frac{v_{th}}{\nu_{collision}} = \frac{v_{th}}{n \sigma v_{th}} = \frac{1}{n \sigma} = \frac{k_B T}{\pi d^2 p}$$

A more rigorous derivation using the relative velocity (which accounts for the fact that both colliding molecules are in motion) introduces a factor of $\sqrt{2}$:

$$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 p}$$

This is the standard textbook expression used in KTU exam problems. Substituting into the Knudsen number definition:

$$Kn = \frac{\lambda}{D_h} = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 p \, D_h}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Slip Flow Velocity Correction

When $0.001 < Kn < 0.1$, the no-slip boundary condition is replaced by the **Maxwell slip boundary condition**:

$$u_{slip} = L_s \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial n} \right)_{wall}$$

Where $L_s$ is the **slip length**, and $\frac{\partial u}{\partial n}$ is the velocity gradient normal to the wall. The slip length is empirically related to the Knudsen number by:

$$L_s \approx \frac{2 - \sigma_v}{\sigma_v} \lambda$$

Where $\sigma_v$ is the **tangential momentum accommodation coefficient (TMAC)**, typically $\sigma_v \approx 0.7$ to $1.0$ for most engineering surfaces.

For Poiseuille flow with slip, the modified velocity profile is:

$$u(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu} \left( -\frac{dp}{dz} \right) \left( R^2 - r^2 + 2 R L_s \right)$$

The modified volumetric flow rate becomes:

$$Q_{slip} = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8 \mu L} \left( 1 + \frac{4 L_s}{R} \right)$$

Define the **apparent slip parameter**:

$$\beta = \frac{4 L_s}{R} \approx \frac{8 (2 - \sigma_v)}{\sigma_v} Kn$$

So the slip-corrected flow rate is:

$$Q_{slip} = Q_{no-slip} \left( 1 + \beta \right) = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8 \mu L} \left( 1 + \beta \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The slip correction is most significant in **gas microflows** in channels smaller than about 10 μm. For **liquid microflows**, $L_s$ is negligible (on the order of nanometers), so the no-slip Hagen–Poiseuille formula is essentially always valid. This is why KTU 2024 questions on slip flow almost always specify a gas (e.g., air, nitrogen, helium).

### 3.4 Derivation: Capillary Pressure and Washburn Equation

When a liquid spontaneously wets a microchannel, the meniscus advances under the action of **Laplace pressure** (capillary pressure). For a circular tube of radius $R$, the Young–Laplace equation gives:

$$p_c = \frac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{R}$$

Where $\theta$ is the contact angle and $\gamma$ is the surface tension. This capillary pressure drives the liquid into the channel against viscous resistance.

The driving force balances the viscous drag over the filled length $L$:

$$\Delta p \cdot \pi R^2 = 2 \pi R L \cdot \tau_{wall}$$

For Poiseuille flow, $\tau_{wall} = 4 \mu v / R = 4 \mu \dot{L} / R$, where $\dot{L} = dL/dt$ is the meniscus velocity. Substituting:

$$\frac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{R} \cdot \pi R^2 = 2 \pi R L \cdot \frac{4 \mu \dot{L}}{R}$$

Simplifying:

$$\pi R \cdot 2 \gamma \cos \theta = 8 \pi \mu L \dot{L}$$

$$\gamma R \cos \theta = 4 \mu L \frac{dL}{dt}$$

Rearranging and integrating with initial condition $L(0) = 0$:

$$4 \mu L \, dL = \gamma R \cos \theta \, dt$$

$$2 \mu L^2 = \gamma R t \cos \theta + C$$

$$L^2 = \frac{\gamma R t \cos \theta}{2 \mu}$$

This is the famous **Washburn equation** (1921). It predicts that the meniscus advances as the square root of time, which has been experimentally verified in countless micro-capillary filling experiments.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Water ($\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\gamma = 0.072$ N/m, $\theta = 0°$) is to flow through a circular microchannel of diameter $D = 50$ μm and length $L = 5$ cm under a pressure drop of $\Delta p = 10$ kPa. Determine:
1. The volumetric flow rate $Q$
2. The mean velocity $v_{mean}$
3. The Reynolds number $Re$
4. The capillary filling time if the channel is filled by capillary action alone

**Solution:**

**(1) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$R = \frac{D}{2} = 25 \, \mu m = 25 \times 10^{-6} \, m$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8 \mu L} = \frac{\pi (25 \times 10^{-6})^4 (10 \times 10^3)}{8 (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) (5 \times 10^{-2})}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$(25 \times 10^{-6})^4 = 3.906 \times 10^{-16} \, m^4$$

$$\pi \cdot 3.906 \times 10^{-16} \cdot 10^4 = 1.227 \times 10^{-11}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$8 \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-2} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$Q = \frac{1.227 \times 10^{-11}}{4.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.07 \times 10^{-8} \, m^3/s = 30.7 \, \mu L/s$$

**(2) Mean velocity:**

$$v_{mean} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{3.07 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi (25 \times 10^{-6})^2} = \frac{3.07 \times 10^{-8}}{1.963 \times 10^{-9}} = 15.6 \, m/s$$

**(3) Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D_h}{\mu} = \frac{(997)(15.6)(50 \times 10^{-6})}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 778$$

The flow is **laminar** (Re < 2300), confirming the Hagen–Poiseuille assumption.

**(4) Capillary filling time:**

Using the Washburn equation, solve for $t$ when $L = 5$ cm = $0.05$ m:

$$t = \frac{2 \mu L^2}{\gamma R \cos \theta} = \frac{2 (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) (0.05)^2}{(0.072) (25 \times 10^{-6}) (1)}$$

$$t = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-6}}{1.8 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.78 \, s$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip — Numerical Strategy:** Always compute Reynolds number FIRST after finding $v_{mean}$. If $Re < 2300$, your Hagen–Poiseuille assumption is validated, and you receive full marks. If $Re > 2300$, you must switch to turbulent flow correlations and may lose marks for not validating the regime.
