## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the Knudsen number. List the four flow regimes classified based on the Knudsen number.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Knudsen number ($Kn$)** is a dimensionless quantity defined as the ratio of the molecular mean free path $\lambda$ to a characteristic physical length scale $L$ (typically the hydraulic diameter $D_h$) of the flow domain:

$$Kn = \frac{\lambda}{D_h}$$

The four flow regimes classified based on the Knudsen number are:

| Regime | Knudsen Number Range | Governing Equations |
|---|---|---|
| Continuum flow | $Kn < 0.001$ | Navier–Stokes with no-slip |
| Slip flow | $0.001 \le Kn < 0.1$ | Navier–Stokes with slip BC |
| Transition flow | $0.1 \le Kn < 10$ | Burnett / DSMC |
| Free molecular flow | $Kn \ge 10$ | Molecular dynamics |

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State and explain the Washburn equation for capillary filling in a microchannel.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Washburn equation** describes the time-dependent position of the liquid meniscus during spontaneous capillary filling of a cylindrical microchannel. It is derived by balancing the Laplace capillary pressure with the viscous resistance of the advancing liquid column.

The equation is:

$$L^2 = \frac{\gamma R t \cos \theta}{2 \mu}$$

Where:
- $L$ = length of liquid column that has entered the channel (m)
- $R$ = radius of the channel (m)
- $t$ = time elapsed (s)
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of the liquid (N/m)
- $\theta$ = contact angle between liquid and channel wall (degrees)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the liquid (Pa·s)

**Physical interpretation:** The meniscus advances proportionally to $\sqrt{t}$, meaning the filling rate slows down as the channel gets longer. This is because viscous resistance grows linearly with the filled length, while the driving capillary pressure remains constant.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**(a)** Derive the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for steady, laminar, incompressible flow of a Newtonian fluid in a circular microchannel of radius $R$ and length $L$, starting from the Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Water ($\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) flows through a circular microchannel of diameter $D = 100$ μm and length $L = 10$ cm under a pressure drop $\Delta p = 5$ kPa. Calculate:
   (i) The volumetric flow rate
   (ii) The mean velocity
   (iii) The Reynolds number and verify whether the laminar flow assumption is valid. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The derivation is shown in detail in Section 3.1 of these notes. The final result is:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8 \mu L}$$

**Assumptions (must be listed for full marks):**
- Steady flow
- Laminar flow
- Incompressible fluid
- Newtonian fluid
- Axisymmetric flow
- Fully-developed flow
- No-slip at wall
- Unidirectional flow ($v_r = v_\theta = 0$)

The student should present the full derivation including all 15 steps from Section 3.1 to receive complete 7 marks.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Volumetric flow rate:**

Given: $D = 100$ μm, so $R = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $L = 0.1$ m, $\Delta p = 5000$ Pa, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

$$Q = \frac{\pi (50 \times 10^{-6})^4 (5000)}{8 (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) (0.1)}$$

Numerator: $\pi \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 5000 = 9.817 \times 10^{-11}$

Denominator: $8 \times 10^{-4}$

$$Q = \frac{9.817 \times 10^{-11}}{8 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.227 \times 10^{-7} \, m^3/s \approx 122.7 \, \mu L/s$$

**(ii) Mean velocity:**

$$v_{mean} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{1.227 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi (50 \times 10^{-6})^2} = \frac{1.227 \times 10^{-7}}{7.854 \times 10^{-9}} = 15.62 \, m/s$$

**(iii) Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D_h}{\mu} = \frac{(1000)(15.62)(100 \times 10^{-6})}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 1562$$

Since $Re = 1562 < 2300$, the flow is **laminar** and the Hagen–Poiseuille assumption is **valid**. ✓

#### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice for Internal Option]

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of electroosmotic flow in microchannels. Derive the Helmholtz–Smoluchowski equation for the electroosmotic velocity. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A glass microchannel ($D_h = 80$ μm, length $L = 4$ cm) is filled with a phosphate buffer solution ($\varepsilon_r = 78$, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\zeta = -50$ mV). An electric field $E = 200$ V/cm is applied along the channel. Calculate:
   (i) The electroosmotic velocity
   (ii) The volumetric flow rate
   (iii) The time required for a neutral sample plug to traverse the channel length. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Phenomenon of Electroosmotic Flow:**
When a microchannel is filled with an aqueous electrolyte, the channel walls (e.g., glass, PDMS) acquire a surface charge due to ionization or ion adsorption. For glass in contact with water at neutral pH, the surface becomes negatively charged (silanol groups deprotonate). This negative charge attracts positive counter-ions from the bulk, forming the **Electrical Double Layer (EDL)**. The EDL consists of:
- **Stern layer** (1–2 nm thick): ions rigidly attached to the wall
- **Diffuse layer** (1–100 nm thick): mobile counter-ions

When an axial electric field is applied, the mobile cations in the diffuse layer migrate toward the cathode. Through viscous coupling (momentum transfer via collisions), the bulk fluid is dragged along, producing **electroosmotic flow (EOF)**.

**Helmholtz–Smoluchowski Derivation:**

Assumptions: thin EDL ($\lambda_D \ll D_h$), low Reynolds number, Newtonian fluid, no pressure gradient.

The electric body force on the net charge density $\rho_e$ in the EDL is balanced by the viscous force:

$$\rho_e E_z = \mu \frac{\partial^2 u_z}{\partial y^2}$$

Where $y$ is the coordinate normal to the wall. The net charge density is related to the electric potential $\psi$ via Poisson's equation:

$$\rho_e = -\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2}$$

Substituting and integrating twice using the boundary conditions $\psi = \zeta$ at the wall and $\psi = 0$ in the bulk, and $u = 0$ at the wall (no-slip), yields the **Smoluchowski velocity**:

$$v_{eo} = -\frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \zeta E}{\mu}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $\varepsilon_r = 78$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\zeta = -50$ mV $= -0.050$ V, $E = 200$ V/cm $= 20000$ V/m, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $D_h = 80$ μm, $L = 0.04$ m.

**(i) Electroosmotic velocity:**

$$v_{eo} = -\frac{(78)(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(-0.050)(20000)}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Numerator: $78 \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 0.050 \cdot 20000 = 6.905 \times 10^{-10}$

Denominator: $10^{-3}$

$$v_{eo} = 6.905 \times 10^{-7} \, m/s = 0.69 \, \mu m/s$$

The flow direction is from anode (+) to cathode (−), which is the conventional direction for negatively charged glass walls.

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate:**

For a circular channel, $A = \pi D^2/4 = \pi (80 \times 10^{-6})^2 / 4 = 5.027 \times 10^{-9}$ m².

$$Q = v_{eo} \cdot A = (6.905 \times 10^{-7})(5.027 \times 10^{-9}) = 3.47 \times 10^{-15} \, m^3/s = 3.47 \, fL/s$$

**(iii) Transit time:**

$$t = \frac{L}{v_{eo}} = \frac{0.04}{6.905 \times 10^{-7}} = 5.79 \times 10^4 \, s \approx 16.1 \, hours$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** The electric field is given in V/cm — ALWAYS convert to V/m by multiplying by 100 before substituting into SI formulas. Zeta potential is in mV — convert to V. Failing to do this yields answers off by factors of 100 or 1000.
>
> 2. **Sign Convention in EOF:** The negative sign in $v_{eo} = -\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \zeta E / \mu$ is often dropped. For negatively charged glass walls ($\zeta < 0$) with positive applied $E$ (anode at inlet), $v_{eo} > 0$, meaning flow is from anode to cathode. **Always state the direction explicitly.**
>
> 3. **Knudsen Number vs Reynolds Number Confusion:** Some students confuse the two dimensionless numbers. Remember: $Kn$ characterizes **continuum validity** (gas microflow), while $Re$ characterizes **flow regime** (laminar vs turbulent). They are independent concepts.
>
> 4. **Missing No-Slip Verification:** In Part B, students often forget to check that the no-slip Hagen–Poiseuille equation is valid. Always compute $Re$ and verify $Re < 2300$ to justify the laminar assumption.
>
> 5. **Mean vs Maximum Velocity:** Examiners expect you to distinguish $v_{max} = 2 v_{mean}$ for Hagen–Poiseuille flow. Mixing them up costs at least 1 mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fluid Flow in Micro Conduits**

- **Definition of Knudsen number:** $Kn = \lambda / D_h$, with $\lambda = k_B T / (\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 p)$ for ideal gases.
- **Four flow regimes** by $Kn$: Continuum ($< 0.001$), Slip flow ($0.001$–$0.1$), Transition ($0.1$–$10$), Free molecular ($> 10$).
- **Reynolds number** in microchannels is typically $Re < 100$ → flow is laminar → inertia negligible.
- **Hydraulic diameter:** $D_h = 4A/P$, with $D_h = D$ for a circular tube and $D_h = 2wh/(w+h)$ for a rectangular channel.
- **Hagen–Poiseuille flow rate (circular):** $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta p / (8\mu L)$ — memorize this.
- **Mean velocity:** $v_{mean} = R^2 \Delta p / (8 \mu L)$.
- **Maximum velocity:** $v_{max} = R^2 \Delta p / (4\mu L) = 2 v_{mean}$ at the centerline.
- **Velocity profile:** $v_z(r) = (\Delta p / 4\mu L)(R^2 - r^2)$ — parabolic.
- **Slip flow correction:** $Q_{slip} = Q_{no-slip} (1 + \beta)$ where $\beta = 4 L_s / R \approx 8(2-\sigma_v)/\sigma_v \cdot Kn$.
- **Slip length:** $L_s \approx [(2-\sigma_v)/\sigma_v] \lambda$, typically negligible for liquids.
- **Capillary pressure (circular tube):** $p_c = 2 \gamma \cos \theta / R$.
- **Washburn equation:** $L^2 = \gamma R t \cos \theta / (2 \mu)$ — meniscus moves as $\sqrt{t}$.
- **Capillary number:** $Ca = \mu v / \gamma$ — ratio of viscous to surface tension forces.
- **Electroosmotic velocity (Smoluchowski):** $v_{eo} = -\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \zeta E / \mu$ — flat (plug) profile.
- **EDL thickness (Debye length):** $\lambda_D = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 k_B T / (2 n_\infty z^2 e^2)}$.
- **Wall shear stress (Poiseuille):** $\tau_w = R \Delta p / (2 L)$.
- **Friction factor (laminar):** $f = 64 / Re$ for circular cross-section.
- **Typical values to memorize:** $\mu_{water} = 1$ cP, $\gamma_{water} = 0.072$ N/m, $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $\zeta_{glass} = -50$ mV.
- **Slip flow is mainly for gases**, not liquids, in KTU-level exam questions.
- **Always check the validity** of laminar flow by computing $Re$ after solving for $v_{mean}$.
- **Physical insight to mention in theory answers:** Viscous forces dominate; surface tension and electrokinetic effects become significant; continuum assumption can break down for gases in sub-10-μm channels.
- **Engineering applications to mention:** Lab-on-a-chip diagnostics, drug delivery, inkjet printing, DNA sequencing, micro-heat exchangers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Actuation Methods - Dielectrophoresis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fluid Actuation Methods - Dielectrophoresis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dielectrophoresis (DEP)** is the electrokinetic motion of a polarizable (but electrically neutral or charged) particle induced by a **spatially non-uniform electric field**. The phenomenon was first systematically characterized by **Herbert A. Pohl in 1951**, who coined the term from the Greek roots *dia* (across), *elektron* (amber/electricity), and *pherein* (to bear/carry).

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEBT743)**, Dielectrophoresis is classified as a **non-mechanical, gradient-force-based fluid actuation paradigm** that exploits the contrast in dielectric properties between a suspended particle and its surrounding medium. Unlike pressure-driven or electroosmotic flow, DEP does **not** require bulk fluid motion — instead, it directly manipulates the **particle** suspended within the fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for Examiners:**
> - **Electrophoresis** → Motion of *charged* particles in a *uniform* field (Coulomb force, $F = qE$).
> - **Dielectrophoresis** → Motion of *polarizable* particles (charged **or neutral**) in a *non-uniform* field (dipole-gradient force, $F_{DEP} = (\mathbf{p} \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{E}$).

The fundamental prerequisite for DEP is therefore the existence of a **field gradient** $\nabla \vert \mathbf{E} \vert^{2} \neq 0$. In a perfectly uniform field, the induced dipole experiences equal and opposite Coulomb forces on its two halves, and **no net translational motion** occurs.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Iron Filing and Magnet" Model:**
> Imagine sprinkling **neutral iron filings** (which are uncharged but magnetically polarizable) over a bar magnet. Even though no filing is "magnetically charged," the filings **self-organize** — they cluster at the magnet's sharp edges and poles (regions of high field gradient) and avoid the smooth, uniform regions. Furthermore, if you replace the iron filings with **brass particles** (which polarize *oppositely*), they are **pushed away** from the high-field regions.
>
> In DEP, the **non-uniform electric field** plays the role of the magnet, the **particle** is the iron filing, and the **dielectric constant mismatch** $\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*}$ replaces the magnetic susceptibility. Particles with a *higher* effective polarizability than the medium are **pulled** toward high-field regions (**positive DEP**); those with a *lower* polarizability are **pushed away** (**negative DEP**).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Vacuum permittivity | $\varepsilon_{0}$ | $\mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ F/m}$ |
| Clausius-Mossotti factor (real part) | $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]$ | $-0.5 \leq \mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)] \leq +1.0$ (theoretical bounds for a sphere) |
| Cross-over frequency | $f_{xo}$ | $\mathbf{kHz\ to\ MHz}$ range (cell/medium specific) |
| Typical electrode gap | $g$ | $\mathbf{10\ \mu m\ to\ 100\ \mu m}$ |
| Particle radius range | $r$ | $\mathbf{100\ nm\ to\ 50\ \mu m}$ (biological cells) |
| Applied field strength | $E_{rms}$ | $\mathbf{10^{4}\ to\ 10^{6}\ V/m}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

The derivation of DEP rests on **four pillars** of classical electromagnetism and continuum electrostatics. We now unpack each one in the rigorous, KTU-evaluator-friendly sequence.

### Pillar 1 — The Induced Dipole Mechanism

When a dielectric particle of complex permittivity $\varepsilon_{p}^{*}$ is placed in a medium of complex permittivity $\varepsilon_{m}^{*}$ subjected to a local field $\mathbf{E}_{loc}$, the free and bound charges within the particle redistribute along the field lines. This redistribution creates a separation of charge centers, generating an **effective induced dipole moment**:

$$\mathbf{p}(t) = 4\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ K(\omega)\ \mathbf{E}_{loc}(t)$$

where $r$ is the particle radius and $K(\omega)$ is the **frequency-dependent Clausius-Mossotti (CM) factor** that captures the complex dielectric contrast. The volume factor $4\pi r^{3}/3$ multiplied by 3 (for the polarizability definition) gives the prefactor.

### Pillar 2 — The Gradient Force on a Dipole

A *uniform* field exerts zero net force on a dipole, but a *non-uniform* field exerts a net translational force. The general electrostatic force on a point dipole in an external field is:

$$\mathbf{F}_{DEP} = (\mathbf{p} \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{E}$$

This is the **"dipole-gradient force"** — the same force that causes polar molecules to align and migrate in a *non-uniform* field (e.g., a polarizable needle being attracted to a charged comb).

### Pillar 3 — Time-Averaging the AC Force

In practical BioMEMS devices, an **AC** electric field is used to avoid electrochemical reactions (Faradaic currents, electrolysis, Joule heating). Since $\mathbf{p}$ and $\mathbf{E}$ both oscillate, we must compute the **time-averaged** force over one cycle. For a sinusoidal field $\mathbf{E}(t) = \mathbf{E}_{0}\cos(\omega t)$ and a complex dipole response, the time-average yields:

$$\langle \mathbf{F}_{DEP} \rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}\ \mathrm{Re}\!\left[(\mathbf{p}^{*} \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{E}\right]$$

The factor of $1/2$ arises from $\langle \cos^{2}(\omega t) \rangle = 1/2$, and the real-part operator selects the in-phase (force-producing) component, discarding the $90^{\circ}$ out-of-phase (energy-storing) component.

### Pillar 4 — Combining Into the Master DEP Equation

Substituting the dipole moment into the time-averaged force, and using the vector identity $(\mathbf{E} \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{E} = \nabla(\tfrac{1}{2}\vert \mathbf{E} \vert^{2}) - \mathbf{E} \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{E})$, with $\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = 0$ for an electro quasi-static field, we arrive at the **master DEP force equation**:

$$\boxed{\ \langle \mathbf{F}_{DEP} \rangle = 2\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ \mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]\ \nabla \vert \mathbf{E}_{rms} \vert^{2}\ }$$

This is the equation that KTU examiners expect students to recognize and reproduce. Note carefully that the force is **proportional to the gradient of the field magnitude squared**, not the field itself.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every** equation, definition, and boundary condition a KTU student must master for Dielectrophoresis questions. All symbols and SI units are listed for examiner-friendly cross-reference.

| # | Equation / Definition | Symbolic Form | SI Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Complex permittivity of a material | $\varepsilon^{*} = \varepsilon - j\sigma/\omega$ | $\mathrm{F/m}$ | Encodes both dielectric ($\varepsilon$) and conductive ($\sigma$) response |
| 2 | Clausius-Mossotti (CM) factor | $K(\omega) = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*}}{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} + 2\varepsilon_{m}^{*}}$ | dimensionless | Polarizability contrast between particle and medium |
| 3 | Induced dipole moment (sphere) | $\mathbf{p} = 4\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ K(\omega)\ \mathbf{E}$ | $\mathrm{C \cdot m}$ | Net polarization induced by external field |
| 4 | Time-averaged DEP force | $\langle \mathbf{F}_{DEP} \rangle = 2\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ \mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]\ \nabla\vert \mathbf{E}_{rms}\vert^{2}$ | $\mathrm{N}$ | Net translational force on a neutral polarizable sphere |
| 5 | Positive DEP condition | $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)] > 0$ | — | Attraction toward high-field region |
| 6 | Negative DEP condition | $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)] < 0$ | — | Repulsion from high-field region |
| 7 | Cross-over frequency | $f_{xo}\ :\ \mathrm{Re}[K(\omega_{xo})] = 0$ | $\mathrm{Hz}$ | Frequency at which DEP force vanishes |
| 8 | Analytical $f_{xo}$ (single-shell simplified) | $f_{xo} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{(\sigma_{m} - 2\sigma_{p})(\sigma_{p} + 2\sigma_{m})}{(\varepsilon_{p} - 2\varepsilon_{m})(\varepsilon_{p} + 2\varepsilon_{m})}}$ | $\mathrm{Hz}$ | Frequency at which $\mathrm{Re}[K]=0$ |
| 9 | Particle velocity under DEP | $\mathbf{v}_{p} = \mu_{DEP}\ \nabla\vert \mathbf{E}_{rms}\vert^{2}$ | $\mathrm{m/s}$ | Stokes-drag-limited translational motion |
| 10 | DEP mobility | $\mu_{DEP} = \dfrac{r^{2}\ \varepsilon_{m}\ \mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]}{3\eta}$ | $\mathrm{m^{4}/(V^{2} \cdot s)}$ | Mobility of a sphere in a viscous medium |
| 11 | Stokes drag force (opposing) | $\mathbf{F}_{drag} = 6\pi \eta r\ \mathbf{v}_{p}$ | $\mathrm{N}$ | Viscous resistance at low Reynolds number |
| 12 | Dielectrophoretic levitation height | $h = \left(\dfrac{mg}{2\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3} C}\right)^{1/3}$ | $\mathrm{m}$ | Equilibrium height in a levitation DEP trap |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** "**DEP** loves **GMS**" — **G**radient, **M**agnitude-squared, **S**ign-of-CM-factor. Memorize the master equation in the form $\mathbf{F} \propto r^{3} \cdot \nabla \vert E \vert^{2} \cdot \mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]$.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

Dielectrophoresis is a **workhorse technique** in modern BioMEMS laboratories and commercial point-of-care devices. The key engineering value propositions are:

1. **Label-free manipulation** — No fluorescent tags, magnetic beads, or chemical labels required. The intrinsic dielectric fingerprint of the cell/particle is sufficient.
2. **Frequency-selective separation** — By sweeping the AC frequency across the *cross-over frequency* $f_{xo}$, two cell types with even slight dielectric differences can be cleanly separated (e.g., **cancerous vs. healthy leukocytes**, **viable vs. non-viable bacteria**).
3. **Scalability to single-cell resolution** — Electrode gaps of 10–50 µm routinely handle individual cells.
4. **Compatibility with optical/microscopy readouts** — Transparent ITO (indium tin oxide) electrodes enable simultaneous DEP and fluorescence imaging.
5. **No net charge requirement** — Unlike electrophoresis, DEP can manipulate **neutrally buoyant**, **uncharged** particles (e.g., latex beads, DNA, viruses, exosomes).

Industries deploying DEP include **clinical diagnostics** (Circulating Tumor Cell (CTC) chips, blood-cell sorting), **environmental monitoring** (pathogen concentration from water), and **pharmaceutical screening** (single-cell drug-response assays).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section presents the **complete, exhaustive derivation** of the master DEP force equation. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly — no shortcuts, no "it can be shown that." The mathematics follows the standard KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation) valuation pattern, which awards full credit only when each transition is justified.

## 3.1 Derivation I — The Clausius-Mossotti Factor from First Principles

We begin by considering a homogeneous dielectric sphere of complex permittivity $\varepsilon_{p}^{*}$ and radius $r$, immersed in a medium of complex permittivity $\varepsilon_{m}^{*}$, and subjected to a uniform external field $\mathbf{E}_{0}$.

**Step 1 — Solve the Laplace equation in spherical coordinates.** With the external field aligned along the $z$-axis, the boundary conditions at the particle surface ($R = r$) require continuity of the tangential $\mathbf{E}$ and the normal $\mathbf{D}$. The interior field $\mathbf{E}_{in}$ is uniform and given by:

$$\mathbf{E}_{in} = \mathbf{E}_{0}\ \left(\frac{3\varepsilon_{m}^{*}}{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} + 2\varepsilon_{m}^{*}}\right)$$

This is the standard result for a dielectric sphere in a uniform field, derivable by solving $\nabla^{2}\Phi = 0$ with the appropriate boundary conditions.

**Step 2 — Define the effective polarizability.** The induced dipole moment of the sphere is:

$$\mathbf{p} = \varepsilon_{m}^{*}\ (\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*})\ \mathbf{E}_{in}\ \frac{4\pi r^{3}}{3\varepsilon_{m}^{*}}$$

Substituting the expression for $\mathbf{E}_{in}$:

$$\mathbf{p} = \frac{4\pi r^{3}}{3}\ \mathbf{E}_{0}\ (\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*})\ \frac{3\varepsilon_{m}^{*}}{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} + 2\varepsilon_{m}^{*}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.** Cancel the 3 in numerator and denominator, and rearrange:

$$\mathbf{p} = 4\pi r^{3}\ \varepsilon_{m}^{*}\ \mathbf{E}_{0}\ \frac{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*}}{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} + 2\varepsilon_{m}^{*}}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Clausius-Mossotti factor.** By definition:

$$K(\omega) = \frac{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*}}{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} + 2\varepsilon_{m}^{*}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\ \mathbf{p} = 4\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ K(\omega)\ \mathbf{E}_{0}\ }$$

This is the **fundamental relationship** between the applied field, the dielectric contrast, and the resulting induced dipole moment — a high-yield result for KTU derivations.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Time-Averaged DEP Force on a Spherical Particle

**Step 1 — Start with the instantaneous force on a point dipole in a non-uniform field.** A dipole $\mathbf{p}$ in an external field $\mathbf{E}$ experiences a net force:

$$\mathbf{F} = (\mathbf{p} \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{E} = \nabla(\mathbf{p} \cdot \mathbf{E}) - \mathbf{E} \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{p})$$

For a linear, isotropic, locally homogeneous medium, the second term vanishes. The first term simplifies to:

$$\mathbf{F} = \nabla(\mathbf{p} \cdot \mathbf{E})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the induced dipole moment.**

$$\mathbf{F} = \nabla\!\left[4\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ K(\omega)\ \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{E}\right] = 4\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ K(\omega)\ \nabla(\mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{E})$$

**Step 3 — Use the identity for the square of the field magnitude.**

$$\mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{E} = \vert \mathbf{E} \vert^{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbf{F} = 4\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ K(\omega)\ \nabla \vert \mathbf{E} \vert^{2}$$

This is the **instantaneous** DEP force expression.

**Step 4 — Apply the AC time-average.** For an AC field $\mathbf{E}(t) = \mathrm{Re}\!\left[\mathbf{E}_{0}\ e^{j\omega t}\right]$ and a complex polarizability $K(\omega) = K'(\omega) - jK''(\omega)$:

$$\mathbf{F}(t) = 4\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ K(\omega)\ \nabla \vert \mathbf{E}_{0}\cos(\omega t) \vert^{2}$$

Compute $\vert \mathbf{E}_{0}\cos(\omega t) \vert^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert \mathbf{E}_{0}\vert^{2}\!\left[1 + \cos(2\omega t)\right]$. The spatial gradient acts only on the field, not on time:

$$\mathbf{F}(t) = 4\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ K(\omega)\ \tfrac{1}{2}\nabla\vert \mathbf{E}_{0}\vert^{2}\!\left[1 + \cos(2\omega t)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Integrate over one period $T = 2\pi/\omega$.** The $\cos(2\omega t)$ term averages to zero. The constant term survives:

$$\langle \mathbf{F}_{DEP} \rangle = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \mathbf{F}(t)\ dt = 4\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ K(\omega)\ \tfrac{1}{2}\nabla\vert \mathbf{E}_{0}\vert^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Take the real part (since only the in-phase component does net work).** The force is purely real, so:

$$\boxed{\ \langle \mathbf{F}_{DEP} \rangle = 2\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ \mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]\ \nabla \vert \mathbf{E}_{rms} \vert^{2}\ }$$

where $\mathbf{E}_{rms} = \mathbf{E}_{0}/\sqrt{2}$.

**Step 7 — Verify units for examiner credit.**

$$\left[\varepsilon_{m}\right] \cdot \left[r^{3}\right] \cdot \left[\nabla \vert E \vert^{2}\right] = \frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{m}} \cdot \mathrm{m^{3}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{V^{2}}}{\mathrm{m^{3}}} = \frac{\mathrm{C/V}}{\mathrm{m}} \cdot \mathrm{V^{2}}/\mathrm{m} = \mathrm{N}\ \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Derivation III — Cross-Over Frequency for a Homogeneous Sphere

The cross-over frequency $f_{xo}$ is the frequency at which $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)] = 0$. Setting the real part of the numerator to zero (denominator is never zero for positive permittivities):

$$\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)] = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mathrm{Re}\!\left[\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*}\right] \cdot \mathrm{Re}\!\left[\varepsilon_{p}^{*} + 2\varepsilon_{m}^{*}\right] + \mathrm{Im}[\cdots]\cdot\mathrm{Im}[\cdots] = 0$$

Substituting $\varepsilon^{*} = \varepsilon - j\sigma/\omega$ and simplifying yields:

$$(\sigma_{p} - \sigma_{m})(\sigma_{p} + 2\sigma_{m}) + \omega^{2}(\varepsilon_{p} - \varepsilon_{m})(\varepsilon_{p} + 2\varepsilon_{m}) = 0$$

Solving for $\omega$:

$$\omega_{xo} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_{m} - \sigma_{p})(\sigma_{p} + 2\sigma_{m})}{(\varepsilon_{p} - \varepsilon_{m})(\varepsilon_{p} + 2\varepsilon_{m})}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\ f_{xo} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_{m} - 2\sigma_{p})(\sigma_{p} + 2\sigma_{m})}{(\varepsilon_{p} - 2\varepsilon_{m})(\varepsilon_{p} + 2\varepsilon_{m})}}\ }$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The cross-over frequency is a function of *both* conductivity and permittivity contrasts. Examiners often give a single medium conductivity and expect students to compute $f_{xo}$ — practice this derivation until fluent.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Example for KTU Board Exam Practice:** A latex microsphere of radius $r = 5\ \mu m$, $\varepsilon_{p} = 2.5\varepsilon_{0}$, $\sigma_{p} = 1\ mS/m$ is suspended in a medium with $\varepsilon_{m} = 80\varepsilon_{0}$, $\sigma_{m} = 0.01\ S/m$. The applied field has $\nabla\vert E_{rms}\vert^{2} = 10^{13}\ V^{2}/m^{3}$ at $f = 100\ kHz$. Compute $\langle F_{DEP} \rangle$.

**Step 1 — Compute the complex permittivities.** With $\omega = 2\pi \times 10^{5}\ rad/s$:

$$\varepsilon_{p}^{*} = 2.5\varepsilon_{0} - j\dfrac{\sigma_{p}}{\omega} = (2.5)(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) - j\dfrac{10^{-3}}{2\pi \times 10^{5}}$$

$$\varepsilon_{p}^{*} = 2.2135 \times 10^{-11} - j\ 1.5915 \times 10^{-9}\ F/m$$

For the medium:

$$\varepsilon_{m}^{*} = 80\varepsilon_{0} - j\dfrac{\sigma_{m}}{\omega} = (80)(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) - j\dfrac{10^{-2}}{2\pi \times 10^{5}}$$

$$\varepsilon_{m}^{*} = 7.0832 \times 10^{-10} - j\ 1.5915 \times 10^{-8}\ F/m$$

**Step 2 — Compute the CM factor.**

$$K(\omega) = \frac{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*}}{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} + 2\varepsilon_{m}^{*}}$$

Numerator: $\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*} = (2.2135 \times 10^{-11} - 7.0832 \times 10^{-10}) - j(1.5915 \times 10^{-9} - 1.5915 \times 10^{-8})$

$$\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*} = -6.862 \times 10^{-10} - j\ (-1.4324 \times 10^{-8}) = -6.862 \times 10^{-10} + j\ 1.4324 \times 10^{-8}$$

Denominator: $\varepsilon_{p}^{*} + 2\varepsilon_{m}^{*} = 2.2135 \times 10^{-11} + 1.4166 \times 10^{-9} - j(1.5915 \times 10^{-9} + 3.1831 \times 10^{-8})$

$$\varepsilon_{p}^{*} + 2\varepsilon_{m}^{*} = 1.4387 \times 10^{-9} - j\ 3.3423 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator.**

$$\vert \text{denom}\vert^{2} = (1.4387 \times 10^{-9})^{2} + (3.3423 \times 10^{-8})^{2} = 2.0699 \times 10^{-18} + 1.1171 \times 10^{-15} \approx 1.1191 \times 10^{-15}$$

Numerator $\times$ denom$^{*}$:

$$\text{Num}\cdot\text{Denom}^{*} = (-6.862 \times 10^{-10} + j1.4324 \times 10^{-8})(1.4387 \times 10^{-9} + j3.3423 \times 10^{-8})$$

Real part: $(-6.862 \times 10^{-10})(1.4387 \times 10^{-9}) - (1.4324 \times 10^{-8})(3.3423 \times 10^{-8})$
$$= -9.872 \times 10^{-19} - 4.788 \times 10^{-16} = -4.798 \times 10^{-16}$$

Imaginary part: $(-6.862 \times 10^{-10})(3.3423 \times 10^{-8}) + (1.4324 \times 10^{-8})(1.4387 \times 10^{-9})$
$$= -2.293 \times 10^{-17} + 2.061 \times 10^{-17} = -2.32 \times 10^{-18}$$

**Step 4 — Divide.**

$$K(\omega) = \frac{-4.798 \times 10^{-16} - j2.32 \times 10^{-18}}{1.1191 \times 10^{-15}} = -0.4287 - j\ 0.00207$$

Therefore $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)] \approx -0.429$, indicating **negative DEP** at 100 kHz (repulsion from high-field regions).

**Step 5 — Compute the DEP force.**

$$\langle F_{DEP} \rangle = 2\pi (7.0832 \times 10^{-10})(5 \times 10^{-6})^{3}(-0.429)(10^{13})$$

$$= 2\pi \times 7.0832 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-16} \times (-0.429) \times 10^{13}$$

$$= 2\pi \times 7.0832 \times 1.25 \times (-0.429) \times 10^{-13}$$

$$= 2\pi \times (-3.799) \times 10^{-13}$$

$$\boxed{\ \langle F_{DEP} \rangle \approx -2.39 \times 10^{-12}\ \mathrm{N}\ }$$

The negative sign confirms **negative DEP** (the particle is pushed away from the high-field electrode edges).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete functional architecture** of a Dielectrophoresis system — from the signal generator through the electrode geometry to the final particle-response output. This is a **block-level functional architecture flow** (in lieu of a complex physical cross-section), optimized for clarity in KTU examination answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SIG["AC SIGNAL GENERATOR"] --> AMP["HIGH VOLTAGE AMPLIFIER 10X to 1000X"]
    AMP --> ELEC["MICROELECTRODE ARRAY PLANAR OR 3D"]
    ELEC --> CHAN["MICROFLUIDIC CHANNEL CONTAINING PARTICLE SUSPENSION"]
    CHAN --> FIELD["NON-UNIFORM ELECTRIC FIELD GRADIENT E FIELD MAGNITUDE"]
    FIELD --> DIP["INDUCED DIPOLE MOMENT IN PARTICLE"]
    DIP --> PDEP{"RE K OMEGA GREATER THAN ZERO"}
    PDEP -- YES --> ATTR["POSITIVE DEP PARTICLE ATTRACTED TO ELECTRODE EDGE"]
    PDEP -- NO --> NDEP["NEGATIVE DEP PARTICLE REPELLED TO FIELD MINIMUM"]
    ATTR --> OUT1["OUTPUT PARTICLE TRAPPING OR COLLECTION"]
    NDEP --> OUT2["OUTPUT PARTICLE LEVITATION OR REPULSION"]
    CHAN --> FB["FEEDBACK TEMPERATURE PH CONDUCTIVITY SENSOR"]
    FB --> CTRL["CONTROL UNIT FREQUENCY AND AMPLITUDE TUNING"]
    CTRL --> SIG
    FIELD --> JOULE["MONITOR JOULE HEATING AND ELECTROCHEMICAL REACTIONS"]
    JOULE --> CTRL
    style SIG fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style AMP fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style ELEC fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style CHAN fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style FIELD fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style DIP fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style PDEP fill:#854d0e,color:#ffffff
    style ATTR fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style NDEP fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff
    style OUT1 fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style OUT2 fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff
    style FB fill:#404040,color:#ffffff
    style CTRL fill:#404040,color:#ffffff
    style JOULE fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.1 Annotated Explanation of the Flow

| Block | Function | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **AC Signal Generator** | Produces sinusoidal voltage from $1\ kHz$ to $100\ MHz$ | Frequency determines $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]$ sign |
| **HV Amplifier** | Boosts $V_{pp}$ to $10$–$100\ V$ to generate strong gradients | Required because $\nabla \vert E \vert^{2} \propto V^{2}$ |
| **Microelectrode Array** | Interdigitated, castellated, or dot-patterned Au/Pt/ITO | Geometry *defines* the field non-uniformity |
| **Microfluidic Channel** | Hosts the particle suspension in low-conductivity buffer | Channel material must be electrically inert (PDMS, glass) |
| **Non-Uniform E Field** | $\nabla \vert E_{rms} \vert^{2}$ strong at electrode edges | Physical origin of the DEP force |
| **Induced Dipole** | Polarization charges align with local $\mathbf{E}$ | Intermediate physical step |
| **Decision Node** | Sign of $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]$ | Determines positive vs. negative DEP |
| **Feedback Loop** | Monitors $T$, pH, $\sigma_{m}$ | Prevents Joule heating drift |
| **Joule Heating** | $P = \sigma_{m} \vert E \vert^{2} V$ — must be $< 1\ K$ rise | Hard limit on usable voltage |

## 4.2 Typical DEP Electrode Geometries (Schematic)

| Geometry | Field Profile | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Interdigitated (IDE)** | Periodic $\nabla \vert E \vert^{2}$ along channel | Continuous flow separation |
| **Quadrupole (polynomial)** | $\nabla \vert E \vert^{2} \propto r^{3}$ null at center | Single-cell levitation/DEP trap |
| **Castellated (comb)** | High field at sharp teeth | Strong pDEP collection |
| **Dot-matrix (insulator-based iDEP)** | Non-uniformity from insulating posts | High-throughput, low-voltage |
| **Traveling-wave (twDEP)** | Phase-shifted electrodes generate a traveling field | Continuous particle transport |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define Dielectrophoresis. How does it differ fundamentally from Electrophoresis?

**Model Answer:**

Dielectrophoresis (DEP) is the motion of a polarizable particle (charged or uncharged) induced by a **spatially non-uniform** electric field. The force arises from the interaction between the field gradient and the **induced dipole moment** of the particle, given by $\langle \mathbf{F}_{DEP} \rangle = 2\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ \mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]\ \nabla \vert \mathbf{E}_{rms} \vert^{2}$.

**Fundamental differences from Electrophoresis:**

| Feature | Electrophoresis | Dielectrophoresis |
|---|---|---|
| Force type | Coulomb force, $F = q\mathbf{E}$ | Dipole-gradient force, $F = (\mathbf{p}\cdot\nabla)\mathbf{E}$ |
| Field requirement | Uniform is sufficient | **Non-uniform is essential** |
| Particle charge | Must carry net charge | Can be **electrically neutral** |
| Medium requirement | Electrolyte buffer | Low-conductivity medium preferred |
| AC vs DC | DC or low-frequency AC | Usually **AC** (kHz–MHz) |
| Direction | Along $\mathbf{E}$ (fixed) | Toward/away from **field extrema** (sign-tunable via $\omega$) |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for tabular comparison)*

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** What is the Clausius-Mossotti factor? Explain its physical significance and the significance of its sign.

**Model Answer:**

The **Clausius-Mossotti (CM) factor** is a frequency-dependent, complex-valued, dimensionless number that quantifies the **effective polarizability** of a spherical dielectric particle suspended in a medium, in response to an applied AC electric field. It is defined as:

$$K(\omega) = \frac{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*}}{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} + 2\varepsilon_{m}^{*}}$$

**Physical significance:**
- It is a **measure of the dielectric contrast** between the particle ($p$) and the surrounding medium ($m$).
- It encapsulates the **frequency-dependent** dielectric and conductive response of both materials through the complex permittivity $\varepsilon^{*} = \varepsilon - j\sigma/\omega$.
- It governs **both the magnitude and direction** of the DEP force.

**Significance of the sign of $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]$:**
- $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)] > 0$ → **Positive DEP (pDEP)** — particle is more polarizable than the medium; it is **attracted** toward regions of high field intensity (electrode edges).
- $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)] < 0$ → **Negative DEP (nDEP)** — particle is less polarizable than the medium; it is **repelled** toward field minima.
- $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)] = 0$ → **Cross-over condition** — the DEP force vanishes at a specific frequency $f_{xo}$.

The theoretical bounds are $-0.5 \leq \mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)] \leq +1.0$ for a homogeneous sphere, but multi-shell biological cells can transiently exceed $1.0$ due to interfacial (Maxwell-Wagner) polarization.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition/formula, 1 mark for physical significance, 1 mark for sign discussion)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — Option (A)  **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`**

**(a)** Starting from the expression for the induced dipole moment of a dielectric sphere in a non-uniform electric field, **derive the time-averaged DEP force equation** in its full form, clearly stating all assumptions. Mention the physical significance of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a biological cell (modeled as a single-shell sphere with cytoplasm permittivity $\varepsilon_{cyto} = 60\varepsilon_{0}$, cytoplasm conductivity $\sigma_{cyto} = 0.5\ S/m$, membrane thickness $d = 5\ nm$, and effective membrane permittivity $\varepsilon_{mem} = 10\varepsilon_{0}$, membrane conductivity $\sigma_{mem} = 10^{-7}\ S/m$) suspended in a medium of $\varepsilon_{m} = 80\varepsilon_{0}$, $\sigma_{m} = 0.05\ S/m$, compute the cross-over frequency $f_{xo}$ for a cell of effective radius $r = 8\ \mu m$. Comment on the practical implications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Derivation

**Assumptions:**
1. Particle is a homogeneous isotropic dielectric sphere of radius $r$.
2. Medium is linear, isotropic, and homogeneous.
3. Quasi-static approximation: $\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = 0$.
4. Dipole approximation valid: $r \ll$ characteristic length scale of field variation.
5. Single-frequency sinusoidal AC field.

**Step 1:** Induced dipole moment in complex notation:
$$\mathbf{p} = 4\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ K(\omega)\ \mathbf{E}$$

**Step 2:** Force on dipole in non-uniform field:
$$\mathbf{F} = (\mathbf{p} \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{E} = \nabla(\mathbf{p} \cdot \mathbf{E})\ \text{(since } \nabla \times \mathbf{p} = 0 \text{)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:
$$\mathbf{F} = 4\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ K(\omega)\ \nabla \vert \mathbf{E} \vert^{2}$$

**Step 4:** AC time-average over one period $T = 2\pi/\omega$:
$$\langle \mathbf{F} \rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 4\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ \mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]\ \nabla \vert \mathbf{E}_{0} \vert^{2}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to RMS:
$$\langle \mathbf{F} \rangle = 2\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ \mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]\ \nabla \vert \mathbf{E}_{rms} \vert^{2}$$

**Physical significance of each term:**

| Term | Significance |
|---|---|
| $r^{3}$ | Force scales as **cube of particle radius** — DEP preferentially acts on larger particles |
| $\varepsilon_{m}$ | Polarizability of the suspending medium |
| $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]$ | Sign and magnitude of dielectric contrast — determines pDEP/nDEP |
| $\nabla \vert \mathbf{E}_{rms} \vert^{2}$ | **Field gradient** — DEP is fundamentally a gradient force |

*(7 marks: 3 for derivation steps, 2 for assumptions, 2 for term significance)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — Cross-over Frequency Computation

For a **single-shell (shelled) sphere**, the effective complex permittivity is:

$$\varepsilon_{p,eff}^{*} = \varepsilon_{mem}^{*}\ \frac{(r/(r-d))^{3} + 2(\varepsilon_{cyto}^{*}/\varepsilon_{mem}^{*})}{(r/(r-d))^{3} - (\varepsilon_{cyto}^{*}/\varepsilon_{mem}^{*})}$$

**Step 1 — Compute geometry factor.**

With $r = 8\ \mu m$, $d = 5\ nm$:

$$r/(r-d) = 8/(8 - 0.005) = 8/7.995 = 1.000625$$

$$(r/(r-d))^{3} = 1.001876$$

**Step 2 — Complex permittivities at the relevant frequency** (let us solve for $f_{xo}$ by setting the imaginary part of the CM factor's numerator and denominator appropriately; we apply the simplified homogeneous-sphere analogue using the effective permittivity):

At low frequency, the membrane dominates; at high frequency, the cytoplasm dominates. The cross-over satisfies:

$$\sigma_{m} - 2\sigma_{p,eff} = 0\quad \text{and}\quad \varepsilon_{p,eff} - 2\varepsilon_{m} = 0\ \text{(at the cross-over)}$$

Equating the conduction condition first:

$$\sigma_{p,eff} = \sigma_{m}/2 = 0.025\ S/m$$

This is achieved by the **Maxwell-Wagner interfacial polarization** of the cell membrane, where the effective conductivity transitions between the membrane-limited low-frequency value and the cytoplasm-dominated high-frequency value. The cross-over frequency for a shelled cell is given by:

$$f_{xo} \approx \frac{1}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{\sigma_{mem}}{2\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{mem}}\cdot\frac{1}{d/r}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\frac{\sigma_{mem}}{2\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{mem}} = \frac{10^{-7}}{2 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 10} = \frac{10^{-7}}{1.7708 \times 10^{-10}} = 564.7\ rad/s$$

$$\frac{d}{r} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-9}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

Therefore:

$$f_{xo} \approx \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{564.7}{6.25 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 9.035 \times 10^{5}\ Hz$$

$$\boxed{\ f_{xo} \approx 143.8\ kHz\ }$$

**Step 4 — Practical implications.**
- Operating below $f_{xo}$ produces **nDEP** — the cell is repelled from electrodes (useful for contactless cell positioning).
- Operating above $f_{xo}$ produces **pDEP** — the cell is attracted and trapped at electrode edges.
- The cell can be **selectively isolated** from background debris by tuning frequency near $f_{xo}$.
- $f_{xo} \sim 144\ kHz$ is in the experimentally convenient range for BioMEMS DEP devices.
- Variations in $f_{xo}$ between healthy and diseased cells (e.g., cancerous vs. normal leukocytes) form the basis of **DEP-based cancer diagnostics**.

*(7 marks: 2 for shell model setup, 2 for numerical substitution, 2 for final computation, 1 for practical implications)*

---

### Question B — Option (B)  **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, explain the working of a **Dielectrophoretic Field-Flow Fractionation (DEP-FFF)** system. Discuss the role of the gravitational/flow lift force and the DEP force in achieving separation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microfluidic DEP device uses interdigitated electrodes with a gap $g = 20\ \mu m$ and applied RMS voltage $V_{rms} = 10\ V$. Estimate the DEP force on a $5\ \mu m$ radius latex particle ($\varepsilon_{p} = 2.5\varepsilon_{0}$, $\sigma_{p} = 0.01\ S/m$) in a medium of $\varepsilon_{m} = 80\varepsilon_{0}$, $\sigma_{m} = 0.001\ S/m$ at a frequency of $1\ MHz$. State the direction of the force. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — DEP-FFF

**Working principle:**

In **DEP-Field-Flow Fractionation**, particles are injected into a thin ribbon-shaped microchannel (typical thickness $\sim 200\ \mu m$) whose floor or ceiling carries an array of microelectrodes. A **laminar parabolic flow** profile (Poiseuille flow) carries the particles along the channel. Simultaneously, a DEP force drives particles **perpendicular** to the flow — toward the electrode surface (pDEP) or away from it (nDEP).

**Force balance perpendicular to flow:**

The particles reach an **equilibrium height** $h_{eq}$ where the DEP force is balanced by the **gravitational/buoyancy force** and the **hydrodynamic lift force**:

$$\mathbf{F}_{DEP}(h_{eq}) + \mathbf{F}_{grav}(h_{eq}) + \mathbf{F}_{lift}(h_{eq}) = 0$$

**Role of each force:**

| Force | Origin | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| DEP force | Dielectric contrast in non-uniform E field | Pushes particle toward or away from electrode (sign of $\mathrm{Re}[K]$) |
| Gravity/buoyancy | $\Delta \rho \cdot g$ | Settles particle based on density |
| Hydrodynamic lift | Shear-gradient + wall effects | Repels particle from walls |
| Drag (parallel to flow) | Stokes drag at low Re | Carries particle downstream |

**Separation mechanism:** Different particle types equilibrate at **different heights** $h_{eq}$ because their $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]$ values differ. Since the parabolic flow velocity is **strongly height-dependent** (maximum at the center, zero at the walls), particles at different heights elute at **different times** — producing separation at the channel outlet.

**Schematic:**

```
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  Inlet  → [Poiseuille Flow] →  Outlet  │
   │  ════════════════════════════════════  │  ← Top wall
   │  ──────── h_eq (small particle) ──────  │
   │  ───────── h_eq (large particle) ──────  │
   │  ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ Electrode Array ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │  ← Bottom wall
   └────────────────────────────────────────┘
   DEP force ────► (perpendicular to flow)
```

*(7 marks: 2 for schematic, 3 for working principle and force balance, 2 for separation mechanism)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical Estimation

**Given:**
- Electrode gap: $g = 20\ \mu m = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ m$
- $V_{rms} = 10\ V$
- Particle radius: $r = 5\ \mu m = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ m$
- $\varepsilon_{p} = 2.5\varepsilon_{0} = 2.2135 \times 10^{-11}\ F/m$
- $\sigma_{p} = 0.01\ S/m$
- $\varepsilon_{m} = 80\varepsilon_{0} = 7.0832 \times 10^{-10}\ F/m$
- $\sigma_{m} = 0.001\ S/m$
- $f = 1\ MHz$ → $\omega = 2\pi \times 10^{6} = 6.283 \times 10^{6}\ rad/s$

**Step 1 — Compute complex permittivities.**

$$\varepsilon_{p}^{*} = 2.2135 \times 10^{-11} - j\dfrac{0.01}{6.283 \times 10^{6}} = 2.2135 \times 10^{-11} - j\ 1.5915 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\varepsilon_{m}^{*} = 7.0832 \times 10^{-10} - j\dfrac{0.001}{6.283 \times 10^{6}} = 7.0832 \times 10^{-10} - j\ 1.5915 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 2 — Clausius-Mossotti factor.**

$$K(\omega) = \frac{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*}}{\varepsilon_{p}^{*} + 2\varepsilon_{m}^{*}}$$

Numerator:
$$\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*} = (2.2135 \times 10^{-11} - 7.0832 \times 10^{-10}) - j(1.5915 \times 10^{-9} - 1.5915 \times 10^{-10})$$
$$= -6.862 \times 10^{-10} - j\ 1.4324 \times 10^{-9}$$

Denominator:
$$\varepsilon_{p}^{*} + 2\varepsilon_{m}^{*} = (2.2135 \times 10^{-11} + 1.4166 \times 10^{-9}) - j(1.5915 \times 10^{-9} + 3.183 \times 10^{-10})$$
$$= 1.4387 \times 10^{-9} - j\ 1.9098 \times 10^{-9}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by complex conjugate of denominator:

$$\vert \text{denom}\vert^{2} = (1.4387 \times 10^{-9})^{2} + (1.9098 \times 10^{-9})^{2} = 2.0699 \times 10^{-18} + 3.6473 \times 10^{-18} = 5.7172 \times 10^{-18}$$

Real part of numerator $\times$ denom$^{*}$:
$$(-6.862 \times 10^{-10})(1.4387 \times 10^{-9}) - (-1.4324 \times 10^{-9})(-1.9098 \times 10^{-9})$$
$$= -9.872 \times 10^{-19} - 2.736 \times 10^{-18} = -3.723 \times 10^{-18}$$

$$\mathrm{Re}[K] = \frac{-3.723 \times 10^{-18}}{5.7172 \times 10^{-18}} = -0.651$$

**Step 3 — Compute field gradient.**

The approximate field magnitude at the electrode edge is $E_{rms} \approx V_{rms}/g = 10/2 \times 10^{-5} = 5 \times 10^{5}\ V/m$.

The gradient over a length scale of order $g$ is:

$$\nabla \vert E_{rms} \vert^{2} \approx \frac{E_{rms}^{2}}{g} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{5})^{2}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{11}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.25 \times 10^{16}\ V^{2}/m^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Compute DEP force.**

$$\langle F_{DEP} \rangle = 2\pi (7.0832 \times 10^{-10})(5 \times 10^{-6})^{3}(-0.651)(1.25 \times 10^{16})$$

$$= 2\pi \times 7.0832 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-16} \times (-0.651) \times 1.25 \times 10^{16}$$

$$= 2\pi \times 7.0832 \times 1.25 \times (-0.651) \times 1.25 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$= 2\pi \times (-7.205) \times 10^{-10}$$

$$\boxed{\ \langle F_{DEP} \rangle \approx -4.53 \times 10^{-9}\ \mathrm{N}\ }$$

**Step 5 — Direction.** The negative sign indicates **negative DEP (nDEP)** — the latex particle is **repelled** from the high-field electrode edges and pushed toward the field minimum (mid-channel at this frequency).

*(7 marks: 2 for CM factor setup, 2 for gradient estimation, 2 for force computation, 1 for direction)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
> 1. **Missing the factor of 2 vs. 1/2 confusion:** A common error is to write the time-averaged DEP force as $4\pi\varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\mathrm{Re}[K]\nabla \vert E \vert^{2}$ instead of the correct $2\pi\varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\mathrm{Re}[K]\nabla \vert E_{rms} \vert^{2}$. Both factors of 1/2 (from time-averaging) and the conversion $E_{rms} = E_{0}/\sqrt{2}$ must be tracked carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting the real part operator:** Many students write $K(\omega)$ instead of $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]$. The complex CM factor has both real and imaginary parts — only the real part contributes to steady-state DEP translation. The imaginary part contributes to **DEP torque** (particle rotation), not translation.
> 3. **Sign confusion in pDEP vs. nDEP:** Always tie the sign of $\mathrm{Re}[K]$ to the dielectric contrast: $\varepsilon_{p}^{*} > \varepsilon_{m}^{*}$ does **not** automatically mean pDEP — the *frequency-dependent* behavior can flip the sign.
> 4. **Units mismatch in the field gradient:** The gradient is of $E_{rms}^{2}$, not of $E_{rms}$. Writing $\nabla E$ instead of $\nabla \vert E \vert^{2}$ is a frequent error worth 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Single-shell vs. homogeneous sphere confusion:** For biological cells, examiners expect recognition of the **membrane-dominated low-frequency** regime and the **cytoplasm-dominated high-frequency** regime. Forgetting the shelled-sphere correction yields an $f_{xo}$ that is off by orders of magnitude.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- **DEP definition** — Motion of polarizable particles in a *non-uniform* electric field.
- **Master equation** — $\langle \mathbf{F}_{DEP} \rangle = 2\pi \varepsilon_{m} r^{3}\ \mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]\ \nabla \vert \mathbf{E}_{rms} \vert^{2}$.
- **CM factor** — $K(\omega) = (\varepsilon_{p}^{*} - \varepsilon_{m}^{*})/(\varepsilon_{p}^{*} + 2\varepsilon_{m}^{*})$.
- **Complex permittivity** — $\varepsilon^{*} = \varepsilon - j\sigma/\omega$.
- **pDEP** — $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)] > 0$, particle moves to high-field region.
- **nDEP** — $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)] < 0$, particle moves to low-field region.
- **Cross-over frequency** — Frequency at which $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)] = 0$; key for cell-type separation.
- **Force scales as $r^{3}$** — DEP is inherently biased toward larger particles.
- **Force scales as $\nabla \vert E \vert^{2}$** — Stronger field gradient = stronger DEP; hence the importance of sharp electrode tips.
- **AC operation** — Avoids electrolysis, Joule heating, electrochemical fouling.
- **Frequency range** — Typically $1\ kHz$ to $100\ MHz$ in BioMEMS DEP.
- **Biological cells need shelled-sphere model** — Membrane + cytoplasm; cross-over frequency in 100 kHz – 10 MHz range.
- **DEP-FFF** — Combines DEP with parabolic Poiseuille flow for continuous-flow separation.
- **Applications** — CTC isolation, pathogen detection, single-cell manipulation, drug screening, particle sorting.
- **Distinguish DEP from EP, EOF, and magnetophoresis** — DEP is field-gradient-based; EP is field-magnitude-based on charged species; EOF is double-layer-based; magnetophoresis requires magnetic labels.
- **Joule heating limit** — Temperature rise must remain $< 1\ K$ to preserve cell viability; restrict $\sigma_{m} \vert E \vert^{2} \leq 10^{6}\ W/m^{3}$.
- **Electrode materials** — Au, Pt, ITO (transparent for imaging), doped-Si; must be bio-compatible and chemically inert.
- **Channel materials** — PDMS, glass, PMMA, COC; must be electrically insulating and optically transparent.

**Exam Tip:** Always state the *sign* of $\mathrm{Re}[K(\omega)]$ in your final answer and explicitly identify whether the particle experiences pDEP or nDEP — this single line often earns 1–2 easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microfluid Dispenser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Microfluid Dispenser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Microfluidic Dispenser** is a microfabricated device engineered to deliver, eject, or release precisely metered volumes of fluid — typically in the **picoliter (10⁻¹² L) to microliter (10⁻⁶ L) range** — into a micro-scale environment such as a microchannel, microwell, reaction chamber, or open-air droplet array. In the context of BioMEMS, microfluidic dispensers form the **actuation interface** between the macro-scale laboratory and the micro-scale analytical world, enabling controlled delivery of reagents, drugs, DNA, proteins, or biological cells with sub-nanoliter precision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** A microfluidic dispenser is fundamentally a *fluidic energy converter* — it transforms an input energy form (electrical, thermal, mechanical, acoustic, or electrostatic) into controlled kinetic energy of a fluid parcel, which is then pinched off to form a free droplet or metered plug.

The three functional objectives of any microfluidic dispenser are:
1. **Metering** — delivering a precise, reproducible volume per actuation.
2. **Directionality** — ejecting fluid toward a defined target (well, channel, surface).
3. **Throughput** — performing the above at high frequency (Hz to kHz range) without cross-contamination.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **inkjet printer head**. Inside that tiny nozzle, a precisely timed electrical pulse vaporizes a microscopic heater, creating a vapor bubble that violently expels a droplet of ink onto paper. A microfluidic dispenser works on the **exact same principle** but for biological and chemical fluids.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Medicine Dropper vs. The Micro-Dispenser:**
> A traditional glass dropper delivers drops of ~50 µL governed only by gravity. A microfluidic dispenser, by contrast, can deliver **drops ~1,000 to 1,000,000 times smaller**, with the drop volume actively *commanded* by a digital signal rather than passively by surface tension. Think of a dropper, but with each drop's size being typed on a keyboard.

A second analogy is the **vending machine for cells**: instead of dispensing a candy, the device dispenses a single cell or a single reagent droplet at a known location, on demand, with the press of an electronic button.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Quantitative Anchors

| Term | Definition | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Drop-on-Demand (DOD)** | Droplet ejected only when a trigger is applied | Single event per pulse |
| **Continuous Jet** | Jet is constantly emitted, droplets formed by instability | kHz natural breakup |
| **Dead Volume** | Fluid trapped inside dispenser that cannot be dispensed | < 1 µL (goal) |
| **Droplet Volume (V_d)** | Volume of one ejected parcel | 1 pL – 1 µL |
| **Dispensing Frequency (f)** | Droplets ejected per second | 1 Hz – 30 kHz |
| **Nozzle Diameter (d_n)** | Aperture through which fluid exits | 5 – 100 µm |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Memory Hook:** The four physical "Pillars" of every microfluidic dispenser are **PRESSURE** (drives the fluid), **PULSE** (times the release), **PINCH-OFF** (surface tension breaks the jet), and **PLACEMENT** (the drop lands where needed). Every dispenser design optimizes at least one of these.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Microfluidic Dispensers

Microfluidic dispensers are classified by the **energy transduction mechanism** used to generate the ejection pulse. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes the four most common categories.

### 2.1.1 Thermal (Bubble-Jet) Dispenser
- A micro-heater (typically a thin-film resistor of **Pt or TiN**) abruptly raises the temperature of the adjacent fluid above its **nucleation threshold (~300 °C)**.
- A vapor bubble nucleates and expands in **< 10 µs**, displacing the fluid in the nozzle.
- When the heater cools, the bubble collapses; the resulting pressure transient ejects a droplet.
- **Energy per pulse:** ~ 1 – 10 µJ.
- **Limitation:** Heat exposure may damage biological samples (proteins, DNA).

### 2.1.2 Piezoelectric Dispenser
- A **piezoelectric actuator** (PZT, lead zirconate titanate) bonded to the dispenser chamber wall deforms under an applied voltage.
- The deformation produces a rapid volumetric change in the fluid chamber, generating a pressure wave that ejects a droplet.
- **Advantage:** No heat — fully bio-compatible.
- **Bandwidth:** Up to **30 kHz**.

### 2.1.3 Electrohydrodynamic (EHD) Dispenser
- A strong **electric field** (10⁶ – 10⁷ V/m) applied between the nozzle and a counter-electrode polarizes the fluid meniscus.
- Tangential electric stresses destabilize the meniscus, forming a fine **Taylor cone** that emits a charged micro-jet.
- Droplet diameter can be **smaller than the nozzle aperture** — a unique feature called "electrospray."
- **Use:** Mass spectrometry, nano-deposition.

### 2.1.4 Acoustic / SAW (Surface Acoustic Wave) Dispenser
- **Interdigitated transducers (IDTs)** on a piezoelectric substrate (LiNbO₃) generate surface acoustic waves.
- The acoustic wave couples into the fluid at the IDT-fluid interface, producing **acoustic streaming** and a localized pressure pulse.
- Droplets are ejected at right angles to the chip surface ("atomizer-on-a-chip").

### 2.1.5 Solenoid / Micro-Solenoid Dispenser
- A miniature electromagnetic coil drives a plunger or deforms a diaphragm.
- Slower than piezo, but capable of dispensing **higher viscosity fluids** (up to 10–100 mPa·s).

### 2.1.6 Other Variants
- **Focused Acoustic** (ultrasonic horn): industrial-scale, ~µL volumes.
- **Capillary / Positive-Displacement** (syringe pump at micro-scale): continuous, no droplet pinch-off.
- **Valve-based** (Quake-style elastomeric valves): pulse a micro-channel.

## 2.2 The Operational Logic — A Step-Wise Description

The dispensing event — whether thermal, piezo, or acoustic — follows a universal five-stage sequence:

1. **Quiescence (t = 0):** The fluid meniscus is held at the nozzle tip by capillary forces. The Laplace pressure inside the meniscus is $\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2\gamma}{R}$, balancing the hydrostatic back-pressure.
2. **Trigger (t = t₁):** An external energy pulse is applied to the actuator. The fluid chamber experiences a sudden volumetric change ΔV.
3. **Acceleration (t₁ < t < t₂):** The fluid column is accelerated toward the nozzle exit. A pressure wave of amplitude **P_peak** (often 1–10 bar) propagates down the nozzle.
4. **Pinch-off (t ≈ t₂):** Surface tension cannot sustain the elongating liquid thread; it necks down to a radius of ~ µm and breaks. A free droplet is born.
5. **Refill (t > t₂):** Capillary action and/or refill pressure re-supply fluid to the nozzle from the reservoir, readying the next cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Pinch-off:** Even though the bulk fluid was driven out by pressure, the drop *separates* from the nozzle because of the **Rayleigh–Plateau instability** — surface tension minimizes the total interfacial area by breaking an extended liquid column into discrete spherical droplets (since a sphere has minimum surface for a given volume).

## 2.3 Governing Physics — Why These Formulas Matter

The droplet is the *result* of a balance between three pressures:
- **Inertial pressure** (the impulse delivered by the actuator): $P_{inertia} = \rho v^2$
- **Capillary pressure** (surface tension resisting deformation): $P_{cap} = \frac{2\gamma}{R}$
- **Viscous pressure** (viscous dissipation in the nozzle): $P_{visc} \sim \frac{\mu v L}{d_n^2}$

The droplet successfully ejects only when inertial pressure **dominates** capillary pressure — quantified by the **Weber number**:

$$We = \frac{\rho v^2 d_n}{\gamma} \gg 1 \quad (\text{ejection criterion})$$

The relative importance of viscous to surface tension effects is captured by the **Capillary number**:

$$Ca = \frac{\mu v}{\gamma}$$

And the relative importance of gravity to surface tension (often negligible in microfluidics) is the **Bond number**:

$$Bo = \frac{\rho g L^2}{\gamma}$$

The flow regime inside the nozzle is characterized by the **Reynolds number** $Re = \frac{\rho v d_n}{\mu}$, which is typically < 100 in microfluidic nozzles — firmly **laminar**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **must-memorize** formula bank for any KTU 2024 question on Microfluidic Dispensers. Units are explicitly listed because KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for missing units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Droplet Volume (sphere) | $V_d = \frac{4}{3}\pi r_d^{3}$ | $r_d$ = droplet radius | m³ |
| 2 | Capillary / Laplace Pressure | $P_{cap} = \frac{2\gamma}{R}$ | $\gamma$ = surface tension, $R$ = meniscus radius | Pa |
| 3 | Hagen–Poiseuille Flow (nozzle) | $Q = \frac{\pi d_n^{4} \Delta P}{128 \mu L}$ | $d_n$ = nozzle dia, $L$ = length, $\mu$ = viscosity | m³/s |
| 4 | Hagen–Poiseuille Resistance | $R_h = \frac{128 \mu L}{\pi d_n^{4}}$ | Hydraulic resistance | Pa·s/m³ |
| 5 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \frac{\rho v d_n}{\mu}$ | Inertia vs. viscous | dimensionless |
| 6 | Weber Number | $We = \frac{\rho v^{2} d_n}{\gamma}$ | Inertia vs. surface tension | dimensionless |
| 7 | Capillary Number | $Ca = \frac{\mu v}{\gamma}$ | Viscous vs. surface tension | dimensionless |
| 8 | Bond Number | $Bo = \frac{\rho g L^{2}}{\gamma}$ | Gravity vs. surface tension | dimensionless |
| 9 | Bubble Volume (thermal) | $V_b \approx \frac{Q_{heat} \cdot t_{pulse}}{\rho_v h_{fg}}$ | $Q_{heat}$ = heater power, $h_{fg}$ = latent heat | m³ |
| 10 | Piezo Volume Displacement | $\Delta V = d_{33} \cdot V_{elec} \cdot A$ | $d_{33}$ = piezo coeff., $A$ = diaphragm area | m³ |
| 11 | EHD Taylor Cone Angle | $\tan \alpha = \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2}}$ | $\epsilon_r$ = relative permittivity | rad |
| 12 | Actuation Frequency Limit | $f_{max} \approx \frac{v_{jet}}{2 r_d}$ | Rayleigh jet breakup limit | Hz |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Microfluidic dispensers are the workhorses of:
- **Drug discovery:** combinatorial dispensing of compounds into 1536-well plates (V_d ≈ 1 nL).
- **Diagnostics:** PCR microfluidic chips (sample and reagent loading).
- **Tissue engineering:** controlled deposition of cell-laden bioinks for 3D bioprinting.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** inkjet-printed pills with precise dosage gradients.
- **Analytical chemistry:** matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI) plate spotting.
- **Display manufacturing:** OLED and quantum-dot pixel printing.

> [!TIP]
> **Connection to other modules:** The dispenser relies on **capillary action** (covered in Module 2 on Microfluidics) and **piezoelectricity / thermal actuation** (foundational to Module 4 on Actuators). Be prepared for an integrated question in the ESE.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Droplet Volume From Nozzle Geometry (for a Saturated Pinch-off)

**Statement:** When a dispenser ejects a single droplet of radius $r_d$ and the droplet is assumed to detach cleanly without satellite formation, derive the droplet volume and the total fluid displaced per N actuations.

### Step 1: Identify the geometry of the free droplet.
A free droplet in the absence of external fields adopts a **spherical shape** to minimize surface energy (Gibbs, 1878). The volume of a sphere of radius $r_d$ is:

$$V_d = \frac{4}{3} \pi r_d^{3}$$

*Logic:* The dispenser pushes out enough fluid to fill a sphere whose radius is governed by the balance of momentum and surface tension at the instant of pinch-off.

### Step 2: Relate the droplet radius to the nozzle diameter.
Empirically, for low-viscosity fluids and We ≈ 5–20, the droplet diameter $D_d$ is approximately **1.5 to 2 times the nozzle diameter** $d_n$ (a relationship from Fromm's classical inkjet analysis, 1984):

$$D_d = k \cdot d_n, \quad k \approx 1.5 - 2.0$$

Therefore, $r_d = \frac{k d_n}{2}$.

### Step 3: Substitute into the sphere volume equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \frac{4}{3} \pi \left(\frac{k d_n}{2}\right)^{3} \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{4}{3} \pi \cdot \frac{k^{3} d_n^{3}}{8} \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{\pi \, k^{3}}{6} \, d_n^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: For N consecutive drops, total displaced volume.

$$
V_{total} = N \cdot V_d = N \cdot \frac{\pi k^{3}}{6} \, d_n^{3}
$$

### Step 5: Numerical example.
For a nozzle with $d_n = 30 \,\mu\text{m}$ and $k = 1.8$:

$$
V_d = \frac{\pi (1.8)^{3}}{6} (30 \times 10^{-6})^{3}
$$

$$
V_d = \frac{\pi \cdot 5.832}{6} \cdot 2.7 \times 10^{-14}
$$

$$
V_d = 3.054 \cdot 2.7 \times 10^{-14} \approx 8.25 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m}^{3} = 82.5 \text{ pL}
$$

*Engineering interpretation:* A 30-µm nozzle, a typical choice for cell printing, dispenses ~ 82 pL per drop — roughly **600× smaller** than the volume of a single human red blood cell-laden droplet could practically encapsulate, illustrating why BioMEMS dispensers are ideal for single-cell assays.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hagen–Poiseuille Flow in the Nozzle

**Statement:** A microfluidic dispenser is fed by a reservoir pressurized at $P_0$. Derive the volumetric flow rate $Q$ through a cylindrical nozzle of length $L$ and diameter $d_n$, neglecting entrance effects.

### Step 1: Assume steady, incompressible, laminar, Newtonian flow.
This assumption is justified because $Re \ll 100$ in typical nozzles (validated by Bond number, Capillary number analysis from Section 2.3). The Navier–Stokes equations reduce to:

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = \mu \left( \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} \right) \right)$$

### Step 2: Integrate once with respect to r.
Using the no-slip condition at the wall ($v_z = 0$ at $r = d_n/2$) and finite velocity on axis:

$$v_z(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu} \left( -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \right) \left[ \left(\frac{d_n}{2}\right)^{2} - r^{2} \right]$$

### Step 3: Integrate over the cross-section to obtain flow rate.

$$
Q = \int_0^{d_n/2} v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{2\mu} \left( -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \right) \int_0^{d_n/2} \left[ \left(\frac{d_n}{2}\right)^{2} - r^{2} \right] r \, dr
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the integral.

$$
\int_0^{d_n/2} \left[ \left(\frac{d_n}{2}\right)^{2} r - r^{3} \right] dr = \left[ \left(\frac{d_n}{2}\right)^{2} \frac{r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_0^{d_n/2}
$$

$$
= \left(\frac{d_n}{2}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{d_n}{2}\right)^{2} - \frac{1}{4} \left(\frac{d_n}{2}\right)^{4} = \frac{d_n^{4}}{64}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute back.

$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{2\mu} \left( -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \right) \cdot \frac{d_n^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \, d_n^{4}}{128 \mu} \left( -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \right)
$$

### Step 6: For a constant pressure gradient along the nozzle length $L$, $\left(-\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{P_0 - P_{atm}}{L}$:

$$
\boxed{\,Q = \frac{\pi d_n^{4}}{128 \mu L} \left( P_0 - P_{atm} \right)\,}
$$

### Step 7: Rearrange to a resistor-like form, $Q = \dfrac{\Delta P}{R_h}$:

$$R_h = \frac{128 \mu L}{\pi d_n^{4}}$$

*Engineering interpretation:* The flow rate has a **fourth-power dependence** on nozzle diameter. Halving the nozzle diameter reduces flow by 16× — a critical scaling law that explains why high-precision pL-volume dispensers need fine process control.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Weber Number Condition for Successful Ejection

**Statement:** Derive the minimum velocity $v_{min}$ required to eject a droplet from a circular nozzle of diameter $d_n$, given a fluid of density $\rho$ and surface tension $\gamma$.

### Step 1: Define the Weber number.
The Weber number compares inertial stress ($\rho v^2$) to capillary stress ($\gamma / L$):

$$We = \frac{\rho v^{2} d_n}{\gamma}$$

### Step 2: State the ejection criterion.
Empirically, ejection requires $We \ge We_{crit} \approx 4$ for DOD dispensers. Set $We = 4$:

$$\frac{\rho v_{min}^{2} d_n}{\gamma} = 4$$

### Step 3: Solve for $v_{min}$.

$$
v_{min}^{2} = \frac{4 \gamma}{\rho d_n}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v_{min} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{\rho d_n}}\,}
$$

### Step 4: Numerical example for water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\gamma = 0.072$ N/m) with $d_n = 30 \,\mu\text{m}$:

$$
v_{min} = 2 \sqrt{ \frac{0.072}{1000 \cdot 30 \times 10^{-6}} } = 2 \sqrt{2.4} \approx 3.1 \text{ m/s}
$$

*Engineering interpretation:* This minimum velocity must be supplied by the actuator. A thermal bubble pulse producing ~ 1 MPa peak pressure at the nozzle inlet can accelerate water to ~ 10 m/s — comfortably above the threshold.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Thermal Dispenser Energy Balance (Bubble Volume)

**Statement:** A thermal dispenser's micro-heater delivers $Q_{heat} = 50$ mW of power for a pulse of duration $t_{pulse} = 5 \,\mu\text{s}$. Estimate the bubble volume if the latent heat of vaporization of the fluid is $h_{fg} = 2.26 \times 10^{6}$ J/kg and the vapor density is $\rho_v = 0.6$ kg/m³.

### Step 1: Compute the energy input.

$$
E_{pulse} = Q_{heat} \cdot t_{pulse} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J} = 250 \text{ nJ}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the mass of fluid vaporized.

$$
m_v = \frac{E_{pulse}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{2.26 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-13} \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the bubble volume from vapor density.

$$
V_b = \frac{m_v}{\rho_v} = \frac{1.1 \times 10^{-13}}{0.6} \approx 1.83 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^{3} = 183 \text{ pL}
$$

*Engineering interpretation:* The vaporized fluid mass is ~ 0.1 nanograms, yet the mechanical impulse from bubble expansion ejects a droplet of comparable volume (~ 100 pL) — the conversion of latent heat to mechanical work is highly efficient (typically 0.1–1 % thermal-to-kinetic efficiency).

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Drop-on-Demand (DOD) Microfluidic Dispenser

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional signal-and-fluid pathway** of a generic DOD dispenser, abstracting the physical hardware into logical blocks. This is the standard representation KTU examiners expect for "explain the working of a microfluidic dispenser" type questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[CONTROL SUBSYSTEM]
        ctrl[Microcontroller FPGA]
        drv[Driver Amplifier Circuit]
        trig[Trigger Pulse Generator]
    end

    subgraph B[ACTUATION SUBSYSTEM]
        act[Actuator Thermal Piezo EHD Acoustic]
        ch[Pressurized Fluid Chamber]
        nzl[Micro Nozzle d_n 5 to 100 um]
    end

    subgraph C[FLUID DELIVERY]
        res[Reservoir]
        ch2[Refill Channel]
        drop[Free Droplet in Flight]
        tgt[Target Well or Substrate]
    end

    subgraph D[FEEDBACK SUBSYSTEM]
        mon[Drop Watch Camera or Optical Sensor]
        fb[Feedback Signal]
    end

    ctrl -->|Digital Command| drv
    drv -->|Conditioned Waveform| trig
    trig -->|Energy Pulse| act
    res -->|Capillary Feed| ch
    act -->|Mechanical Strain or Heat| ch2
    ch2 -->|Pressure Wave| nzl
    nzl -->|Jet Ejection| drop
    drop -->|Ballistic Flight| tgt
    tgt -.->|Image Capture| mon
    mon -->|Velocity and Volume Data| fb
    fb -.->|Closed Loop Correction| ctrl
```

The following Mermaid diagram abstracts the **physical cross-section** of a single thermal nozzle. This is the architecture studied in 95 % of KTU textbook figures.
