topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Micro Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3: Micro Pumps

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> A **Micro Pump** is a micro-scale fluidic actuator (MEMS device) capable of generating a controlled, metered flow of fluid — typically in the range of **µL/min to nL/min** — by converting an input energy form (electrical, mechanical, chemical, or surface-based) into hydraulic work (pressure × volume) within a confined microchannel or chamber network.

In the strict KTU BioMEMS context, micro pumps are the **"fluidic heart"** of any Lab-on-Chip (LoC), Point-of-Care (PoC), or implantable drug delivery system. They are defined as devices where at least one critical dimension (chamber, membrane, or channel width) is in the **sub-millimeter regime**, i.e., typically between **1 µm and 1000 µm**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Syringe Pump**
> Imagine a doctor slowly pushing the plunger of a syringe to deliver insulin over several hours. The plunger movement converts the mechanical force of the doctor's thumb into a precise fluid flow. A **micro pump does the exact same job, but on a chip.**
> - The "plunger" is replaced by a vibrating piezoelectric disc, a thermal bubble, or even an electric field.
> - The "syringe barrel" is a micro-machined chamber etched into silicon/glass/PDMS.
> - The "fluid" is blood, reagent, buffer, or drug — often only a few microliters.

So in simple English: **A micro pump is a tiny machine-on-a-chip that pushes microscopic amounts of liquid exactly where and when you want, without any human hand touching it.**

### 1.3 Why Micro Pumps Matter (KTU High-Yield Context)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> KTU Module 3 emphasizes that **all biomedical micro-systems ultimately need controlled fluid motion.** Without a pump:
> - A glucose sensor cannot draw blood continuously.
> - A DNA analyzer cannot transport reagents.
> - An implantable drug delivery device cannot dose medication.

Micro pumps are therefore **non-negotiable functional blocks** in BioMEMS.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

The performance of any micro pump is evaluated using the following **standardized metrics**:

- **Flow Rate (Q):** Volume delivered per unit time, typically **µL/min to mL/min** for medical micro pumps.
- **Back Pressure (ΔP):** Maximum pressure the pump can overcome, typically **kPa to tens of kPa** (sometimes up to MPa for high-pressure systems).
- **Power Consumption (P):** Electrical input, typically **mW range** for battery-operated implants.
- **Response Time:** Time to reach steady flow from zero, often **milliseconds to seconds.**
- **Self-Priming Capability:** Ability to start flow against an air gap (critical for dry/dry-loaded systems).
- **Dead Volume:** Unrecoverable fluid retained inside the pump — for BioMEMS this **must be < 1 µL.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Micro Pumps (Most Critical Topic for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Module 3 Topic:** "Classify micro pumps with examples."
> Students must memorize the table below.

The most authoritative KTU/Stanford BioMEMS classification splits micro pumps into **two super-categories** based on whether moving mechanical parts exist.

### 2.1.1 Category A — Mechanical / Actuated (Reciprocating/Displacement) Pumps

These pumps have a **moving solid boundary** (a diaphragm, membrane, or piston) that displaces fluid.

| Sub-Type | Actuation Principle | Key Feature | Typical Q | Typical ΔP |
|----------|--------------------|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Piezoelectric** | Inverse piezo: voltage → disc deflection | High frequency (kHz), high precision | 10 µL/min – 1 mL/min | Up to ~100 kPa |
| **Electrostatic** | Coulomb force between charged plates | Low power, low stroke | < 10 µL/min | Low (< 10 kPa) |
| **Electromagnetic** | Lorentz force on a coil/magnet | High force, larger form factor | mL/min range | Tens of kPa |
| **Thermopneumatic** | Heating fluid → expansion → bubble | No moving solid, but uses gas | 10 – 100 µL/min | Up to ~50 kPa |
| **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** | Thermal phase transition of NiTi | Very large stroke, slow | µL/min | High |
| **Bimetallic / Thermal Expansion** | Differential expansion of two metals | Simple fabrication | Low | Low |

### 2.1.2 Category B — Non-Mechanical / Dynamic (Continuous) Pumps

These pumps have **no moving solid boundary**; fluid is driven by body forces or surface effects acting on the fluid itself.

| Sub-Type | Driving Principle | Key Feature | Typical Q |
|----------|-------------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Electroosmotic (EO)** | Tangential electric field drags diffuse double layer | No moving parts, needs high voltage | nL/min – µL/min |
| **Electrowetting-on-Dielectric (EWOD)** | Modifying surface tension by voltage | Droplet-based digital microfluidics | Discrete droplets |
| **Electrohydrodynamic (EHD)** | Body force on ions in non-uniform E-field | Works with insulating fluids | Very low |
| **Capillary / Surface Tension** | Spontaneous wetting in hydrophilic channels | Passive, no power required | Passive only |
| **Acoustic / SAW** | Surface acoustic waves push droplets | Contactless, high frequency | pL – nL/min |
| **Centrifugal** | Rotational inertia on a CD-like disc | Parallel processing, single use | µL – mL/min |
| **Magnetohydrodynamic (MHD)** | Lorentz force on ionic fluid | No moving parts, needs strong B-field | nL – µL/min |

### 2.2 Reciprocating vs Continuous: The KTU-Mandated Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:**
> - **Reciprocating (Displacement) pumps** have a **cyclic check-valve** that converts alternating diaphragm motion into a net DC flow. Examples: piezoelectric, thermopneumatic.
> - **Continuous (Dynamic) pumps** generate a **steady body force** on the fluid. Examples: EO, MHD, EHD.
> Reciprocating pumps can build **high back pressure** but produce **pulsatile flow**; continuous pumps deliver **smooth (pulse-free) flow** but struggle against high ΔP.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **must-memorize formula set** for the KTU ESE on this topic. Use $\vert$ instead of the vertical bar to keep markdown tables valid.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Variables |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta P}{R_h}$ | Ohm's law analogue for microfluidics (Q is flow, ΔP is pressure drop, $R_h$ is hydraulic resistance) | $Q$: m³/s, $\Delta P$: Pa, $R_h$: Pa·s/m³ |
| 2 | $R_h = \dfrac{8 \mu L}{\pi r^{4}}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille hydraulic resistance of a **circular** microchannel | $\mu$: Pa·s, $L$: m, $r$: m |
| 3 | $R_h = \dfrac{12 \mu L}{w h^{3}} \left[1 - \dfrac{192 h}{\pi^{5} w} \sum_{n=1,3,5}^{\infty} \dfrac{\tanh(n \pi w / 2h)}{n^{5}} \right]$ | Hydraulic resistance of a **rectangular** microchannel (approximated as $12\mu L / (w h^{3})$ for $w \gg h$) | $w$: width, $h$: height |
| 4 | $\Delta V = A_{d} \cdot d_{0}$ | Displacement volume of a reciprocating pump per stroke | $A_{d}$ = diaphragm area, $d_{0}$ = center deflection |
| 5 | $d_{0} = \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot d_{31} \cdot \dfrac{L^{2}}{h_{p}^{2}} \cdot V$ | Center deflection of a **piezoelectric diaphragm** (edge-clamped, circular) | $d_{31}$: piezo strain coefficient, $L$: radius, $h_{p}$: piezo thickness, $V$: applied voltage |
| 6 | $Q_{avg} = f \cdot \Delta V$ | Average flow rate of a reciprocating pump at frequency $f$ | $f$: Hz |
| 7 | $v_{EO} = \mu_{EO} \cdot E$ | Electroosmotic flow velocity | $\mu_{EO}$ = electroosmotic mobility, $E$ = electric field |
| 8 | $\mu_{EO} = \dfrac{\varepsilon \zeta}{\eta}$ | Helmholtz–Smoluchowski electroosmotic mobility | $\varepsilon$: permittivity, $\zeta$: zeta potential, $\eta$: viscosity |
| 9 | $P_{bubble} = P_{0} + \dfrac{2 \sigma}{r_{b}}$ | Young–Laplace pressure inside a thermopneumatic bubble | $\sigma$: surface tension, $r_{b}$: bubble radius |
| 10 | $\Delta P_{max} = \dfrac{E \cdot h \cdot d_{0}^{3}}{L^{4}}$ | Max back-pressure capability of a circular diaphragm (for $h \ll d_{0}$) | $E$: Young's modulus, $h$: diaphragm thickness |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Implantable insulin pumps** use **piezoelectric micro pumps** because they offer precise dosing at low power.
- **Lab-on-Chip DNA sequencers** use **electroosmotic pumps** because the flow is pulse-free and electronically programmable.
- **Disposable COVID/antigen lateral-flow devices** use **capillary pumps** (passive) because they require zero external power — ideal for resource-limited settings.
- **Centrifugal microfluidic discs (lab-on-a-CD)** exploit rotational inertia to process many samples in parallel.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are **scaffolded line-by-line** for KTU ESE answer-writing. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hagen–Poiseuille Flow Rate (Foundation Equation)

This is the most fundamental equation in all of BioMEMS microfluidics, and **KTU expects the full derivation.**

**Starting Assumptions:**
- Steady, laminar, incompressible Newtonian flow.
- Long, straight, rigid cylindrical channel of radius $r$ and length $L$.
- No-slip at the wall.
- Pressure-driven (Poiseuille) flow.

**Step 1 — Set up the Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates.**
For an axisymmetric, fully-developed flow in the $z$-direction, the only non-zero velocity is $v_z(r)$, and the Navier–Stokes equation reduces to the Hagen–Poiseuille ODE:

$$
\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dv_{z}}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate once with respect to $r$.**

$$
r \frac{dv_{z}}{dr} = \frac{1}{\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}\cdot \frac{r^{2}}{2} + C_{1}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $r$ and integrate a second time.**

$$
v_{z}(r) = \frac{1}{4\eta}\frac{dP}{dz} r^{2} + C_{1} \ln(r) + C_{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions.**
By symmetry at the centerline, the velocity must be finite, so $C_{1} = 0$. By the no-slip condition at the wall, $v_{z}(r = R) = 0$:

$$
0 = \frac{1}{4\eta}\frac{dP}{dz} R^{2} + C_{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_{2} = -\frac{R^{2}}{4\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}
$$

**Step 5 — Write the parabolic velocity profile.**

$$
v_{z}(r) = -\frac{1}{4\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the volumetric flow rate by integrating over the cross-section.**

$$
Q = \int_{0}^{R} v_{z}(r)\, 2\pi r\, dr
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute and integrate.**

$$
Q = -\frac{2\pi}{4\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}\int_{0}^{R} r\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right)dr
$$

$$
Q = -\frac{\pi}{2\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}\left[\frac{R^{2}r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{R}
$$

$$
Q = -\frac{\pi}{2\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}\left(\frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4}\right)
$$

$$
Q = -\frac{\pi R^{4}}{8\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}
$$

**Step 8 — Identify the pressure drop.**
Since $\frac{dP}{dz} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ (pressure drops along flow direction), we get:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \eta L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Hagen–Poiseuille Equation:**
> $$\boxed{\,Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \eta L}\,}$$
> The **$R^{4}$ dependence** is the most physically significant aspect — halving the channel radius reduces flow by a factor of 16. This is the dominant design rule in BioMEMS.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydraulic Resistance and the Microfluidic Ohm's Law

Starting from the Hagen–Poiseuille result, define hydraulic resistance analogously to electrical resistance:

$$
R_{h} = \frac{\Delta P}{Q}
$$

Substituting the result of Derivation 1:

$$
R_{h} = \frac{8 \eta L}{\pi R^{4}}
$$

Therefore, the **microfluidic Ohm's law** is:

$$
\boxed{\,Q = \dfrac{\Delta P}{R_{h}}\,}
$$

For a **series network** of $N$ channel segments (typical of branched BioMEMS chips), resistances add:

$$
R_{h,total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_{h,i}
$$

For a **parallel network**:

$$
\frac{1}{R_{h,total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{R_{h,i}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters (KTU context):** The fluidic-network analogy allows the **same Kirchhoff laws** used in electrical circuits to solve complex BioMEMS channel networks — a favorite problem in KTU exams.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Flow Rate of a Reciprocating Piezo Pump

**Setup:** A circular piezoelectric diaphragm of radius $a$ and thickness $h_p$ is bonded to a pump chamber. When a voltage $V$ is applied, the diaphragm bows outward by a center deflection $d_0$. Each stroke displaces a fluid volume $\Delta V$ and the pump cycles at frequency $f$.

**Step 1 — Center deflection of an edge-clamped circular piezo disc.**
From classical plate theory, for a piezoelectric disc in the $d_{31}$ mode, the static center deflection is:

$$
d_{0} = \frac{3}{4} d_{31} \frac{a^{2}}{h_{p}^{2}} V
$$

**Step 2 — Displaced volume per stroke.**
The diaphragm bows into a roughly spherical cap. For small deflections ($d_0 \ll a$), the displaced volume is well approximated as:

$$
\Delta V \approx \frac{2}{3} \pi a^{2} d_{0}
$$

**Step 3 — Average flow rate.**
The pump completes $f$ strokes per second; assuming perfect check-valve rectification, the average flow is:

$$
Q_{avg} = f \cdot \Delta V
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{avg} = \frac{2}{3} \pi a^{2} d_{0} f = \frac{\pi a^{4} d_{31} f}{2 h_{p}^{2}} V\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum sustainable back-pressure.**
For a thin elastic diaphragm of Young's modulus $E$ and thickness $h$, clamped at radius $a$ and deflected by $d_0$, the elastic restoring pressure is:

$$
\Delta P_{max} = \frac{E h\, d_{0}^{3}}{a^{4}}
$$

Setting this equal to the work done per stroke gives the maximum back-pressure the pump can overcome — the **head curve** of a micro pump.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight:**
> Increasing $d_0$ (higher voltage) raises BOTH $Q$ and $\Delta P_{max}$ — but $\Delta P_{max} \propto d_0^{3}$ while $Q \propto d_0$, so a stiffer, more deflected diaphragm is doubly beneficial.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The two diagrams below are Mermaid-rendered functional schematics. The second adapts an otherwise complex physical drawing (valve timing in a peristaltic pump) into a clear block-level functional architecture.

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Classification of Micro Pumps (Full KTU Taxonomy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Micro Pumps]
    R --> A[Mechanical Reciprocating Pumps]
    R --> B[Non Mechanical Continuous Pumps]

    A --> A1[Piezoelectric]
    A --> A2[Electrostatic]
    A --> A3[Electromagnetic]
    A --> A4[Thermopneumatic]
    A --> A5[Shape Memory Alloy SMA]
    A --> A6[Bimetallic Thermal]

    B --> B1[Electroosmotic EO]
    B --> B2[Electrowetting EWOD]
    B --> B3[Electrohydrodynamic EHD]
    B --> B4[Capillary Surface Tension]
    B --> B5[Acoustic SAW]
    B --> B6[Centrifugal]
    B --> B7[Magnetohydrodynamic MHD]

    A1 --> A1note[High freq kHz, high precision]
    A4 --> A4note[Uses heated bubble expansion]
    B1 --> B1note[Driven by zeta potential]
    B6 --> B6note[Lab on a CD platform]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Peristaltic Micro Pump

This block diagram represents the **three-phase peristaltic actuation** that is otherwise difficult to draw physically.

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[Fluid Inlet] --> CV1[Check Valve 1 Inlet]
    CV1 --> CH1[Chamber 1 with PZT Actuator]
    CH1 --> CH2[Chamber 2 with PZT Actuator]
    CH2 --> CH3[Chamber 3 with PZT Actuator]
    CH3 --> CV2[Check Valve 2 Outlet]
    CV2 --> OUT[Fluid Outlet]

    CTRL[Sequential Controller Phase Generator] --> CH1
    CTRL --> CH2
    CTRL --> CH3

    CTRL --> P1[Phase 1 Chamber 1 expands and draws fluid in]
    CTRL --> P2[Phase 2 Chamber 2 expands and transfers fluid forward]
    CTRL --> P3[Phase 3 Chamber 3 expands and ejects fluid out]
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU answer tip):** Three piezo chambers are fired in **phase-shifted sequence** so that a "pumping wave" travels from inlet to outlet — mimicking peristalsis in the human intestine. This produces **valveless, pulse-free flow** and is widely asked about in KTU ESE.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Operating Principle of a Thermopneumatic Pump

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Electrical Heater ON] --> S2[Step 2 Working Fluid in sealed chamber boils and bubble expands]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Diaphragm deflects upward and pushes process fluid out through check valve]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Heater OFF and bubble collapses by cooling]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Diaphragm returns and draws fresh process fluid in through inlet check valve]
    S5 --> S1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a micro pump and list any four essential performance parameters used to evaluate it.**

**Model Answer (Expected 3-Mark Key):**
A micro pump is a MEMS-based fluidic actuator that delivers controlled volumes of fluid — typically in the microliter to nanoliter range — within micro-scale channels, for applications such as drug delivery, lab-on-chip diagnostics, and biomedical implants.

Four essential performance parameters:
1. **Flow rate (Q)** — volume per unit time, usually in µL/min.
2. **Back pressure (ΔP)** — maximum pressure the pump can overcome.
3. **Power consumption (P)** — electrical input, typically in mW.
4. **Response time** — time taken to reach steady-state flow.

(Other acceptable: dead volume, self-priming capability, biocompatibility, pulsatility.)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between reciprocating and continuous micro pumps. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (Expected 3-Mark Key):**

| Aspect | Reciprocating (Displacement) | Continuous (Dynamic) |
|--------|------------------------------|----------------------|
| Mechanism | Moving solid boundary (diaphragm) | Steady body force on fluid |
| Flow nature | Pulsatile | Pulse-free |
| Back pressure capability | High | Low to moderate |
| Example | Piezoelectric diaphragm pump | Electroosmotic pump |
| Moving parts | Yes | Generally no |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Classify micro pumps with suitable examples. Discuss the working principle of a piezoelectric micro pump with a neat diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for flow through a cylindrical microchannel and explain the significance of the $R^{4}$ dependence. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Classification of Micro Pumps (7 marks)**

Refer to the hierarchical classification from Section 4.1 of these notes. The student is expected to draw a clear **two-broad-category** tree:

- **Mechanical / Reciprocating pumps** — piezoelectric, electrostatic, electromagnetic, thermopneumatic, SMA, bimetallic.
- **Non-mechanical / Continuous pumps** — electroosmotic, EWOD, EHD, capillary, SAW, centrifugal, MHD.

**Working Principle of a Piezoelectric Micro Pump (5–6 marks of the 7):**

A piezoelectric disc is bonded onto a thin silicon/glass diaphragm that forms one wall of a micro-machined pump chamber. When an AC voltage is applied, the piezo disc undergoes inverse piezoelectric effect and bends the diaphragm. The diaphragm alternates between **upward (suction)** and **downward (compression)** strokes. Two passive check valves (inlet and outlet) rectify this alternating motion into a net DC flow. The diaphragm typically operates at 1 Hz to 10 kHz.

**Diagram (essential, 2 marks):** A simple cross-section showing — inlet channel → inlet check valve → pump chamber with piezo disc on diaphragm → outlet check valve → outlet channel.

**(b) Hagen–Poiseuille Derivation (7 marks)**

Full derivation as shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The examiner's key expects:
- Starting Navier–Stokes reduction (1 mark)
- Two integrations (2 marks)
- Application of boundary conditions (2 marks)
- Final equation $Q = \pi R^{4} \Delta P / (8 \eta L)$ (1 mark)
- **Significance of $R^4$** (1 mark): Because flow scales with the **fourth power of radius**, microchannels are extremely sensitive to size — a 50% reduction in radius causes a 94% drop in flow. This is why microchannels must be carefully designed and why **electroosmotic / capillary** drives are often preferred at the sub-100 µm scale, where pressure-driven viscous resistance becomes overwhelming.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of an **electroosmotic micro pump**. Derive the expression for electroosmotic mobility using the Helmholtz–Smoluchowski model. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A micro channel of length 5 cm and circular cross-section of radius 100 µm carries water ($\eta = 1$ mPa·s). A pressure difference of 10 kPa is applied. Calculate the volumetric flow rate and the hydraulic resistance. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Electroosmotic Micro Pump (7 marks)**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
In a microchannel with charged walls (typically glass → negatively charged silanol groups), a thin layer of counter-ions (e.g., $Na^+$) forms the **electrical double layer (EDL)** adjacent to the wall. When a high voltage (kV range) is applied across the electrodes at the inlet and outlet, the tangential electric field exerts a Coulomb force on the net charge in the EDL. This force drags the entire fluid column along via viscous coupling, producing **flat (plug-like) flow** — completely unlike the parabolic Poiseuille profile.

**Sketch (essential, 2 marks):** Inlet reservoir (+ electrode) → micro channel with negative wall and positive diffuse layer → Outlet reservoir (− electrode), with $E$ field arrow and velocity profile.

**Derivation of Electroosmotic Mobility (2 marks):**
At steady state, the electric body force on the EDL is balanced by viscous shear:

$$
\rho_{e} E = \eta \frac{d^{2} v}{dy^{2}}
$$

Integrating the linearized Poisson–Boltzmann equation (Debye–Hückel) twice and matching the zeta potential at the wall yields the **Helmholtz–Smoluchowski equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,\mu_{EO} = \dfrac{\varepsilon \zeta}{\eta}\,}
$$

and the **plug-flow velocity:**

$$
\boxed{\,v_{EO} = \mu_{EO} E = \dfrac{\varepsilon \zeta E}{\eta}\,}
$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

Given:
- $L = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m
- $R = 100$ µm $= 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $\eta = 1$ mPa·s $= 1 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $\Delta P = 10$ kPa $= 1 \times 10^{4}$ Pa

**Step 1 — Compute the hydraulic resistance.**

$$
R_{h} = \frac{8 \eta L}{\pi R^{4}}
$$

$$
R_{h} = \frac{8 \times (1\times 10^{-3}) \times 0.05}{\pi \times (1\times 10^{-4})^{4}}
$$

$$
R_{h} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times 10^{-16}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{12}}{\pi}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{h} \approx 1.273 \times 10^{12} \ \text{Pa·s/m}^{3}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the flow rate.**

$$
Q = \frac{\Delta P}{R_{h}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{4}}{1.273 \times 10^{12}}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 7.85 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{m}^{3}\text{/s} \ \approx 7.85 \ \mu\text{L/s}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to practical units.**

$$
Q \approx 7.85 \ \mu\text{L/s} \times 60 \approx 471 \ \mu\text{L/min}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit trap:** Forgetting to convert 5 cm → 0.05 m and 100 µm → $10^{-4}$ m is the **#1 reason students lose marks.** Always write the SI conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Formula confusion:** Many students write $Q = \pi R^{4}\Delta P / (8 \eta L)$ and accidentally use $D$ (diameter) instead of $R$ (radius). The $R^4$ term is the most error-prone; verify it carefully.
> 3. **Missing Ohm's-law step:** $Q = \Delta P / R_h$ is a **two-step problem** — compute $R_h$ first, then $Q$. Skipping $R_h$ is acceptable but mixing units is not.
> 4. **For Part (a):** Marks are split roughly 3 (working) + 2 (sketch) + 2 (derivation). A missing sketch typically costs **2 marks outright** in KTU ESE.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Print this checklist and revise the night before the KTU ESE.

### Core Concepts
- [ ] Micro pump = MEMS device delivering **µL/min to nL/min** flow.
- [ ] **Two super-categories:** Mechanical (reciprocating) and Non-mechanical (continuous).
- [ ] Reciprocating pumps = **pulsatile, high back pressure**; continuous pumps = **pulse-free, low back pressure.**
- [ ] **Piezoelectric** is the most widely used actuation mechanism for implantable and drug-delivery micro pumps.
- [ ] **Electroosmotic pumps** generate a flat velocity profile — ideal for capillary electrophoresis and lab-on-chip.

### Must-Memorize Equations
- [ ] Hagen–Poiseuille: $Q = \pi R^{4} \Delta P / (8 \eta L)$
- [ ] Hydraulic resistance: $R_h = 8 \eta L / (\pi R^{4})$
- [ ] Microfluidic Ohm's law: $Q = \Delta P / R_h$
- [ ] Piezo deflection: $d_0 = (3/4)\, d_{31}\, a^{2}\, V / h_{p}^{2}$
- [ ] Reciprocating pump flow: $Q_{avg} = f \cdot \Delta V$
- [ ] Helmholtz–Smoluchowski EO mobility: $\mu_{EO} = \varepsilon \zeta / \eta$

### Critical Physical Insights
- [ ] The $R^{4}$ dependence means **microchannels are 10⁶× more resistive** than millimeter-scale tubes of equivalent length.
- [ ] EO flow is **flat (plug)** because the driving force acts on the EDL, which is then transferred by viscosity to the entire cross-section.
- [ ] Thermopneumatic pumps use a **sealed working fluid** (often Freon or FC-72) — the process fluid is pushed by a flexible diaphragm, so **no contamination.**
- [ ] Capillary (passive) pumps are **the only ones with zero power consumption** — perfect for disposable diagnostics.
- [ ] Centrifugal pumps scale with $\omega^{2} r$ — hence the **lab-on-a-CD** platform.

### Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid
- [ ] Don't confuse **dynamic viscosity $\eta$ (Pa·s)** with **kinematic viscosity $\nu$ (m²/s)**.
- [ ] Don't use $D$ (diameter) where the formula requires $R$ (radius) — check the **$R^4$ term** explicitly.
- [ ] In the Helmholtz–Smoluchowski derivation, the **zeta potential $\zeta$** is measured in **mV**, not V — keep units consistent.
- [ ] For piezoelectric deflection, $d_{31}$ is **strain per unit field** (C/N) and is **NOT** a distance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Micro Valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Micro Valve** is a microfluidic control element fabricated at the microscale (typical characteristic dimension **< 1 mm**) that regulates the flow rate, direction, pressure, or complete on/off state of fluid (gas or liquid) streams within a microchannel network. In the KTU 2024 BioMEMS framework, micro valves are classified as **functional microfluidic components** that translate an external stimulus (electrical, thermal, pneumatic, magnetic, or chemical) into a mechanical or interfacial response to modulate fluid transport.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A micro valve is *not* merely a scaled-down conventional valve. The physics at the microscale — dominated by **surface tension**, **viscous forces**, and **capillary effects** — fundamentally changes the design equations compared to macro-scale fluidics. The Reynolds number is typically **Re < 100**, making Stokes flow the governing regime.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **garden hose connected to a kitchen tap**. The tap knob is a macro-scale valve: you rotate a mechanical screw-thread that pushes a rubber washer against the pipe, mechanically blocking water. The rotation creates a large displacement force and works on **inertia-dominant** fluid dynamics.

Now shrink the entire system **1,000 times**. The rubber washer becomes a thin flexible membrane (a few microns thick), the screw-thread is replaced by a tiny electrostatic or thermal actuator, and the water no longer "shoots" out — it **creeps** slowly because viscous forces dominate inertia. To stop it, you don't slam a gate; you either:
- **Pinch the channel** like squeezing a straw (mechanical/pneumatic valves),
- **Pull the liquid back** using surface tension (capillary valves), or
- **Repel the fluid** with a hydrophobic surface (hydrophobic burst valves).

This shift from *mechanical force-balance* to *interfacial force-balance* is the defining paradigm of micro valves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Exams:** A **passive valve** uses the fluid's own energy (pressure, surface tension) to operate — no external power needed. An **active valve** requires an external actuator (voltage, current, pressure, heat) to switch states.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded for Visibility)

| Metric | Typical Range in BioMEMS |
|---|---|
| Characteristic length | **1 µm – 1000 µm** |
| Operating pressure | **1 kPa – 100 kPa** |
| Response time | **1 ms – 1 s** |
| Dead volume | **nL – µL** |
| Leakage rate | **µL/min or lower** |
| Reynolds number | **Re < 100 (laminar)** |

## 1.4 Broad Classification Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Master Categories under KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> 1. **Passive Micro Valves** — Capillary valves, Hydrophobic valves, Nozzle-Diffuser valves, Check valves
> 2. **Active Micro Valves** — Pneumatic, Thermopneumatic, Piezoelectric, Electrostatic, Electromagnetic, Shape Memory Alloy (SMA), Bimetallic
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — Structured Logic Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Passive Valves

**(a) Capillary (Burst) Valves**
- **Why it works:** The fluid must overcome the **capillary pressure barrier** at a geometric expansion (sudden widening of the channel).
- **How it works:** As long as the applied pressure P_applied < P_burst, the meniscus is pinned at the geometric step. When P_applied exceeds P_burst, the meniscus suddenly advances — the valve "bursts" open.
- **Key physical law:** Young-Laplace equation at the curved meniscus.

**(b) Hydrophobic Valves**
- **Why it works:** A hydrophobic patch (contact angle θ > 90°) creates an energetic barrier for an aqueous fluid.
- **How it works:** The fluid resists wetting the hydrophobic surface. Sufficient pressure must be applied to force the fluid across the patch.
- **Critical condition:** Burst pressure P_burst = -2γ cos(θ_B) / w for rectangular microchannels.

**(c) Nozzle-Diffuser Valves**
- **Why it works:** Geometric asymmetry — a **nozzle** (converging) and a **diffuser** (diverging) of identical throat dimension produce different pressure drops depending on flow direction.
- **How it works:** Flow from nozzle-to-diffuser is "forward" (low ΔP), flow from diffuser-to-nozzle is "reverse" (high ΔP). This creates a passive diode-like rectification.
- **Diode factor (D):** D = ΔP_reverse / ΔP_forward, ideally D → ∞.

**(d) Check Valves**
- **Why it works:** A movable flap or ball is pushed by the fluid; reverse flow pushes it back into the seat.
- **How it works:** Forward flow lifts the flap; reverse flow pushes the flap against the seat, blocking flow.

### 2.1.2 Active Valves

**(a) Pneumatic Micro Valves (Quake / Push-down style)**
- **Why it works:** A thin elastomeric membrane (PDMS) separates the fluid channel from a pressurized gas channel.
- **How it works:** When gas pressure is applied, the membrane deflects downward, pinching the fluid channel closed.
- **State-of-the-art reference design:** The **Quake valve** (2000, Stanford) — used in nearly all modern PDMS microfluidics.

**(b) Thermopneumatic Valves**
- **Why it works:** Heating a sealed gas (or volatile liquid) increases its pressure.
- **How it works:** A microheater boils a working fluid in a sealed cavity; the expansion deflects a flexible membrane that closes the fluid channel.

**(c) Piezoelectric Valves**
- **Why it works:** Inverse piezoelectric effect — applied voltage → mechanical strain.
- **How it works:** A PZT (lead zirconate titanate) disc elongates by ~0.1% and pushes against a seat to block flow.

**(d) Electrostatic Valves**
- **Why it works:** Coulombic attraction between charged electrodes.
- **How it works:** Applied voltage pulls a flexible membrane toward a fixed electrode, closing the channel. Fast (sub-ms) but requires high voltages (~50–100 V).

**(e) Electromagnetic Valves**
- **Why it works:** Lorentz force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field.
- **How it works:** Current pulse deflects a flexible membrane carrying an embedded coil. Lower voltage but requires integrated coils.

**(f) Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) Valves**
- **Why it works:** Nitinol wires remember their original shape and contract when heated above transition temperature (~70 °C).
- **How it works:** Resistive heating of the wire pulls a membrane or pinches a channel shut.

**(g) Bimetallic Valves**
- **Why it works:** Differential thermal expansion of two bonded metals.
- **How it works:** Heating causes the bimetallic strip to bend, mechanically closing the channel.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Capillary (Laplace) pressure across a curved meniscus | **Pa** |
| 2 | $P_{burst} = -\dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta_B}{w}$ | Burst pressure for a hydrophobic valve in a rectangular channel of width $w$ | **Pa** |
| 3 | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot \pi \cdot r^4}{8 \mu L}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille volumetric flow (circular channel) | **m³/s** |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot w \cdot h^3}{12 \mu L} \cdot \left[1 - \sum_{n,\text{odd}} \dfrac{192}{n^5 \pi^5} \cdot \dfrac{h}{w} \tanh\left(\dfrac{n\pi w}{2h}\right)\right]$ | Volumetric flow in rectangular channel (height $h$, width $w$) — full form | **m³/s** |
| 5 | $P_{pneumatic} = \dfrac{2 E t^3 w}{3 L^3 (1-\nu^2)} \cdot \delta$ | Pressure to deflect an elastomeric membrane of thickness $t$ by displacement $\delta$ (Pister model) | **Pa** |
| 6 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number — flow regime indicator (laminar if < 2300) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Ca = \dfrac{\mu v}{\gamma}$ | Capillary number — ratio of viscous to surface tension forces | dimensionless |
| 8 | $Bo = \dfrac{\rho g D_h^2}{\gamma}$ | Bond number — ratio of gravitational to surface tension forces | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\delta_{piezo} = d_{33} \cdot V$ | Piezoelectric displacement (with $d_{33}$ as charge coefficient) | **m** |
| 10 | $F_{elec} = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{d^2}$ | Electrostatic actuation force on a parallel plate capacitor | **N** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units to Memorize for KTU:** $\gamma$ (surface tension) = **N/m** or **mN/m**; $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity) of water ≈ **1 mPa·s** at 20 °C; $\theta$ (contact angle) of water on PDMS ≈ **110°** (hydrophobic).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drug delivery implants:** SMA and thermopneumatic valves release insulin on demand.
- **Lab-on-a-chip diagnostics:** Quake-style pneumatic valves form peristaltic pumps and multiplexers in genetic analysis chips (e.g., Illumina sequencing library prep).
- **Point-of-care devices:** Capillary valves enable **passive, power-free** fluid sequencing in pregnancy test strips and COVID-19 lateral flow assays.
- **Space bioprobes:** Nozzle-diffuser valves in micropropulsion systems use no moving parts, ideal for vacuum operation.
- **Neural implants / Brain-on-chip:** Electrostatic valves precisely dose neurotransmitter solutions onto cultured neurons.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Capillary Burst Pressure for a Hydrophobic Micro Valve

We derive the **burst pressure** of a hydrophobic valve — the pressure threshold at which a fluid pinned against a hydrophobic patch suddenly advances. This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU exams under Module 3.

### 3.1.1 Step 0 — Setup and Geometry

Consider a rectangular microchannel of **width $w$** and **height $h$**, with a hydrophobic patch of contact angle $\theta_B > 90°$ placed on the channel floor. The fluid is in equilibrium against the patch.

**Coordinate system:** The channel is long along the $x$-axis; the cross-section is in the $y$–$z$ plane. The meniscus curvature is dominated by the **smaller dimension** because Laplace pressure scales as $1/R$ for the principal radius of curvature.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Identify the Governing Physical Law

The pressure difference across a curved liquid-gas interface is given by the **Young-Laplace equation**:

$$
\Delta P = \gamma \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right)
$$

**Logic:** Two principal radii of curvature $R_1$ and $R_2$ act on the meniscus. The product of surface tension and curvature gives the restoring pressure.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Apply Geometric Simplification

For a rectangular channel, one principal radius is set by the **height $h$** (small dimension, tight curvature), and the other is set by the **contact-line geometry**. In the limit $w \gg h$ (wide channel), the height-dominated curvature dominates.

$$
\frac{1}{R_1} \approx \frac{2 \cos\theta_B}{h}, \quad \frac{1}{R_2} \approx 0
$$

**Logic:** When the channel is very wide, the meniscus is essentially cylindrical across the height, so the second radius of curvature (along the width) is much larger and contributes negligibly. The contact angle $\theta_B$ measured through the liquid enters via Young's equation.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Substituting into Young-Laplace

$$
P_{burst} = \gamma \cdot \frac{2 \cos\theta_B}{h}
$$

**Logic:** This is the pressure the fluid must overcome to advance. Since $\theta_B > 90°$ for a hydrophobic surface, $\cos\theta_B$ is **negative**, meaning $P_{burst}$ is **negative** with respect to the gas side. The fluid experiences resistance to wetting.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Magnitude Form for Engineering Use

The **magnitude** of the burst pressure (a positive quantity engineers use) is:

$$
P_{burst} = \frac{2 \gamma \, \vert \cos\theta_B \vert}{h}
$$

**Logic:** Engineering design uses the absolute value. The deeper the hydrophobicity (larger $\theta_B$) or the smaller the channel height, the higher the burst pressure.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Numerical Example for KTU Practice

> Given: $\gamma = 72$ mN/m (water at 20 °C), $\theta_B = 110°$, $h = 50$ µm. Calculate $P_{burst}$.

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$
\cos(110°) = -\cos(70°) = -0.342
$$

$$
P_{burst} = \frac{2 \times 0.072 \, \text{N/m} \times \vert -0.342 \vert}{50 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}}
$$

$$
P_{burst} = \frac{2 \times 0.072 \times 0.342}{50 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
P_{burst} = \frac{0.04925}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 985 \, \text{Pa} \approx 0.985 \, \text{kPa}
$$

**Interpretation:** A pressure of less than ~1 kPa is sufficient to burst open the valve. This is in the ideal range for finger-actuated, **power-free** point-of-care devices.

## 3.2 Derivation: Hagen-Poiseuille Flow Through a Circular Microchannel

This is the foundation for understanding the **flow resistance** offered by any microvalve when open.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Governing Equation (Navier-Stokes Simplified)

For steady, incompressible, fully developed, laminar flow in a circular tube, the Navier-Stokes equation reduces to:

$$
\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dz}
$$

**Logic:** Inertial terms vanish because $Re \ll 1$. The pressure gradient $dP/dz$ is constant along the channel for fully developed flow.

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Integration (First Pass)

Integrate once with respect to $r$:

$$
r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{r^2}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} + C_1
$$

$$
\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{r}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} + \frac{C_1}{r}
$$

**Logic:** Direct integration, treating $dP/dz$ as constant.

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Apply Boundary Condition 1 (No-Singularity at Center)

At $r = 0$, $v_z$ must be finite. This forces $C_1 = 0$.

$$
\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{r}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dz}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Second Integration

$$
v_z(r) = \frac{r^2}{4\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} + C_2
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Apply Boundary Condition 2 (No-Slip at Wall)

At $r = R$, $v_z = 0$:

$$
0 = \frac{R^2}{4\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = -\frac{R^2}{4\mu} \frac{dP}{dz}
$$

### 3.2.6 Step 6 — Final Velocity Profile (Paraboloid)

$$
v_z(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} \left( r^2 - R^2 \right)
$$

**Logic:** The negative sign of $dP/dz$ (pressure drops in flow direction) gives a positive velocity. The profile is a paraboloid with maximum at the center.

### 3.2.7 Step 7 — Volumetric Flow Rate

Integrate over the cross-section $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$:

$$
Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \, 2\pi r \, dr
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} \int_0^R \left( r^3 - R^2 r \right) dr
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} - \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} \right]_0^R
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} \left( \frac{R^4}{4} - \frac{R^4}{2} \right) = \frac{\pi}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} \left( -\frac{R^4}{4} \right)
$$

### 3.2.8 Step 8 — Replace $dP/dz$ with Finite Difference

$$
\frac{dP}{dz} = \frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{L}
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi R^4}{8 \mu L} \Delta P
$$

**Logic:** This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** — a cornerstone of microfluidic design. Note the **fourth-power dependence on radius**; halving the channel diameter reduces flow by 16×.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Valve Selection (KTU Exam Favorite)

| Application Requirement | Best Valve Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Power-free, low cost, single-use** | Capillary / Hydrophobic passive | No actuator, no electronics |
| **High-speed multiplexing** (genetic analysis) | Pneumatic (Quake) | Sub-100 ms response, scalable arrays |
| **On-chip precision dosing (nL scale)** | Piezoelectric | Sub-nm displacement resolution |
| **High pressure biomedical systems** | Electromagnetic | Robust force, low voltage |
| **Implanted drug delivery** | SMA or Thermopneumatic | Self-contained, body-heat compatible |
| **Harsh environment / vacuum** | Nozzle-Diffuser | No moving parts |
| **CMOS-integrated smart chips** | Electrostatic | Compatible with IC voltages (~5–40 V) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Micro Valve Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Micro Valves] --> B[Passive Valves]
    A --> C[Active Valves]

    B --> B1[Capillary Burst Valve]
    B --> B2[Hydrophobic Valve]
    B --> B3[Nozzle Diffuser Valve]
    B --> B4[Check Valve]

    C --> C1[Pneumatic Quake Valve]
    C --> C2[Thermopneumatic Valve]
    C --> C3[Piezoelectric Valve]
    C --> C4[Electrostatic Valve]
    C --> C5[Electromagnetic Valve]
    C --> C6[SMA Valve]
    C --> C7[Bimetallic Valve]

    B1 --> S1[Geometry Step or Expansion]
    B2 --> S2[Contact Angle Greater than 90 deg]
    B3 --> S3[Asymmetric Channel Profile]
    B4 --> S4[Movable Flap or Ball]

    C1 --> T1[PDMS Membrane Deflection]
    C2 --> T2[Heated Gas Expansion]
    C3 --> T3[PZT Disc Strain]
    C4 --> T4[Coulombic Attraction]
    C5 --> T5[Lorentz Force on Coil]
    C6 --> T6[Nitinol Phase Transition]
    C7 --> T7[Differential Thermal Expansion]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pneumatic Quake Valve Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Src[Gas Pressure Source] --> Reg[Pressure Regulator]
    Reg --> Ctrl[Solenoid Control Valve]
    Ctrl --> GasChan[Gas Control Channel Above Membrane]
    GasChan --> PDMS[Thin PDMS Membrane]
    PDMS --> FluidChan[Fluid Channel Below Membrane]
    FluidChan --> Out[Downstream Microfluidic Network]

    Sens[Pressure Sensor Feedback] --> Ctrl
    Ctrl -. Feedback Loop .-> Sens
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Capillary Burst Valve Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Channel Width w1] --> B[Geometric Step Expansion]
    B --> C[Outlet Channel Width w2 where w2 greater than w1]
    C --> D[Fluid Meniscus Pinned at Step]
    D --> E{Applied Pressure exceeds Burst Pressure}
    E -->|No| F[Valve Closed Flow Blocked]
    E -->|Yes| G[Fluid Advances Valve Opens]
```

## 4.4 Operational Sequence — Hydrophobic Valve Actuation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1 Fluid Approaches Hydrophobic Patch] --> P2[Step 2 Meniscus Pinned at Patch Edge]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Pressure Builds Up Below Burst Threshold]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Pressure Surpasses Pburst]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Fluid Crosses Patch Wets Surface]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Valve Open Flow Continues]
```

> **Mermaid Safety Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `B1`, `C2`, `P1`, `PDMS`) and contain no reserved keywords. Labels are raw text without markdown bold or HTML formatting, ensuring clean rendering in GitHub, VS Code, and most Markdown viewers.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Differentiate between active and passive micro valves. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribute 1.5 marks per sub-point):**

| Aspect | Active Micro Valves | Passive Micro Valves |
|---|---|---|
| External energy | **Required** (electrical, pneumatic, thermal) | **Not required** (self-regulated) |
| Control | **User-triggered** (switchable ON/OFF) | **Fluid-triggered** (pressure-dependent) |
| Complexity | **Higher** (actuator + control circuit) | **Lower** (geometry + surface chemistry) |
| Response time | **Fast** (ms scale) | **Threshold-based** (instant once pressure is reached) |
| Examples | Pneumatic, Piezoelectric, SMA | Capillary burst, Hydrophobic, Nozzle-diffuser |
| Typical use | Lab-on-a-chip, drug delivery implants | Point-of-care, lateral flow assays |

**Examples (1 mark):**
- Active: Pneumatic (Quake) valve, Piezoelectric valve.
- Passive: Capillary burst valve, Hydrophobic valve.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Explain the working principle of a nozzle-diffuser micro valve. Define the diode factor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A nozzle-diffuser valve consists of two converging-diverging sections: a **nozzle** (converging in flow direction) and a **diffuser** (diverging). When fluid flows in the **forward direction** (nozzle-to-diffuser), streamlines remain attached and the pressure drop $\Delta P_{forward}$ is small. When fluid flows in the **reverse direction** (diffuser-to-nozzle), flow separation occurs in the diverging section, creating vortices and a much larger pressure drop $\Delta P_{reverse}$.

The **diode factor** $D$ is defined as:

$$
D = \frac{\Delta P_{reverse}}{\Delta P_{forward}}
$$

For an ideal passive valve, $D \to \infty$, meaning flow is permitted in only one direction. Typical practical $D$ values range from **2 to 10** for micro-fabricated nozzle-diffuser valves.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) With a neat schematic, explain the working of a pneumatic (Quake) micro valve. Derive an expression for the pressure required to fully close the valve in terms of membrane dimensions and material properties. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the working of capillary burst valves and derive an expression for the burst pressure in a rectangular microchannel of height $h$ and width $w$. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Schematic Description (2 marks):**
A pneumatic Quake valve consists of two orthogonal channels fabricated in PDMS — a **fluid channel** (carrying the sample) and a **gas control channel** stacked above it, separated by a thin elastomeric membrane (~10–30 µm thick). The gas channel is connected to an external pressure source via a solenoid.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
When gas pressure is applied to the control channel, the membrane deflects **downward** into the fluid channel. Sufficient pressure fully **pinches off** the fluid channel, stopping flow. On releasing the gas pressure, the elastic PDMS membrane **rebounds** to its original position due to its stored elastic energy, re-opening the fluid channel.

**Derivation of Closing Pressure (3 marks):**

Treat the membrane as a **clamped circular plate** of radius $a$ and thickness $t$, subjected to uniform pressure $P$ on one side, undergoing small deflection $\delta$.

The classical plate-theory (Pister / Gere model) gives the pressure-deflection relation for a clamped circular plate:

$$
P = \frac{16 E t^3 \delta}{3 a^4 (1 - \nu^2)}
$$

**Logic steps:**
1. Apply small-deflection Kirchhoff plate theory.
2. Boundary conditions: zero deflection and zero slope at the clamped edge $r = a$.
3. The bending stiffness $D = \dfrac{Et^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$.
4. Solving the biharmonic equation $\nabla^4 w = P/D$ for axisymmetric loading yields the relation above.

**Closure condition (1 mark):**
The valve fully closes when the central membrane deflection $\delta$ equals the channel depth $h$. Substituting $\delta = h$:

$$
P_{close} = \frac{16 E t^3 h}{3 a^4 (1 - \nu^2)}
$$

For typical PDMS ($E \approx 2$ MPa, $\nu \approx 0.48$, $t = 20$ µm, $a = 100$ µm, $h = 50$ µm), $P_{close} \approx 20$–$40$ kPa — easily supplied by a hand syringe or small pump.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Working of Capillary Burst Valves (3 marks):**
A capillary burst valve exploits the **capillary pressure discontinuity** at a sudden geometric expansion in a microchannel. The fluid meniscus is pinned at the step edge. As long as the applied pressure is less than the **Laplace (capillary) pressure** of the pinned meniscus, the fluid does not advance. Once the applied pressure exceeds the burst pressure, the meniscus is unpinned and the fluid rushes forward into the wider section — the valve "opens."

**Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply the Young-Laplace equation to a meniscus pinned at a step in a rectangular channel of height $h$ and width $w$ (assuming $w \gg h$):

$$
P_{burst} = 2 \gamma \left( \frac{1}{h} + \frac{1}{w} \right) \approx \frac{2 \gamma}{h}
$$

**Step 2:** The pressure in the narrow section is higher than in the wide section by the Laplace jump. The fluid is held back until the external pressure overcomes this jump.

**Step 3:** Therefore, the burst pressure is:

$$
P_{burst} = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{h}
$$

where $\theta$ is the contact angle. For an aqueous fluid in a hydrophilic PDMS channel ($\theta \approx 110°$, $\cos\theta < 0$), the **magnitude** of the burst pressure is:

$$
\vert P_{burst} \vert = \frac{2 \gamma \vert \cos\theta \vert}{h}
$$

**Step 4 — Design implication:** Smaller channel height $h$ → higher burst pressure. Designers tune the geometry and surface energy to set the desired opening threshold without any electronics.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Alternative Choice]

**(a) Explain the working of a piezoelectric micro valve with a diagram. List two advantages and one limitation. (7 marks)**

**(b) Describe hydrophobic micro valves. How is the contact angle related to the wettability and the burst pressure? A hydrophobic patch in a microchannel of width 200 µm and height 50 µm has a contact angle of 120°. Calculate the burst pressure for water at 20 °C. Given: surface tension of water = 72 mN/m, $\cos(120°) = -0.5$. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Working (4 marks):**
A piezoelectric micro valve uses a **PZT (lead zirconate titanate) disc** as the actuator. The disc is mechanically coupled to a valve seat via a plunger or membrane. When a **voltage is applied** across the PZT, the inverse piezoelectric effect produces a small mechanical displacement $\delta = d_{33} V$ (where $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric strain coefficient, typically $300 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V for PZT-5H). The plunger is pushed against the seat, blocking fluid flow. On removing the voltage, the PZT returns to its original shape and a return spring reopens the valve.

**Diagram description (textual):** A fluid inlet channel leads to a seat. Above the seat sits a PZT disc bonded to a plunger. The plunger enters the channel to seal it. A spring provides return force.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Very fast response** (microseconds to milliseconds) — suitable for high-frequency switching.
2. **Precise displacement** — sub-nanometer resolution allows fine flow metering.
3. **Self-locking** — holds position with no power consumption (zero static power).

**Limitation (1 mark):**
1. **Small displacement** (~0.1% strain) — requires mechanical amplification (levers, flextensional structures) to achieve useful valve travel.
2. **Hysteresis** in PZT materials can cause position drift.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Description of Hydrophobic Micro Valves (2 marks):**
A hydrophobic micro valve is a **passive** valve that uses a surface-energy discontinuity to pin an aqueous meniscus. A small patch of the channel is coated with a hydrophobic material (e.g., fluoropolymer, silane-treated glass) with contact angle $\theta > 90°$. The fluid cannot wet the patch and is held back. A burst pressure $P_{burst}$ must be exceeded to force the fluid across the patch.

**Relation between contact angle, wettability, and burst pressure (2 marks):**
- For $\theta < 90°$ (**hydrophilic**), $\cos\theta > 0$. The fluid wets the surface spontaneously. The capillary pressure aids flow — no effective barrier.
- For $\theta > 90°$ (**hydrophobic**), $\cos\theta < 0$. The fluid is repelled. The capillary pressure opposes flow. The **magnitude of burst pressure** is:

$$
P_{burst} = \frac{2 \gamma \vert \cos\theta \vert}{h}
$$

- The **larger the contact angle** (more hydrophobic), the **higher the burst pressure** required to open the valve.

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given:
- $w = 200$ µm $= 200 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $h = 50$ µm $= 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\theta = 120°$, so $\cos(120°) = -0.5$
- $\gamma = 72$ mN/m $= 0.072$ N/m

Since $w > h$, the height dominates the curvature. Using the rectangular-channel form (taking the smaller dimension $h$):

$$
P_{burst} = \frac{2 \gamma \vert \cos\theta \vert}{h}
$$

$$
P_{burst} = \frac{2 \times 0.072 \times \vert -0.5 \vert}{50 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
P_{burst} = \frac{2 \times 0.072 \times 0.5}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.072}{50 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
P_{burst} = \frac{0.072}{0.00005} = 1440 \, \text{Pa} = 1.44 \, \text{kPa}
$$

**Answer:** The burst pressure is **1.44 kPa** (or 1440 Pa).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse burst pressure direction with wettability.** Many students write $P_{burst} = 2\gamma \cos\theta / h$ *without* taking the absolute value and lose 1 mark for not clarifying the physical meaning.
> 2. **Hagen-Poiseuille is for circular channels only.** For rectangular channels, the full infinite-series form or the approximation $Q \approx \Delta P \, w h^3 / (12 \mu L) \cdot [1 - 0.63 h/w]$ must be used. Using the circular formula for rectangular microchannels is a **common 2-mark loss**.
> 3. **In the Quake valve derivation,** students frequently forget the **clamped edge boundary condition** (zero slope), which is essential to get the coefficient 16 in the formula. Check this.
> 4. **For nozzle-diffuser valves,** the diode factor is the **ratio of pressure drops**, not flow rates. Mixing these up is a frequent error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Micro valves** are NOT just miniaturized macro valves — surface tension and viscosity dominate over inertia.
- **Two main classes:** **Active** (externally powered) and **Passive** (fluid-energy-driven).
- **Passive valves:** Capillary, Hydrophobic, Nozzle-Diffuser, Check.
- **Active valves:** Pneumatic (Quake), Thermopneumatic, Piezoelectric, Electrostatic, Electromagnetic, SMA, Bimetallic.
- **Master equation 1:** $P_{burst} = 2\gamma \vert \cos\theta \vert / h$ — for hydrophobic and capillary valves.
- **Master equation 2:** $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta P / (8 \mu L)$ — Hagen-Poiseuille for circular microchannels.
- **Master equation 3:** $P_{close} = 16 E t^3 h / [3 a^4 (1 - \nu^2)]$ — Pister model for Quake valve.
- **Key dimensionless numbers:** $Re$ (laminar if < 2300), $Ca$ (capillary forces), $Bo$ (gravity vs. surface tension — usually << 1 at microscale, so gravity is negligible).
- **Selection rule of thumb:**
  - Single-use, low cost → **passive**.
  - High speed, reusable, complex fluid routing → **pneumatic (Quake)**.
  - On-chip integration with electronics → **electrostatic** or **piezoelectric**.
- **Dead volume** is a critical BioMEMS metric — Quake valves have ~nL dead volume, much better than solenoid valves (~µL).
- **Burst pressure scales as $1/h$** — smaller channels = stronger capillary barrier.
- **Flow resistance scales as $1/R^4$** — halving diameter increases resistance 16×; this is why microchannels need high pressures for meaningful flow rates.
- **Surface energy engineering** (plasma treatment, silanization, fluoropolymer coating) is the designer's primary tool for tuning passive valves.
- **Nozzle-diffuser valves are diode-like** — they rectify flow without any moving parts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CAD for MEMS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT743 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOMEMS AND MICROFLUIDICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CAD for MEMS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAD for MEMS (Computer-Aided Design for Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)** is a multi-domain, multi-physics design framework that combines **process simulation**, **device-level physical simulation**, **mask layout**, and **system-level behavioral modeling** to predict the fabrication outcome, mechanical response, electrical behaviour, and coupled energy interactions of micro-scale devices before they are physically manufactured.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, MEMS CAD is treated as a **hierarchical, top-down design environment** that bridges the gap between a behavioral specification (system level) and the GDS-II mask files used in foundry fabrication.

The four classical pillars of MEMS CAD are:

| Pillar | Purpose | Typical Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **System-Level Design** | Behavioural & reduced-order modeling | Simulink, SABER, MEMSBUOY |
| **Device-Level (FEA) Simulation** | Multi-physics field solution | ANSYS, COMSOL, CoventorWare |
| **Process Simulation** | Predicts 3D geometry from recipe | Sentaurus Process, ATHENA |
| **Mask / Physical Layout** | 2D lithographic representation | Cadence, L-Edit, KLayout |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The MEMS Architect's Blueprint Kit"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are an architect designing a **smart house** that *thinks* (electronic control), *moves* (mechanical actuators), *heats up* (thermal effects), and *gets rained on* (fluidic effects) — all at the size of a grain of sand.
>
> A normal civil engineer uses **AutoCAD** to draw beams and walls.
> A MEMS designer uses **MEMS CAD** to draw beams, electrodes, cavities, channels and then *automatically checks* what happens when an applied voltage pulls a beam down, when air squeezes out from a 1 µm gap, and when the joule-heating bends the structure — **all in the same software session**.
>
> In short: **AutoCAD tells you the shape — MEMS CAD tells you the *behaviour*.**

### 1.3 Why MEMS CAD is Fundamentally Different from VLSI CAD

Standard IC CAD only solves **electrons in silicon**. MEMS devices, however, couple **multiple energy domains simultaneously**:

- **Electrical** (voltage, current, charge)
- **Mechanical** (force, displacement, stress, strain)
- **Thermal** (heat conduction, Joule heating)
- **Fluidic** (pressure, flow, viscosity)
- **Magnetic** (Lorentz force)
- **Optical / Chemical / Biological** (in BioMEMS)

This **multi-physics coupling** is the central reason MEMS CAD tools are far more complex than conventional EDA (Electronic Design Automation) tools.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants Used in MEMS CAD

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants appear in almost every MEMS CAD derivation:
> - **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{F/m}$
> - **Permittivity of vacuum/air gap:** $\varepsilon \approx \varepsilon_0$
> - **Young's Modulus of Silicon:** $E_{Si} = 130 \text{ – } 190 \; \text{GPa}$ (orientation dependent)
> - **Density of Silicon:** $\rho_{Si} = 2330 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$
> - **Viscosity of air at 300 K:** $\mu_{air} \approx 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{Pa·s}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The MEMS Design Flow (Top-Down)

A typical KTU-style MEMS CAD flow proceeds through four levels of abstraction:

1. **Specification Level** — Define inputs, outputs, constraints (e.g., "design a pressure sensor with 1 mV/Pa sensitivity").
2. **System Level** — Build a behavioural model using lumped elements (mass–spring–damper, R–L–C equivalents). Verify stability and bandwidth.
3. **Device Level** — Translate behaviour to 3D geometry. Solve coupled PDEs (Poisson + Navier–Stokes + Heat equation) using FEA / BEM.
4. **Process / Mask Level** — Generate fabrication recipe. Run DRC (Design Rule Check) and LVS (Layout vs Schematic).

### 2.2 Energy-Domain Modelling — The Analogy Principle

The most elegant theory in MEMS CAD treats **all physical domains as energy systems** and uses the **Hamiltonian / Lagrangian formalism**. Two canonical analogies are:

#### (a) Force–Current Analogy (Mobility Analogy)
- Force $F \;\leftrightarrow\;$ Current $I$
- Velocity $v \;\leftrightarrow\;$ Voltage $V$
- Mass $m \;\leftrightarrow\;$ Capacitance $C$
- Damper $b \;\leftrightarrow\;$ Conductance $G = 1/R$
- Spring $k \;\leftrightarrow\;$ Inverse Inductance $1/L$

#### (b) Force–Voltage Analogy (Impedance Analogy)
- Force $F \;\leftrightarrow\;$ Voltage $V$
- Velocity $v \;\leftrightarrow\;$ Current $I$
- Mass $m \;\leftrightarrow\;$ Inductance $L$
- Damper $b \;\leftrightarrow\;$ Resistance $R$
- Spring $k \;\leftrightarrow\;$ Inverse Capacitance $1/C$

> [!NOTE]
> The **Force–Current analogy** is more widely used in MEMS CAD because it leads to a **parallel RLC circuit**, which is intuitive for mechanical resonators with squeeze-film damping.

### 2.3 Reduced-Order Modelling (ROM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> FEA gives a system of $\sim 10^4$–$10^6$ DOF (degrees of freedom). For system-level simulation, we need a **compact model** with only $\sim 5$–$10$ DOF.
>
> This compression is called **Reduced-Order Modelling (ROM)**. Methods include:
> - **Modal superposition** (Karhunen–Loève / POD)
> - **Arnoldi / Lanczos reduction**
> - **Energy-based lumped element extraction (e.g., MEMSBUOY)**

### 2.4 Process Simulation — The Fabrication Translator

Process simulation predicts the **3D geometry** produced by a sequence of fabrication steps (oxidation, deposition, etching, doping, lithography). The output is a **discretized mesh** fed directly into the device-level FEA solver — achieving a true **process-to-performance** link.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Domain | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's 2nd law (mechanical) | $F = m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx$ | Mechanical | N |
| 2 | Equivalent circuit (Force–Current) | $I = C\dot{V} + \tfrac{1}{R}V + \tfrac{1}{L}\!\int V\,dt$ | Electrical | A |
| 3 | Parallel-plate capacitance | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | Electrostatic | F |
| 4 | Electrostatic force on plate | $F_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{d^2}$ | Coupled | N |
| 5 | Squeeze-film damping coeff. (Reynolds) | $b = \dfrac{\mu A_g^2}{\beta \, d_0^3}$ | Fluidic | N·s/m |
| 6 | Natural frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Mechanical | Hz |
| 7 | Quality factor | $Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{km}}{b}$ | Mechanical | — |
| 8 | Joule heating power | $P = I^2 R$ | Thermal | W |
| 9 | Heat conduction (Fourier) | $q = -k_{th}\nabla T$ | Thermal | W/m² |
| 10 | Mass-spring equivalent of beam | $k = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ | Mechanical | N/m |

> ⚠️ **Note on table syntax:** Absolute value bars inside any of the formulas are written as `\vert` (e.g., $\vert F \vert$) to keep the markdown table rendering safe.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

MEMS CAD enables:
- **Design of pressure sensors** (Bosch BMP series) — predicting diaphragm deflection under pressure.
- **Accelerometers** (STMicro, ADXL) — simulating proof-mass displacement with squeeze-film damping.
- **Inkjet print-heads** (HP, Canon) — coupled fluidic–mechanical–thermal simulation of the firing chamber.
- **BioMEMS** — drug-delivery micropumps, microfluidic channels, lab-on-chip devices.
- **RF MEMS switches** — electromechanical + RF co-simulation to predict pull-in voltage and S-parameters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalent Circuit of a Mass–Spring–Damper System (Force–Current Analogy)

**Statement:** A micromechanical structure of effective mass $m$, damping coefficient $b$, and stiffness $k$ is subjected to an external force $F(t)$. Derive its **electrical equivalent circuit** and prove it is a **parallel RLC network**.

**Step 1 — Write the governing mechanical ODE:**

The net force on the mass equals the sum of inertial, damping, and spring forces:

$$F(t) = m\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + b\frac{dx}{dt} + kx$$

**Step 2 — Convert to velocity form** (divide throughout by an arbitrary reference, but more directly, write $v = dx/dt$):

$$F(t) = m\frac{dv}{dt} + b\,v + k\int v\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Force–Current Analogy mapping:**

$$F \;\rightarrow\; I, \quad v \;\rightarrow\; V, \quad m \;\rightarrow\; C, \quad b \;\rightarrow\; \tfrac{1}{R}, \quad k \;\rightarrow\; \tfrac{1}{L}$$

Substituting:

$$I(t) = C\frac{dV}{dt} + \frac{V}{R} + \frac{1}{L}\int V\,dt$$

**Step 4 — Identify the circuit elements from KCL:**

Kirchhoff's Current Law applied to a node with three parallel branches gives the *same* equation:
- Capacitor branch current: $I_C = C\dfrac{dV}{dt}$
- Resistor branch current: $I_R = \dfrac{V}{R}$
- Inductor branch current: $I_L = \dfrac{1}{L}\!\int V\,dt$

$$I_{total} = I_C + I_R + I_L$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** Hence a mechanical mass–spring–damper is **exactly equivalent to a parallel RLC circuit**:

$$\boxed{\;\text{Mechanical resonator} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Parallel RLC network}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the single most important result in MEMS CAD — it is the theoretical foundation of all reduced-order behavioural simulators such as **MEMSBUOY, SABER, and Simulink-based MEMS blocksets**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Electromechanical Coupling in a Parallel-Plate Capacitor Actuator

**Statement:** A fixed plate and a movable plate of area $A$ separated by a gap $d(x) = d_0 - x$ form a voltage-controlled actuator. Derive the **electrostatic force** and show how the mechanical and electrical domains couple.

**Step 1 — Capacitance as a function of displacement:**

$$C(x) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - x}$$

**Step 2 — Stored electrostatic co-energy:**

$$W_e^{\prime} = \frac{1}{2} C(x) V^2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2(d_0 - x)}$$

**Step 3 — Electrostatic force from co-energy gradient:**

The force on the movable plate (positive = attractive) is:

$$F_e = \frac{\partial W_e^{\prime}}{\partial x}\bigg|_{V=\text{const}}$$

**Step 4 — Carry out the differentiation:**

$$F_e = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2(d_0 - x)}\right]$$

$$F_e = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[(d_0 - x)^{-1}\right]$$

$$F_e = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2} \cdot \left[+\frac{1}{(d_0 - x)^2}\right]$$

$$\boxed{\;F_e(x) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2(d_0 - x)^2}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Linearization around the rest position** ($x \ll d_0$):

$$F_e \approx \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d_0^{\,2}} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2x}{d_0}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Coupled equation of motion** (combining mechanical and electrical domains):

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = \underbrace{\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d_0^{\,2}}}_{F_e^{\,0}} + \underbrace{\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{d_0^{\,3}}}_{k_e}\,x$$

> [!NOTE]
> The term $k_e$ acts as a **negative electrical stiffness**, which destabilises the system at the **pull-in voltage** $V_{PI} \approx \sqrt{8 k d_0^{\,3}/(27\,\varepsilon_0 A)}$. This is the textbook failure mechanism every MEMS CAD tool must predict.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Natural Frequency of a Clamped–Clamped Beam

**Statement:** A beam of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, density $\rho$, and Young's modulus $E$ is fixed at both ends. Derive its fundamental resonance frequency — the canonical benchmark for MEMS CAD validation.

**Step 1 — Equivalent spring stiffness** (from beam mechanics):

$$k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent moving mass** (using Rayleigh's method with the first mode shape):

$$m_{eq} = 0.735 \,\rho\, L\, w\, t$$

**Step 3 — Natural frequency:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eq}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{E w t^3 / (4 L^3)}{0.735\,\rho L w t}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{E\,t^2}{4 \times 0.735\,\rho\,L^4}} = \frac{t}{2\pi L^2}\sqrt{\frac{E}{2.94\,\rho}}$$

$$\boxed{\;f_0 = \frac{t}{2\pi L^2}\sqrt{\frac{E}{2.94\,\rho}}\;}$$

This expression is the **standard sanity check** in KTU board exams whenever a numerical FEA result for a clamped–clamped beam is to be verified.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MEMS CAD Design Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **complete top-down design flow** of a MEMS device using CAD — the very flow KTU examiners expect you to draw or describe in a 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specifications & Requirements] --> B[System Level Behavioural Model]
    B --> C[Reduced Order Model ROM]
    C --> D[3D Geometry Construction]
    D --> E[Process Simulation]
    E --> F[Device Level FEA Solver]
    F --> G[Multi Physics Coupling Check]
    G --> H{Meets Specs?}
    H -- No --> B
    H -- Yes --> I[Mask Layout Generation]
    I --> J[DRC and LVS Verification]
    J --> K[Fabrication Tapeout GDS II]
    B --> L[Energy Domain Library]
    L --> C
    D --> M[Material Database]
    M --> F
    E --> N[Foundry Process Recipe]
    N --> I
```

### 4.2 Multi-Domain Coupling Schematic (Force–Current Analogy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electrical Domain V and I] -->|Couples via Capacitance| B[Electrostatic Force Fe]
    B --> C[Mechanical Domain x F m b k]
    C -->|Back Reaction via dC/dx| A
    C -->|Squeeze Film| D[Fluidic Domain mu and P]
    D --> C
    C -->|Heat Conduction| E[Thermal Domain T]
    E --> A
    A --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> The loop **A → B → C → A** is the *electromechanical* coupling loop that is the central feature of every MEMS CAD solver. The arrows in the **A → E → A** loop form the *electrothermal* coupling (Joule heating and temperature-dependent resistivity).

### 4.3 Process-to-Performance Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Process Recipe Specification] --> P2[Deposition]
    P2 --> P3[Photolithography]
    P3 --> P4[Etching]
    P4 --> P5[3D Meshed Geometry]
    P5 --> P6[Device Simulation FEA]
    P6 --> P7{Performance OK?}
    P7 -- No --> P1
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Mask Generation]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEBT743]**
*Define MEMS CAD. List the four major levels of abstraction used in any MEMS design environment.*

**Model Answer:**

> **MEMS CAD (Computer-Aided Design for Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)** is a unified design framework that allows engineers to model, simulate, and verify the multi-physical behaviour of micro-scale devices before fabrication.

The four major levels of abstraction in MEMS CAD are:
1. **System Level** — Behavioural / lumped-element modelling of the device (e.g., mass–spring–damper equivalent).
2. **Device Level** — 3D finite-element (or boundary-element) simulation of coupled physical fields.
3. **Process Level** — Numerical simulation of fabrication steps to predict the resulting geometry.
4. **Mask / Layout Level** — 2D lithographic representation following foundry design rules (GDS-II output).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEBT743]**
*What is the Force–Current analogy in MEMS CAD? State the mapping of variables.*

**Model Answer:**

> The **Force–Current (mobility) analogy** is an energy-domain mapping that converts a mechanical system into an electrical one so that SPICE-like circuit simulators can analyse MEMS behaviour.

| Mechanical Variable | Electrical Analogue |
|---|---|
| Force $F$ | Current $I$ |
| Velocity $v$ | Voltage $V$ |
| Mass $m$ | Capacitance $C$ |
| Damping $b$ | Conductance $1/R$ |
| Stiffness $k$ | Inverse Inductance $1/L$ |
| Displacement $x$ | Charge $Q$ |

Using this mapping, a mass–spring–damper becomes a **parallel RLC network**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEBT743]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete design flow** used in MEMS CAD. Mention the role of process simulation and reduced-order modelling in this flow.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A clamped–clamped silicon beam has the following parameters: $L = 500 \,\mu\text{m}$, $w = 50 \,\mu\text{m}$, $t = 5 \,\mu\text{m}$, $E = 170 \,\text{GPa}$, $\rho = 2330 \,\text{kg/m}^3$. Compute (i) the equivalent stiffness, (ii) the equivalent mass, and (iii) the fundamental natural frequency.

---

**Model Answer for Q-A (a):**

The MEMS CAD design flow consists of the following sequential stages, represented by the diagram drawn above in Section 4.1:

1. **Specification**: Define performance targets (sensitivity, range, bandwidth).
2. **System-Level Modelling**: Build a behavioural block diagram using energy-domain equivalents (mechanical ↔ electrical ↔ thermal ↔ fluidic).
3. **Reduced-Order Modelling (ROM)**: Compress high-DOF FEA results into a small set of ODEs suitable for SPICE/Simulink simulation. *Purpose:* to enable fast system-level optimisation.
4. **3D Geometry Construction**: Parametric CAD model in CoventorWare, IntelliSuite, or SolidWorks.
5. **Process Simulation**: Tools such as Sentaurus Process predict the post-fabrication geometry from a given recipe (oxidation, etching, doping).
6. **Device-Level FEA**: Solves coupled PDEs (electrostatic, structural, fluidic, thermal) on the resulting mesh.
7. **Verification & Iteration**: If performance is not met, return to the system level and adjust parameters.
8. **Mask Layout & Tapeout**: Final 2D GDS-II output for foundry.

---

**Model Answer for Q-A (b):**

**Given:** $L = 500 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$, $w = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$, $t = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$, $E = 170 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa}$, $\rho = 2330 \,\text{kg/m}^3$.

**(i) Equivalent stiffness:**

$$k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3} = \frac{170 \times 10^9 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times (5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{4 \times (500 \times 10^{-6})^3}$$

Compute numerator:

$$170 \times 10^9 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 125 \times 10^{-18} = 1.0625 \times 10^{-9}$$

Compute denominator:

$$4 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-13} = 5.0 \times 10^{-13}$$

$$k = \frac{1.0625 \times 10^{-9}}{5.0 \times 10^{-13}} = 2125 \,\text{N/m}$$

**(ii) Equivalent mass:**

$$m_{eq} = 0.735 \times \rho L w t = 0.735 \times 2330 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$m_{eq} = 0.735 \times 2330 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-13} = 2.14 \times 10^{-10} \,\text{kg}$$

**(iii) Natural frequency:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eq}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2125}{2.14 \times 10^{-10}}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{9.93 \times 10^{12}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \times 3.151 \times 10^{6}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_0 \approx 501.5 \,\text{kHz}\,}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEBT743]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **electrical equivalent circuit** of a mass–spring–damper system using the **Force–Current analogy**, and identify each element of the resulting network.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A parallel-plate electrostatic actuator has $A = 200 \times 200 \,\mu\text{m}^2$, $d_0 = 2 \,\mu\text{m}$, and is driven by $V = 10\,\text{V}$. Compute the **electrostatic force** at the rest position. Comment on what happens as the gap reduces to $0.5\,\mu\text{m}$.

---

**Model Answer for Q-B (a):**

(See Section 3.1 above for the full derivation.)

Final result: a **parallel RLC circuit** with
- Capacitor $C = m$ (mass)
- Resistor $R = 1/b$ (inverse damping)
- Inductor $L = 1/k$ (inverse stiffness)

The governing equation $F = m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx$ maps exactly to $I = C\dot{V} + (1/R)V + (1/L)\!\int V\,dt$, which is KCL at the top node of a parallel RLC tank.

---

**Model Answer for Q-B (b):**

**Given:** $A = (200 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-8} \,\text{m}^2$, $d_0 = 2 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}$, $V = 10\,\text{V}$.

**At the rest position** ($x = 0$):

$$F_e = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d_0^{\,2}} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times (10)^2}{2 \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

Compute numerator: $8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times 100 = 3.5416 \times 10^{-17}$

Compute denominator: $2 \times 4 \times 10^{-12} = 8 \times 10^{-12}$

$$F_e = \frac{3.5416 \times 10^{-17}}{8 \times 10^{-12}} = 4.43 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{N} = 4.43 \,\mu\text{N}$$

**At $d = 0.5 \,\mu\text{m}$** (i.e. $d_0 - x = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$):

$$F_e = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times 100}{2 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-6})^2} = \frac{3.5416 \times 10^{-17}}{5 \times 10^{-13}}$$

$$F_e = 7.08 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{N} = 70.8 \,\mu\text{N}$$

> The force increases by a factor of **16** when the gap is reduced by a factor of **4** — a non-linear runaway effect that drives the **pull-in instability** in MEMS actuators.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in the formula sheet** — always write $\text{N/m}$, $\text{Hz}$, $\text{F}$, $\text{Pa·s}$.
> 2. **Mixing the two analogies** — students often confuse Force–Current with Force–Voltage. Memorise: in **Force–Current**, *mass becomes a capacitor* (because both store energy in a "potential-like" variable).
> 3. **Skipping the linearisation step** in the electrostatic derivation — the $x/d_0$ term is essential to show *electrical stiffness* and *pull-in*.
